Abstract: Effective multi-task learning for surgical scene understanding is fundamentally hindered by annotation granularity mismatch; temporal workflow tasks such as phase recognition, step recognition and anticipation benefit from dense frame-level supervision, whereas pixel-level spatial tasks including instrument segmentation and action recognition are only sparsely annotated on selected keyframes due to prohibitive labeling costs. This supervision imbalance undermines shared representation learning and limits joint optimization across heterogeneous surgical tasks. To address this, we propose Flow-guided Annotation for Robust Operating Scenes (FAROS), a flow-guided label interpolation framework, that combines zero-shot segmentation-based mask propagation with optical flow estimation to overcome the limitations of appearance-based propagation under challenging surgical conditions such as occlusion, smoke, and motion blur, generating temporally consistent dense pseudo labels from sparse keyframe annotations. The densified instrument masks and action labels are integrated into a unified Transformer-based multi-task framework that jointly learns surgical phase recognition, step recognition, anticipation, instrument segmentation, and action recognition, enabling balanced optimization between dense temporal supervision and sparse spatial supervision. The label interpolation quality of FAROS is first validated on the DAVIS 2017 benchmark under a sparse ground-truth protocol, confirming robust propagation beyond the surgical domain. Extensive experiments on GraSP, MISAW, and AutoLaparo benchmarks further demonstrate that FAROS significantly improves cross-task representation learning and enhances holistic surgical scene understanding performance across spatio-temporal tasks.

</details>


### [27] [FracEvent: Event-Camera Simulation via Fractional-Relaxation Pixel Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26636)
*Langyi Chen,Chuanzhi Xu,Haoxian Zhou,Pengfei Ye,Ziyu Luo,Haodong Chen,Qiang Qu,Xiaoming Chen,Weidong Cai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FracEvent is a new event-camera simulator that models fractional-relaxation voltage dynamics to generate more temporally faithful events, improving downstream task performance such as image reconstruction and optical flow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current event-camera simulators rely on contrast-threshold models with possible additions (noise, filtering, tunable parameters), which can distort the pixel-level temporal structure and timing of events. There is a need for a simulator that preserves the temporal dynamics of each pixel to improve realism and transfer to downstream tasks.

Method: From a log-intensity trajectory, FracEvent drives a compact stack of relaxation modes to produce a continuous voltage state. It emits ON/OFF events by local threshold crossings on this voltage, and updates the reference while retaining the memory modes, linking residual voltage response to future event timing.

Result: Evaluations include event-stream comparison and downstream transfer on image reconstruction and optical flow. Across multiple datasets, FracEvent improves the temporal structure of generated events and achieves stronger downstream-transfer results than competing simulators, demonstrating practical value for event-camera simulation.

Conclusion: FracEvent provides a more faithful pixel-level event model via fractional-relaxation dynamics, yielding better temporal fidelity and improved downstream performance over existing simulators.

Abstract: Event cameras asynchronously report brightness changes with microsecond-level temporal resolution, but real event data remain difficult to collect at scale because specialized sensors, careful synchronization, and task-specific annotations are required. Event-camera simulation is therefore important to event-based vision tasks. Most practical simulators build on contrast-threshold event generation, some with additional filtering, stochastic noise, or hand-tuned sensor parameters. While effective, such formulations often simplify the temporal structure produced by the lifecycle of each pixel, which can distort event timing and weaken downstream transfer. We introduce FracEvent, an event simulator that models this pixel-level lifecycle with fractional-relaxation voltage dynamics. Given a log-intensity trajectory, FracEvent drives a compact stack of relaxation modes, combines their responses into a voltage state, emits ON/OFF events by localizing threshold crossings on the continuous voltage trajectory, and updates the reference while retaining the underlying memory modes. This retained state links residual voltage response to later event timing. We evaluate FracEvent through event-stream comparison and downstream transfer on image reconstruction and optical flow estimation. Across multiple datasets, FracEvent improves the temporal structure of generated events and achieves stronger downstream-transfer results than competing simulator baselines, showing its practical value for event-camera simulation.

</details>


### [28] [LayersReg: A Layer-by-Layer Progressive Regressor for Reliable Intraoperative 3D/2D Registration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26647)
*Xiyuan Wang,Zhenchao Wang,Xinran Chen,Junkai Liu,Chuan Chen,Feng Yin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LayersReg is a progressive, layer-wise regression framework for 3D/2D registration that injects 3D anatomical awareness and performs iterative, feature-space pose searching, improving accuracy under large offsets and multimodality, with real-time capability on X-ray/CT and slice localization tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional optimization-based registration is inefficient and error-prone intraoperatively; existing deep learning methods treat registration as memory-driven pose retrieval and lack task-specific understanding of image alignment, limiting generalization in complex scenarios.

Method: LayersReg introduces a layer-by-layer progressive pose search in feature space, coupled with node-wise regression within a progressive registration framework. It models correlations between moving and fixed images to capture pixel-flow trends and iteratively converge to the correct spatial pose, mirroring iterative classical criteria.

Result: Under large offsets and multimodality, LayersReg achieves high accuracy: X-ray/CT registration 0.68° / 1.41 mm and slice localization 0.73° / 1.55 mm, outperforming state-of-the-art methods while meeting intraoperative real-time demands.

Conclusion: A novel regression paradigm with 3D anatomical awareness and progressive pose search that enhances spatial pose perception and accuracy in challenging registration tasks, with demonstrated real-time performance for intraoperative use.

Abstract: 3D/2D registration serves as a cornerstone technique in surgical navigation. Traditional iterative optimization algorithms suffer from low efficiency and high failure rates in intraoperative settings. Deep learning-based methods reformulate registration from iterative optimization to a regression problem that maps image appearance features to spatial pose, typically achieving improved real-time performance and accuracy. However, such learnable methods are confined to memory-driven retrieval of specific pose features rather than understanding the task of image alignment itself, which limits their generalization in complex scenarios. We propose LayersReg, a pioneering regression paradigm that endows the model with 3D anatomical awareness and searches for the correct pose in a progressive, layer-by-layer manner. Inspired by the iterative pose-searching optimization criterion of classical registration, LayersReg searches for correlations between the moving and fixed images in feature space, capturing the trend of pixel flow and thereby converging iteratively toward the correct spatial pose transformation. We further design a coupling of node-wise regression with the progressive registration framework to enhance the model's perception of spatial pose changes. Experimental results demonstrate that under large offsets and multimodality conditions, LayersReg achieves high accuracy on both X-ray/CT registration (0.68°, 1.41 mm) and slice localization (0.73°, 1.55 mm) tasks, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods while meeting the intraoperative demands for precision and real-time capability.

</details>


### [29] [Disco-LoRA: Disentangled Composition of Content, Style, and Motion for Multi-concept Video Customization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26668)
*Xuancheng Xu,Gengyun Jia,Bing-Kun Bao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage, LoRA-based framework (Disco-LoRA) for multi-concept video customization that jointly controls content, style, and motion in T2V, using Content-Style and Content-Motion disentanglement with an Iterative Dual-LoRA framework and Z-score regularization to align weight distributions for robust composability. Builds a benchmark and demonstrates improved preservation of appearance, style, and motion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The demand to jointly control multiple aspects of video generation (content, style, motion) is unmet by existing image/video stylization or single-concept methods. A unified, disentangled framework with a benchmark is needed to enable flexible, controllable T2V generation.

Method: Two-stage decomposition: (1) Content-Style and Content-Motion, each tackled by an Iterative Dual-LoRA Disentanglement Framework to separate distinct concepts; (2) analysis of layer-wise weight trends to define LoRA identity and weight magnitudes to govern composability; introduces a Z-score-based statistical regularization to align weight distributions across LoRAs, preserving trends while reducing interference.

Result: Extensive experiments indicate Disco-LoRA effectively achieves multi-concept video customization, preserving appearance, style, and motion in controllable text-to-video generation more consistently than baselines.

Conclusion: Disco-LoRA provides a principled approach to disentangle and recombine content, style, and motion in video generation, with a benchmark to facilitate future research and a regularization strategy to maintain stable, interferenceminimized LoRA combinations.

Abstract: Video customization based on Text-to-Video (T2V) models aims to learn specific features from reference data to generate controllable videos. While significant strides have been made in image stylization and video motion customization, simultaneously controlling multiple concepts, such as content, style, and motion, remains a major challenge. In this work, we systematically define the task of multi-concept video customization, which requires the joint control of content, style, and motion. To facilitate research in this area, we construct a comprehensive benchmark and propose Disco-LoRA, a unified framework designed to tackle this problem by disentangling and flexibly recombining different concepts in two stages: (1) We decompose the objective into two sub-tasks: Content-Style and Content-Motion. Each sub-task is addressed using our Iterative Dual-LoRA Disentanglement Framework, which effectively disentangles distinct concepts within the data. (2) We identify layer-wise weight trends as crucial for LoRA identity, while weight magnitudes dictate composability. To harmonize these scales, we propose a Z-score-based statistical regularization that aligns weight distributions, preserving layer-wise trends while minimizing interference between different LoRAs. Extensive experiments show that Disco-LoRA excels in multi-concept video customization, effectively preserving appearance, style, and motion for controllable text-to-video generation.

</details>


### [30] [DeCoFlow: Structural Decomposition of Normalizing Flows for Continual Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26687)
*Hun Im,Jungi Lee,Subeen Cha,Pilsung Kang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Continual anomaly detection for evolving industrial categories using Normalizing Flows with a frozen base and task-specific adapters, achieving state-of-the-art AUROC with zero forgetting under proper routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting in normalizing flows for sequential tasks in industrial anomaly detection while preserving exact density estimation and invertibility.

Method: DeCoFlow decomposes NF subnets into a frozen universal base and task-specific low-rank adapters; leverages affine coupling's validity; adds Task-Specific Alignment, Auxiliary Coupling Layers, Tail-Aware Loss to mitigate rigidity and distribution shifts.

Result: Achieves 98.40% AUROC on MVTec-AD and 93.00% on VisA; zero forgetting (0.00% FM) under correct routing with 2.27M parameters per task.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective continual anomaly detection with preserved NF properties and scalable parameter budget; competitive performance on standard industrial benchmarks.

Abstract: In industrial environments, new product categories arrive sequentially, requiring continual anomaly detection without access to past data. Normalizing Flows (NFs) provide exact density estimation but suffer from catastrophic forgetting as parameter updates across tasks distort the density manifold. While parameter isolation can prevent interference, it must preserve the strict invertibility and Jacobian validity of NFs. To satisfy these requirements, we exploit the inherent property that affine coupling layers maintain transformation validity regardless of subnet parameterization. Based on this, we propose DeCoFlow, which decomposes subnets into a frozen universal base and task-specific low-rank adapters to isolate updates. We further introduce Task-Specific Alignment, Auxiliary Coupling Layers, and Tail-Aware Loss to compensate for frozen-base rigidity. DeCoFlow achieves state-of-the-art image-level AUROCs of 98.40% on MVTec-AD and 93.00% on VisA, while maintaining parameter-level zero forgetting (0.00% FM under correct routing) with only 2.27M parameters per task.

</details>


### [31] [PhysEditWorld: A Large-Scale Dataset Toward Physics-Editable World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26694)
*Bin Hu,Yanwen Ma,Jiehui Huang,Ziliang Zhang,Haoning Wu,Ruicheng Zhang,Yaokun Li,Zijun Wang,Yuechen Zhang,Chun-Mei Tseng,Hanhui Li,Shengju Qian,Jun Zhou,Kaipeng Zhang,Xiaodan Liang,Jiaya Jia,Xiu Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PhysEditWorld is a gravity-focused multimodal dataset with a replay-based UE5 pipeline enabling explicit, controlled physics variation for 12 scenes, enabling gravity-faithful dynamics and scalable controllable world modeling research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current game-world models learn physics implicitly and lack explicit, manipulable variables for designed environments. Introducing a gravity-focused, replayable dataset provides attributable physical variation and a testbed for gravity-aware modeling.

Method: Built on a UE5 replay-and-rendering pipeline: replays initial state, character controller, action sequence, and camera policy under multiple gravity configurations. 12 cinematic UE5 scenes, >100 hours of interactions, >60M rendered frames. Each sample includes synchronized RGB, depth, normals, audio, action traces, camera trajectory, engine states, semantic annotations, and explicit gravity labels.

Result: Initial utility studies show PhysEditWorld improves gravity-faithful dynamics modeling for generative video and world-understanding models, enhances consistency under physical edits, and offers a scalable foundation for controllable world modeling research.

Conclusion: PhysEditWorld provides a practical, scalable platform for explicit physics manipulation in world modeling, enabling gravity-aware evaluation and future expansion to other physical parameters in authored environments.

Abstract: Recent game world models can synthesize visually plausible, action-conditioned rollouts. However, their interaction behaviors often remain limited to exploratory or wandering trajectories, and physical dynamics are typically learned as implicit correlations from data rather than as controllable variables. This limitation hinders their applicability to authored game environments, where physical rules are deliberately designed and require explicit manipulation. We introduce PhysEditWorld, a multimodal dataset with physical parameters, with a primary focus on gravity in this initial version. At its core, PhysEditWorld is built upon a replay paradigm implemented with a UE5 replay-and-rendering pipeline. Each scenario records a normalized action trace and replays the same initial state, character controller, action sequence, and camera policy under multiple gravity configurations, enabling controlled and attributable physical variation. PhysEditWorld contains 12 cinematic UE5 scenes, over 100 hours of gameplay interactions, and more than 60 million rendered rollout frames. Each sample provides synchronized multimodal signals, including RGB, depth, normals, audio, action traces, camera trajectory, engine states, semantic annotations, and explicit gravity labels. We further conduct initial utility studies on both generative video models and world understanding models, demonstrating that PhysEditWorld enables improved gravity-faithful dynamics modeling, enhances consistency under physical edits, and provides a scalable foundation for controllable world modeling research.

</details>


### [32] [Intracranial Aneurysm Classification and Segmentation via Tri-Axial ROI and Multi-Task Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26706)
*Pengcheng Shi,Kaiyuan Yang,Houjing Huang,Jiawei Chen,Yan Lu,Jiaqi Liu,Murong Xu,Bjoern Menze,Xinglin Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-task framework for simultaneous aneurysm and vessel segmentation and aneurysm classification across 13 locations and 4 imaging modalities, using a two-stage 2D ROI extractor and a 3D multi-task nnU-Net with dual decoders, cross-attention, and modality-specific heads; ensemble achieves 2nd place in RSNA 2025 challenge; code and a 3D Slicer plugin are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current automated methods are mainly binary detectors and lack fine-grained anatomical classification or multi-class segmentation across multiple locations and imaging modalities; precise rupture risk assessment and treatment planning require location-aware, modality-robust analysis.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: Stage 1 - fast 2D tri-axial ROI extraction; Stage 2 - 3D multi-task nnU-Net backbone with a dual-decoder to mitigate aneurysm-vessel class imbalance; cross-attention pooling; modality-specific auxiliary heads; multi-label classification for 13 locations; multi-class segmentation for aneurysm and vessel across 13 locations and 4 modalities; two-fold ensemble; code, weights, and a 3D Slicer plugin provided.

Result: Achieved 2nd place in the RSNA 2025 Intracranial Aneurysm Detection challenge; public release of code, model weights, and a 3D Slicer plugin.

Conclusion: The pipeline demonstrates effective handling of class imbalance and heterogeneity via dual decoders and modality-aware heads, enabling fine-grained, multi-task segmentation and localization that can inform rupture risk assessment and treatment planning; promotes reproducibility through publicly available resources.

Abstract: Intracranial aneurysms are often asymptomatic until rupture, which carries high mortality. Rupture risk assessment and treatment planning depend on both aneurysm morphology and anatomical location, yet existing automated methods remain limited to binary detection without fine-grained anatomical classification or multi-class segmentation. We present a multi-task framework that simultaneously performs multi-label classification, multi-class aneurysm segmentation, and multi-class vessel segmentation across 13 anatomical locations and four imaging modalities (CTA, MRA, T2, T1-post). Our two-stage approach combines a fast 2D tri-axial Region of Interest (ROI) extraction method with a 3D multi-task nnU-Net backbone. A dual-decoder design mitigates the extreme volume imbalance between aneurysm and vessel classes, while cross-attention pooling and modality-specific auxiliary heads improve feature learning across heterogeneous inputs. Our two-fold ensemble achieved 2nd place in the RSNA 2025 Intracranial Aneurysm Detection challenge. Code, model weights, and a 3D Slicer plugin are publicly available.

</details>


### [33] [Mask to Concept: Auto-Promptable SAM3 via Efficient Test-Time Concept Embedding Search for Few-Shot Annotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26711)
*Quan Zhou,Shaoqing Zhai,Qiang Hu Jia Chen,Qiang Li,Zhiwei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: M2C adapts SAM3 for medical few-shot segmentation by learning a concept embedding within the frozen model, enabling auto-promptable segmentation and an active human-in-the-loop via Hybrid Uncertainty Estimation, achieving state-of-the-art results with high annotation efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scale medical image annotation by reducing reliance on external matchers or retraining; SAM3's concept segmentation lacks clinical nuance and robust, automatic concept prompting for medical data.

Method: M2C freezes SAM3, initializes a learnable concept embedding, uses it to prompt segmentation, and updates the embedding via gradients to minimize concept segmentation error. A Hybrid Uncertainty Estimation (HUE) module computes prediction entropy and maps concept predictions back to box prompts to detect inconsistency. Uncertain samples are flagged for human correction, and corrected masks are fed back to further refine the concept embedding in a self-enhancing loop.

Result: On medical segmentation benchmarks, M2C achieves state-of-the-art performance in few-shot settings and demonstrates outstanding annotation efficiency, with a code release at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: M2C provides a practical, scalable pathway for accurate medical image labeling by discovering transferable visual concepts inside a frozen foundation model, minimizing expert effort while maintaining high segmentation quality.

Abstract: Transforming foundation segmentation models from human-prompted tools into auto-promptable annotators is critical for scalable medical data annotation. Current methods commonly depend on external feature matchers or auxiliary networks to automate geometric prompting, but introducing architectural overhead and limiting performance scalability. Although SAM3 natively supports concept segmentation via reusable text prompts, its direct use in medical imaging is hindered by a lack of fine-grained clinical knowledge and the ambiguity of human-written descriptions. In this work, we propose Mask to Concept (M2C), an efficient framework that adapts SAM3 for medical few-shot annotation without external modules, parameter retraining, or manual text engineering. Using only a few labeled images, M2C enables SAM3 to automatically search for transferable visual concepts entirely within its frozen architecture: it initializes a learnable concept embedding, uses it to prompt segmentation, and updates the embedding by gradients of minimizing the concept segmentation error. We further introduce a Hybrid Uncertainty Estimation (HUE) module that calculates the prediction entropy and maps concept predictions back to the box prompts, measuring concept-geometry prompting inconsistency. Highly uncertain samples are flagged actively for human correction, and the corrected masks are then fed back to M2C to continuously search for more precise concept embeddings, forming a self-enhancing annotation loop with minimal expert effort. Experiments on medical segmentation benchmarks show that our method achieves SOTA few-shot segmentation performance and outstanding annotation efficiency, offering a practical and efficient pathway toward scalable medical image labeling. Codes are at https://github.com/Huster-Hq/M2C.

</details>


### [34] [Extracting Neural Materials from Multi-view Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26715)
*Kim Youwang,Jon Hasselgren,Peter Kocsis,Andrea Weidlich,Tae-Hyun Oh,Jacob Munkberg*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NeuMatEx introduces a differentiable inverse rendering pipeline to extract spatially varying neural materials from images, using a Large Material Reconstruction Model to initialize and an uncertainty-guided optimization, achieving superior visual quality and material decomposition compared to PBR baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural materials can capture complex specular reflections and scattering efficiently, but acquiring and authoring such materials is challenging due to the nonconvex, high-dimensional latent spaces and sensitivity to lighting.

Method: NeuMatEx combines differentiable inverse rendering with a Large Material Reconstruction Model (LMRM) that directly predicts initial base color, neural material latents, and aleatoric uncertainty from images. An inverse path tracing optimization refines materials, with the predicted uncertainty anchoring high-confidence regions to the LMRM prediction to prevent lighting/specular effects from being baked into materials.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real assets show NeuMatEx extracts complex materials with superior visual quality and material decomposition compared to PBR-based methods.

Conclusion: NeuMatEx provides robust extraction of spatially varying neural materials, leveraging a principled initialization via LMRM and uncertainty-guided optimization to improve robustness against lighting and complex reflectance, outperforming traditional PBR-based approaches.

Abstract: Neural materials can represent complex specular reflections and scattering effects in a compact, universal basis. However, acquiring and authoring such materials remains challenging. We present NeuMatEx, a differentiable inverse rendering method for extracting spatially varying neural materials from images. The nonlinear structure of neural material latent spaces makes optimization with naive inverse rendering infeasible. To address this, we train a Large Material Reconstruction Model (LMRM) that directly predicts initialbase color, neural material latents, and aleatoric uncertainty guides from images. This material prior provides a good initialization and better constrains our subsequent optimization using inverse path tracing. The predicted uncertainty further helps by anchoring high-confidence regions more tightly to the LMRM prediction, preventing lighting and complex specular effects from being baked into materials. Experiments on synthetic and real assets show that NeuMatEx extracts complex materials with better visual quality and material decomposition than PBR-based methods.

</details>


### [35] [Full spectrum Unlearnable Examples via Spectral Equalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26719)
*Jiale Cai,Gezheng Xu,Zhihao Li,Ruiyi Fang,Ruizhi Pu,Di Wu,Qicheng Lao,Charles Ling,Boyu Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper shows that existing unlearnable examples rely on high-frequency perturbations and lose effectiveness under low-pass filtering, and introduces FUSE (Full-spectrum Unlearnable examples via Spectral Equalization) to generate spectrum-agnostic perturbations using Random Spectral Masking (RSM) and Cross-Band Guidance (CBG), achieving robust unlearnability across the full spectrum while preserving image semantics, validated by extensive experiments across datasets and architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current unlearnable examples depend mainly on high-frequency components and degrade under spectral filtering; there is a need for spectrum-agnostic perturbations that induce unlearnability across the full spectrum without compromising data fidelity.

Method: FUSE framework with Random Spectral Masking (RSM) during generator training to randomly remove a contiguous frequency band, enforcing unlearnability in remaining bands; Cross-Band Guidance (CBG) enforces mutual consistency between high- and low-frequency components to enhance low-frequency unlearnability and regulate high-frequency perturbations.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, architectures, and spectral filters demonstrate strong, cross-spectrum protection of models from learning, validating the effectiveness of FUSE.

Conclusion: FUSE enables spectrum-agnostic unlearnable perturbations with improved cross-band consistency and preserved semantic fidelity, addressing the primary limitation of existing unlearnable examples under spectral filtering.

Abstract: Unlearnable examples (UEs) protect training data by injecting imperceptible perturbations so that models fail to extract exploitable representations. In this paper, we reveal that existing UEs exhibit a critical failure once low-pass filtering is applied, indicating that the effective perturbation signals for unlearnability concentrate predominantly in high frequencies. Hence, we argue that reliable UEs should remain effective across the full spectrum. To this end, we propose Full-spectrum Unlearnable examples via Spectral Equalization (FUSE), which aims to generate spectrum-agnostic perturbations by equalizing the contributions from different bands and enforcing cross-band consistency. Specifically, FUSE adopts a Random Spectral Masking (RSM) strategy during generator training, which randomly removes a contiguous frequency band, forcing the remaining bands to maintain unlearnability. In addition, FUSE further integrates Cross-Band Guidance (CBG), which enforces mutual consistency between high- and low-frequency components, thereby further enhancing low-frequency unlearnability and regulating high-frequency perturbations to preserve the semantic fidelity of images. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, architectures, and spectral filtering demonstrate the strong protection achieved by FUSE.

</details>


### [36] [Robust Onion: Peeling Open Vocab Object Detectors Under Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26734)
*Priyank Pathak,Mukilan Karuppasamy,Aaditya Baranwal,Shruti Vyas,Yogesh S Rawat*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Comprehensive empirical study of OV-OD robustness; feature-collapse drives robustness; backbone similarity predicts robustness; image domain dominates; introduces lightweight plug-and-play robustness approach; clarifies prior work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why open-vocabulary detectors degrade under real-world noise, disentangle factors contributing to robustness, and reconcile conflicting robustness reports across datasets and training regimes.

Method: Controlled synthetic degradations applied layer-by-layer to OV-ODs; analyze feature representations for collapse; compare backbones and training variations; cross-dataset robustness (COCO, LVIS, ODinW-13); validate with real-world datasets (BDD100K, WiderFace, VisDRONE) using a lightweight NN & TK0 approach; show parameter efficiency (96x fewer trainable params).

Result: Backbone similarity predicts robustness via similar layer-wise feature-collapse; pretraining, architecture details, and caption supervision have minimal impact; image domain dominates robustness over annotations; ODinW-13's inflated robustness due to object scale; real-world robustness improved with lightweight method; provides explanations for prior observations.

Conclusion: Robustness of OV-ODs is governed mainly by image domain and intrinsic feature-collapse dynamics; propose practical, parameter-efficient robustness augmentation; helps unify understanding across datasets and training settings.

Abstract: The impact of real-world noise on Open Vocabulary Object Detectors (OV-ODs) remains poorly understood due to their architectural complexity. We present our comprehensive analysis Robust Onion, an empirical study that uses controlled synthetic visual degradations to peel OV-ODs layer-by-layer, revealing how, why, and where robustness degrades, systematically analyzing feature collapse. Our findings reveal that models with similar vision backbones exhibit comparable robustness, driven by similar feature collapse at similar layers, while factors such as pretraining strategy, architectural nuances, and caption supervision contribute little. Robustness is primarily governed by the image domain rather than annotations, explaining the similar robustness impact on COCO and LVIS, and why datasets like ODinW-13 can give an impression of inflated robustness due to large, isolated objects. Finally, we validate our insights by improving robustness on real-world BDD100K, WiderFace, and VisDRONE via our lightweight plug-and-play NN & TK0 approach, using 96x fewer trainable parameters than end-to-end training. We also explain the prior works' robustness observations.

</details>


### [37] [Do Image Editing Models Understand Lighting?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26738)
*Tim Küchler,Johann-Friedrich Feiden,Matthias Nießner,Carsten Rother*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces the 3D-anchored Light Probe (3DLP) benchmark with a real-world HDR dataset (1K indoor image pairs) where light probes are toggled on/off, plus region annotations; evaluates image-editing models' lighting plausibility with two new metrics; finds model performance varies and nearly physics-consistent in best cases but with gaps, especially in regions receiving less light; VLMs are unsuitable for pixel-level light transport; dataset and benchmark will be public.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether contemporary generative image editing models encode intrinsic knowledge of real-world lighting and provide a fine-grained, real-world evaluation beyond synthetic data or VLM-based judgments.

Method: Create 1,000 HDR image pairs of diverse indoor scenes with physical light probes turned on/off; annotate regions such as cast shadows and metallic surfaces; design two new evaluation metrics to mitigate AI-induced photographic effects (e.g., white balance adjustments); benchmark multiple state-of-the-art image-editing models on lighting edits; additionally test vision-language models for the task; release the dataset and benchmark.

Result: Model performance varies substantially across methods; differences are smaller for regions with specular highlights; top-performing models align well with real physics but still show imperfections; regions with less light from the probe exhibit higher error rates across models; VLMs fail to deliver reliable pixel-level light-transport analysis; the authors plan to release the benchmark and dataset publicly.

Conclusion: The 3DLP benchmark provides a granular, real-world evaluation of lighting plausibility in image editing and serves as a resource to push toward physics-consistent editing; public release will foster further research and method development.

Abstract: While recent advancements in generative image editing models have achieved stunning visual fidelity, it remains an open question whether these systems possess an intrinsic knowledge of real-world lighting. Existing benchmarks typically evaluate high-level plausibility of perceptual light transport on curated internet imagery, using VLMs or human judgement, or they rely on synthetically generated datasets. In this work, we introduce the 3D-anchored Light Probe (3DLP) benchmark, for which we have captured a new high-fidelity HDR dataset of real-world lighting changes. The dataset consists of 1K image pairs of diverse indoor scenery in which light probes are physically turned on and off. To allow for a granular performance analysis, we annotated specific image regions such as cast shadows or metallic surfaces. With this data, we evaluate a range of state-of-the-art image editing models by measuring how well their light probe edits align with reality. The evaluation uses two new scores to compensate for AI-generated photographic effects, such as adjusted white balance. Our results show that the overall performance of models differs considerably, with differences slightly less pronounced for specular highlights. The best image editing models are remarkably consistent with real-world physics, however, they still leave room for improvement. We observe that image regions that receive less light from the light probe are more prone to errors for all models. Furthermore, building on their success in evaluating macroscopic lighting plausibility, we test VLMs on our task but find that they are unsuitable for pixel-level light transport analysis. We will make the benchmark, together with the real-world dataset, publicly available to encourage future research on this topic.

</details>


### [38] [LiveEdit: Towards Real-Time Diffusion-Based Streaming Video Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26740)
*Xinyu Wang,Chongbo Zhao,Fangneng Zhan,Yue Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Three-stage distillation from a bidirectional foundation model to a fast unidirectional streaming editor, plus an AR-oriented mask cache, enabling stable, frame-by-frame streaming video editing with real-time performance (12.66 FPS) and high fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two core challenges in streaming video editing: long-horizon background/content stability over time, and low latency for real-time interaction, which existing streaming generation methods fail to satisfy due to strict content preservation and region-specific control.

Method: Propose a three-stage distillation pipeline that progressively transfers editing capability from a powerful bidirectional foundation model to an efficient unidirectional streaming editor. Introduce an AR-oriented mask cache to reuse region-related computation across frames and accelerate inference. Operate in a causal, frame-by-frame editing regime and establish a dedicated streaming video editing benchmark.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art visual quality among streaming baselines and drastically increases inference speed to 12.66 FPS, validating real-time interactive and AR applicability. Extensive evaluations support its effectiveness and efficiency.

Conclusion: The three-stage distillation plus region-caching framework enables stable long-horizon edits with high fidelity in a streaming, real-time setting, making practical streaming video editing feasible for interactive and augmented reality applications.

Abstract: Streaming video editing has made rapid progress, yet practical deployment is still limited by two core issues: maintaining stable backgrounds and non-edited regions over time, and achieving the low latency required for real-time interactive scenarios. Meanwhile, recent streaming video generation methods are mostly developed for synthesis and cannot be directly applied to editing due to the strict preservation requirement and region-specific control. In this work, we present a novel streaming video editing framework that performs causal, frame-by-frame editing with strong content preservation and real-time responsiveness. Our key design is a three-stage distillation pipeline that progressively transfers editing capability from a powerful bidirectional foundation model to an efficient unidirectional streaming editor, enabling stable long-horizon edits without sacrificing visual fidelity. To further support real-time deployment, we introduce an AR-oriented mask cache that reuses region-related computation across frames, substantially reducing redundant processing and accelerating inference. Finally, we establish a dedicated benchmark for streaming video editing. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art visual quality among streaming baselines while drastically boosting inference speed to 12.66 FPS, making it suitable for interactive and augmented reality applications.

</details>


### [39] [Depth-Semantic Alignment and Affinity-Guided Fusion for Structured Radar Point Cloud Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26743)
*Amjad Hussain,Xin Qiu,Fuyuan Ai,Yuchen Tan,Zecheng Li,Chunyi Song,Wenjie Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Multimodal vision-radar fusion to generate denser, more accurate radar point clouds by using image semantics and sparse completion, improving object detection/tracking robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Radar point clouds are often sparse, noisy, and incomplete, limiting downstream perception. Integrating image semantic cues can impose structure and align modalities to enhance perception performance.

Method: Fuse vision and radar data, leverage image semantic information to impose structural constraints and achieve spatial alignment of radar points, and apply a sparse completion strategy to increase point density and recover missing structures before evaluation in detection/tracking tasks.

Result: Generated point clouds show improved quality and, when used in perception models, yield higher detection accuracy and robustness in complex environments during object detection and tracking evaluations.

Conclusion: The approach provides a practical, multisensor solution for high-quality point cloud generation that enhances intelligent perception systems.

Abstract: Point clouds are an important carrier of three-dimensional spatial information, and their quality directly affects the performance of downstream perception tasks such as object detection and tracking. However, millimeter-wave radar point clouds are typically sparse, noisy, and structurally incomplete. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a multimodal point cloud generation method based on vision-radar fusion. The proposed method leverages image semantic information to impose structural constraints and achieve spatial alignment for radar point clouds, while incorporating a sparse completion strategy to enhance point density and recover missing structures. The generated point clouds are further evaluated in object detection and tracking tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively improves point cloud quality and enhances the detection accuracy and robustness of perception models in complex environments, providing a practical solution for multisensor point cloud generation and intelligent perception systems.

</details>


### [40] [Capacity-Controlled Multi-View Stylization of 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26754)
*Zhihao Wen,Yixin Yang,Bojian Wu,Yang Zhou,Dani Lischinski,Daniel Cohen-Or,Hui Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A capacity-controlled semi-balanced optimal transport framework for multi-view stylization of 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS), improving cross-view coherence and controlled style feature allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D stylization methods apply 2D per-view style losses, leading to unstable style allocation, many-to-one feature reuse, and poor cross-view consistency in 3DGS.

Method: Formulate local style matching as a semi-balanced OT problem with explicit column-capacity constraints (tunable strength) to mitigate many-to-one matching and control style feature allocation. Add cross-view matching guidance to align scene content and style; and apply geometric regularizations to improve Gaussian primitives for finer textures during stylization.

Result: Extensive experiments show significant improvement in multi-view stylistic consistency, producing stable, expressive 3D stylizations while preserving the scene's semantic structure.

Conclusion: A transport-based objective provides a principled trade-off between feature coverage and stylistic diversity, improving cross-view coherence; cross-view guidance and geometric regularizations further enhance stylization quality.

Abstract: While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) provides an efficient and explicit representation for novel view synthesis, enforcing stylistic coherence across viewpoints remains challenging. Existing 3D stylization methods typically apply 2D feature-matching losses independently per rendered view, which leads to unstable style allocation, many-to-one feature reuse, and limited cross-view consistency. We propose a capacity-controlled framework for multi-view stylization of 3DGS, grounded in optimal transport. Specifically, we reformulate local style matching as a semi-balanced optimal transport problem. By introducing explicit column-capacity constraints with tunable strength, our formulation mitigates many-to-one matching and enables controllable allocation of style features. This transport-based objective provides a principled mechanism for balancing feature coverage and stylistic diversity while maintaining stable correspondences across viewpoints. To further enhance cross-view coherence, we incorporate a novel cross-view matching guidance to constrain correspondences between scene content and style patterns. In addition, we introduce several geometric regularizations to enhance the vanilla 3DGS, thereby enabling optimized Gaussian primitives to represent finer-grained textures during stylization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly improves multi-view stylistic consistency and produces stable, expressive 3D stylizations while preserving the core semantic structure of the scene.

</details>


### [41] [ProtoKV: Streaming Video Understanding under Delayed Query with Summary-State Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26762)
*Le Tu Ngoc Minh,Jinyeong Lim,Dongsu Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ProtoKV introduces a constant-footprint memory for streaming video understanding (SVU) by maintaining an exact near-window key-value cache and a semantic-spatial prototype bank for older content, exposed as bounded pseudo-tokens compatible with standard attention. It delivers up to 12.5-point accuracy gains over token-retention baselines under long-delayed queries with matched budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SVU must answer asynchronously as video tokens stream, but queries can arrive long after content has updated. A fixed memory footprint is needed to prevent eviction of decisive cues and to respect latency budgets; token retention alone scales poorly for long delays.

Method: ProtoKV maintains a fixed-capacity summary state. It preserves an exact near-window KV cache and aggregates older content into a semantic-spatial prototype bank with residual statistics. At query time, each prototype is exposed via a bounded pseudo-token interface that integrates with standard attention.

Result: Under matched budgets and comparable query-time cost, ProtoKV improves accuracy by up to 12.5 points over token-retention baselines on SVU benchmarks in the long-delay regime, with gains that grow as query delay increases.

Conclusion: ProtoKV offers a constant-footprint SVU memory solution that preserves decisive cues through a near-window cache and a prototype-based summarization of far history, enabling accurate answers to delayed queries with compatible attention mechanisms; results show substantial gains in long-delay scenarios.

Abstract: Streaming video understanding (SVU) must answer queries that arrive asynchronously while visual tokens stream continuously under strict GPU-memory and query-time latency budgets. A key challenge is delayed query: decisive cues may appear briefly, yet many subsequent updates occur before the query arrives, increasing the risk that those cues are evicted or diluted under bounded memory. We propose ProtoKV, a constant-footprint SVU memory that represents far history as a fixed-capacity summary state rather than retaining token instances. ProtoKV keeps an exact near-window KV cache and aggregates older content into a semantic-spatial prototype bank with residual statistics. At query time, each prototype is exposed through a bounded pseudo-token interface that is drop-in compatible with standard attention. Under matched budgets and comparable query-time cost, ProtoKV improves accuracy by up to 12.5 points over token-retention baselines on SVU benchmarks in the long-delay regime, with gains that grow as query delay increases.

</details>


### [42] [Calibrated Harmonic Overlaid Implicit Neural Representations for Multi-Dimensional Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26763)
*Honghang Chen,Xiujun Zhang,Xiaoli Sun,Mingqing Xiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CHOIR introduces Coordinated Harmonic Superposition (CHS) to replace traditional function composition in periodic-activation implicit neural representations, improving optimization stability as depth increases. It also adds Perceptual Spectrum Calibration (PSC) to mitigate spectrum bias by enforcing a natural image power-law prior and a log-uniform spectrum. Empirically, CHOIR achieves state-of-the-art results on multidimensional data recovery tasks; code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address optimization instability of deep periodic-INR architectures caused by function composition and the spectrum bias in learned representations. Leverage connections to generalized Fourier series and natural image statistics to enable stable, physically plausible reconstructions.

Method: Introduce Coordinated Harmonic Superposition (CHS) to overlay harmonics across coordinates, replacing recursive function composition used in sine-activated INRs. Implement Perceptual Spectrum Calibration (PSC) to inject a natural power-law spectrum prior and steer the learned spectrum toward a log-uniform distribution, with a calibration module and corresponding loss terms.

Result: Empirical evaluation on various multidimensional data recovery tasks demonstrates superior performance over state-of-the-art methods, with ablations validating the contributions of CHS and PSC. Code is available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: CHOIR provides a stable, high-performing INR framework by combining harmonically layered representations with physics-inspired spectrum calibration, addressing core limitations of prior periodic-activation INRs and aligning learned spectra with natural image statistics.

Abstract: Implicit neural representation (INR) has emerged as a powerful prior for multi-dimensional data (e.g., multispectral images and videos). However, most INR methods employing periodic activation functions (e.g., Sine) predominantly rely on function composition. This mechanism introduces optimization instability as network depth increases, thereby limiting their performance. Meanwhile, these methods fail to incorporate proper physical priors to effectively alleviate spectrum bias. To address these issues, inspired by the commonalities between deep periodic networks and generalized Fourier series, we propose a novel Calibrated Harmonic Overlaid Implicit Neural Representation (CHOIR). Specifically, we utilize Coordinated Harmonic Superposition (CHS) to replace the conventional function composition used in most INRs, thereby ensuring optimization stability when scaling network depth. Furthermore, we introduce a Perceptual Spectrum Calibration (PSC) to mitigate spectrum bias. This calibration embeds the ubiquitous power-law spectrum prior of natural images and adjusts the globally fixed spectrum towards a physically plausible log-uniform distribution. Extensive experiments on various multidimensional data recovery problems demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/chorl0229/CHOIR.

</details>


### [43] [Anatomy-Guided Residual Motion Diffusion for Controllable 4D Cardiac MRI Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26764)
*Yiheng Cao,Gustavo Andrade-Miranda,Jiatian Zhang,Lingxiao Zhao,Xin Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A 4D cardiac MRI data-augmentation framework using a semi-supervised VAE to capture anatomy and segmentation, with cascaded latent diffusion models that separate static anatomy from temporal dynamics for controllable 4D synthesis; improves cross-vendor segmentation with limited annotations; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limited annotated 4D medical data and inter-vendor domain shifts by providing anatomically consistent, controllable 4D data augmentation.

Method: A semi-supervised variational autoencoder learns latent anatomical representations and predicts aligned segmentation masks. Anatomy is disentangled from temporal dynamics via a cascaded latent diffusion model (LDM): a static anatomy LDM conditioned on clinical priors (diagnosis, volumes) and a motion LDM for residual latent motion to ensure temporal coherence in 4D sequences. Evaluated on cine cardiac MRI with cross-dataset experiments and nnU-Net segmentation.

Result: High static anatomy controllability (Pearson r > 0.8) and strong temporal coherence (FVD = 288.08). In cross-vendor tests, synthetic 4D augmentation improves segmentation: overall Dice +1.4% and LV Dice +2.8%; Hausdorff Distance reductions of 3.0 mm (overall) and boundary error reduction of 5.4 mm; code available at GitHub.

Conclusion: The framework offers scalable, controllable 4D medical image synthesis to bolster model robustness under limited annotations and cross-vendor variability.

Abstract: Developing robust artificial intelligence models for 4D (3D + time) medical imaging is constrained by limited annotated data, inter-device domain shifts, and privacy restrictions. To address this, we propose a 4D controllable generative framework for anatomically consistent data augmentation. A semi-supervised variational autoencoder learns a compact latent representation of anatomical volumes while jointly predicting aligned segmentation masks in a unified framework. Anatomical structure is then disentangled from temporal dynamics through a cascaded latent diffusion model (LDM). A static LDM generates subject-specific anatomy conditioned on clinical priors (diagnosis and volumes measures) and a subsequent motion LDM estimates residual latent motions, ensuring strict temporal coherence across the 4D sequence. The proposed approach was evaluated on cine cardiac MRI as a representative 4D imaging application. Experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate high controllability of static anatomy (Pearson r > 0.8) and strong temporal coherence (FVD = 288.08). In cross-vendor generalization experiments, augmenting training sets with synthetic 4D sequences significantly improves downstream segmentation performance. Using nnU-Net, the proposed augmentation strategy improves the average Dice score by 1.4% and reduces the Hausdorff Distance by 3.0mm compared to training on real data alone, for the left ventricle, Dice improves by 2.8% with a 5.4mm reduction in boundary error. Overall, this framework provides a scalable and controllable solution for 4D medical image synthesis, supporting the development of more robust models with limited annotations and cross-vendor variability. Code available on https://github.com/cyiheng/4DCardiacMRISynthesis.

</details>


### [44] [LearniBridge: Learnable Calibration of Feature Caching for Diffusion Models Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26778)
*Xuyue Huang,Zhe Chen,Wang Shen,Xiao-Ping Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes LearniBridge, a learnable, low-rank calibration for feature caching in Diffusion Transformers, enabling cross-timestep feature correction with few-shot training and yielding substantial inference speedups on multiple video/image generation benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion Transformers are computationally expensive; existing feature caching uses historical features but suffers severe error accumulation at high acceleration; a unified, efficient calibration across prompts and timesteps is needed.

Method: Introduce LearniBridge, a lightweight calibration module based on LoRA-like updates that bridge multiple timesteps through a shared low-rank subspace, enabling cross-timestep feature correction with select 3–5 training samples; trains adapters that adjust cached intermediate representations.

Result: Achieves up to 5.87×, 5.75×, and 4.10× acceleration on FLUX, HunyuanVideo, and WAN2.1 respectively; on WAN2.1, improves VBench by 1.28% over previous SOTA at 4.10×; code released.

Conclusion: A learnable, low-rank calibration mechanism can effectively correct cached diffusion features across timesteps, enabling large-speedups with minimal data and maintaining quality across image and video generation.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have driven substantial progress in image and video generation but suffer from prohibitive computational costs. Feature caching accelerates inference by reusing intermediate representations. Existing methods rely on historical features for implementation simplicity, yet suffer from severe error accumulation at high acceleration ratios. To address this limitation, we investigate the nature of the requisite feature correction. We demonstrate that the optimal calibration update is characterized by a shared low-rank subspace across diverse prompts. Guided by this structural insight, we propose LearniBridge, a learnable calibration mechanism for feature caching that bridges multiple timesteps through lightweight LoRA updates. This mechanism enables effective calibration requiring only 3-5 training samples. Extensive experiments on image and video generation show that LearniBridge achieves up to $5.87\times$, $5.75\times$, and $4.10\times$ acceleration on FLUX, HunyuanVideo, and WAN2.1, respectively. On WAN2.1, it improves VBench by 1.28% over the previous SOTA at $4.10\times$ acceleration. Our code is available at https://github.com/Iiiiiiirene/LearniBridge.

</details>


### [45] [Event-based Gaze Control System for Accurate Real-time Spin Estimation in Professional Ball Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26780)
*Yunpu Hu,Fabian Schilling,Valentina Cavinato,Asude Aydin,Agis Politis,Ricardo Tapiador Morales,Kirk Y. W. Scheper,Peter Dürr,Naoya Takahashi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An event-based active vision system tracks and measures spin of balls in real time by combining an event camera, high-speed actuation, and a focus-tunable lens; it includes an offline sphere-specific spin estimation (s-CMax) achieving state-of-the-art accuracy across sports, and an online, latency-aware method for table tennis that uses a CNN trained on pseudo-ground-truth spins plus a GPU-accelerated refinement, demonstrated with a three-view setup (high accuracy, low latency, high throughput).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spin affects ball trajectory in ball sports and is difficult to measure with conventional frame-based cameras due to small ball size, high speed, and rapid spin. There is a need for high-temporal-resolution, low-latency spin estimation to enable real-time analysis and feedback in sports.

Method: A hybrid detection/tracking pipeline: 2D event-based detection for centering; 3D ball localization for re-initialization. An offline method (s-CMax) performs contrast maximization on the sphere for spin estimation. An online, latency-aware approach for table tennis uses an uncertainty-aware CNN trained on pseudo-ground-truth spins from the offline method, with a GPU-accelerated batch implementation of contrast maximization for refinement. System hardware includes an event camera, high-speed pan/tilt mirrors, and a focus-tunable telephoto lens. A three-view setup demonstrates real-time tracking. 

Result: Offline sphere-based spin estimation (s-CMax) achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on static balls across table tennis, baseball, tennis, and golf (mean magnitude error 2.1%, axis error 4.0°). The online table tennis method attains 8.8% magnitude error and 6.4° axis error with 3 ms latency and 750 Hz throughput, confirming reliable real-time spin tracking in professional matches.

Conclusion: The proposed system demonstrates that event-based sensing combined with sphere-specific spin estimation yields accurate, low-latency spin measurements across sports and enables real-time tracking in challenging conditions. The hybrid offline-online approach leverages high-accuracy offline spin labels to train fast online estimators, validated here on a three-view setup in professional play.

Abstract: Spin plays a crucial role in many ball sports due to its effect on the trajectory of the ball. Vision-based estimation of the ball's spin during a game with conventional cameras is challenging due to the ball's small size, high speed, and fast rotation. To address these challenges, we propose an event-based active vision system that can track unmodified balls and measure their spin in real-time. The system consists of an event camera for its high temporal resolution and minimal motion blur, high-speed pan/tilt galvanometer mirrors to keep the ball in the field of view, and a low-latency focus-tunable telephoto lens to increase the spatial resolution on the ball and keep it in focus. To track the ball, we use a hybrid approach that combines 2D event-based detection for centering and 3D positions from a ball localization system for re-initialization. For high-accuracy spin estimation, we propose an offline method that performs contrast maximization on the sphere (s-CMax). This method achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on static balls across multiple sports (table tennis, baseball, tennis, and golf), with mean magnitude and axis errors of 2.1% and 4.0 degrees, respectively. We then develop a low-latency online method for table tennis as a case study in real-time applications. This method uses an uncertainty-aware convolutional neural network trained on pseudo-ground-truth spin labels from the offline approach, combined with a GPU-accelerated batch implementation of contrast maximization for refinement. We demonstrate reliable tracking and spin estimation with a three-view setup during professional table tennis matches, with high accuracy (8.8% magnitude and 6.4 degrees axis mismatch), 3 ms latency, and 750 Hz throughput.

</details>


### [46] [ReasonCLIP-58M: Visually Grounded Commonsense Reasoning Supervision for CLIP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26794)
*Sicheng Zhang,Muzammal Naseer,Binzhu Xie,Naufal Suryanto,Shi Qiu,Jamal Bentahar,Naveed Akhtar,Mubarak Shah*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReasonCLIP-58M adds continual reasoning supervision to CLIP-style models to improve visually grounded reasoning and compositionality, using a two-stage training strategy and two datasets (ReasonLite-42M and ReasonPro-16M) plus a diagnostic benchmark (RCLIP-Bench). It serves as a drop-in encoder for multimodal LLMs and achieves gains in reasoning and zero-shot retrieval; code and data released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CLIP-style pretraining emphasizes descriptive image-text alignment, but downstream tasks require visually grounded commonsense reasoning and compositional inference. The work aims to expand CLIP representations to support such reasoning without architectural changes.

Method: A two-stage continual pretraining: stage 1 progressively injects reasoning supervision while preserving descriptive alignment; stage 2 adds category-structured reasoning supervision. Constructed ReasonLite-42M (open-form, visually verifiable reasoning captions) and ReasonPro-16M (category-specific reasoning). Diagnostic benchmark RCLIP-Bench. Train a family of ReasonCLIP; evaluate as a drop-in visual encoder for multimodal LLMs (e.g., LLaVA-NeXT). All resources released.

Result: ReasonCLIP improves visually grounded commonsense and compositional reasoning and enhances zero-shot retrieval. It provides consistent gains as a drop-in encoder without extra inference cost when integrated into multimodal LLM pipelines.

Conclusion: Structured reasoning supervision can augment the expressive capacity of CLIP-style visual representations, enabling better multimodal reasoning without sacrificing descriptive alignment; datasets, models, and training code are publicly available for reproducibility.

Abstract: CLIP and its variants are widely adopted visual backbones in multimodal systems, but their pretraining remains dominated by descriptive image-text alignment. As downstream applications increasingly demand visually grounded commonsense inference and compositional reasoning, it remains unclear whether CLIP-style encoders can support such reasoning without architectural changes. To address this, we present ReasonCLIP-58M, a continual pretraining framework that integrates large-scale reasoning supervision into CLIP-style models through our two-stage strategy, which progressively integrates reasoning signals while preserving descriptive alignment, followed by category-structured reasoning supervision. To support this framework, we construct two complementary datasets and a benchmark: ReasonLite-42M, with open-form, visually verifiable reasoning captions; ReasonPro-16M, with category-specific reasoning supervision; and RCLIP-Bench for diagnostic evaluation of visually grounded reasoning. We train a family of ReasonCLIP that improves visually grounded commonsense and compositional reasoning while also enhancing zero-shot retrieval performance. As a drop-in visual encoder for multimodal large language models such as LLaVA-NeXT, ReasonCLIP delivers consistent gains without additional inference cost, demonstrating that structured reasoning supervision enhances the expressive capacity of CLIP-style visual representations. All datasets, models, and training code are available at https://github.com/RISys-Lab/ReasonCLIP.

</details>


### [47] [NaviCache: Test-Time Self-Calibration Caching for Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26795)
*Zheqi Lv,Zhibo Zhu,Jinke Wang,Qi Tian,Shengyu Zhang,Zhengyu Chen,Chengxi Zang,Zhou Zhao,Fei Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NaviCache is a plug-and-play test-time self-calibration method for video diffusion models that enables error-bounded computation skipping by modeling feature evolution as an inertial navigation system with dual-state estimation and uncertainty-aware updates, achieving better accuracy and overall performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video diffusion models are computationally expensive. Offline calibration-based acceleration suffers from data-dependence, long calibration times, and distribution shifts, while offline calibration-free methods rely on instantaneous zero-order approximations that are vulnerable to observational noise and ignore momentum in the diffusion trajectory. There is a need for a calibration-free, robust, and efficient approach that accounts for non-stationary feature evolution.

Method: NaviCache adopts a dual-state estimation architecture that adaptively tracks the feature change ratio and its latent drift, initialized by an Initial Alignment phase. It reconceptualizes feature evolution as an Inertial Navigation System problem, modeling the coupling between input and output variations. It integrates a time-dependent noise schedule with an uncertainty-aware Measurement Update, enabling error-bounded computation skipping.

Result: Empirical evaluation on HunyuanVideo, Wan, and Open-Sora series shows NaviCache yields more accurate error judgments for computation skipping and achieves strong overall performance.

Conclusion: NaviCache provides a theoretically grounded, plug-and-play solution for error-bounded computation skipping in video diffusion models, bridging the domain gap and non-stationary diffusion dynamics, and enabling robust acceleration.

Abstract: Video Diffusion Models (VDMs) is constrained by immense computational costs. While offline calibration-based acceleration suffers from calibration data dependency, prohibitive calibration duration, and susceptibility to distribution shifts, offline calibration-free methods eliminate these hurdles. However, since they rely on instantaneous zero-order approximations where the mapping between input and output differences varies in real-time, they are susceptible to observational noise and ignore the intrinsic momentum within the diffusion trajectory. In this paper, we propose NaviCache, a plug-and-play test-time self-calibration method re-conceptualizing feature evolution as an Inertial Navigation System (INS) problem. NaviCache bridges the fundamental domain gap and the non-stationary nature of diffusion by modeling the relative coupling between input and output variations. We introduce a dual-state estimation architecture that adaptively tracks the feature change ratio and its latent drift, initialized via a specialized Initial Alignment phase. By integrating a time-dependent noise schedule with an uncertainty-aware Measurement Update mechanism, NaviCache provides a theoretically grounded mechanism for error-bounded computation skipping. Extensive experiments on the HunyuanVideo, Wan, and Open-Sora series demonstrate that NaviCache exhibits more accurate error judgment for computation skipping and achieves outstanding comprehensive performance.

</details>


### [48] [Multi-modality Image Fusion under Adverse Weather: Mask-Guided Feature Restoration and Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26812)
*Xilai Li,Xiaosong Li,Haishu Tan,Tao Ye,Huafeng Li,Hongbin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Mask-guided multi-modality image fusion (AMG-Fuse) for adverse weather. It introduces a Pseudo Ground Truth, a mask generation mechanism to quantify per-modality contribution, and a mask-guided cross-modal attention with degradation-aware learning to balance restoration and interaction, achieving state-of-the-art results on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adverse weather degrades multimodal images, hindering feature learning and necessitating simultaneous restoration and cross-modal fusion. Existing methods struggle to learn robust representations under degradation.

Method: 1) Propose Pseudo Ground Truth to simplify training. 2) Develop a mask generation mechanism that derives modality contributions from the mapping between fused output and source images. 3) Introduce a mask-guided cross-modal cross-attention to selectively attend to informative features. 4) Implement mask-guided learning and task-coupled degradation-aware learning to balance restoration and interaction.

Result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real datasets show the proposed method surpasses state-of-the-art methods in visual quality, quantitative metrics, and downstream tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed mask-guided MMIF framework effectively balances feature restoration and cross-modal interaction, mitigates overfitting to the pseudo ground truth, and demonstrates strong generalization across synthetic and real-world data; source code is provided.

Abstract: Multi-modality image fusion (MMIF) enhances scene representation by exploiting complementary cues from different modalities. Adverse weather, however, causes significant image degradation, disrupting feature representation and requiring simultaneous feature restoration and cross-modal complementarity. Existing methods often struggle with effective representation learning under such conditions, limiting their practical performance. To address these challenges, we propose a mask-guided MMIF method that integrates feature restoration and interaction. We first introduce "Pseudo Ground Truth" to simplify training, promoting faster and more effective feature learning. Then, we design a mask generation mechanism based on the mapping relationship between the fused result and the source images, quantifying the relative contribution of each modality during the fusion process. By incorporating the proposed mask-guided cross-modal cross-attention mechanism, the network is encouraged to selectively attend to informative features during modality interaction, mitigating the risk of overfitting to the static distribution of the "Pseudo Ground Truth". Additionally, we propose a mask-guided learning strategy and a task-coupled degradation-aware learning strategy to balance feature restoration and interaction. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method surpasses state-of-the-art approaches in visual quality, quantitative metrics, and downstream tasks. The source code is available at https://github.com/ixilai/AMG-Fuse.

</details>


### [49] [Learning Adversarial Augmentation Policies for Robust Garlic Seedling Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26828)
*Soeun Lee,Chanho Kim,Yeji Kang,YoungKi Hong,Byeongkeun Kang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Ground-based garlic seedling detector robust to variable outdoor illumination via adversarial augmentation policy learning, achieving AP50 91.6% with no inference-time overhead, plus strong downstream seedling localization performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust seedling detection under severe, spatially heterogeneous outdoor illumination remains underexplored; existing methods rely on extra preprocessing or features and may increase inference cost, hindering practical deployment for real-time monitoring.

Method: Jointly train a stochastic augmentation policy agent with an object detector using adversarial augmentation policy learning. Include a structural penalty to discourage unrealistic distortions while encouraging challenging augmentations during training, achieving illumination robustness without additional inference-time computation.

Result: AP50 of 91.6%, a 0.9 percentage-point improvement over the baseline and 0.2 pp over the previous best. Downstream localization achieves 75.0% precision and 67.0% F1-score, improving baseline by 4.8 and 2.0 pp respectively. No extra inference-time overhead reported.

Conclusion: The framework effectively boosts ground-based garlic seedling detection under complex outdoor lighting, and improves downstream missing seedling localization without adding inference-time cost, demonstrated on a new garlic seedling dataset collected under variable illumination.

Abstract: Accurate seedling detection during early growth stages is essential for timely replanting and effective crop management in precision agriculture. However, existing studies are mostly evaluated under relatively stable imaging conditions, such as UAV imagery or greenhouse environments, leaving robust detection under severe and spatially heterogeneous illumination in ground-based outdoor monitoring insufficiently explored. In addition, many illumination-robust detection methods rely on additional enhancement or feature-extraction modules, which increase inference-time overhead and are not tailored to seedling detection and downstream missing seedling localization. To address these gaps, we construct a new garlic seedling dataset captured using a ground-based monitoring platform under real outdoor field conditions with highly variable illumination. We further propose an illumination-robust seedling detection framework based on adversarial augmentation policy learning. The proposed method jointly optimizes a stochastic augmentation policy agent and an object detector, enabling the detector to learn robust representations under challenging visual conditions. A structural penalty is introduced to prevent unrealistic distortions while encouraging challenging augmentations during training. Extensive experiments show that the proposed approach achieves an AP$_{50}$ of 91.6%, improving the baseline by 0.9 percentage points and outperforming the previous best-performing method by 0.2 percentage points. For downstream missing seedling localization, it achieves 75.0% precision and a 67.0% F1-score, improving the baseline by 4.8 and 2.0 percentage points, respectively. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework for practical ground-based agricultural monitoring under complex outdoor lighting conditions without additional inference-time computational overhead.

</details>


### [50] [Identifying the Unknown: Prompt-Free Open Vocabulary Anomaly Recognition for Robot-Object Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26829)
*Philipp Allgeuer,Jan-Gerrit Habekost,Stefan Wermter*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AnomNOVIC combines a masked autoencoder-based anomaly detector with a prompt-free open vocabulary classifier to achieve strong open-world object detection and classification without predefined candidate lists, showing competitive performance on robot and in-the-wild datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable real-time, open-world object detection for robots with a prompt-free, efficient approach that does not rely on predefined class lists, addressing the gap between open vocabulary methods and deployment efficiency in continuous, real-world settings.

Method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) MAE-based anomaly detection generates generic, object-agnostic bounding boxes; (2) NOVIC provides fast, prompt-free open vocabulary classification on salient regions, allowing class determination without a fixed candidate set.

Result: On NICOL humanoid robot tabletop setup: 47.1% AP / 57.5% AP50 for prompt-free recognition; 59.0% AP / 72.5% AP50 with candidate classes provided. On additional datasets including an in-the-wild test with 48 objects, up to 82.6% prompt-free detection/classification accuracy. Outperforms baselines such as YOLO-World-v2, OWLv2, and YOLOE.

Conclusion: AnomNOVIC demonstrates that combining MAE-based anomaly localization with a fast open vocabulary classifier yields robust, prompt-free object detection and recognition in open-world robotic scenarios, with strong cross-dataset generalization and clear gains over established open-vocabulary baselines.

Abstract: Robots operating in real-world environments must in general be able to recognize previously unseen objects. As robotic systems move toward open-world autonomy, there is a growing, yet largely unmet, need for open vocabulary object detectors that are prompt-free and efficient enough for continuous deployment. We present AnomNOVIC, a two-stage known-workspace framework that combines a masked autoencoder (MAE) trained for anomaly detection, with NOVIC, a powerful real-time prompt-free open vocabulary image classifier. The MAE produces generic object-agnostic bounding boxes, allowing NOVIC to classify salient image regions without requiring a predefined candidate class list. We evaluate AnomNOVIC against strong open vocabulary baselines in a tabletop robot-object environment featuring the NICOL humanoid robot, reaching 47.1% AP / 57.5% AP50 for prompt-free recognition, and 59.0% AP / 72.5% AP50 if class candidates are provided. Across additional datasets, including an in-the-wild test set with 48 unique objects, AnomNOVIC reaches up to 82.6% prompt-free detection and classification accuracy. These results significantly surpass all tested open vocabulary baselines, including YOLO-World-v2, OWLv2, and YOLOE.

</details>


### [51] [Liquid Fusion of Heterogeneous Representations Towards General Salient Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26849)
*Ke Chen,Ling Zhou,Guangqi Jiang,Gengshen Wu,Yi Liu,Shoukun Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LFNet harmonizes CNN and SSM representations for general salient object detection via a Liquid Fusion dynamic gating mechanism and a Saliency-Guided Upsampling operator, achieving SOTA performance across RGB and multi-modal SOD tasks with scalable, efficient design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CNNs and SSMs have complementary spectral biases; dataset-level spectral analysis suggests combining them can improve semantic representation for SOD. There is a need for a framework that dynamically fuses heterogeneous features and preserves spatial details across modalities.
