Method: LFNet introduces a Liquid Fusion mechanism: treat VMamba features (continuous evolving state) and ConvNeXt features (exogenous stimulus) as dynamic states; use a content-aware dynamic gate to fuse them. Extend to multi-modal cues, enabling scalable fusion across modalities. Introduce SGU (Saliency-Guided Upsampling) with spectral-spatial co-design to reduce upsampling artifacts. Two backbones: VMamba and ConvNeXt; end-to-end training; handles five tasks RGB, RGB-D, RGB-T, VSOD, VDT.

Result: Extensive experiments across five tasks show improved performance and favorable efficiency trade-offs; state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks; code released at https://github.com/cke520/LFNet.

Conclusion: LFNet provides a principled, flexible framework to bridge spectral biases between CNNs and SSMs for general SOD, with scalable multi-modal support and competitive accuracy-efficiency balance.

Abstract: General Salient Object Detection (SOD) aims to identify and segment visually interesting objects from uni-modality or multi-modality scenes, recently advanced by cutting-edge State Space Models (SSMs). However, a critical limitation of current approaches is their neglect of the inherent spectral biases exhibited by different neural network paradigms. By digging to the dataset-level spectral analysis of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and SSMs, their semantic representations are inherently complementary based on their complementary frequency preferences. Inspired by this, we harmonize heterogeneous representations from SSMs and CNNs to bridge their spectral biases for general salient object detection. To this end, inspired by the dynamic information propagation of Liquid Neural Networks (LNNs), we introduce a liquid fusion to dynamically integrates features from two backbones, including VMamba and ConvNeXt, referred to Liquid Fusion Network (LFNet). Concretely, by treating the continuous VMamba features and ConvNeXt features as evolving states and exogenous stimulus, respectively, LFNet employs a dynamic gating mechanism for content-aware feature aggregation. Crucially, this state-stimulus paradigm enables to scale to multi-modal cues, resulting in flexibility in general SOD. Besides, a Saliency-Guided Upsampling (SGU) operator to propagate the features to the shallow layer, which leverages a spectral-spatial co-design to suppress upsampling artifacts while preserving semantics. Extensive experiments across five diverse tasks (RGB, RGB-D, RGB-T, VSOD, and VDT) demonstrate that LFNet achieves state-of-the-art performance, offering a superior trade-off between detection accuracy and model efficiency. Code has been released at https://github.com/cke520/LFNet.

</details>


### [52] [Rolling Shutter Relative Pose Estimation Made Practical](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26863)
*Daniel Barath*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A new RS-aware relative pose solver uses affine correspondences to make rolling-shutter two-view geometry practical, solving with 7 ACs by linearizing RS constraints, eliminating RS unknowns, and solving a degree-20 system; it achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on RS data and competitive GS performance, with millisecond runtime and available code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rolling shutter distortions couple with camera motion, making RS relative pose Estimation challenging and requiring many correspondences in existing solvers, which is impractical for RANSAC. A practical RS solver is needed that leverages weaker, richer feature relations.

Method: Introduce affine correspondences (ACs) into RS two-view geometry and derive RS-corrected affine constraints that add two equations per correspondence beyond the standard epipolar constraint. Develop a linearized solver exploiting small RS parameters, eliminate 12 RS unknowns via null-space projection, and solve a degree-20 system via action matrices using 7 ACs, achieving 1.2 ms runtime.

Result: Experimentally, the method achieves the best pose and RS parameter accuracy on the TUM RS benchmark and can also provide accurate translational velocity estimates (unlike other RS solvers). On the GS EuRoC MAV dataset, it shows comparable accuracy to the standard 5-point GS solver.

Conclusion: Affine correspondences enable practical RS relative pose estimation; the proposed RS-corrected affine constraints and linearized solver make robust, fast RS pose estimation feasible, with good generalization to GS data and available code.

Abstract: Rolling shutter (RS) cameras equip virtually all consumer devices, yet RS-aware relative pose estimation has remained impractical: the state-of-the-art solver requires a minimum of 20 point correspondences, making RANSAC-based robust estimation prohibitively expensive due to the exponential dependence of the iteration count on the sample size. We make RS relative pose estimation practical by introducing affine correspondences (ACs) into the RS two-view geometry. We derive novel \emph{RS-corrected affine constraints} that account for the coupling between point perturbations and the row-dependent essential matrix, providing two equations per correspondence beyond the standard epipolar constraint. Building on these constraints, we develop a linearized algebraic solver that estimates pose and RS motion from only 7 ACs. The solver exploits the physical smallness of RS parameters to linearize the constraints, eliminates the 12 RS unknowns via null-space projection, and solves the remaining degree-20 system via action matrices in 1.2\,ms. On the TUM RS benchmark, our method achieves the best pose and RS parameter accuracy among all tested methods and, uniquely among RS solvers, provides accurate translational velocity estimates -- which are poorly conditioned from point correspondences alone due to a $\vec{v}$-$\vec{t}$ coupling. On the global-shutter EuRoC MAV dataset, the solver achieves comparable accuracy to the standard 5-point algorithm, demonstrating that it generalizes well to the GS setting. Code is at https://github.com/danini/rolling_shutter_made_practical.

</details>


### [53] [SpatialFlow-GRPO: Where Spatial Credit Drives Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26872)
*Yankai Yang,Yancheng Long,Wei Chen,Xingyu Lu,Hongyang Wei,Bin Wen,Fan Yang,Tingting Gao,Han Li,Shuo Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SpatialFlow-GRPO, a spatially aware RL framework for image editing that uses region-level rewards and a region-aligned optimization signal to overcome whole-image reward uniformity, improving editing quality across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Whole-image rewards in Flow-GRPO mask region-specific contributions, hindering fine-grained editing; there is a need for region-aware feedback that aligns with latent-space updates.

Method: Proposes SpatialFlow-GRPO: converts region-aware rewards into semantic-region-level optimization signals and aligns region advantages with latent positions during policy updates; trains SFReward; constructs SFReward-14K with region-annotated editing samples; introduces MultiEditBench for evaluation.

Result: On OmniGen2 and FLUX.2-klein-4B, SpatialFlow-GRPO outperforms Flow-GRPO on GEdit-Bench, ImgEdit-Bench, and MultiEditBench.

Conclusion: SpatialFlow-GRPO effectively converts local feedback into spatially aligned update signals and improves editing quality.

Abstract: Recent online reinforcement learning has substantially improved image editing quality. However, existing Flow-GRPO-style methods usually rely on a single whole-image reward, which makes fine-grained editing optimization difficult. We observe that a key obstacle in image editing is this spatial uniformity assumption: a whole-image reward cannot distinguish how different spatial regions contribute to image quality. To address this issue, we propose SpatialFlow-GRPO, a training framework that introduces spatially fine-grained reward feedback. The framework converts region-aware rewards into semantic-region-level optimization signals and aligns region advantages with the corresponding latent positions during policy updates. We also train a region-aware reward model, SFReward, construct SFReward-14K with region-annotated editing samples, and introduce MultiEditBench to evaluate multi-region editing ability. On OmniGen2 and FLUX.2-klein-4B, SpatialFlow-GRPO outperforms Flow-GRPO on GEdit-Bench, ImgEdit-Bench, and MultiEditBench. The results show that SpatialFlow-GRPO converts local feedback into spatially aligned update signals and improves editing quality.

</details>


### [54] [RIS-Assisted Proactive Handover for Reliable mmWave Wireless Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26885)
*Alaa Adnan,Mohammad Al-Quraan,Ahmed Zoha,M. Majid Butt,Sami Muhaidat,Muhammad Ali Imran,Marco Di Renzo,Lina Mohjazi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RIS-assisted proactive handover for mmWave in blockage-prone environments, using offline PSO to optimize RIS element allocation to balance energy and link quality under timing constraints; saves ~10% dissipated energy with 12% fewer elements and achieves 15–30 dB better connectivity in blocked regions without hurting SNR or PHO timing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: mmWave links suffer from LoS blockages; vision-aided handovers help anticipate failures, but when no nearby BS is available, RIS can restore connectivity. RIS configuration time grows with element count, limiting responsiveness in time-sensitive PHO. This work aims to reduce RIS complexity and energy consumption while preserving link quality and timely handovers.

Method: Formulates an end-to-end RIS link optimization problem and solves it offline using particle swarm optimization (PSO) to determine the optimal RIS element allocation and configuration. The offline solution bypasses latency constraints and is evaluated in terms of dissipated energy, SNR, and blocked-region performance.

Result: A 12% reduction in RIS elements yields a 10% reduction in dissipated energy without degrading SNR. The RIS-assisted link provides 15–30 dB improvement in blocked regions while maintaining accurate PHO timing.

Conclusion: Offline PSO-based RIS element optimization can make RIS-assisted PHO energy-efficient and effective in mitigating mmWave blockages, enabling timely handovers with reduced RIS complexity. Potential benefits include enhanced coverage in blocked regions and preserved link quality with lower energy expenditure.

Abstract: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) networks are highly susceptible to line-of-sight (LoS) blockages. Vision-aided wireless communications (VAWC) enable proactive handovers (PHO) to mitigate such blockages; however, PHO becomes challenging when no nearby base station (BS) is available. In such cases, reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS) can be used to restore connectivity. To ensure timely PHO, the RIS configuration time must be taken into account, as the large number of RIS elements can limit responsiveness in time-sensitive scenarios. This work proposes a novel RIS-assisted PHO approach that optimizes the number of allocated RIS elements to balance signal processing complexity and link quality under handover timing constraints, making the RIS-assisted link more energy-efficient. An optimization problem based on particle swarm optimization (PSO) is formulated to determine the optimal end-to-end RIS link setup that runs offline to bypass latency constraints. Results show that reducing the number of RIS elements by 12\% leads to a 10\% decrease in dissipated energy without compromising the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Moreover, the RIS-assisted link achieves a 15--30 dB improvement in blocked regions while maintaining accurate PHO timing.

</details>


### [55] [FlameVQA: A Physically-Grounded UAV Wildfire VQA Benchmark with Radiometric Thermal Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27128)
*Mobin Habibpour,John Spodnik,Niloufar Alipour Talemi,Fatemeh Afghah*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlameVQA is a UAV wildfire VQA benchmark built on FLAME 3 using RGB imagery and radiometric thermal TIFFs, with 34 multiple-choice questions per image across six capability groups; open-source dataset and code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wildfire scenes in UAV data are challenging for RGB-only interpretation due to smoke, scale variation, and occlusion. FlameVQA provides a cross-modal, temperature-grounded reasoning benchmark to advance reliable wildfire intelligence.

Method: Construct FlameVQA from the FLAME 3 dataset by pairing RGB frames with radiometric thermal TIFFs, and generate 34 multiple-choice questions per image spanning six capability groups (detection, localization, distribution/coverage, cross-modal reasoning, and flight planning). Ensure label reliability via MLLM-assisted annotation, deterministic thermal rules, cross-question consistency checks, and human auditing. Evaluate representative MLLMs to establish baselines.

Result: The benchmark yields strong performance when explicit cross-modal cues are available, but notable failures remain for presence detection under heavy smoke and for coverage estimation, indicating current MLLMs require domain-specific adaptation for disaster and wildfire monitoring.

Conclusion: Domain-specific adaptation of MLLMs is needed for effective disaster/wildfire monitoring; FlameVQA dataset and benchmark code are open-source to enable future research.

Abstract: Wildfire monitoring from UAVs requires reliable reasoning over complex aerial scenes, where smoke, scale variation, and occlusions often limit RGB-only interpretation. We introduce FlameVQA, a multiple-choice visual question answering benchmark for UAV-based wildfire intelligence built on FLAME 3, leveraging paired RGB imagery and radiometric thermal TIFFs for temperature-grounded, safety-critical reasoning. FlameVQA includes 34 multiple-choice questions per image spanning six operational capability groups, covering tasks such as detection, localization, distribution/coverage estimation, cross-modal reasoning, and flight planning. To ensure label reliability, we combine MLLM-assisted annotation with deterministic thermal rules and cross-question consistency checks, followed by human auditing. We also evaluate representative MLLMs on FlameVQA to provide baselines for future work. Results show strong performance when explicit cross-modal cues are available, but notable failures on presence detection under heavy smoke and on coverage estimation. These findings suggest that current MLLMs require domain-specific adaptation to better support disaster and wildfire monitoring. The dataset and benchmark code are open-source at github.com/mobiiin/WildFire_VQA

</details>


### [56] [Bridging Vision and Language Concepts through Optimal Transport Semantic Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26891)
*Chenyang Zhang,Anqi Dong,Guangming Zhu,Nuoye Xiong,Siyuan Wang,Lin Mei,Liang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Treats concept alignment as dynamic cross-modal transport rather than static projection, using inverse OT to learn semantic costs and unbalanced OT flow to link visual patches to concepts, achieving better accuracy and faithfulness with velocity-based activations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual–text alignment is central to Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs). Existing vision–language CBMs rely on pre-aligned encoders or global cosine similarity, which misrepresent fine-grained localization and true semantic geometry. A dynamic, geometry-aware matching framework can better capture semantic relationships and transitions between modalities.

Method: 1) Learn a data-driven semantic cost via Inverse Optimal Transport to quantify cross-modal distances between image patches and textual concepts. 2) Apply unbalanced optimal-transport-based flow matching to model semantic transitions from visual patches to textual concepts. 3) Use velocity-based concept activation to produce interpretable geometric relations without requiring ODE integration.

Result: OTF-CBM achieves superior classification accuracy and concept faithfulness compared with existing vision–language CBMs, demonstrating a geometric and dynamical view of interpretable cross-modal reasoning.

Conclusion: Concept alignment is effectively reframed as a geometric, dynamical transport problem. OTF-CBM provides a novel framework for interpretable cross-modal reasoning and opens avenues for further multimodal transport-based CBMs.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) promise transparent reasoning by predicting through human-interpretable concepts, yet their effectiveness fundamentally depends on how well visual and textual representations are aligned or matched. Existing vision-language CBMs often rely on pre-aligned encoders or global cosine similarity, which obscures fine-grained concept localization and fails to reflect true semantic geometry. In this work, we rethink concept alignment as a dynamic cross-modal transport process instead of static projection and propose the Optimal Transport Flow Concept Bottleneck Model (OTF-CBM). It first learns a data-driven semantic cost via Inverse Optimal Transport to measure cross-modal distances, and then performs unbalanced optimal-transport-based flow matching to model semantic transitions between visual patches and textual concepts. With velocity-based concept activation, OTF-CBM captures interpretable geometric relations without ODE integration. Experiments further show that OTF-CBM achieves superior classification accuracy and concept faithfulness, offering a new geometric and dynamical perspective for interpretable cross-modal reasoning.

</details>


### [57] [Modeling Local, Global, and Cross-Modal Context in Multimodal 3D MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26894)
*Minh Duc Do,Tillmann Rheude,Noel Kronenberg,Roland Eils,Benjamin Wild*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MICViT is a 3D Vision Transformer that models intra- and cross-modal interactions for multimodal brain MRI, improving brain age prediction across multiple datasets and modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional 3D multimodal MRI data suffer from limited sample sizes; effective integration of multiple modalities is challenging yet beneficial for clinical interpretation.

Method: MICViT introduces four attention mechanisms: modality-specific local and global attention for intra-modal learning, and cross-modal local and global attention for inter-modal interactions, within a 3D Vision Transformer framework.

Result: MICViT outperforms state-of-the-art CNN and transformer baselines on brain age prediction across UK Biobank, SOOP, and Cam-CAN; gains from adding more modalities (T1, FLAIR, DWI, SWI) and 3D settings.

Conclusion: Explicit modeling of intra- and cross-modal interactions via MICViT is crucial to unlock multimodal brain MRI potential and represents a promising direction for representation learning in neuroimaging.

Abstract: Brain MRI poses a fundamental challenge for machine learning: models must learn from high-dimensional 3D data spanning multiple co-registered modalities, despite the limited sample sizes typical of neuroimaging studies relative to the diversity in anatomy, pathology, and acquisition conditions. While multimodal imaging provides complementary information critical for clinical interpretation, effectively integrating these signals remains difficult. We propose Multimodal Intra- and Cross-Context Vision Transformer (MICViT), a 3D vision transformer that explicitly models both modality-specific representations and cross-modal interactions across local and global contexts. Concretely, MICViT combines four attention mechanisms: modality-specific local and global attention for intra-modal feature learning, and cross-modal local and global attention to capture interactions between modalities. We evaluate MICViT on brain age prediction across three heterogeneous datasets (UK Biobank, n=41,404; SOOP, n=1,062; Cam-CAN, n=613) using multiple MRI modalities (e.g. T1, FLAIR, DWI, SWI). MICViT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art CNN and transformer baselines in 3D settings. Notably, it benefits more strongly from multimodal inputs, yielding larger performance gains as additional modalities are incorporated. These results demonstrate that explicitly modeling intra- and cross-modal interactions is key to unlocking the full potential of multimodal brain MRI, highlighting a promising direction for representation learning in neuroimaging.

</details>


### [58] [OctoSense: Self-Supervised Learning for Multimodal Robot Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27317)
*Anthony Bisulco,Jeremy Wang,Kostas Daniilidis,Randall Balestriero,Pratik Chaudhari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OctoSense introduces a multi-modal sensor platform and dataset with a late-fusion masked autoencoder for self-supervised learning, achieving fast, robust multi-sensor perception that outperforms image-only models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic perception requires robust integration of heterogeneous sensors with different modalities, rates, and noise levels. Large-scale real-world multi-modal datasets are scarce, hindering self-supervised learning for multi-sensor fusion. The work aims to enable fast, robust perception under degraded sensing conditions using self-supervised learning on real data.

Method: Proposes a late-fusion masked autoencoder with modality-specific tokenizers and token caching at inference. Uses OctoSense dataset featuring stereo RGB, event cameras, LiDAR, thermal camera, IMU, RTK-GPS, and proprioception (CAN and joint angles). 59 hours of time-synchronized data across diverse environments, including degraded sensing conditions. Demonstrates multi-modal self-supervised learning on real-world robotics data.

Result: The model operates fast: 6.68 ms on NVIDIA 5090 and 112 ms on Orin NX to compute representations. It outperforms existing image-only foundation models on tasks such as optical flow, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, and ego-motion (translation, rotation, steering). It shows robust performance at night and under sensor degradation.

Conclusion: The approach substantiates the feasibility and benefits of fast, robust multi-modal self-supervised learning for autonomous perception using real-world, heterogeneous sensor data. The OctoSense dataset and code are publicly available for further research.

Abstract: We present OctoSense, an open-source sensor platform with stereo RGB and event cameras, LiDAR, a thermal camera, an inertial measurement unit, RTK-corrected global positioning system, and proprioception (CAN bus data from a car, and joint angles for a quadruped robot). The eponymous OctoSense dataset contains 59 hours of time-synchronized driving data across different types of environments at different times of the day, including situations with highly degraded sensors. We demonstrate multi-modal self-supervised learning using such real-world robotics data, where sensors have different representations, frequencies, latencies and noise. Our approach, a "late-fusion" masked autoencoder, (i) uses modality-specific tokenizers to account for different spatiotemporal characteristics of these sensors, and (ii) caches modality-specific tokens at inference time to process new measurements as they come. This architecture (i) is fast (6.68 ms and 112 ms on NVIDIA 5090 and Orin NX respectively, to compute the representation), (ii) performs better than existing image-only foundation models on tasks such as estimation of optical flow, depth, semantic segmentation, and ego-motion (translation, rotation, and steering angle), and (iii) predicts robustly at nighttime or in situations where sensory data is degraded. See our project page for links to the dataset, code, and supplementary videos: https://abisulco.com/octosense/.

</details>


### [59] [Tractography-Driven Synthetic Data Generation for Fiber Bundle Segmentation in Tracer Histology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26898)
*Kyriaki-Margarita Bintsi,Sparsh Makharia,Yaël Balbastre,Joselyn Romero Avila,Julia F. Lehman,Suzanne N. Haber,Anastasia Yendiki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A synthetic-data augmented framework using ex vivo dMRI tractography as a prior to synthesize training patches for automated fiber bundle segmentation in macaque tracer histology; achieves state-of-the-art–like performance with 3x less manual annotation, but synthetic-only data underperforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce manual histology annotation burden for validating dMRI tractography with gold-standard tracer data, and improve generalization across brains and fiber bundle densities.

Method: Use ex vivo dMRI tractography as a generative prior to synthesize 2D image patches representing fiber bundles; foreground from tractography textures, backgrounds from blockface photos, diversified via domain randomization; train a 2D U-Net on a mix of real and synthetic patches.

Result: On held-out brains, mixed real+synthetic training improves generalization across brains and fiber densities versus real-data-only training; training with synthetic data alone performs poorly; performance is comparable to the state-of-the-art.

Conclusion: Synthetic data augmentation with partial real supervision reduces manual annotation requirements (≈3× fewer annotations) while maintaining accuracy; synthetic data cannot fully replace real labels.

Abstract: Diffusion MRI (dMRI) tractography enables non-invasive reconstruction of white-matter pathways, but its accuracy is fundamentally limited by indirect, low-resolution measurements of axonal organization. Tracer injection studies in non-human primates provide a gold standard for validating dMRI tractography. This, however, requires time-consuming manual annotation of fiber bundles in histology sections. We propose a synthetic-data augmented framework for automated fiber bundle segmentation in macaque tracer histology. Our approach uses ex vivo dMRI tractography as a generative prior to synthesize 2D image patches for training. This provides us with sufficiently realistic foreground texture, which we compose with backgrounds from blockface photos and diversify via domain randomization. A 2D U-Net is trained on mixed real and synthetic patches. Experiments on held-out brains demonstrate improved generalization across brains and fiber bundle densities compared to training with real data only. Training with synthetic data only leads to poor performance, underscoring the need for real supervision. Overall, our approach achieves performance comparable to the state-of-the-art while requiring 3x less manually annotated data.

</details>


### [60] [Confidence-Aware Tool Orchestration for Robust Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26904)
*Yangfan He,Yujin Choi,Jaehong Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Robust-TO, an agentic video understanding framework that accounts for per-frame reliability to mitigate blind trust; uses reliable frames to select tools and weight evidence; achieves improved accuracy on clean and corrupted video reasoning benchmarks with a confidence-aware reward.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video reasoning models assume every frame is equally reliable; real-world perturbations (motion blur, glare, occlusion) degrade evidence without detection; need per-frame reliability-aware reasoning.

Method: Unifies heterogeneous perception tools under a common evidence interface; for each query, selects trustworthy frames using a reliability-relevance score; tools return predictions, temporal grounding, and calibrated reliability; evidence weighted in a three-tier (high/med/low) synthesis; introduces a confidence-cost GRPO reward optimizing correctness, evidence reliability, and efficiency.

Result: On two video reasoning benchmarks across eight tasks: 56.4% average accuracy on clean inputs, +10.6 percentage points vs strongest open-source baseline; outperforms Gemini-2.5-Pro (46.2%). Under five corruptions, 54.3% average accuracy, +5.8 percentage points over strongest open-source; smallest clean-to-corrupted drop among methods.

Conclusion: Per-frame trustworthiness integration yields robustness to common perturbations, improves efficiency and reliability of video reasoning pipelines, and establishes competitive results against strong baselines.

Abstract: Video reasoning language models implicitly assume that every input frame is equally reliable. This leads to what we term the Blind Trust Problem: under realistic perturbations such as motion blur, glare, or occlusion, frontier video reasoning models can suffer 15-30%p accuracy drops on real-world embodied benchmarks, while remaining unaware that their visual evidence has been degraded. To address this challenge, we propose Robust-TO, an agentic video understanding framework that explicitly integrates per-frame trustworthiness into every stage of reasoning. Robust-TO organizes heterogeneous visual perception tools under a unified evidence interface. Each tool receives a sub-query derived from the original question and a set of trustworthy frames selected by the reliability-relevance score. It returns evidence in a shared format: a concrete prediction (e.g., a bounding box, motion trajectory, recognized text, or action label), temporal grounding, and a calibrated reliability score. During reasoning, these calibrated scores guide evidence weighting in a three-tier synthesis process (high/medium/low) and define a confidence-cost GRPO reward that jointly optimizes correctness, evidence reliability, and efficiency. On two video reasoning benchmarks spanning eight tasks, Robust-TO achieves 56.4% average accuracy on clean inputs, surpassing the strongest open-source baseline by 10.6%p and outperforming Gemini-2.5-Pro (46.2%). Under five realistic corruption types, Robust-TO maintains 54.3% average accuracy, 5.8%p above the strongest open-source baseline, while exhibiting the smallest clean-to-corrupted accuracy drop among all compared methods.

</details>


### [61] [Qwen-Image-Agent: Bridging the Context Gap in Real-World Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26907)
*Zekai Zhang,Jiahao Li,Jie Zhang,Kaiyuan Gao,Kun Yan,Lihan Jiang,Ningyuan Tang,Shengming Yin,Tianhe Wu,Xiaoyue Chen,Xiao Xu,Yan Shu,Yanran Zhang,Yixian Xu,Yuxiang Chen,Zhendong Wang,Zihao Liu,Zikai Zhou,Huishuai Zhang,Dongyan Zhao,Chenfei Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Qwen-Image-Agent is a unified agentic framework that bridges the Context Gap in text-to-image generation by integrating planning, reasoning, searching, memory, and feedback; it introduces IA-Bench and reports state-of-the-art results on IA-Bench, Mindbench, and WISE-Verified.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: T2I models often fail to meet real-world requests due to underspecified, implicit, or up-to-date knowledge requirements. A context gap exists between user input and generation context; bridging it requires dynamic acquisition and grounding of external cues.

Method: Proposes Qwen-Image-Agent with Context-Aware Planning to identify missing context and plan its acquisition; Context Grounding to gather context from Reason, Search, Memory, and Feedback; integrates Plan, Reason, Search, Memory, Feedback in a context-centric loop; introduces IA-Bench benchmark with four capabilities (Plan, Reason, Search, Memory).

Result: Experiments on IA-Bench, Mindbench, and WISE-Verified show Qwen-Image-Agent outperforms strong baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: A context-centric agentic framework effectively bridges the Context Gap in image generation and is validated by comprehensive benchmarks, suggesting broad potential for improving T2I systems with agentic strategies.

Abstract: While text-to-image (T2I) models have achieved remarkable progress, they struggle with real-world requests that are often underspecified, implicit, or dependent on up-to-date knowledge. We identify this challenge as the Context Gap: the mismatch between the user context and the sufficient generation context for T2I models. To bridge this gap, we propose Qwen-Image-Agent, a unified agentic framework that integrates plan, reason, search, memory and feedback in a context-centric manner. Qwen-Image-Agent treats user input as partial context and progressively constructs the generation context through Context-Aware Planning and Context Grounding. Specifically, Context-Aware Planning identifies missing context and plans how it should be acquired and used, while Context Grounding gathers this context from reason, search, memory, and feedback. To evaluate agentic image generation, we further introduce Image Agent Bench (IA-Bench), a benchmark covering four core image agent capabilities: Plan, Reason, Search, and Memory. Experiments on IA-Bench, Mindbench and WISE-Verified show that Qwen-Image-Agent outperforms strong baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [62] [PhysRAG: Enhancing Physics-Awareness in Video Generation via Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26916)
*Kexu Cheng,Zicheng Liu,Mingju Gao,Chunhe Song,Hao Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PhysRAG introduces a retrieval-augmented generation pipeline to endow video generation with physical awareness, using a two-stage data-filtered 7K-video subset from WISA-80K and a dedicated physical video database to inject physics into a diffusion model via learnable queries, achieving state-of-the-art on PhyGenBench and VBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome data scarcity and the need to model diverse physical phenomena (thermal, mechanical, optical) in video generation by leveraging retrieval-augmented generation and explicit physical knowledge integration.

Method: Two-stage data filtering on the WISA-80K dataset to curate ~7K high-quality videos; construction of a physical video database; integration of physical knowledge into a video diffusion model through learnable queries within a RAG framework; extensive ablations on data filtering, RAG, and physical-information extraction.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in visual quality and physical-rule compliance on PhyGenBench and VBench; demonstrates effective components via ablations; code/data/models prepared for release at the provided repository.

Conclusion: PhysRAG validates that combining curated physical data, a physical knowledge base, and RAG-based conditioning improves physical realism in video generation, with resources to support further research.

Abstract: Developing physically aware video generation models remains a significant challenge due to the difficulty in capturing diverse physical phenomena, such as thermal dynamics, mechanics, and optics. In this work, we introduce PhysRAG, a novel pipeline that enhances physical awareness in video generation through Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). To address the issue of limited high-quality data, we design a two-stage data filtering pipeline based on the WISA-80K dataset, resulting in a curated set of 7K high-quality videos for training. Furthermore, we construct a physical video database and develop a mechanism to inject physical knowledge into a video diffusion model using learnable queries. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both visual quality and physical rule compliance, surpassing existing models in benchmarks such as PhyGenBench and VBench. We conduct extensive ablation studies to validate the effectiveness of our key components, including the data filtering pipeline, RAG mechanism, and method for physical information extraction. To facilitate future research, our code, data, and models are prepared for release at https://github.com/sediment1024/PhysRAG.

</details>


### [63] [PortraitGen: Exemplar-Driven GRPO with Dual-Reward Guidance for Photorealistic Portrait Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26930)
*Xiaomin Li,Qian Liang,Yinan Li,Ying Zhang,Chen Li,Jing Lyu,Huchuan Lu,Xu Jia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PortraitGen extends GRPO-based post-training for text-to-image by incorporating real images into sampling via inversion and introducing a dual reward system (OmniReward and AI-Portrait) to reduce AI artifacts and improve photorealism in portraits; it also provides PortraitBench for benchmarking, with reported superior results over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL-like GRPO post-training suffers from limited exposure to real images, constraining exploration beyond the original distribution, and from rewards that do not target fine-grained artifacts (e.g., oily skin, AI artifacts), hindering photorealism.

Method: (1) Introduce real images into GRPO sampling groups and use image inversion to estimate their transition probabilities and latents. (2) Implement dual rewards: OmniReward for general quality and AI-Portrait for human-centric fidelity. (3) Create PortraitBench, a portrait-focused benchmark. (4) Conduct extensive experiments comparing with baselines to assess artifact suppression and realism.

Result: PortraitGen significantly outperforms existing baselines, effectively suppressing AI artifacts and achieving unprecedented photorealism in portraits, as demonstrated on PortraitBench through extensive experiments.

Conclusion: The approach addresses GRPO limitations by enabling exposure to real-image distributions and targeted fidelity rewards, yielding higher-fidelity, artifact-free photorealistic portraits and providing a standardized portrait benchmark for future research.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has significantly advanced text-to-image post-training. However, current methods often favor superficial aesthetics, such as over-saturated colors, leaving critical flaws like AI artifacts and biological implausibilities unresolved. We attribute these limitations to two primary factors: (1) The absence of real images during post-training confines GRPO sampling to the original distribution, failing to break inherent generative boundaries; (2) the optimization process lacks specific rewards targeting fine-grained artifacts like overly oily skin and other AI artifacts. To address this, we propose PortraitGen, a novel framework tailored for photorealistic portrait generation. First, we break inherent generative boundaries by directly introducing real images into the GRPO sampling groups, where image inversion is employed to obtain their transition probabilities and latents. Second, to explicitly steer the model toward photorealism, we introduce a complementary dual-reward mechanism: OmniReward for general quality and AI-Portrait for human-centric fidelity. Furthermore, we curate PortraitBench, a comprehensive portrait-centric benchmark. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PortraitGen significantly outperforms existing baselines, effectively suppressing AI artifacts and achieving unprecedented photorealism.

</details>


### [64] [Focusing on What Matters: Saliency-Harnessing Accurate Routing for Diffusion MoE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26938)
*Haoyou Deng,Keyu Yan,Chaojie Mao,Xiang Wang,Yu Liu,Changxin Gao,Nong Sang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SharpMoE is a post-training saliency-guided routing framework for diffusion MoEs that uses clean latent features to guide token routing and introduces a trajectory routing loss to allocate compute along the denoising steps, achieving state-of-the-art performance in visual generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In diffusion MoEs, routers rely on noise-corrupted latent features during denoising, which obscures salient tokens and hinders efficient compute allocation; there is a need for reliable saliency guidance to improve routing decisions.

Method: A post-training framework SharpMoE that uses clean latent features as a noise-free guidance signal for routing, bypassing noise-distorted inputs. It also introduces a trajectory routing loss to constrain compute allocation along the multi-step denoising trajectory, providing precise resource allocation during generation.

Result: Empirical evaluations show SharpMoE improves pretrained, converged MoE models and achieves state-of-the-art performance in visual generation, while remaining plug-and-play and versatile across models.

Conclusion: Providing noise-free saliency guidance and a trajectory-based compute-allocation constraint enhances routing accuracy and efficiency in diffusion MoEs, enabling better performance without retraining from scratch.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have emerged as a powerful paradigm for scaling diffusion models in visual generation. Recent advancements have focused on adaptively allocating computational resources across diverse tokens to improve efficiency and performance. However, we identify a routing assignment problem in existing diffusion MoE frameworks: the router fails to accurately allocate more computational resources to salient tokens. Our analysis attributes this failure to the router's reliance on noise-corrupted latent features throughout the denoising process. Such stochastic noise obscures the critical structural and textural information, thereby preventing the router from effectively distinguishing salient tokens. To address this, we propose SharpMoE, a post-training framework with a saliency-harnessing accurate routing mechanism, which utilizes clean latent features as a noise-free guidance signal for routing. By bypassing the noise-distorted inputs, SharpMoE provides the router with clear saliency guidance, enabling the identification of salient tokens even in high-noise stages. Furthermore, we introduce a trajectory routing loss to constrain the compute allocation throughout the multi-step denoising trajectory, ensuring precise resource allocation along the generation rollout. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SharpMoE serves as a versatile, plug-and-play solution that further enhances the pretrained, converged MoE models, achieving state-of-the-art performance in visual generation.

</details>


### [65] [TraMP-LLaMA: Generative Interpretability with Decoupled Instruction Tuning for Facial Expression Quality Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26942)
*Shuchao Duan,Alan Whone,Hossein Rahmani,Jun Liu,Majid Mirmehdi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TraMP-LLaMA jointly predicts severity scores and generates structured textual reports from facial motion cues for facial expression quality assessment, using RGB appearance and landmark trajectories with decoupled instruction-tuning; introduces PFED5-plus for richer motion descriptions; achieves superior severity prediction and report-generation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FEQA methods mainly output a severity score, offering limited interpretability and making it hard to inspect the motion evidence backing predictions, especially in Parkinson's disease assessment.

Method: A unified multimodal framework (TraMP-LLaMA) that fuses RGB appearance and landmark trajectories to predict severity and generate textual reports; employs a decoupled instruction-tuning strategy to minimize task interference between severity prediction and language generation; extends the PFED5 dataset with expert-guided textual motion descriptions to create PFED5-plus.

Result: TraMP-LLaMA outperforms competitive video-language baselines in report generation and achieves the best severity prediction among compared methods under joint multi-expression training, with Spearman's rank correlation improved by at least 4.39 percentage points across competing methods.

Conclusion: The approach enhances interpretability by providing structured textual motion evidence alongside severity scores, and the PFED5-plus dataset supports this joint task; code and text annotations are publicly available.

Abstract: Existing facial expression quality assessment (FEQA) methods typically produce only a severity score, without explicitly communicating the observable facial motion evidence that supports the prediction. This limits interpretability and makes it difficult to inspect the basis of model outputs in Parkinson's disease assessment. To address this gap, we propose TraMP-LLaMA, a unified multimodal framework that jointly predicts severity scores and generates structured textual reports from facial motion cues. The framework integrates RGB appearance and landmark trajectory cues, and adopts a decoupled instruction-tuning strategy to reduce task interference between severity prediction and language generation. To support this task, we further extend the PFED5 dataset with expert-guided textual motion descriptions and construct PFED5-plus. Experiments on PFED5-plus show that TraMP-LLaMA outperforms competitive video-language baselines in report generation and achieves the best severity prediction performance among the compared methods under joint multi-expression training, improving Spearman's rank correlation by at least 4.39 percent over all competing methods. The text annotations and code are available at https://github.com/shuchaoduan/TraMP-LLaMA.

</details>


### [66] [Scaling Multi-Reference Image Generation with Dynamic Reward Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26947)
*Wenwang Huang,Yusen Fu,Junjie Wang,Mengfei Huang,Yulin Li,Gan Liu,Jing Cai,Yancheng He,Zhuotao Tian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces OmniRef-Bench to evaluate complex MRIG with many mixed-type references; presents DyRef, a two-stage training framework with DAR and DRS, yielding significant gains on OmniRef-Bench and single-image editing benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-reference image generation (MRIG) benchmarks do not adequately capture the complexity of real-world tasks, especially with mixed reference types and large reference sets. Most open-source models struggle in such scenarios, hindering progress.

Method: Construct OmniRef-Bench to cover complex combinations of reference image types and a large number of references. Propose DyRef, a two-stage training framework: (1) supervised fine-tuning to instill basic complex MRIG capability; (2) Difficulty-aware Advantage Reweighting (DAR) to adapt optimization for many mixed-type references and Discriminative Reward Scaling (DRS) to enlarge intra-group reward differences for better policy optimization.

Result: Open-source models exhibit deteriorating performance as the number of mixed-type references increases. DyRef substantially improves performance on OmniRef-Bench and single-image editing benchmarks, indicating strong effectiveness and generalization.

Conclusion: DyRef effectively addresses complex MRIG tasks through a staged training and adaptive objective design, revealing current model gaps and offering a generalizable solution for complex reference-based image generation.

Abstract: While personalized image generation has achieved remarkable progress, multi-reference image generation (MRIG) remains a challenging task. Most existing benchmarks fail to adequately evaluate complex MRIG scenarios, hindering further progress in this area. To better assess model performance on complex MRIG tasks, we introduce OmniRef-Bench, a benchmark that covers complex combinations of reference image types and a large number of reference images. Evaluations on OmniRef-Bench show that mainstream open-source models struggle in complex MRIG scenarios, and their performance deteriorates significantly as the number of mixed-type reference images increases. To address this issue, we propose DyRef, a two-stage training framework. In the first stage, supervised fine-tuning equips the model with the basic capability to handle complex MRIG tasks. In the second stage, we introduce Difficulty-aware Advantage Reweighting (DAR) and Discriminative Reward Scaling (DRS). DAR dynamically adjusts the optimization objective to improve performance when handling a large number of mixed-type reference images. DRS enlarges intra-group reward differences for more effective policy optimization. Experiments demonstrate that DyRef significantly improves the performance of open-source models on OmniRef-Bench and single-image editing benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness and generalization capability of our approach.

</details>


### [67] [Computer Vision for MOBA Analytics: A Dataset and Baseline for Visibility Analysis in Dota 2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26970)
*Ricardo da Rocha Carvalho,Eloísa Oliveira,Luiz Bernardo Martins Kummer,Emerson Cabrera Paraiso,Rayson Laroca*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A video-based visibility-analysis dataset for Dota 2 (Dota2-Vis) using dual-view footage and minimap annotations to track opponent visibility; YOLOv11l (large) best detects player icons on minimaps; reveals visibility-driven patterns not captured by structured MOBA data; dataset/code released publicly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Structured MOBA datasets miss what players can actually see during a match. Dota2-Vis provides video-based visibility signals to complement traditional analytics and improve understanding of information dynamics in professional play.

Method: Collect and annotate 144 matches from The International 2025 from both team perspectives (288 Full HD videos) plus 2,477 manually annotated minimap images. Evaluate several object-detector variants for player-icon detection; use best model to estimate opponent-visible player presence over time and derive visibility curves across players, heroes, roles, and teams.

Result: YOLO11l (large) yielded best performance for detecting minimap player-icons in dense clutter; visibility curves reveal patterns that complement conventional MOBA analytics and highlight behavioral differences difficult to infer from structured data alone; dataset and code are publicly available.

Conclusion: Dota2-Vis provides a scalable, publicly accessible resource for visibility analysis in MOBA matches and establishes a baseline pipeline that translates minimap icons into time-resolved visibility signals to enrich MOBA analytics.

Abstract: Introduction: Most Multiplayer Online Battle Arena (MOBA) analytics studies rely on structured data, which does not directly capture what each team could actually see during a match. Objective: This work introduces Dota2-Vis, a video-based dataset, and a baseline pipeline for visibility analysis in professional Dota 2 matches. Methodology: The dataset comprises all 144 matches from The International 2025, recorded from both team perspectives, totaling 288 Full HD videos, together with 2,477 manually annotated minimap images. We evaluate multiple variants of a modern object detector for player-icon detection and use the best-performing model to estimate opponent-visible player presence over time. Results: YOLO11l (large) achieved the best overall performance, reliably identifying player icons even in dense and visually cluttered minimap scenes. The resulting visibility curves reveal player, hero, role, and team-level patterns that complement conventional MOBA analytics, highlighting behavioral differences that are difficult to obtain from structured data alone. The dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/RicardoRCarvalho/dota2-vis/.

</details>


### [68] [Geometric Gradient Rectification for Safe Open-Set Semi-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26973)
*Jiahe Chen,Qian Shao,Qiyuan Chen,Jiaying He,Jintai Chen,Jian Wu,Hongxia Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GGR is a plug-in gradient rectification method for open-set SSL that aligns auxiliary gradients with the supervised objective by projecting onto an admissible region, with a subspace-aware extension; it improves CIFAR/ImageNet OSSL baselines and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-set semi-supervised learning (OSSL) must leverage unlabeled data containing out-of-distribution (OOD) samples without harming in-distribution (ID) performance. Traditional approaches trade off between aggressive filtering (discarding informative hard ID samples) and using unlabeled objectives (risking conflicting gradients when pseudo labels are wrong). A gradient-space control method can mitigate this conflict.

Method: Geometric Gradient Rectification (GGR) is a plug-in framework that treats the supervised gradient as an anchor and projects auxiliary gradients (from unlabeled objectives) onto an admissible region in gradient space, ensuring first-order non-opposition within the rectified coordinate block while preserving orthogonal components. It includes a subspace-aware rectification extension to stabilize the anchor under noisy mini-batch gradients.

Result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR and ImageNet benchmarks shows that GGR improves representative OSSL baselines in most settings, with gains in both closed-set generalization and open-set robustness.

Conclusion: GGR provides a practical, gradient-level mechanism to balance supervision and unlabeled objectives in OSSL. It avoids harsh filtering and noisy pseudo-labels by rectifying gradients instead, and a subspace-aware variant enhances stability. Code is available at the provided link.

Abstract: Open-set semi-supervised learning aims to leverage unlabeled data that may contain out-of-distribution outliers while maintaining performance on in-distribution classes. Existing methods mainly follow two paradigms: filtering suspicious samples or incorporating unlabeled objectives with soft weighting. We argue that both face a common trade-off: aggressive filtering can discard informative but hard ID samples, whereas utilization can introduce auxiliary gradients that conflict with supervised learning when pseudo labels are wrong. We therefore shift the focus from sample selection to gradient-level control. We propose \textit{Geometric Gradient Rectification} (GGR), a plug-in framework that uses the supervised gradient as an anchor and projects conflicting auxiliary gradients onto an admissible region in gradient space. This makes the applied auxiliary update first-order non-opposing within the rectified coordinate block while preserving orthogonal components that may still carry useful representation signals. We further extend GGR with subspace-aware rectification to stabilize the anchor under noisy mini-batch gradients. Experiments on CIFAR and ImageNet benchmarks show that GGR improves representative OSSL baselines in most settings and yields gains in both closed-set generalization and open-set robustness. Code will be available at https://github.com/JiaheChen2002/GGR.

</details>


### [69] [Unison: Benchmarking Unified Multimodal Models via Synergistic Understanding and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26984)
*Jinyu Liu,Xincheng Shuai,Henghui Ding,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unison introduces a comprehensive benchmark and judge for joint understanding and generation in unified multimodal models, with diagnostic tracks and human-aligned evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations treat understanding and generation separately, failing to capture synergy in unified models.

Method: Construct Unison with 2,169 unified samples across four evaluation dimensions (internal consistency, understanding-guided generation, generation-guided understanding, mutual enhancement); provide unified and decoupled tracks; develop Unison-Judge aligned with human judgments; release code and data.

Result: Systematic evaluations reveal critical limitations in current unified multimodal systems and demonstrate the benchmark's diagnostic and attribution capabilities; Unison enables measuring gains from unified modeling.

Conclusion: Unison offers a holistic, diagnostic, and human-aligned framework to advance unified multimodal research, with open resources to facilitate future work.

Abstract: Unified multimodal models capable of both understanding and generation have achieved remarkable strides. However, despite their unified designs, existing evaluations typically assess understanding and generation capabilities in isolation, overlooking the synergy between comprehension and generation. To bridge this gap, we introduce Unison, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 2,169 high-quality unified task samples, designed to evaluate joint understanding and generation in unified multimodal models. Unison offers three key strengths: 1) Comprehensive Dimensions: Unison encompasses internal consistency, understanding-guided generation, generation-guided understanding, and mutual enhancement to enable holistic evaluation. 2) Diagnostic Evaluation: it provides both unified and decoupled tracks for understanding and generation, allowing fine-grained attribution of failure modes and quantitative analysis of the gains from unified modeling. 3) Human Alignment: we also introduce Unison-Judge, an evaluation model well aligned with human judgments to ensure reliable assessment. Based on systematic evaluations of state-of-the-art models on Unison, we uncover critical limitations in current unified multimodal systems and highlight promising directions for future research. Codes, Unison and Unison-Judge are publicly available at https://github.com/FudanCVL/Unison.

</details>


### [70] [Event-Aware Instructed Assistant for Referring Video Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26994)
*Jinyu Liu,Henghui Ding,Shuting He,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes EVIS for referring video segmentation by decomposing videos into simple events via learnable Event Queries, with event-aware features and Object-Pixel-Hybrid Learning, achieving strong results on 5 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses hallucinations/confusion when treating a video as a single event; natural language cues often segment a video into distinct events; need event-level understanding for robust referring segmentation.

Method: Introduce learnable Event Queries to partition video into simple events; extract event-aware visual-text features for hierarchical understanding; use text guidance; introduce Object-Pixel-Hybrid Learning to track targets in long videos by fusing fine-grained pixel features with prior object queries.

Result: Extensive experiments on 5 public benchmarks show EVIS achieves strong performance for referring video segmentation.

Conclusion: Event-aware, hierarchical decomposition mitigates information overload and reduces hallucinations; improves interpretability and long-term tracking; potential for broader video understanding tasks.

Abstract: Existing referring video segmentation methods often treat a video as a single event consisting of multiple images, overlooking the fact that a video typically contains multiple distinct events. Under such a mechanism, the model needs to directly understand all the complex content in the video and text, which can easily lead to confusion and hallucinations. To address this issue, we propose to decompose a video to a set of simple events by learnable Event Query, and understand complex video content in an event-by-event, easy-to-understand manner. This is based on the observation that natural language expressions often divide a video into distinct, text-related segments, each representing a separate event within a compound event. We introduce EVIS, an Event-Aware Video Instructed Segmentation Assistant, which utilizes text-guided Event Queries to partition a video into simple events, extracting event-aware visual-text features to achieve a hierarchical understanding of the video. Additionally, we propose Object-Pixel-Hybrid Learning, which enables the MLLMs to track targets in long-term videos by integrating fine-grained pixel features with prior object queries. Extensive experimental results on 5 public benchmarks demonstrate EVIS's strong performance in addressing the referring video segmentation task.

</details>


### [71] [On-board Remote-Sensing Foundation Models for Unsupervised Change Detection of Disaster Events](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27018)
*S. Ramírez-Gallego*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unsupervised anomaly detection for Earth Observation using Remote Sensing Foundation Models (RSFMs) with a ResNet + untrained FPN, enabling training-free, on-board, high-resolution change detection across successive satellite passes; aims for generalization and reduced development effort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for autonomous, label-efficient, onboard anomaly/change detection in Earth Observation to maximize limited power and compute, while supporting multiple tasks without bespoke training.

Method: Unsupervised change detection using RSFM + FPN. Detects semantic shifts in latent space between successive orbital passes. Employs an untrained FPN architecture and its priors to enable efficient image-level generation and higher-resolution mapping with minimal or no training (training-free) compared to patch-based, trained methods.

Result: Claims comparable performance to prior, patch-based approaches while eliminating bespoke training; reports efficient image-level generation, higher-resolution mapping, and strong generalization across terrains and sensors.

Conclusion: RSFMs can serve as a versatile, low-training foundation for unsupervised EO change detection, reducing development effort and enabling adaptable, high-performance monitoring across diverse scenes and platforms.

Abstract: Remote Sensing Foundation Models (RSFMs) have emerged as a powerful alternative to supervised models for Earth Observation, allowing satellites to autonomously trigger high-resolution captures or adjust tasking parameters upon detecting an anomaly, thereby maximizing the utility of the mission's limited power and computational resources. RSFMs are versatile, unified encoders that optimize onboard storage for multiple orbital applications while ensuring high-fidelity feature extraction. In particular, unsupervised change detection with RSFMs offers a well-informed and transformative path for disaster monitoring without expensive labels. In this paper, we present a novel unsupervised detection method based on ResNet (RSFM) + FPN which identifies a wide spectrum of anomalies by detecting subtle semantic shifts in the latent space between successive orbital passes. By relying on an untrained FPN architecture and its intrinsic priors, the system achieves efficient image-level generation and higher resolution mapping with minimal effort (training-free) compared to previous proposals (patch-based, trained). And by replacing tailored models with RSFMs, we can achieve comparable results through an approach that eliminates the need for bespoke training and extensive development effort and adds customization, while ensuring high-performance generalization across diverse terrains and sensors.

</details>


### [72] [PanoImager: Geometry-Guided Novel View Synthesis and Reconstruction from Sparse Panoramic Views](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27071)
*Zhisong Xu,Takeshi Oishi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PanoImager provides an SfM-free, diffusion-guided, depth-aware framework for reconstructing 3D scenes from sparse panoramic views, improving robustness under rotation-dominant, low-parallax motion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D reconstruction from sparse panoramas is ill-conditioned when parallax is weak and rotation dominates; SfM/SLAM struggle to initialize, hindering offline map refinement.

Method: Decompose panoramas into local perspective views; incorporate feed-forward pose/depth priors; apply geometry-conditioned diffusion-based view completion; perform depth-guided 3D geometric optimization; synthesize auxiliary observations to enrich sparse evidence; stabilize Gaussian optimization to enforce cross-view consistency.

Result: Experiments on multiple benchmarks show improved stability under extreme sparsity; PanoImager can function as an offline/background map refinement component when SfM/SLAM fails to initialize.

Conclusion: Offers a robust SfM-free solution for panoramic multi-view reconstruction under challenging motion regimes, enabling reliable offline map refinement.

Abstract: Panoramic sensing offers wide field-of-view coverage, yet 3D reconstruction from sparse panoramas remains challenging under rotation-dominant, weak-parallax motion. In such regimes, SfM/SLAM initialization is often ill-conditioned and unreliable. We present PanoImager, an SfM-free framework that combines feed-forward pose/depth priors, geometry-conditioned diffusion view completion, and depth-guided 3DGS optimization. Given only a few panoramic images, PanoImager decomposes them into local perspective views, synthesizes auxiliary observations to enrich sparse evidence, and stabilizes Gaussian optimization for improved cross-view consistency. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show improved stability under extreme sparsity, suggesting PanoImager as an offline/background component for map refinement when SfM/SLAM fails to initialize.

</details>


### [73] [Pseudo-Text-Conditioned 3D Grounding DINO for Organ Localization in Abdominal CT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27084)
*Siqi Chen,Han Gong,Keyi Hou,Jingxuan Yang,Sheethal Bhat,Andreas Maier*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight 3D detector (CT-3GDINO) localizes fixed abdominal organs in CT using a Grounding-DINO-inspired architecture with frozen pseudo-text tokens, achieving 0.5830 mAP over IoU 0.1–0.7. It excels at coarse localization but lags at precise box alignment, and is released as an open baseline to spur future localization-aware pretraining and multimodal conditioning for injury-focused detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide efficient, text-conditioned-like 3D organ localization to support trauma analysis, without a separate real text encoder, by using frozen pseudo-text tokens and a lightweight backbone.

Method: A 3D Grounding-DINO-style detector (CT-3GDINO) with Swin3D backbone, bidirectional feature enhancement, pseudo-text-guided query selection, and a cross-modality decoder. Predicts normalized 3D boxes for liver, spleen, left/right kidney, and bowel. Trained from scratch on 193 RSNA/RATIC CT volumes with segmentation-derived boxes; evaluated across multiple IoU thresholds (0.1–0.7).

Result: 0.5830 overall top-1 class-wise mAP over IoU 0.1–0.7. Outperforms fixed- and trainable-backbone variants (0.5570 and 0.4657 mAP). AP@IoU=0.1 is 0.9649; AP@IoU=0.7 is 0.1552. Coarse localization is strong; strict box alignment remains challenging.

Conclusion: CT-3GDINO serves as an open-source baseline for pseudo-text-conditioned 3D organ localization and motivates future work on localization-aware pretraining, richer multimodal conditioning, and injury-focused detection.

Abstract: Reliable organ localization in abdominal CT can provide spatial priors for downstream trauma analysis. We propose CT-3GDINO, a lightweight 3D detector that adapts a Grounding-DINO-style query-based architecture to fixed organ localization using frozen pseudo-text class tokens instead of a real text encoder. The model combines a Swin3D visual backbone, bidirectional feature enhancement, pseudo-text-guided query selection, and a cross-modality decoder to predict normalized 3D boxes for liver, spleen, left kidney, right kidney, and bowel. We train and evaluate on 193 matched RSNA/RATIC CT volumes with segmentation-derived boxes. The best multi-scale model, trained from scratch, achieves 0.5830 overall top-1 class-wise mAP over 3D IoU thresholds from 0.1 to 0.7, outperforming fixed- and trainable-backbone classification-pretrained variants with 0.5570 and 0.4657 mAP. Performance is strong for coarse localization, with 0.9649 AP at IoU 0.1, but remains limited for strict box alignment, with 0.1552 AP at IoU 0.7. These results establish CT-3GDINO as an open-source baseline for pseudo-text-conditioned 3D organ localization and motivate future work on localization-aware pretraining, richer multimodal conditioning, and injury-focused detection.

</details>


### [74] [SubdivAR: Autoregressive Next-Scale Prediction for Neural Mesh Subdivision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27088)
*Huipeng Guo,Zikai Song,Hang Long,Jielei Zhang,Wenbing Li,Junkai Lin,Tianhao Zhao,Jinshen Zhang,Tianle Guo,Wei Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SubdivAR reframes mesh subdivision as an autoregressive next‑scale prediction using Mesh Autoregressive Representation (MAR) and a Hybrid Topology‑Aware Transformer, achieving superior detail while preserving topology; a 40k mesh dataset (FII-40K) supports supervision, with state-of-the-art gains in Hausdorff and Chamfer metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical rule-based subdivision often yields over-smoothed surfaces, and existing neural subdivision models rely on local context with limited generalization. A global, topology-aware, autoregressive approach promises better detail synthesis and topology preservation across refinement scales.

Method: Introduce Mesh Autoregressive Representation (MAR) by arranging mesh levels into an ordered scale sequence and predicting next-scale vertex offsets autoregressively. Use Hybrid Topology‑Aware Transformer to fuse global semantic attention with topology-constrained local aggregation. Train with multi-scale supervision on FII-40K (≈40k high-quality meshes).

Result: SubdivAR outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, reducing Hausdorff Distance by 18.8% and Chamfer Distance by 14.2%; demonstrates robustness on complex open-surface geometries.

Conclusion: MAR-enabled SubdivAR effectively improves neural mesh subdivision by preserving topology while enhancing geometric detail, with strong generalization and a valuable new multi-scale mesh dataset (FII-40K) to support future work.

Abstract: Mesh subdivision is a fundamental operation for converting coarse, editable meshes into high-resolution surfaces, with broad applications in digital asset creation. Classical rule-based schemes rely on fixed local refinement rules and often produce over-smoothed surfaces. Recent neural subdivision methods improve detail synthesis, but remain constrained by local modeling and exhibit limited generalizability. We present SubdivAR, a neural mesh subdivision framework based on our proposed Mesh Autoregressive Representation (MAR). MAR arranges meshes at different subdivision levels into an ordered scale sequence, reformulating subdivision as autoregressive next-scale prediction. To support this formulation, we introduce a Hybrid Topology-Aware Transformer that combines global semantic attention with topology-constrained local feature aggregation. SubdivAR adopts a next-scale coordinate prediction paradigm, regressing vertex offsets at each refinement stage to preserve subdivision topology while recovering fine-grained geometric details. To enable reliable learning, we construct FII-40K, a curated dataset of nearly 40,000 high-quality meshes with multi-level subdivision supervision. Experiments show that SubdivAR outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, reducing Hausdorff Distance and Chamfer Distance by 18.8% and 14.2%, respectively, and demonstrates strong robustness on complex open-surface geometries.

</details>


### [75] [TMP: Tree-structured Mixed-policy Pruning for Large-scale Image Generation and Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27089)
*Peizhen Zhang,Yang Li,Xunsong Li,Songtao Liu,Zewen Liu,Qiangqiang Hu,Guotong Guo,Jupeng Ding,Yifu Sun,coopersli,Jian Zhang,Zhao Zhong,Liefeng Bo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TMP is a Tree-structured Mixed-policy Pruning framework that generalizes T2I/TI2I tasks and MoE/DiT architectures, enabling substantial parameter reduction (e.g., 80B→20B in HunyuanImage-3.0, 6B→4B in Z-Image Turbo) with modest or negligible quality loss and practical single-GPU inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art image generation models are increasingly massive, demanding enormous compute and memory. There is a need for a universal, architecture-agnostic pruning framework that preserves multi-task capabilities and enables practical deployment on limited hardware.

Method: Proposes TMP, a tree-structured mixed-policy pruning approach that (i) unifies pruning across T2I and TI2I tasks and (ii) handles diverse architectures (MoE and Diffusion Transformer). It targets the final pruning stage (last stage) and can operate on step-distilled models. It also includes inference-engineering to enable 24GB GPU deployment. The experiments use HunyuanImage-3.0 and Z-Image Turbo, assessing substantial parameter reductions with controlled degradation.

Result: Applied to HunyuanImage-3.0 (80B → 20B, ~75% reduction) with limited generation quality loss and single 24GB GPU inference, and to Z-Image Turbo (6B → 4B, ~33% reduction) with negligible degradation. Inference scripts and weights integrated into public repositories (GitHub, HuggingFace).

Conclusion: TMP demonstrates effective, generalizable pruning for large image-generation models across tasks and architectures, achieving substantial compression with manageable quality tradeoffs and facilitating deployment on consumer GPUs; validated on multiple models and publicly released.

Abstract: Modern image generation model rapidly grows their sizes to meet high-fidelity image synthesis. However, they gradually become unaffordable for their enormous parameter consumption and computation budget that lead to massive resources requirement and gpu memory footprint. In this paper, we propose TMP, the first Tree-structured Mixed-policy Pruning framework that generalizes prevalent image tasks (T2I and TI2I) and architectures (Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and Diffusion transformer (DiT)). It could be applied to the step-distilled models and contribute as the last stage. We perform experiments upon current open-sourced SOTA HunyuanImage-3.0 instruct and a popular efficient model Z-Image turbo. The proposed pruning framework manages to compress HunyuanImage 3.0 from 80B to 20B parameters at 75% reduction ratio, sacrificing limited generation quality. We also optimize to enable the inference of the pruned 20B version of HunyuanImage 3.0 on a single 24GB 4090 GPU by engineering skills. The inference script and model weight have been integrated into the existing HunyuanImage3.0 open-source github and huggingface repository. Besides, we prove the efficacy of TMP by compressing Z-Image turbo from 6B to 4B (33% reduction) with negligible degradation.

</details>


### [76] [Safe Autoregressive Image Generation with Iterative Self-Improving Codebooks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27147)
*Yunqi Xue,Zhijiang Li,Philip Torr,Jindong Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Iterative self-improving codebooks for safe autoregressive multimodal generation: identify unsafe outputs with the model, carve a Harmful Space, fix harmful mappings, then fine-tune on the safe space; repeat until convergence to a safety-enhanced codebook.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address safety in autoregressive, discretized, token-based image generation by leveraging the model's own judgment to identify and remove unsafe mappings without external feedback.

Method: Two-step iterative loop: (1) Use the unified model to flag unsafe generations and construct Harmful and Safe image–text pairs; update codebook to remove harmful mappings within a Harmful Space. (2) Adaptive fine-tuning of the codebook on harmless space with safe image–text pairs to preserve/gain image quality. Repeat until no further improvement.

Result: Claims a safety-enhanced codebook achieved through self-guided iteration, reducing harmful outputs without external supervision.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a self-contained approach to mitigating harmfulness in autoregressive multimodal generation by iteratively pruning the codebook and re-tuning within safe regions; effectiveness depends on the model's internal judgement and may face convergence or false-positive/negative risks.

Abstract: Unlike diffusion-based models that operate in continuous latent spaces, autoregressive unified multimodal models produce images by sequentially predicting discretized visual tokens. These tokens are derived from a codebook that maps embeddings to quantized visual patterns. The language-like architecture enables unified multimodal models to effectively capture text conditional information for generation, making them promising for text-to-image tasks. This also raises an interesting question: how safe are the images generated in such an autoregressive way? In this work, we propose iterative self-improving codebooks for safe autoregressive generation. We leverage the understanding and judgment capabilities of the unified multimodal model itself to identify unsafe generated images without human annotation. Subsequently, the inherent representations in the codebook are fixed to eliminate harmful mappings. Our method comprises two steps: first, we use the unified model to identify unsafe generations and construct corresponding harmful and safe image-text pairs. These pairs are used to construct the Harmful Space and guide updates to the codebook, thereby eliminating harmful outputs. Second, we perform adaptive fine-tuning on the codebook within the harmless space using safe image-text pairs to ensure the quality of generated images. These two steps are repeated until no further improvement is observed, producing a safety-enhanced model codebook. Without additional external feedback, the safety of models is improved iteratively.

</details>


### [77] [HarmVideoBench: Benchmarking Harmful Video Understanding in Large Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27187)
*Jiajun Wu,Haoyu Kang,Yining Sun,Jiacheng Hou,Heng Zhang,Danyang Zhang,Zhenjun Zhao,Haochi Zhang,Leixin Sun,Eric Hanchen Jiang,Yushan Li,Ruiyu Li,Mengkai Huang,Yan Gao,Xu Zhang,Guancheng Wan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HarmVideoBench introduces a hierarchical, multi-layered benchmark for harmful video understanding and BCR, a dynamic context-retrieval method, achieving 84.4% macro on 19 LVLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks rely on binary harm labels and offer no explanatory rationales. There is a need to evaluate and explain model reasoning across multiple layers of harm beyond surface cues.

Method: HarmVideoBench comprises 1,379 videos and 4,137 multiple-choice questions, evaluated along three dimensions: Observable Evidence, Clip-Internal Meaning, and Beyond-Clip Reasoning. 19 LVLMs are assessed. BCR (benchmark-aligned context retrieval) predicts reasoning boundaries and retrieves context only when needed.

Result: BCR raises macro-average performance from 61.7% to 84.4%, achieving state-of-the-art results in harmful video understanding.

Conclusion: The work advances multidimensional, explainable evaluation for harmful video understanding and demonstrates the effectiveness of a boundary-aware retrieval strategy to improve reasoning and context utilization.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have recently shown immense potential in automated content moderation, sparking growing interest in developing harmful-video benchmarks. However, we identify two primary limitations in existing works: 1) The multi-layered characteristics of harmful videos are overlooked. Existing benchmarks predominantly formulate evaluation as a binary classification task, failing to capture implicit or deep contextual harms. 2) Explanatory rationales are completely absent.
