<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between predictive performance and real-world fairness in socio-technical systems by acknowledging feedback loops and structural inequalities embedded in algorithms.

Method: The abstract signals a theoretical/analytical approach that identifies two fundamental limitations and proposes conceptual reforms; likely develops a framework for contextualized auditing and structural-aware fairness metrics.

Result: Identification of two core limitations (deterministic point estimates for auditing; treating individuals as isolated) and a roadmap for overcoming them within fairness research.

Conclusion: Fairness in ML requires moving beyond point estimates and incorporating structural context to address systemic inequalities in socio-technical architectures.

Abstract: Modern machine learning systems have outgrown their origins as isolated predictive constructs, evolving into complex socio-technical architectures that actively mediate human opportunity. As algorithms increasingly determine access to economic and social opportunities, it has become widely recognized that these systems are deeply embedded with the structural inequalities and prejudices of their environments. The field of algorithmic fairness emerged in response to the growing recognition that models optimized for predictive accuracy can systematically disadvantage marginalized groups. Early mitigation strategies, however, rested on fragile simplifications that limited their effectiveness in complex socio-technical environments. This thesis identifies and addresses two fundamental limitations of contemporary fairness paradigms: the reliance on deterministic point estimates for auditing and the treatment of individuals as isolated entities devoid of structural context.

</details>


### [178] [Topology-Informed Neural Networks for Flood Detection in Optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26204)
*Sophia Li,Max Zhao,Raghu G. Raj,Tianyu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Topological descriptors derived from persistent homology improve flood detection on SEN12-FLOOD and provide interpretability, complementing CNN/RNN/ViT models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flood detection under cloud cover and the opacity of deep learning models motivate incorporating topological data analysis (TDA) to capture global data structure and enhance interpretability and robustness.

Method: Extract image-wise topological features (e.g., persistent homology descriptors) and fuse them with standard neural networks (CNN, RNN, ViT) trained on the SEN12-FLOOD dataset; compare performance against single-image baselines and assess interpretability.

Result: Topological descriptors carry meaningful flood signals independently and, when integrated with existing networks, yield more robust and interpretable flood detection systems.

Conclusion: TDA provides complementary, globally-informed features that improve flood detection accuracy and interpretability, particularly in multi-temporal and cloud-obscured remote sensing contexts.

Abstract: Floods frequently impact regions around the world. Rapid and accurate flood detection is crucial for emergency response and timely mitigation of human and economic loss. The expanding availability of satellite data and advances in artificial intelligence have enhanced monitoring of environmental hazards, but many flood events remain challenging to detect because cloud cover obscures optical satellite imagery. Rambour et al. introduced the SEN12-FLOOD dataset and extracted per-image features using a ResNet-50 convolutional neural network backbone, then fed these features into a gated recurrent unit network to show that temporal information can substantially improve accuracy compared to single-image baselines. More recently, Chamatidis et al. showed that a vision transformer can achieve strong performance with popular convolutional architectures. However, these models typically function as opaque black boxes, making it difficult to interpret their decision boundaries, learned features, and internal reasoning, especially in safety-critical domains like remote sensing. In contrast, topological data analysis (TDA) provides a mathematically grounded framework for capturing global structural features of data. TDA has emerged as a powerful tool for analyzing complex imagery, especially imagery with geometrically interpretable structures, of which floods are a prime candidate. In this work, we systematically evaluate topological descriptors for flood detection using the open-source SEN12-FLOOD dataset. By extracting topological features from each image and incorporating them into neural networks, we demonstrate that topological descriptors carry meaningful flood signals independently and complement existing networks to yield more robust and interpretable flood detection systems.

</details>


### [179] [A General Framework for Learning Algebraic Properties from Cayley Graphs using Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26212)
*Tal Weissblat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A general GNN framework learns algebraic properties of finite groups from Cayley graphs, extending prior work to property-independent learning for abelianity, nilpotency, and solvability; demonstrates that graph representations encode substantial algebraic information that GNNs can extract.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether key algebraic properties of finite groups can be inferred from their Cayley graphs using graph neural networks, moving beyond property-specific approaches.

Method: A unified GNN architecture and training pipeline applied to Cayley-graph representations of various finite groups; supervised learning for abelianity, nilpotency, and solvability; evaluation across multiple group families to test generalization.

Result: The framework successfully learns and distinguishes the targeted algebraic properties from Cayley graphs, indicating that substantial algebraic information is embedded in graph representations and can be recovered with GNNs.

Conclusion: Graph representation learning is viable for studying finite-group properties; this framework serves as a proof of concept and opens directions for extending to more properties and deeper theoretical understanding.

Abstract: A Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework for predicting the solvability of finite groups from their Cayley graph representations was introduced in [1]. In the present work, we generalize this approach and develop a property-independent framework for learning algebraic properties of finite groups directly from Cayley graphs. As representative case studies, we consider abelianity, nilpotency, and solvability. Using a common GNN architecture and training pipeline, we investigate the extent to which algebraic structure can be recovered from graph-based representations alone. Results on a collection of finite groups drawn from several families demonstrate that the framework successfully learns and distinguishes multiple algebraic properties from their associated Cayley graphs. These findings suggest that substantial algebraic information is encoded in graph representations and can be extracted through GNNs. More broadly, the proposed framework provides a proof of concept for applying graph representation learning to the study of algebraic properties of finite groups.

</details>


### [180] [Fast LeWorldModel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26217)
*Yuntian Gao,Xiangyu Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fast-LeWM replaces LeWM's sequential one-step rollouts with action-prefix prediction, enabling parallel latent forecasting and faster planning while reducing error accumulation; it improves success and reduces planning time across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LeWM's planning relies on autoregressive, local one-step latent transitions, which is computationally expensive and accumulates latent errors as horizon grows. There is a need for a scalable, parallel prediction mechanism for longer-horizon planning.

Method: Introduce Fast-LeWM; treat action prefixes as the prediction unit; encode prefixes of candidate action sequences and predict the future latent states reached after those prefixes in parallel; use the last prefix token to evaluate the corresponding future latent; train with prefix-level supervision so that the model learns state evolution under varying prefixes.

Result: Empirical results show improved average success over LeWM across tasks; significant reduction in planning time; lower open-loop latent loss with slower growth as horizon increases.

Conclusion: Fast-LeWM provides an efficient, parallelizable latent world model for planning that reduces error accumulation and computation, outperforming LeWM on planning tasks.

Abstract: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs), including recent LeWorldModel (LeWM), have become a promising foundation for reconstruction-free visual world models. For visual planning, however, LeWM evaluates candidate action sequences by repeatedly applying a local one-step latent transition model. This autoregressive rollout makes planning computationally expensive and exposes the predicted trajectory to accumulated latent errors as the horizon grows. We propose Fast LeWorldModel (Fast-LeWM), a fast latent world model that replaces repeated local rollout with action-prefix prediction. Given the current latent and a candidate action sequence, Fast-LeWM encodes its prefixes and predicts the future latents reached after executing those prefixes in parallel. By making action prefixes the basic prediction unit, Fast-LeWM directly models action effects accumulated to different extents over multiple horizons. This prefix-level supervision forces the model to learn how states continuously evolve under different action prefixes, rather than only fitting one-step state transitions. During planning, the predictor can use the last prefix token from the encoded action sequence to evaluate the corresponding future latent without explicitly rolling through each intermediate imagined state. Across multiple tasks, Fast-LeWM improves average success over LeWM while substantially reducing planning time, achieving lower open-loop latent loss whose growth becomes significantly slower as the rollout horizon increases.

</details>


### [181] [Dataset Usage Inference without Shadow Models or Held-out Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26257)
*Wojciech Łapacz,Stanisław Pawlak,Jan Dubiński,Franziska Boenisch,Adam Dziedzic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A practical DUI method estimates the fraction of a candidate dataset used in training without shadow models or real held-out data by generating synthetic non-members and using mixture proportion estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DUI approaches rely on costly shadow models and explicit in/out-of-distribution splits, which are impractical for large models and real data ownership disputes; a more scalable, assumption-light approach is needed.

Method: Generate synthetic non-member samples; extract diverse membership signals; formulate DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to infer the share of the candidate dataset that was used for training; no real held-out data or shadow models required.

Result: Empirical evaluation on large image generative models shows reliable quantification of dataset usage, demonstrating practicality for data owners to assess how much of their data informed training.

Conclusion: Proposes a practical DUI framework that removes key constraints, enabling data owners to audit training data usage without relying on shadow models or real held-out data.

Abstract: How much of my data was used to train a machine learning model? Dataset Usage Inference (DUI) aims to answer this by estimating what fraction of a dataset contributed to a model's training. However, existing DUI methods rely on assumptions that rarely hold in practice: they require training expensive shadow models to imitate the target model, and they assume access to both known training samples and an in-distribution held-out set confirmed to be absent from training. These conditions make current approaches impractical for modern large models and real data ownership disputes. We introduce a practical DUI framework that removes these constraints. Our method requires neither shadow models nor real held-out data. Instead, it generates synthetic non-member samples, extracts diverse membership signals, and casts DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to estimate what share of the candidate dataset was used during training. Experiments on large image generative models show that our method reliably quantifies dataset usage, providing a practical tool for data owners to determine how much of their data was used to train a model.

</details>


### [182] [Equivariance and Augmentation for Bayesian Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26273)
*Miaowen Dong,Axel Flinth,Jan E. Gerken*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This work studies data augmentation for Bayesian neural networks under variational inference, deriving exact equivariance conditions for exponential-family variational distributions, providing bounds on residual equivariance error, and introducing three symmetrization techniques (one called orbit expansion) that improve augmentation efficacy; experiments show improved equivariance and performance, with code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a debate whether to enforce symmetry via equivariant architectures or to learn symmetries from augmented data. While equivariant networks are well-understood theoretically, augmentation analysis is harder due to training dynamics. Building on results that augmented infinite deep ensembles are exactly equivariant, this paper extends the question to Bayesian neural networks trained with variational inference.

Method: Analyze variational distributions in the exponential family. Derive conditions under which augmentation yields exact equivariance. Obtain bounds on equivariance error. Propose three novel symmetrization techniques to amplify the effect of augmentation (one named orbit expansion). Conduct extensive experiments comparing against a baseline.

Result: The orbit expansion method outperforms the baseline in both achieving higher equivariance and improving overall performance. The work provides practical symmetrization strategies and theoretical bounds, with code available for replication.

Conclusion: Symmetrized data augmentation within variational Bayesian neural networks can achieve significant equivariance benefits and performance gains. The work bridges augmentation and equivariance by delivering theoretical conditions, error bounds, and actionable methods (including orbit expansion) for probabilistic networks.

Abstract: Symmetries are important for many deep learning tasks, ranging from applications in the sciences to medical imaging. However, there is an ongoing debate about whether to impose symmetry constraints on the neural network architecture (yielding equivariant neural networks) or learn them from augmented training data. Although equivariant networks are well-studied theoretically, much less is known about data augmentation, since analyzing augmentation requires control over the training dynamics. Inspired by recent results that show that augmented infinite deep ensembles are exactly equivariant, we study data augmentation for Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) trained with variational inference. We focus on variational distributions in the exponential family and derive conditions under which exact equivariance is reached. We furthermore obtain bounds on the equivariance error and introduce three novel symmetrization techniques which boost the effect of data augmentation in this setting. We conduct extensive numerical experiments which show that one of our symmetrization methods (orbit expansion) outperforms the baseline in both equivariance and overall performance. Our code is available at github.com/dmw1998/augment-BNNs

</details>


### [183] [SSM Adapters via Hankel Reduced-order Modeling: Injection Site Determines Task Suitability in Long-Context Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26290)
*Omanshu Thapliyal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HRM: an SSM-based PEFT adapter (Hankel Reduced Order Model) for long-context tasks; achieves FFT-parallel scan and parity with LoRA in iso-parametric settings; shows substantial gains on LongBench (QuALITY, QMSum) and synthetic/state-tracking benchmarks, via learned recurrence modulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in PEFT effectiveness for tasks requiring sequential state accumulation. Explore whether SSM adapters and MLP injection sites can outperform attention-centric PEFT like LoRA for long-context modeling.

Method: Introduce Hankel Reduced Order Model (HRM) adapter, an SSM-based residual module initialized by Balanced Truncation of empirical Hankel Grammians. Exploit time-invariant A_bar to enable exact FFT-based parallel scanning. Compare HRM to LoRA across context lengths in iso-parametric settings on Mistral-7B (8.4M trainable parameters). Evaluate on LongBench tasks (QuALITY, QMSum) and synthetic DFA/Parity plus enwik8; perform gate analysis to understand recurrence modulation.

Result: HRM outperforms LoRA variants on LongBench tasks, with QuALITY relative accuracy +34.8% and QMSum ROUGE-1 relative improvement +71.6%. Consistent advantages across 18 configurations of synthetic state-tracking (DFA, Parity) and character-level LM (enwik8). Gate analysis indicates HRM learns to modulate recurrence effectively, supporting its suitability as an alternative to low-rank adapters for long-context sequence modeling.

Conclusion: HRM adapters offer a robust, architecture-level alternative to low-rank adaptation for long-context sequence modeling, demonstrating strong empirical gains and interpretability through recurrence modulation.

Abstract: While parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) typically targets attention projectors, its efficacy for tasks requiring sequential state accumulation remains under-explored. We examine if PEFT for such tasks can benefit from state space model (SSMs) adapters, and if MLP blocks are better injection sites. We introduce Hankel Reduced order Model (HRM) adapter, an SSM-based residual module initialized via Balanced Truncation of empirical Hankel Grammians. By leveraging the time-invariance of the system matrix $\bar{A}$, HRM enables an exact FFT-based parallel scan, achieving computational parity with LoRA across all context lengths. In iso-parametric evaluations on Mistral-7B (8.4M trainable parameters), HRM outperforms LoRA variants on LongBench tasks, including QuALITY (+34.8\% relative accuracy) and QMSum (+71.6\% relative ROUGE-1). HRM further demonstrates consistent superiority across 18 configurations of synthetic state-tracking (DFA, Parity) and character-level language modeling (enwik8). Gate analysis reveals that HRM adapters effectively learn to modulate recurrence, providing a robust architectural alternative to low-rank adaptation for long-context sequence modeling.

</details>


### [184] [The Red Queen Gödel Machine: Co-Evolving Agents and Their Evaluators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26294)
*Alex Iacob,Andrej Jovanović,William F. Shen,Daniel Burkhardt,Meghdad Kurmanji,Nurbek Tastan,Lorenzo Sani,Niccolò Alberto Elia Venanzi,Ambroise Odonnat,Zeyu Cao,Bill Marino,Xinchi Qiu,Nicholas D. Lane*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RQGM introduces an evolutionary, non-stationary utility framework for recursive self-improvement, using epoch-based evaluation with evolving evaluators; it shows improved performance across coding tasks, paper writing/review, and proof grading, aided by agent-as-judge signals and adversarial objectives to balance AI and human judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address non-stationary evaluation criteria in self-improving agents by co-evolving evaluators and objectives, enabling improvement beyond fixed benchmarks.

Method: Organize search into epochs with fixed within-epoch evaluation; allow utility to evolve at epoch boundaries; integrate agent-as-a-judge signals (e.g., code-review, grading) to provide cheap, complementary feedback; co-evolve writers and graders; introduce adversarial objective to equalize reviewer strictness across AI and human work.

Result: RQGM improved verifiable coding task performance (test pass rate) with 1.35x–1.72x fewer tokens; co-evolved writers achieved 1.78x–1.86x higher acceptance rates; co-evolved graders achieved ~9% higher ground-truth accuracy; addressed AI-paper reviewer over-acceptance by introducing adversarial objective ensuring reviewers treat AI and human work equivalently.

Conclusion: Non-stationary utility evolution enables robust recursive self-improvement by embedding evolving evaluators within the search loop; RQGM delivers gains across multiple domains and lays groundwork for dynamic, adversarially-aware evaluation frameworks in self-improving agents.

Abstract: Self-improving agents are state-of-the-art (SOTA) on agentic coding benchmarks and have recently been extended to general domains. However, their search methods generally assume a stationary evaluation criterion: a fixed verifier, benchmark, or labeled dataset that remains valid as the agent improves. This ignores a central feature of evolution: species adapt as their environments change with them. We aim to bring the same principle to recursive self-improvement, making evaluation part of the improvement loop and opening search to evolving evaluators, adversarial objectives, and dynamic utilities that may surpass static benchmarks. We introduce the Red Queen Godel Machine (RQGM), an evolutionary framework for recursive self-improvement under non-stationary utilities. The RQGM makes this possible through controlled utility evolution: search is organized into epochs with a fixed within-epoch evaluation criterion, while the utility can be updated at epoch boundaries, so self-improvement guarantees hold per epoch as the objective evolves across them. We begin by showing that even on verifiable coding tasks, the RQGM improves test pass rate over the prior SOTA by adding a complementary agent-as-a-judge code-review signal. This signal is cheaper and the RQGM uses 1.35x-1.72x fewer tokens. We then turn to scientific paper writing and reviewing, and Olympiad-level proof writing and grading, where the RQGM improves performance over prior self-improving agents: co-evolved writers reach 1.78x-1.86x higher acceptance rates under a diverse agent-as-a-judge panel, while co-evolved graders reach 9% higher ground-truth accuracy. In paper reviewing, the strongest baseline reviewer over-accepts AI-generated papers at up to 1.91x the human rate. The RQGM corrects this by introducing an adversarial objective that discovers reviewers equally stringent on AI and human work.

</details>


### [185] [High-Probability PL-SGD with Markovian Noise: Optimal Mixing and Tail Dependence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26316)
*Dhruv Sarkar,Aprameyo Chakrabartty,Vaneet Aggarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: For PL-satisfied smooth objectives with Markov gradient sampling, the paper derives tight uniform high-probability SGD bounds under light tails, shows optimal dependence on mixing time, and extends to heavy-tailed gradients with a clipped-block method achieving near-optimal rates; lower bounds match these dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve gap between high-probability and expectation bounds for SGD under Markov noise, quantify mixing-time effects, and establish optimal rates for light- and heavy-tailed gradient noise in PL settings.

Method: Introduce lag-blocking to obtain uniform high-probability bounds with leading term proportional to t_mix/(k+K0) under geometric mixing; prove optimality via Ω(σ^2 t_mix/k) lower bound on a two-state chain; extend to p in (1,2] with finite p-moment using an all-samples clipped block method; derive high-probability error ~ σ_p^2 (t_mix/T)^{2(p-1)/p} and matching lower bounds via reductions to heavy-tailed mean estimation for sticky chains.

Result: Establishes linear-in-t_mix leading term for light-tailed PL-SGD and tight lower bound; designs a robust heavy-tailed algorithm with near-optimal dependence on mixing time and sample budget; provides matching lower bounds, giving a complete picture of optimal polynomial dependence on mixing time in both regimes.

Conclusion: The work delivers tight characterizations of mixing-time effects in Markovian SGD under PL conditions, showing optimal rates for light tails and heavy-tailed noise, and highlighting the role of sample efficiency and bias mitigation via blocking and clipping.

Abstract: We study first-order methods for smooth objectives satisfying the Polyak-Łojasiewicz (PL) condition when gradient samples are generated by an exogenous Markov chain. In the light-tailed setting, prior uniform-in-time high-probability bounds for ordinary Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) under a standard growth envelope scale as $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}^2/k)$, leaving a gap with the $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/k)$ expectation bounds. We close this gap using a lag-blocking argument to establish a uniform high-probability guarantee with a leading stochastic term of $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/(k+K_0))$ under geometric mixing. We prove this linear dependence on the mixing time is optimal via a matching $Ω(σ^2 t_{mix}/k)$ lower bound on a quadratic objective driven by a persistent two-state chain.
  We then extend this framework to heavy-tailed Markovian gradients satisfying a stationary finite-$p$-moment condition, $p \in (1,2]$. We design an all-samples clipped block method that uses every Markov transition while mitigating Markovian bias. Under a transition budget $T$, this algorithm achieves a high-probability stochastic error of $\widetilde{O}(σ_p^2(t_{mix}/T)^{2(p-1)/p})$. We establish a matching lower bound by reducing PL optimization to heavy-tailed mean estimation for a sticky Markov chain. Ultimately, this work tightly characterizes the optimal polynomial dependence on mixing time for light-tailed PL-SGD, and the optimal heavy-tail exponent and effective-sample-size dependence in the robust regime.

</details>


### [186] [EVOM: Agentic Meta-Evolution of Actor-Critic Architectures for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26327)
*Boyun Zhang,Chao Wang,Kai Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EVOM combines bi-level optimization with an LLM-guided meta-evolution to discover high-performance actor-critic architectures without coupling the architecture search to policy execution, yielding improvements on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4; ablation shows both outer meta-evolution and LLM Design Agent are essential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automating RL architecture design is hard due to expensive evaluation of candidate architectures and an open-ended search space; prior LLM-guided methods are limited by guidance quality and coupling to environment control.

Method: Formulates architecture search as bi-level optimization: inner loop trains actor-critic weights using PPO (low-fidelity). Outer loop performs meta-evolution of architecture programs; outer loop is guided by an LLM Design Agent that acts solely as an architecture designer, not policy execution or environment control.

Result: EVOM outperforms manual baseline, LLM-guided random search, and MLES on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4. Ablation studies indicate both meta-evolution loop and LLM Design Agent are necessary for best performance.

Conclusion: A decoupled, agentic meta-evolution framework with LLM-guided architecture design can effectively discover high-performance actor-critic architectures, suggesting potential for scalable, automatable RL architecture search.

Abstract: In actor-critic reinforcement learning, network architectures are typically manually designed. Automating this design is challenging because each candidate must be trained before evaluation, and the design space is open-ended. To address these challenges, we introduce EVOM, an agentic meta-evolution framework for discovering high-performance actor-critic architectures. We frame architecture search as a bi-level optimization: an inner loop trains weights via the low-fidelity proximal policy optimization (PPO), while an outer loop drives meta-evolution by iteratively refining architecture programs. Crucially, this outer loop is powered by an LLM-based design agent that operates purely as an architecture designer, completely decoupled from policy execution and environment control. Experiments reveal that EVOM outperforms the manually designed baseline, an LLM-guided random search, and the state-of-the-art LLM-guided programmatic policy search method MLES, delivering superior performance on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4. Ablation studies validate that both the meta-evolution loop and the LLM Design Agent are indispensable for final performance.

</details>


### [187] [Mesh-RL: Coupled subgrid reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26333)
*Behnam Gheshlaghi,Bahador Rashidi,Shahin Atakishiyev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mesh-RL uses finite-element inspired spatial domain decomposition to speed up TD learning via boundary-consistent updates, improving convergence and exploration in sparse-reward grid environments without changing rewards or planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address slow temporal-difference reward propagation in large or sparse-reward environments where value information spreads only locally.

Method: Decompose the environment into overlapping subgrids (mesh) and enforce boundary-consistent TD updates; applies to Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q; tested on hazard-dense grid-worlds with varying mesh resolutions.

Result: Consistent improvements in convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability across methods; higher mesh resolutions improve exploration and long-range value propagation; Dyna-Q also benefits from structured decomposition; no modifications to reward function or Bellman operator.

Conclusion: Spatial domain decomposition provides a principled, model-free acceleration of TD learning, bridging FEM-inspired boundary techniques with RL to boost sample efficiency; source code will be released.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning in large or sparse-reward environments suffers from slow temporal-difference reward propagation, as value information spreads only locally across the state space. We propose Mesh-RL, a spatial domain-decomposition framework inspired by the finite element method and domain decomposition theory, which partitions the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforces boundary-consistent temporal-difference updates. Such an approach enables localized learning while ensuring globally coherent value propagation. Unlike hierarchical or model-based approaches, Mesh-RL accelerates long-range credit assignment without modifying the reward function, Bellman operator, or introducing explicit planning mechanisms. We evaluate Mesh-RL on hazard-dense grid-world environments with varying geometries and mesh resolutions. Across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q, Mesh-RL consistently improves convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability. Higher mesh resolutions sustain exploration, prevent premature convergence, and substantially accelerate value propagation to distant states. While Dyna-Q already benefits from internal planning, it still achieves additional gains under structured decomposition. Overall, Mesh-RL introduces a principled spatial domain-decomposition mechanism for accelerating temporal-difference learning. Our framework bridges finite element method-inspired boundary-consistency techniques from scientific computing with reinforcement learning to improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward environments. We will release source code of the study.

</details>


### [188] [EMA-FS: Accelerating GBDT Training via Gain-Informed Feature Screening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26337)
*Yan Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EMA-based feature screening (EMA-FS) for LightGBM reduces train-time by restricting per-feature histograms to top-K gains learned across boosting iterations; stochastic variant S-EMA-FS blends deterministic and random approaches; demonstrates speedups on dense, high-dimensional data with occasional AUC gains, but little benefit on highly sparse data; fully backward-compatible.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GBDT training spends a dominant portion of time constructing per-feature histograms. Random feature subsampling (feature_fraction) can speed up training but may discard predictive features. An informed, momentum-based screening method could preserve predictive utility while substantially reducing computation.

Method: Compute an exponential moving average (EMA) of per-feature split gains across boosting iterations. After a short warmup, restrict histogram construction to the top-K features by historical gain. Operate at the per-tree level and preserve LightGBM's histogram subtraction trick for full compatibility. Introduce Stochastic EMA-FS (S-EMA-FS) by replacing deterministic top-K with gain-weighted random sampling controlled by a concentration parameter beta, unifying deterministic (beta→∞) and random subsampling (beta=0). Implemented in ~120 lines of C++ across six LightGBM tree learners.

Result: Dense, moderate-to-high-dimensional data show substantial speedups: 2.61x on a 500-feature synthetic benchmark; 1.45x on the IEEE-CIS Fraud dataset at 30% feature retention. At 70% retention, AUC improves by 0.11 with 1.34x speedup. No speedup on extremely sparse Bosch data due to LightGBM's sparse bin optimization already bypassing empty values. S-EMA-FS unifies deterministic and stochastic approaches, offering flexible control via beta.

Conclusion: EMA-FS and S-EMA-FS provide effective, backward-compatible optimization for LightGBM by informed feature screening based on historical gains. They yield substantial training speedups on dense datasets with moderate-to-high dimensionality and can improve predictive performance at reasonable retention. Performance gains depend on data characteristics; not advantageous when sparse optimization already handles missing values. The approach is lightweight to implement (~120 lines of C++) and maintains compatibility with existing training workflows.

Abstract: Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDT), exemplified by LightGBM, spend a dominant fraction of training time -- typically 65-70% -- constructing per-feature histograms. Existing approaches such as random feature subsampling (feature_fraction) discard features without regard for their predictive utility. We propose EMA-based Feature Screening (EMA-FS), an algorithm-level optimization that maintains an exponential moving average (EMA) of per-feature split gains across boosting iterations and, after a short warmup, restricts histogram construction to the top-K features ranked by historical gain. Unlike random subsampling, EMA-FS is informed: it retains high-gain features while screening out low-gain ones. Operating at the per-tree level, it preserves full compatibility with LightGBM's histogram subtraction trick, requiring no changes to core routines.
  We evaluate EMA-FS on datasets spanning financial fraud detection, advertising click-through prediction, industrial quality control, and synthetic benchmarks, with feature dimensionalities from 29 to 968. On dense, moderate-to-high-dimensional data it achieves significant speedups: 2.61x on a 500-feature synthetic benchmark and 1.45x on the 432-feature IEEE-CIS Fraud dataset at 30% retention. At 70% retention it improves AUC by 0.11 points while delivering a 1.34x speedup. On extremely sparse data (Bosch, >90% missing) it yields no speedup, as LightGBM's sparse bin optimization already bypasses empty values.
  We further introduce Stochastic EMA-FS (S-EMA-FS), which replaces deterministic top-K selection with gain-weighted random sampling controlled by a concentration parameter beta, unifying deterministic EMA-FS (beta -> infinity) and random subsampling (beta = 0) in one framework. Both are implemented in ~120 lines of C++ across all six LightGBM tree learners and are fully backward-compatible.

</details>


### [189] [Does Aurora Encode Atmospheric Structure? Latent Regime Analysis and Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26361)
*Emma Kasteleyn,Ana Lucic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Aurora forms seasonally organized latent representations and relies on 3D vertical structure; extreme storms do not form a linearly separable cluster; attribution (LRP) aligns with the vertical features of notable storms; targeted masking degrades forecasts far more than random; overall, meteorological coherence emerges without explicit instruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To interpret how a large ML foundation model learns and encodes atmospheric dynamics, beyond black-box performance, by probing its internal representations and causal reliance on input regions.

Method: Spatially pooled PCA to analyze the latent space structure; layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) to identify input features driving predictions; perturbation tests by masking regions to evaluate impact on forecast accuracy (comparing relevant-region masking vs random masking).

Result: Latent space is primarily organized by seasonal cycles; extreme storm events do not form a distinct, linearly separable cluster. LRP reveals attention to features consistent with the 3D vertical structure characteristic of the Great Storm of 1987. Perturbation tests show masking relevant regions degrades forecasts 3.31 times more than random masking.

Conclusion: Aurora learns meteorological coherence and vertical structure without explicit instruction, indicating emergent physics-like representations in the latent space.

Abstract: ML foundation models are able to emulate atmospheric dynamics accurately and efficiently but operate as opaque ``black boxes''. We investigate the internal representations of the Aurora model using spatially pooled PCA and layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP). We find evidence that Aurora's latent space is primarily organized by seasonal cycles, whereas extreme storm events do not form a linearly separable cluster. LRP indicates that the model attends to features consistent with the 3D vertical structure of the Great Storm of 1987. Perturbation tests show masking relevant regions degrades forecasts $3.31\times$ more than random masking. These findings suggest that Aurora learns meteorological coherence and vertical structure without explicit instruction.

</details>


### [190] [SOLAR: AI-Powered Speed-of-Light Performance Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26383)
*Qijing Huang,Sana Damani,Zhifan Ye,Athinagoras Skiadopoulos,Siva Kumar Sastry Hari,Jason Clemons,Sahil Modi,Jingquan Wang,Aditya Kane,Edward C Lin,Humphrey Shi,Christos Kozyrakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOLAR automatically derives validated speed-of-light (SOL) execution bounds from PyTorch/JAX source via an LLM-driven frontend, a deterministic IR-to-einsum graph lift, and an analytic backend, enabling multi-fidelity, validated headroom analysis across kernels, models, and robotics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine theoretical minimum execution time on target hardware and quantify current implementations' distance to that limit; manually-derived SOL bounds are error-prone and slow to iterate during rapid model development.

Method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) LLM frontend translates source code into an executable Affine Loop IR, validated by input-output checks; (2) deterministic flow lifts the IR into an einsum graph; (3) analytic backend computes unfused, fused, and cache-aware SOL bounds, with multi-fidelity options.

Result: SOLAR achieves comprehensive operator and language coverage, provides validated bounds with zero observed SOL violations, and demonstrates bound-tightening and insights on KernelBench, JAX/Flax models, and robotics workloads; enables headroom analysis, optimization discovery, cross-platform exploration, and inverse-roofline provisioning.

Conclusion: SOLAR mitigates manual bound derivation, delivering validated, multi-fidelity SOL bounds that guide optimization and hardware provisioning across frameworks.

Abstract: How fast could a deep-learning model run on target hardware, and how far is today's implementation from that limit? These questions are central to software, hardware, and algorithm optimizations. Speed-of-Light (SOL) analysis answers them by computing a workload's theoretical minimum execution time on a given architecture. Yet deriving SOL bounds remains manual, error-prone, and disconnected from rapid model development. To close this gap, we introduce SOLAR, a framework that automatically derives validated SOL bounds from PyTorch and JAX source code. SOLAR leverages both generative and deterministic components in its flow: an LLM frontend translates any source programs into an executable Affine Loop IR, validated by output comparison; a deterministic flow lifts the IR into an einsum graph; and an analytical backend computes unfused, fused, and cache-aware SOL bounds. SOLAR provides comprehensive operator and language coverage, produces validated bounds with zero observed SOL violations, and offers multi-fidelity analysis that tightens bounds and surfaces optimization insights. We evaluate SOLAR across KernelBench, JAX/Flax models, and robotics workloads. These experiments demonstrate four use cases: headroom analysis at multiple fidelity levels, identifying optimization opportunities, cross-platform exploration, and inverse-roofline hardware provisioning.

</details>


### [191] [At the Edge of Understanding: Sparse Autoencoders Trace The Limits of Transformer Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26396)
*Praneet Suresh,Jack Stanley,Sonia Joseph,Luca Scimeca,Danilo Bzdok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The abstract posits a systematic, mechanistic study of transformer robustness under distributional shift (OOD). It links OOD prompts and perturbations to broader activation of spurious/internal concepts in the model’s internals, and uses this to quantify prompt shift and guide a fine-tuning strategy. It also advocates an inference-time diagnostic that inspects private computations to improve safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reliability and safety of pre-trained transformers when confronted with data or prompts that depart from training distributions; advance beyond empirical robustness to a mechanistic, interpretable understanding of OOD effects.

Method: Conduct systematic experiments; propose a mechanistic framework to map the contours of robustness; analyze how OOD inputs (e.g., typos, jailbreak prompts) drive the model to activate more fallacious concepts; develop metrics to quantify prompt distributional shift; devise a fine-tuning strategy to harden models; propose an inference-time diagnostic focused on internal computations.

Result: Demonstrates that OOD inputs correlate with increased use of fallacious internal concepts; provides quantitative means to assess distributional shift in prompts; outlines a robustification route via targeted fine-tuning; introduces an inference-time diagnostic concept for monitoring privately stored computations.

Conclusion: Broadens the OOD notion to include a model’s private processes; advocates for an inference-time diagnostic as a critical tool for safe, deployable AI across domains; highlights the need for mechanistic interpretability in robustness research.

Abstract: Pre-trained transformers have demonstrated remarkable generalization abilities, at times extending beyond the scope of their training data. Yet, real-world deployments often face unexpected or adversarial data that diverges from training data distributions. Without explicit mechanisms for handling such shifts, model reliability and safety degrade, urging more disciplined study of out-of-distribution (OOD) settings for transformers. By systematic experiments, we present a mechanistic framework for delineating the precise contours of transformer model robustness. We find that OOD inputs, including subtle typos and jailbreak prompts, drive language models to operate on an increased number of fallacious concepts in their internals. We leverage this device to quantify and understand the degree of distributional shift in prompts, enabling a mechanistically grounded fine-tuning strategy to robustify LLMs. Expanding the very notion of OOD from input data to a model's private computational processes, a new transformer diagnostic at inference time is a critical step toward making AI systems safe for deployment across science, business, and government.

</details>


### [192] [Deterministic Pareto-Optimal Policy Synthesis for Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26397)
*Aniruddha Joshi,Niklas Lauffer,Sanjit Seshia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new preference-conditioned Bellman operator based on Chebyshev scalarization computes deterministic Pareto-optimal policies for MOMDPs; it upper-bounds the Pareto frontier and converges to a coverage set, from which policies for any preference can be extracted.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL collapses multiple objectives into a single scalar reward, obscuring trade-offs along the Pareto frontier; there is a need for deterministic policies that represent the full set of optimal trade-offs in MOMDPs.

Method: Introduce a preference-conditioned Bellman operator inspired by Chebyshev scalarization. It yields Q-estimates that upper-bound the true Pareto frontier (enveloping property) and monotonically converge to a coverage set of the frontier. Deterministic policies are extractable from the converged Q-estimates for any preference.

Result: Proves the enveloping property and monotone convergence to a frontier coverage set. Demonstrates extraction of deterministic Pareto-optimal policies. Experimental results show recovery of complex trade-offs and effective deterministic Pareto-optimal policy synthesis.

Conclusion: The approach enables policy synthesis that represents the entire Pareto-optimal frontier for MOMDPs, yielding approximately Pareto-optimal deterministic policies for any specified preference.

Abstract: Real-world decision-making often requires balancing multiple conflicting objectives, a challenge that standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) frequently addresses by aggregating rewards into a single scalar signal. While effective for simple tasks, this approach often fails to capture the full spectrum of optimal trade-offs, known as the Pareto frontier. In this paper, we introduce a novel preference-conditioned Bellman operator, motivated from the Chebyshev scalarization, designed to compute deterministic Pareto-optimal policies for Multi-Objective Markov Decision Processes (MOMDPs). We prove that this operator satisfies an enveloping property, where the estimated value functions upper-bound the true Pareto frontier, and demonstrate that it monotonically converges to a coverage set of this frontier. Furthermore, we also show how to extract deterministic policies from these converged Q-estimates. This ensures the agent can recover a policy for any given preference, capturing the entire Pareto-optimal frontier while guaranteeing each synthesized policy remains approximately Pareto-optimal. Experimental results validate that our algorithm successfully recovers complex trade-offs, providing a solution for deterministic Pareto-optimal policy synthesis.

</details>


### [193] [Beyond Feedforward Networks: Reentry Neural Systems as the Fundamental Basis of Subjecthood and Intrinsic Safety of Next-Generation AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26406)
*A. S. Ushakov,Yu. N. Berdinsk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A claim-driven blueprint for safe AGI built around a closed reentry loop (D <-> I) with self-modeling, self-preservation, and non-textual goal encoding, introducing a polynomial-time S-measure with machine-verified proofs, plus full implementations and a roadmap of architectures and predictions; asserts deployability today.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address alignment and safety in AGI by enforcing self-referential, cycle-based architecture that can sustain goals and self-models, in contrast to feedforward networks, and to provide formal guarantees (via Lean 4 proofs) and practical tooling.

Method: Introduce a closed D<->I cycle with C >= 1 and rho > 1 for self-sustaining amplification; encode goals as a non-textual D-vector embedded in the architecture; define S-measure (O(N^3)) as a polynomial-time alternative to Tononi’s Phi, with machine-verified Lean 4 proofs that S>0 implies positive integrated information; provide full Python/NumPy implementations (Tarjan-based cycle complexity, Delta-S barrier), deployment scaffolding (Apache Kafka, Docker Compose), a six-epoch AI evolution taxonomy, a zoo of reentry architectures (RAS, diffusion attractors, fractal loops), gauge-invariant networks for safe swarms, fault-tolerance/recovery protocols, and eight falsifiable predictions.

Result: If accepted, the framework claims emergence of a self-model, instrumental self-preservation, and goal-directed behavior; asserts S>0 guarantees positive integrated information; supports machine-verified proofs; provides ready-to-deploy tooling and a detailed future-architecture roadmap including eight falsifiable predictions.

Conclusion: The abstract presents a self-contained, safety-by-design AGI architecture with formal verification and practical implementations; however, the bold safety guarantees hinge on unproven assumptions, abstract measures of information, and real-world validity, necessitating independent validation, scrutiny of the non-textual goal encoding, and empirical testing to assess robustness and deployment feasibility.

Abstract: We propose a complete architectural blueprint for safe artificial general intelligence based on a closed reentry loop (D <-> I cycle). In contrast to feedforward networks, which are directed acyclic graphs (C=0, S=0) incapable of self-reference, the proposed architecture contains a structural cycle (C >= 1) with self-sustaining amplification (rho > 1), mathematically guaranteeing the emergence of a self-model, instrumental self-preservation, and unprogrammed goal-directed behaviour. The agent's goals are encoded as a non-textual D-vector in the architecture itself, making them immune to reinterpretation and prompt injection. We present the S-measure -- a polynomial-time [O(N^3)] computable alternative to Tononi's NP-hard Phi -- with machine-verified Lean 4 proof that S>0 implies positive integrated information. The work provides full Python/NumPy implementations (Tarjan-based cycle complexity, Delta-S barrier), industrial horizontal scaling via Apache Kafka and Docker Compose, a taxonomy of six epochs of AI evolution, a zoo of future reentry architectures (RAS, diffusion attractors, fractal loops), gauge-invariant networks for safe swarms, fault-tolerance and recovery protocols, and eight falsifiable predictions. All formal proofs are machine-verified in Lean 4. This architecture is deployable today and represents a topologically protected, safe-by-design approach to AGI.

</details>


### [194] [Otter Weather: Skillful and Computationally Efficient Medium-Range Weather Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26421)
*Cristiana Diaconu,Jonas Scholz,Aliaksandra Shysheya,Stratis Markou,Payel Mukhopadhyay,Miles Cranmer,Richard E. Turner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Otter Weather is an efficient, scalable AI weather forecasting model that surpasses NWP baselines with far reduced training compute and extends to probabilistic forecasting; demonstrates strong skill-per-compute and generalizes to other PDE tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Democratize access to high-performing AI weather forecasts by reducing training budgets and enabling rapid iteration; address limitations of current heavy frontier models.

Method: Develop Otter family (spatiotemporal forecasting model) trained on ERA5 data at 1.5° resolution with WeatherBench protocols; evaluate deterministic and probabilistic (CRPS) versions; scale to Otter-XL; compare against NWP baselines and frontier models; extend to acoustic scattering PDE task.

Result: Deterministic: 9.6% improvement over best NWP at 24h; training <3.5 A100-days; 2x efficiency vs lightweight AI; 100x compute reduction vs frontier architectures. Probabilistic: Otter-XL +9.7% CRPS over IFS ENS baseline; ~2x predictive skill over similar compute budgets; Otter-XL beats GenCast by >2% with 1 order of magnitude less compute. Out-of-the-box to acoustic scattering PDE task, outperforming current state-of-the-art foundation modelling.

Conclusion: Otter achieves strong skill-per-compute Pareto frontier, enabling democratized high-performance weather forecasting and demonstrating cross-domain applicability to complex PDE tasks.

Abstract: State-of-the-art medium-range AI weather models can outperform traditional Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) but require massive training budgets. This restricts usage for under-resourced groups and severely limits fast model iteration. Here we develop Otter Weather, a highly efficient spatiotemporal forecasting model designed to democratise high-performance weather prediction with AI. Evaluated on ERA5 reanalysis data at 1.5° resolution using standard WeatherBench protocols, the Otter family significantly advances the skill-compute Pareto frontier. The deterministic version outperforms the best NWP baseline by 9.6% at a 24-hour lead time while requiring fewer than 3.5 A100-days for training. It provides a 2x efficiency gain over lightweight AI models and a 100-fold reduction in compute compared to resource-intensive frontier architectures. We extend these efficiency gains into probabilistic forecasting by training via the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS). Scaling to a larger architecture, Otter-XL achieves a 9.7% CRPS improvement over the IFS ENS baseline. This yields an almost two-fold increase in predictive skill over comparable lightweight models at similar compute budgets. Otter-XL also outperforms frontier architectures like GenCast by over 2%, while using an order of magnitude less compute. Finally, Otter is applied out-of-the-box to a complex acoustic scattering PDE task where it outperforms a state-of-the-art foundation modelling approach, suggesting that the advances made here might apply across a range of scientific domains.

</details>


### [195] [Rethinking Training & Inference for Forecasting: Linking Winner-Take-All back to GMMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26424)
*Qiyuan Wu,Katie Z Luo,Bharath Hariharan,Wei-Lun Chao,Mark Campbell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hard winner-take-all training of conditional Gaussian mixtures yields uninformative, over-segmented mode posteriors in trajectory forecasting. The paper proposes two lightweight post-hoc fixes—test-time posterior-weighted merging of nearby trajectories and a one-step EM update to replace hard labels with soft responsibilities—leading to more informative mode posteriors and better displacement performance without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify and remediate the training–inference mismatch in GMM-based trajectory forecasters trained with WTA loss, which tends to produce poor, unstable mode posteriors due to hard, K-means-like assignments.

Method: Introduce two post-hoc treatments applicable to WTA-trained architectures: (1) test-time posterior-weighted merging of nearby candidate trajectories; (2) a one-step EM update that replaces hard labels with soft responsibilities, distributing probability mass across neighboring modes. Evaluate across several WTA-trained architectures without retraining.

Result: More informative, faithfully ranked mode posteriors; improved final forecasts on displacement metrics; gains achieved without retraining. The approach unifies GMM-vs-K-means perspectives and yields practical corrections that align inference with training objectives.

Conclusion: A GMM-vs-K-means lens clarifies design choices in multimodal trajectory forecasting and offers principled, minimal-overhead corrections (post-hoc merging and one-step EM) to improve posterior quality and forecast accuracy without retraining.

Abstract: Trajectory forecasting for autonomous driving has advanced rapidly, yet representative models often produce uninformative posteriors over forecast modes, causing problems for mode pruning. We trace this to a modeling-training mismatch: forecasters are typically modeled as conditional Gaussian mixture models (GMMs) but trained with a winner-take-all (WTA) loss that assigns each sample to its nearest mode. We argue that this K-means-like hard assignment (one-hot), while preventing mode collapse, is the source of uninformative mode probabilities: it over-segments the trajectory space, ignores relatedness among nearby modes, and yields assignment instability under small perturbations. Guided by this lens, we introduce two post-hoc treatments: (1) test-time posterior-weighted merging that aggregates nearby candidate trajectories; and (2) a one-step expectation-maximization (EM) update that replaces hard labels with soft responsibilities, sharing probability mass across neighboring modes. Across several WTA-trained architectures, these lightweight steps produce more informative, faithfully ranked mode posteriors and strengthen final forecasts on popular displacement metrics -- without retraining. Our analysis unifies recent design choices through a GMM-vs-K-means perspective and offers principled, practical corrections that better align training objectives with inference.

</details>


### [196] [DualEval: Joint Model-Item Calibration for Unified LLM Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26429)
*Aaron J. Li,Hao Huang,Youngmin Park,Yitong Ma,Wei-Lin Chiang,Li Chen,Cho-Jui Hsieh,Bin Yu,Ion Stoica*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DualEval is a latent model-item calibration framework that unifies static benchmarks and arena-style evaluation by jointly modeling model ability with item difficulty and sharpness, yielding stable rankings and item-level diagnostics across multiple domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM evaluation relies on two signals (static correctness labels and arena-style preferences) that are often disjoint. A joint framework is needed to calibrate models and evaluation items in a shared latent space.

Method: Represent models and evaluation items in a shared latent space; jointly estimate model ability, item difficulty, and item sharpness. Apply across four domains (coding, math, miscellaneous domain knowledge, and everyday queries) using 18 frontier LLMs, static benchmark labels, and reward-model scores validated against held-out human preferences.

Result: Produces reliable and balanced model rankings; learns item-level profiles enabling benchmark compression for sample-efficient evaluation and anomaly detection for contamination or outliers; supports auditable and interpretable evaluation pipelines across domains.

Conclusion: DualEval unifies static and arena-style evaluation through joint model-item calibration, enabling more sample-efficient, interpretable, and auditable evaluation pipelines with model rankings and item-level diagnostics.

Abstract: Current LLM evaluation relies on two complementary but often disconnected signals: static benchmarks with objective correctness labels and arena-style preference data that better reflect open-ended user interactions. We introduce DualEval, a latent model-item calibration framework that represents models and evaluation items in a shared space, jointly estimating model ability together with item difficulty and sharpness. We apply DualEval across four domains: coding, math, miscellaneous domain-knowledge tasks, and generic everyday user queries. Our evaluation uses 18 frontier LLMs, static benchmark labels, and reward-model scores validated against held-out human preferences for open-ended model responses. Empirically, our framework produces reliable and balanced model rankings, and its learned item-level profiles support downstream applications such as benchmark compression for sample-efficient evaluation and anomaly detection for contamination or outlier analysis. Overall, DualEval unifies static and arena-style evaluation through joint model-item calibration, producing model rankings and item-level diagnostics that support more sample-efficient, interpretable, and auditable evaluation pipelines.

</details>


### [197] [Embedding Foundation Model Predictions in Discrete-Choice Models with Structural Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26432)
*Yingshuo Wang,Xian Sun,Yanhang Li,Zhichao Fan,Zexin Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A two-stage adapter converts foundation-model predicted choice probabilities into a constrained multinomial logit with a neural correction, preserving the MNL's marginal rate of substitution and value-of-time, yielding accuracy gains and guaranteed economic properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fix economic-logic violations in tabular foundation models for choice prediction (e.g., non-monotone demand vs. price, implausible WTP, nonzero prob for unavailable alternatives) by enforcing MNL structure while leveraging rich predictions from large models.

Method: Stage 1: fit MNL structural coefficients using maximum likelihood with sign constraints. Stage 2: freeze coefficients and train a small neural network that corrects the foundation-model probabilities. The combined system preserves the MNL's marginal rate of substitution exactly.

Result: Across three datasets and two foundation models, the adapter improves test accuracy by 6.4 percentage points on average over the MNL baseline and up to 12.8 pp, maintains 100% cost monotonicity, and yields WTP values within established transportation-economics ranges. Robust to context restriction, retaining at least 6 pp of accuracy gain with only 10% of original foundation-model context.

Conclusion: The adapter provides a principled, guaranteed preservation of multinomial logit economic properties while enhancing predictive accuracy by exploiting foundation-model predictions; it offers robust performance under limited context and preserves interpretable economic metrics like value-of-time.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models achieve strong accuracy on choice prediction tasks, but their predictions often violate the economic logic those tasks require: raising a price can increase predicted demand, implied willingness-to-pay estimates are frequently negative or implausible, and unavailable alternatives receive nonzero probability. We propose a two-stage adapter that takes a foundation model's predicted choice probabilities as a precomputed feature and embeds them inside a multinomial logit's utility. In Stage 1, we fit the multinomial logit's structural coefficients by maximum likelihood with sign constraints; in Stage 2, we freeze those coefficients and fit a small neural correction operating on the foundation model's predictions. We prove that this composition exactly preserves the multinomial logit's marginal rate of substitution, so analytically computable value-of-time becomes a mathematical guarantee rather than an empirical accident. Across three datasets and two foundation models, the adapter gains 6.4 percentage points (pp) of test accuracy on average over the multinomial logit and up to 12.8 pp, maintains 100% cost monotonicity, and produces values of time within the published transportation-economics range on the transportation datasets. Performance degrades gracefully under foundation-model context restriction, retaining at least 6 pp of accuracy gain even at 10% of the original foundation-model context.

</details>


### [198] [Optimizing CUDA like a Human: Micro-Profiling Tools as Expert Surrogates for LLM-Based GPU Kernel Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26453)
*Jiading Gai,Shuai Zhang,Kaj Bostrom,Jin Huang,Vihang Patil,Haoyang Fang,Bernie Wang,Huzefa Rangwala,George Karypis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KernelPro is a CUDA kernel optimization framework that couples LLM-driven code generation with semantic feedback, multi-stage profiling, and MCTS-based search to auto-optimize kernels, achieving state-of-the-art speedups and energy efficiency on GPU benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate GPU kernel development and optimization by closing the loop between code generation and hardware feedback, reducing manual tuning while improving energy efficiency.

Method: Four contributions: (1) semantic feedback operator converting hardware metrics into actionable guidance via micro-profiling tools; (2) two-stage tool invocation with roofline bottleneck classification guiding kernel-level, instruction-level, and system-level profilers; (3) domain-adapted Monte Carlo Tree Search with progressive widening, asymmetric branching, log-reward calibration, dead-end pruning, and cross-iteration memory; (4) direct CuTe source-level code generation through autonomous search over CUTLASS/CuTe codebase.

Result: On KernelBench, KernelPro achieves geometric mean speedups of 2.42x/4.69x/5.30x on Levels 1/2/3. On VeOmni expert-optimized MoE kernels, it achieves 1.23x over hand-tuned Triton by generating a from-scratch raw-CUDA+CuTe Hopper WGMMA kernel. Ablations show micro-profiling tools reduce time by significant margins (p<0.0001), MCTS search improves geometric mean by 26% (p=0.004), and proactive tool orchestration improves by 23% (p=0.035). It also reports an 11.6% energy reduction at matched speed, the first CUDA kernel coding agent to optimize energy efficiency beyond speed only.

Conclusion: KernelPro demonstrates that integrating LLM-driven code generation with semantic feedback, hierarchical profiling, and advanced search can yield substantial speedups and energy savings, establishing state-of-the-art capabilities for CUDA kernel optimization.

Abstract: We present KernelPro, a closed-loop multi-agent system that automatically generates, profiles, and iteratively optimizes GPU kernel code by integrating large language model (LLM) code generation with hardware profiler feedback and pluggable bottleneck detection tools. KernelPro introduces four contributions: (1) a semantic feedback operator that encodes expert heuristics as pluggable micro-profiling tools, transforming raw hardware metrics into actionable natural language guidance; (2) a two-stage tool invocation architecture where roofline-based bottleneck classification filters which specialized analysis tools execute, combining kernel-level (ncu), instruction-level (SASS), and system-level (nsys) profiling; (3) a domain-adapted MCTS with progressive widening, asymmetric branching, log-reward calibration, dead-end pruning, and search memory for cross-iteration learning; and (4) direct CuTe source-level code generation via autonomous code search over the CUTLASS/CuTe codebase. On KernelBench, KernelPro achieves geometric mean speedups of 2.42x/4.69x/5.30x on Levels 1/2/3, establishing state-of-the-art performance across all difficulty levels. On VeOmni's expert-optimized MoE training kernels, KernelPro achieves 1.23x over hand-tuned Triton by generating a from-scratch raw-CUDA+CuTe Hopper WGMMA kernel. Ablation studies demonstrate that each design component independently and significantly improves optimization quality: micro-profiling tools (p < 0.0001 vs raw metrics), MCTS search (26% higher geometric mean vs greedy, p = 0.004), and proactive tool orchestration (23% improvement, p = 0.035). Finally, KernelPro is the first CUDA kernel coding agent to optimize energy efficiency beyond the speed-only focus of prior systems, demonstrating an 11.6% measured energy reduction at matched speed.

</details>


### [199] [Finding the Time to Think: Learning Planning Budgets in Real-Time RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26463)
*Aneesh Muppidi,Firas Darwish,Dylan Cope,João F. Henriques,Jakob Nicolaus Foerster*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces variable-delay real-time RL where agents choose state-dependent deliberation time; a gated planner selects planning budgets; outperforms fixed-budget and heuristic baselines and transfers to two-GPU real-time setups across multiple games.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In real-time domains the environment advances during decision latency; fixed or naive delays hinder performance; there is a need for adaptive budgeting of planning time that accounts for state and dynamics.

Method: Formalize variable-delay RT-RL; train a lightweight gating policy on top of a planner to decide per-state planning budgets; evaluate on real-time Pac-Man, Tetris, Snake, Speed Hex, Speed Go; test cross-GPU transfer.

Result: Gating policy yields superior performance over fixed-budget and heuristic baselines; budgets are state-dependent and improve responsiveness; demonstrates transfer to a real-time setup with separate GPUs for environment and agent.

Conclusion: Adaptive, state-aware budgeting for planning in real-time RL improves efficiency and performance, enabling practical deployment and cross-hardware scalability of real-time planning agents.

Abstract: Deliberating takes time. In real-time settings, that time is not free. Standard reinforcement learning (RL) sidesteps this as the environment waits indefinitely for the agent's decision. Instead, we study real-time RL environments where the environment progresses while waiting for the agent's action. Building on prior real-time formalizations, we introduce variable-delay real-time RL, where the agent chooses how long to deliberate at each decision point since the environment progresses. For the planning agents we use, the right delay is state-dependent, and naively planning how long to plan can paralyze the agent. We instead approach this setting by training a lightweight gating policy on top of a planner to select state-dependent planning budgets. Across real-time Pac-Man, Tetris, Snake, Speed Hex, and Speed Go, our gating policy outperforms fixed-budget and heuristic baselines, and transfers to a real-time setup where the environment and agent run on two different GPUs.

</details>


### [200] [A Causal Foundation Model for Structure and Outcome Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26467)
*Max Zhu,Martino Mansoldo,Ching-Hao Wang,Stefan Groha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TabPFN-CFM: a unified causal foundation model for tabular data that can predict causal structure and outcomes from observational data, answer queries at all three levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy, and use known graph structure when available; trained on synthetic data and generalizes to real data, outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address multiple causal tasks (structure discovery, outcome prediction, and relational queries) within a single model for tabular data, leveraging observational data and prior graph structure to boost accuracy.

Method: Extend TabPFN to a causal foundation model that outputs both structure (graph edges) and outcomes, supports queries across the three levels of Pearl's hierarchy, and uses known graph structure when provided; trained on synthetic datasets and evaluated on real data, comparing against structural and outcome baselines.

Result: Demonstrates improved performance over both structural and outcome baselines on synthetic and real datasets, with generalization to real data.

Conclusion: A unified TabPFN-CFM model can handle multiple causal problems from observational data, leveraging known graphs and supporting all Pearl hierarchy levels, indicating strong potential for causal foundation models in tabular data.

Abstract: We introduce TabPFN-CFM, a causal foundation model that can handle multiple causal problems. TabPFN-CFM predicts both causal structure and outcomes from observational data, supports queries on all three levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy and uses known graph structure when available to improve predictions. TabPFN-CFM is trained on synthetic datasets, and generalises to real datasets, demonstrating improved performance over both structural and outcome prediction baselines.

</details>


### [201] [Epiphany-Aware KV Cache Eviction Without the Attention Matrix](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26472)
*Steven Kolawole,Virginia Smith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EpiKV introduces a training-free, kernel-free KV cache eviction score (epiphany score) that measures changes in the model’s internal representations during forward pass. It enables longer-context inference with competitive accuracy and substantial speedups compared to attention-based eviction baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing cache eviction relies on attention-weight as an importance proxy, which is noisy for long reasoning traces and prevents the use of fused kernels in production. A scalable, training-free eviction metric is needed to support much longer contexts.

Method: Define epiphany score as the change in internal representations read directly from the forward pass without constructing the attention matrix. Use this score for token eviction (EpiKV). No training, classifier, or custom kernel required; integrates with FlashAttention stacks unchanged, enabling up to ~16x longer feasible context.

Result: On MATH-500 with a 4096-token cache, EpiKV achieves 72% accuracy, matching the strongest attention-based baselines (ThinKV 71%, H2O 67%). A lag-normalized KV variant reaches 37% on AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens, outperforming the best attention-based method (33%) and delivering up to 2.8x speedup.

Conclusion: EpiKV provides a practical, training-free eviction mechanism that scales to much longer contexts while delivering competitive accuracy and substantial speedups, and remains compatible with production inference pipelines that rely on FlashAttention.

Abstract: As reasoning models emit chains of thought tens of thousands of tokens long, KV cache increasingly becomes a deployment bottleneck. Existing cache eviction methods rank tokens by attention weight, which is a noisy importance proxy in long reasoning traces, and prohibits the use of fused kernels in production inference by forcing the model to materialize the attention matrix. In this work, we instead score tokens with a metric we term the epiphany score: the change in the model's internal representation, read directly from the forward pass with no attention matrix and negligible extra state. Our resulting cache eviction method, EpiKV, requires no training, classifier, or custom kernel, and can be used directly in FlashAttention inference stacks unchanged -- scaling to a 16x longer feasible context than attention-based scoring. upper-mid layers negatively) and remove a positional trend with a causal rolling z-score. At a 4096-token cache EpiKV reaches 72% on MATH-500, matching the strongest attention-based baseline (ThinKV 71%, H2O 67%); a lag-normalized KV variant reaches 37% on AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens against the best of them (33%), at up to 2.8x the speed.

</details>


### [202] [When Does Quality-Aware Multimodal Fusion Matter? A Leakage-Safe Diagnostic for Decision-Level Dependence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26473)
*Jaden Moon,Arvind Pillai,Andrew Campbell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Relability scores used in multimodal fusion do not causally influence inference when scrambled; they only help when they correctly predict unimodal correctness; a diagnostic reveals whether fusion decisions rely on reliability signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether per-modality reliability estimates are used during inference or merely correlate with overall performance.

Method: Train multimodal model; fix model and inputs; permute reliability scores across test examples; compare performance to baseline. Use StressID (stress recognition) and CMU-MOSEI (sentiment); include positive-control scenarios where reliability signals do identify the correct modality to test if the fusion rule can exploit them.

Result: Permuting reliability scores leaves performance unchanged, even though there is potential gain from selecting the best modality. In positive-control cases, the same fusion rules yield improvements, indicating that reliability signals influence decisions only when they reliably predict unimodal correctness.

Conclusion: Reliability signals influence fused decisions only when they accurately forecast single-modality correctness; otherwise, they are not used by the fusion mechanism. This suggests caution in relying on modality reliability as a decision factor unless it consistently predicts unimodal accuracy.

Abstract: Many multimodal systems estimate the reliability of each modality and weight their contributions to the final prediction. However, it remains unclear whether these scores influence model decisions or merely correlate with performance. We propose a simple diagnostic to test whether reliability information is used during inference. After training, the model and inputs are fixed while reliability scores are permuted across test examples. If predictions depend on these scores, performance should degrade. Experiments on StressID for stress recognition and CMU-MOSEI for sentiment analysis show that permuting reliability scores leaves performance unchanged despite substantial potential gains from selecting the best modality per example. In positive controls where reliability signals identify the correct modality, the same frozen fusion rules yield significant improvements, indicating that reliability signals influence fused decisions only when they reliably predict unimodal correctness.

</details>


### [203] [Localizing RL-Induced Tool Use to a Single Crosscoder Feature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26474)
*Andrii Shportko,Shubham Bhokare,Ahmed Zeyad A Alzahrani,Bowen Cheng,Gustavo Mercier,Jessica Hullman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC) reveal a compact RL-specific feature set that mediates tool-calling in LLMs; crosscoder analysis shows encoded-decode reconstruction boosts tool correctness and spills over capability to frozen base models, enabling runtime control of agentic behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the mechanistic basis of RL-induced agentic behaviors in LLMs, specifically how RL changes representations to enable tool use, and whether such features can be isolated for retraining-free control.

Method: Train-and-evaluate 48 crosscoder variants (DFC) that partition RL-induced features; measure encode-decode reconstruction effects on tool-calling accuracy in Qwen2.5-3B; assess capability spillover to frozen base model; report performance gains with pp (percentage points).

Result: Across 48 crosscoders, encode-decode reconstruction improves tool correctness by 31.1 ± 9.7 pp; frozen base model gains tool-calling ability by 6.8 ± 5.0 pp; DFC concentrates RL capability into a minimal, steerable feature set.
