Conclusion: DFC partitioning yields a compact, manipulable feature set that captures RL-induced tool-use capabilities, enabling runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs without extensive retraining.

Abstract: Fine-tuning through RL reshapes the internal representations of language models to enable agentic behaviors such as tool use, yet the mechanistic basis of these changes remains poorly understood. While RL substantially improves structured tool-call generation, it is unclear which features emerge, which are preserved, and whether identified features can be leveraged for retraining-free behavioral control. In this work, we show that $\textit{Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC)}$ isolate a compact set of RL-specific features that mediate tool-calling capability in $\texttt{Qwen2.5-3B}$. Across a $48$-crosscoder hyperparameter sweep, encode-decode reconstruction improves the RL model's tool correctness by $+31.1 \pm {9.7}$ pp and passively transfers tool-calling ability to the frozen base model by $+6.8 \pm 5.0$ pp which we call a $\textit{capability spillover}$. Our findings show that DFC partitioning concentrates RL-introduced capability into a minimal, steerable feature set that enables runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs.

</details>


### [204] [Retrieval-Warmed Energy-Based Reasoning: A Five-Arm Ablation Methodology for Diffusion-as-Inference on Structured Reasoning Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26476)
*Libo Sun,Po-Wei Harn,Zewei Zhang,Peixiong He,Xiao Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RW-EBR with a five-arm ablation isolates how warm-started diffusion gains arise in a retrieval-augmented energy-based reasoning system, showing graph-aligned value reuse dominates on connectivity graphs, with task-dependent blocking components (value quality in diffusion, key quality in Sudoku).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand which parts of warm-started diffusion pipelines drive performance gains and provide a diagnostic framework to separate confounded effects (class-prior bias, stochastic warm-start, value reuse) when applied to structured, spatio-temporal reasoning tasks.

Method: Propose RW-EBR, an energy-based diffusion model augmented with Modern Hopfield trajectory memory. Introduce a five-arm ablation suite (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) to disentangle effects. Adapt diagnostic from LLM-RAG (graph reachability diagnostic) and apply to connectivity-2 ER graphs and a Sudoku task with a task-specific key encoder.

Result: Aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing yields +35 pp balanced accuracy on a fixed 1,000-graph diagnostic; per-query random init falls below cold, with alignment being the dominant factor over bias or stochasticity. In deployed cold-prediction, stored-value quality gates fail. Sudoku shows a different blocking component (key quality). The analysis identifies the first blocking component per task and frames the problem within structured/spatio-temporal reasoning.

Conclusion: The framework clarifies that graph-aligned value reuse is a key driver of performance in retrieval-augmented diffusion on connectivity graphs, while revealing task-specific failure modes (value quality vs. key quality). It provides a diagnostic lens to explain and localize breakdowns in iterative diffusion with memory, suggesting broader applicability to structured reasoning tasks and future work on memory retrieval quality and cross-task transfer of the diagnostic methodology.

Abstract: Warm-started diffusion samplers accelerate iterative inference, but it is rarely clear which part of the pipeline carries the gain. We study \textbf{retrieval-warmed energy-based reasoning (RW-EBR)} -- an IRED energy-based diffusion model \cite{du2024ired} augmented with a Modern Hopfield trajectory memory -- and contribute a \textbf{five-arm ablation methodology} (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) that separates three confounded effects: class-prior bias shift, stochastic warm-starting, and graph-aligned value reuse. The diagnostic decomposition is adapted from LLM-RAG evaluation \cite{ru2024ragchecker}. On \textbf{connectivity-2} (Erdős--Rényi all-pairs reachability), the aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing reaches \textbf{$+35$\,pp} balanced accuracy on a fixed 1{,}000-graph validation-set diagnostic, with value distribution and retrieval mechanics fixed, only per-graph alignment destroyed, while per-query random initialisation falls below cold -- per-graph alignment, not bias shift or stochasticity, dominates. Yet the \emph{deployable} cold-prediction pipeline misses the acceptance gate at stored-value quality. The same diagnostic logic, stopped at the key-quality screen, applied to \textbf{Sudoku} with a task-specific key encoder produces a clean negative at a \emph{different} component -- key quality, under the current setup. The decomposition names the first blocking component on each task. The setting -- graph reachability refined by an iterative diffusion sampler, with explainability of failure modes as the lens -- places the work within structured and spatio-temporal reasoning.

</details>


### [205] [What Survives When You Compress a Recursive Reasoner for the Edge?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26488)
*Pearse Jim,Steven Kolawole,Opegbemi Matthias Busoye,Glory Bagai,Virginia Smith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Aggressive compression preserves local predictions but destroys global recursive reasoning; per-channel calibrated INT4 (no retraining) plus streaming embeddings and INT8 enable edge deployment with maintained accuracy; carry-trajectory fidelity predicts damage and recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how compression errors propagate in recursive reasoning and how to design deployment strategies for edge hardware.

Method: Comprehensive precision sweep across tasks and two recursive architectures; evaluate quantization (INT4/INT8), distillation, linear attention; analyze impact on cell-level accuracy vs puzzle-exact/global accuracy; introduce carry-trajectory fidelity; test architectural collapse and recovery; examine streaming embeddings, calibration, etc.

Result: Local cell accuracy survives aggressive compression, global puzzle accuracy collapses under naive INT4 pruning; token-level objectives cannot fix; collapse is architectural (MLP-mixing) not with attention; per-channel calibrated INT4 reverses collapse without retraining; streaming embeddings remove a 99.4MB bottleneck; INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy with 6x fewer FLOPs on 8MB SoC; calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller; carry-trajectory fidelity correlates with damage and recovery.

Conclusion: Deployment recommendations: use flash-streamed embeddings, INT8 to maintain accuracy with reduced FLOPs, and calibrated INT4 for ultra-compact devices; monitor carry-trajectory fidelity to predict damage and recovery; recognize architecture-specific effects of quantization, as naive compression can obliterate global recursive reasoning.

Abstract: Recursive reasoning models can solve complex structured tasks with only a few million parameters by repeatedly updating a latent state. Deploying these models on edge hardware requires significant compression, but unlike conventional sequence models, quantization errors compound across recursive reasoning cycles rather than across output tokens. As a result, standard intuitions about compression fail to apply. In this work, we ask what survives when recursive reasoners are compressed. Across a full precision sweep, three tasks, and two recursive architectures, we find that aggressive compression preserves local prediction but destroys global reasoning: cell accuracy holds while puzzle-exact accuracy collapses to zero under naive INT4 pruning, distillation, and linear attention alike. Token-level objectives, including quantization-aware training, cannot repair it. The collapse is architectural -- it strikes MLP-mixing recursion but not attention on the same task -- and we reverse it with per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining. We also introduce carry-trajectory fidelity, the cosine similarity to the full-precision reasoning path, as a label-free signal that predicts this damage and its recovery before a task evaluation. The combined result is a deployment recipe: flash-streamed embeddings remove a 99.4MB bottleneck, INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy at 6x fewer FLOPs (8MB SoC), and calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller.

</details>


### [206] [Learning Probabilistic Filters with Strictly Proper Scoring Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26497)
*Eviatar Bach,Ricardo Baptista,Jochen Bröcker,Bohan Chen,Andrew Stuart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PSEF (proper scoring ensemble filter) is a transformer-based, permutation-invariant ensemble data assimilation method trained on synthetic state-observation trajectories using strictly proper scoring rules (energy score) to approximate Bayesian filtering; it outperforms classical methods and MSE-trained learners, with guidance: use EnKF corrections for near-Gaussian problems and end-to-end non-Gaussian models for highly non-Gaussian cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty quantification in Bayesian filtering requires the full conditional distribution, but the true filter is rarely available as a supervised target. Synthetic data generation enables training an analysis map to approximate the filtering distribution.

Method: Train a permutation-invariant, transformer-based analysis map that maps a forecast ensemble and observations to an analysis ensemble. Use strictly proper scoring rules (energy score) as the training objective. The population objective is minimized by the true Bayesian filtering distribution under a realizability assumption. Derive a finite-ensemble empirical objective and relate it to the population objective via a mean-field consistency argument.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate that the learned filter captures nonlinear, non-Gaussian, and multi-modal posteriors and achieves stronger performance than classical data assimilation methods or learning-based methods trained with mean-squared error objectives.

Conclusion: PSEF provides a principled, probabilistic filtering approach that leverages synthetic data and proper scoring rules. It offers guidance on method choice: interpolate with EnKF corrections for near-Gaussian problems and prefer end-to-end non-Gaussian filtering for highly non-Gaussian cases.

Abstract: Bayesian filtering of partially and noisily observed dynamical systems seeks to infer the evolving conditional distribution of the state of a dynamical system, given observations, in an online fashion. This Bayesian filtering distribution is the natural object for uncertainty quantification, but it is rarely available as a supervised learning target. However, one can often use the forecast model to generate synthetic system trajectories, along with synthetic observations. We introduce the proper scoring ensemble filter (PSEF), an ensemble data assimilation method based on training an analysis map to approximate the filtering distribution using only synthetic state--observation trajectories. The analysis step is represented as a permutation-invariant, transformer-based map that takes as input a forecast ensemble and observations, producing an analysis ensemble. Training is based on strictly proper scoring rules -- with the energy score used in our implementation -- so that probabilistic accuracy is rewarded over the whole probability distribution. We prove that, under a realizability assumption, the population objective is minimized by the true Bayesian filtering distribution. We also derive the finite-ensemble empirical objective used in training and relate its single state--observation trajectory form to the population objective, using a mean-field consistency argument. Numerical experiments show that the learned filter accurately approximates challenging filtering distributions, including nonlinear, non-Gaussian, and multi-modal posteriors, and achieves stronger performance in data assimilation tasks than classical methods or learning-based methods with mean-squared-error objectives. For close-to-Gaussian problems, learning a correction to the EnKF is the best approach, while for highly non-Gaussian problems an end-to-end approach that discards this inductive bias is superior.

</details>


### [207] [Multipath Adaptive Gated Bottleneck Latent ODE with Raman Data Fusion for Cell Culture Process Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26520)
*Johnny Peng,Thanh Tung Khuat,Ellen Otte,Katarzyna Musial,Bogdan Gabrys*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An adaptive, regime-aware forecasting framework for mammalian cell culture using a gated latent ODE with multi-path fine-tuning and Raman fusion, achieving superior multi-step forecasts across 38 runs and 14 conditions compared to a global baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early, multi-day forecasts are crucial for timely interventions in bioprocessing but data are sparse, irregular, and heterogeneous; standard forecasting struggles to capture divergent futures from similar early behaviour.

Method: 1) GB-Latent ODE with variable-wise gating and a mask-aware bottleneck to compress sparse high-dimensional inputs; 2) Multi-Path Just-In-Time Fine Tuning (MP-JIT-FT): retrieve similar histories, cluster into regimes, fine-tune a per-regime model to generate multiple plausible paths with reconstruction-based confidence; 3) Raman fusion via a machine-learning soft sensor mapping dense Raman spectra to pseudo-observations to enrich data.

Result: On 38 fed-batch 5L bioreactor runs across 14 conditions, MP-JIT-FT with Raman fusion achieves best average rank and outperforms a global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables. Local-divergence metrics show multi-path gains peak when early prefixes converge but later diverge; Raman fusion most helpful when early dynamics predict later behavior.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptive, regime-aware, multi-path forecasting framework with Raman data fusion improves early-stage bioprocess forecasts under sparse data, enabling timely process adjustments; gains are especially notable in regime-divergent localities and when early dynamics are informative of later outcomes.

Abstract: Mammalian cell-culture processes underpin the manufacture of many biopharmaceuticals, yet keeping a run on track is hard: critical process parameters drift over days, and an off-specification trend is often confirmed too late to intervene. Early-stage, multi-day forecasts could enable timely adjustment of feeding, sampling, and control, but bioprocess forecasting is challenging because measurements are sparse and irregularly sampled, operating conditions are heterogeneous across cell lines and media, and runs with near-identical early behaviour can diverge into different futures. We propose an adaptive framework combining a Gated Bottleneck Latent Ordinary Differential Equation (GB-Latent ODE) with Multi-Path Just-In-Time Fine Tuning (MP-JIT-FT). The GB-Latent ODE augments the stan dard Latent ODE with learnable variable-wise gating and a mask-aware bottleneck that compress high-dimensional sparse inputs, improving learning under limited data. Given a partially observed run, MP-JIT-FT retrieves similar historical trajectories, clusters the local neighbourhood into candidate regimes, and fine-tunes a separate model per regime to produce multiple plausible paths, each with a reconstruction-based confidence score, not a single averaged forecast. We further fuse Raman spectroscopy data: a machine-learning soft sensor turns dense Raman spectra into pseudo-observations that enrich the sparse offline measurements for more robust training. On 38 fed-batch 5L bioreactor runs spanning 14 conditions, MP-JIT-FT with Raman fusion achieves the best average rank and outperforms a global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables. Using local-divergence metrics, we show the multi-path gains are largest when locally similar prefixes diverge, whereas Raman fusion helps most when early dynamics are representative of later behaviour.

</details>


### [208] [Theory-Scale Auto-Formalization of Logics for Computer Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26525)
*Yuming Feng,Frederick Pu,One An,Osbert Bastani,Li Zhang,Jiani Huang,Xujie Si,Ziyang Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LCS-Bench is a comprehensive theory-scale auto-formalization benchmark for Logics for Computer Science, built with a semi-automated pipeline; it covers 327 textbook items, 4k+ Lean declarations, and 85k+ lines of Lean code, supporting five evaluation tracks and a definitional equivalence protocol. State-of-the-art models perform only ~20% on auto-formalization tasks, indicating the benchmark’s difficulty and the need for improved methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable scalable, theory-scale auto-formalization in formal verification by providing a faithful, scalable benchmark that captures hundreds of interdependent definitions, lemmas, and theorems, addressing issues of consistency, faithfulness, scalability, and correctness that plague existing isolated-statement benchmarks.

Method: A novel semi-automated agentic pipeline (concept graphs, formal signature planning, issue tracking, counter-example guided 'sorry-filled' steps, and human-expert faithfulness reviews) builds LCS-Bench from Logics for Computer Science, yielding 327 textbook items, 4,076 Lean declarations, and >85k lines of Lean code. A data engine derives five evaluation tracks to assess auto-formalization and theorem-proving; a definitional equivalence checker-based evaluation protocol is introduced for finer fidelity.

Result: LCS-Bench is demonstrated to be high quality, consistent, and faithful. It is challenging for current models, with state-of-the-art achieving 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks. The analysis identifies key insights into theory-scale auto-formalization and outlines promising directions for future work.

Conclusion: LCS-Bench provides a rigorous, theory-scale benchmark and evaluation protocol that enables robust assessment of auto-formalization and theorem-proving capabilities; it highlights existing gaps and points toward directions for advancing theory-scale formalization research.

Abstract: Auto-formalization is critical for scalable formal verification, but existing progress largely focuses on isolated statements, while theory-scale auto-formalization, which coherently translates hundreds of interdependent definitions, lemmas, and theorems, remains open due to challenges in consistency, faithfulness, scalability, and correctness. In this paper, we introduce LCS-Bench, a stand-alone, theory-scale benchmark based on Logics for Computer Science. LCS-Bench is built through a novel semi-automated agentic pipeline that leverages concept graphs, formal signature planning, issue tracking, sorry-filling with counter-example search, complemented by faithfulness review from human experts. The resulting artifact covers 327 textbook items, over 4,076 Lean declarations, and more than 85K lines of Lean code. The dataset supports broad evaluation through a data engine that automatically derives five tracks of evaluation benchmarks, measuring different aspects of auto-formalization and theorem-proving capabilities. We also introduce a novel evaluation protocol featuring definitional equivalence checkers, enabling more fine-grained and faithful assessment. Through extensive evaluation on 14 models, we demonstrate that (1) LCS-Bench is of high quality, consistent, and faithful; (2) the benchmark is challenging, with state-of-the-art models achieving only 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks; and (3) our analysis reveals key findings regarding theory-scale auto-formalization and suggests promising directions for future work.

</details>


### [209] [Sample-efficient Transfer Reinforcement Learning via Adaptive Reward Shaping and Policy-Ratio Reweighting Strategy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26527)
*Wenjie Huang,Yang Li,Jingjia Teng,Mingwei Jin,Kai Song,Yougang Bian,Yongfu Li,Qisong Yang,Helai Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A safe transfer reinforcement learning framework for autonomous highway lane changing that combines adaptive teacher interventions, teacher-guided reward shaping, and weighted optimization to improve safety and learning efficiency under distribution shifts; validated on NGSIM with substantial gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transfer learning in policy learning for autonomous highway lane changing is hampered by distribution shifts between source and target domains, causing training oscillations and unsafe exploration. Target-domain adaptation requires exploration, which conflicts with safety-critical settings.

Method: 1) Adaptive.teacher intervention based on instantaneous safety cost with progressive fading; provides dual-source samples for joint training and offers theoretical return bounds for mixed policies. 2) Teacher-guided safe transfer module uses teacher policy action evaluation in reward shaping to enhance safety and learning efficiency; guidance decays as policy safety improves. 3) Teacher-guided weighted optimization adjusts sample weights via a likelihood-ratio factor to stabilize transfer performance.

Result: Empirical evaluation across varied traffic densities and real-world NGSIM data shows the method surpasses baselines by >52.2% in safety and ~5.0% in learning efficiency.

Conclusion: A safety-aware transfer reinforcement learning framework with adaptive teacher interventions, reward shaping, and weight-adjusted training robustly improves safe autonomous lane-changes under distribution shifts and varying traffic conditions.

Abstract: Transfer learning improves policy learning efficiency by reusing knowledge from source tasks, providing a feasible paradigm for safe and efficient autonomous highway lane changing decision-making. Existing methods frequently encounter transfer mismatch induced by distribution shifts between source and target domains, leading to training oscillation and performance decline. Besides, target domain adaptation depends on exploratory interactions, which struggles to guarantee training safety in safety-critical lane changing cases. To tackle these limitations, this paper proposes a safe transfer reinforcement learning framework for autonomous highway lane changing. First, we design an adaptive teacher intervention mechanism based on instantaneous safety cost to restrain risky exploration and fade intervention strength progressively, with theoretical analysis on return bounds for mixed behavior policy. This intervention also produces dual-source samples for joint training. Second, a teacher-guided safe transfer module embeds action evaluation information of teacher policy into student learning via reward shaping to boost training safety and efficiency, with teacher guidance decaying as policy safety rises. Third, a teacher-guided weighted optimization mechanism adjusts sample weights in policy optimization using a likelihood ratio factor to stabilize transfer performance. Experiments under varied traffic densities and validations on real-world NGSIM dataset reveal that our method surpasses baseline approaches by over 52.2% in safety and 5.0% in learning efficiency. Results verify the efficacy and robustness of our safety-aware transfer strategy for autonomous highway lane changing under various traffic conditions.

</details>


### [210] [CascadeFormer: Depth-Tapered Transformers Motivated by Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26538)
*Huzama Ahmad,Cao Viet Hai Nam,Se-Young Yun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two efficiency methods for deep Transformers exploit gradient fan-in asymmetry: CascadeFormer and CascadeFlow Pruning. CascadeFormer tapers width with depth to match information flow, achieving similar perplexity with 8.6% lower latency and 9.4% higher throughput. CascadeFlow Pruning removes layers using accumulated training gradients, outperforming standard heuristics on perplexity and rank-stability while remaining competitive on downstream accuracy. A Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA) framework explains why deeper layers contribute less, with evidence from models up to 1.2B parameters and across Transformers and ResNets. The work suggests structure, not magnitude, is the bottleneck; equalizing gradient norms does not restore late-layer value, while increasing downstream path counts via parameter sharing can.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep Transformer stacks exhibit diminishing returns in the deepest layers due to asymmetric gradient flow. The work seeks practical methods to improve efficiency by leveraging this asymmetry and to provide a theoretical account (GFA) for the observed phenomena.

Method: 1) CascadeFormer: taper width with depth in a Pre-LayerNorm residual stack to align capacity with information flow. 2) CascadeFlow Pruning: prune layers based on accumulated training gradients, without post hoc analysis. 3) Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA): theoretical account where late-layer gradients decay due to the sum of an identity path and downstream paths, with linear (and quadratic under deep supervision) fan-in decay. 4) Empirical validation on models from scratch up to 1.2B parameters across Transformers and ResNets; interventional and correlational analyses of gradient structure; tests of gradient-magnitude equalization vs increased downstream path counts (parameter sharing).

Result: CascadeFormer achieves comparable perplexity to a uniform baseline at the same training budget while reducing latency by 8.6% and increasing throughput by 9.4%. CascadeFlow Pruning outperforms standard heuristics on perplexity and rank-stability and remains competitive on downstream accuracy. GFA is supported by correlational and interventional evidence across models up to 1.2B parameters, with two key interventions indicating structure (not magnitude) bottlenecks: equalizing per-layer gradient norms does not restore late-layer value; increasing downstream path counts via parameter sharing rescues and elevates it.

Conclusion: The study posits Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry as a structural reason for late-layer underutilization in deep networks. It shows that adjusting architecture (tapered width) and layer selection (pruning guided by gradients) yields practical efficiency gains. The bottleneck appears to be structure rather than mere magnitude, suggesting future work should explore very large-scale models (100B+ parameters) to assess whether gradient fan-in dynamics persist or change.

Abstract: Deep Transformers are composed of uniformly stacked residual blocks, yet their deepest layers often add little value. We present two efficiency methods that exploit this asymmetry. CascadeFormer tapers width with depth to match the uneven information flow across layers, achieving comparable perplexity to a uniform baseline at the same training budget while reducing latency by 8.6% and increasing throughput by 9.4%. CascadeFlow Pruning removes layers using accumulated training gradients, with no post hoc analysis. It outperforms standard heuristics on perplexity and rank-stability and stays competitive on downstream accuracy. To motivate these methods, we propose Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA) as a structural account of why deeper layers contribute less. In Pre-LayerNorm residual stacks, the gradient at a layer is the sum of an identity path and all downstream functional paths, producing a gradient fan-in that decays linearly with depth (and quadratically under deep supervision), yielding richer gradients for early layers and sparser ones for later layers. We provide correlational and interventional evidence for GFA on models trained from scratch up to 1.2B parameters. Across Transformers and ResNets, accumulated training gradients follow the theoretical fan-in and are associated with post hoc layer importance. Two interventions point to structure rather than magnitude as the bottleneck: equalizing per-layer gradient norms does not restore late-layer value, while increasing downstream path counts via parameter-shared repetition restores and elevates it. Whether gradient magnitude proxies fan-in beyond high-rank regimes, and how these dynamics behave at the 100B+ scale, remain open questions.

</details>


### [211] [Can Large Language Models Reliably Code Qualitative Humanitarian Data? A Benchmark Study Against Human Expert Adjudication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26541)
*Jerome Marston,Tino Kreutzer,Salomé Garnier,Ella Boone,Phuong N Pham,Patrick Vinck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs can reliably code humanitarian transcripts at levels comparable to expert coders under structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations, but aggregate metrics alone are insufficient; governance, structured codebooks, and oversight are essential; open-weight, self-hosted models offer a governance-friendly path for sensitive data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve timely, scalable interpretation of nuanced humanitarian needs from affected populations by leveraging LLMs to code qualitative data at scale.

Method: Benchmark study comparing 46 LLMs to a human Gold Standard using 150 high-fidelity synthetic humanitarian transcripts; evaluation combines Krippendorff's alpha for inter-coder reliability, discrepancy analysis (correct/near-correct/incorrect codes), and qualitative assessment across humanitarian-specific criteria (discrimination, complex need hierarchies, non-standard communication styles); experiments compare structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations.

Result: Several LLMs achieved deductive coding reliability at levels comparable to experienced human coders, especially with structured prompts and reasoning-enabled setups; aggregate reliability metrics alone are insufficient for deployment decisions. Models differed in recognizing indirect needs, out-of-scope categories, and protection-relevant concerns (e.g., safety, discrimination). Open-weights models on self-hosted infrastructure may balance scalability with governance; LLMs are augmentative, not substitutes for human judgment.

Conclusion: LLMs can materially expand humanitarian analytical capacity but require structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, theme-specific performance monitoring, and tiered human oversight; for sensitive data, governance-forward deployment (e.g., self-hosted, open-weights models) is advisable to combine scalability with data governance.

Abstract: Data from affected populations are crucial for informing humanitarian response, but their value depends on timely and consistent interpretation of nuanced accounts of need. Humanitarian organizations often lack the staff, time, and specialist expertise required to analyze this information at scale. Large language models (LLMs) may expand this capacity, but their reliability for coding qualitative humanitarian data has not been directly established. This benchmark study compares 46 LLMs to a human Gold Standard using 150 high-fidelity synthetic humanitarian transcripts. Evaluation combined inter-rater reliability testing with Krippendorff's alpha, discrepancy analysis distinguishing correct, near-correct, and incorrect codes, and qualitative assessment across humanitarian-specific criteria including discrimination, complex needs hierarchies, and non-standard communication styles. The authors find that multiple LLMs can perform deductive coding at reliability levels comparable to experienced human coders, especially when structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations are used. At the same time, aggregate reliability metrics alone are insufficient for deployment decisions. Models varied in recognizing needs expressed indirectly, needs outside predefined categories, and protection-relevant concerns such as physical safety and discrimination. These findings suggest that LLMs can materially expand humanitarian analytical capacity, but not as substitutes for human judgment. Appropriate use requires structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, attention to theme-specific performance, and tiered oversight focused on categories where miscoding would have the greatest programmatic consequences. For sensitive humanitarian data, open-weights models deployed on self-hosted infrastructure may offer a viable path for combining analytical scalability with stronger data governance.

</details>


### [212] [Revisiting Action Factorization for Complex Action Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26574)
*Timothy Flavin,Sandip Sen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study benchmarks hybrid-action factorization methods across PPO, SAC, and DQN on four lightweight environments, finding branching dueling architectures offer the best compute-performance balance, Auto-Regressive actions deliver top performance, and continuous SAC outperforms discrete/hybrid variants at higher compute cost; introduces CoopPush and Hybrid-Shoot to facilitate targeted testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world control often involves hybrid discrete-continuous actions; existing benchmarks are either heavyweight or limited in factorization method scope. A cross-sectional study across action spaces and RL algorithms is needed to guide method selection and to provide lightweight, reproducible environments.

Method: Evaluate factorization methods: independent networks, shared encoder, VDN, QPLEX, Joint, Auto-Regressive; across algorithms PPO, SAC, DQN; action spaces discretized, hybrid, continuous; four lightweight environments Platform, hybrid-LunarLander, Hybrid-Shoot, CoopPush; account for invalid pairings; total 220 configurations; implement two new environments CoopPush and Hybrid-Shoot; propose VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX with branching critic.

Result: Out of 220 configurations, results show branching dueling architectures balance compute and performance; Auto-Regressive actions reach highest performance overall; native continuous SAC outperforms discrete/hybrid variants, but with higher computational cost.

Conclusion: Branching architectures and Auto-Regressive actions are favorable for hybrid-action problems; continuous SAC offers best performance but requires more compute; provide guidance for practitioners balancing compute and performance; new environments enable isolation of state-dependent interaction dependencies.

Abstract: Many real-world control problems involve hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces. For example, steering and signaling in autonomous driving, and aiming and firing in robotics or video-games. Despite real-world hybrid factorization and reinforcement learning framework support for complex action spaces (e.g., Gymnasium, PettingZoo, TorchRL, SeedRL, Mujoco, etc), the default environments within those frameworks often implement uniform action space configurations (LunarLander, Walker2D, Cheetah, SMAC, SUMO, Ant, Atari). Landmark hybrid-action benchmarks (RoboCup 2D HFO, SC2LE, Platform, CARLA, etc) are mostly heavyweight or archival implementations originating from papers which test one or a small number of competing factorization methods on one kind of control. This article provides a cross-sectional study of factorization methods [independent networks, shared encoder, VDN, QPLEX, Joint, Auto-Regressive] on each of three families of algorithms [PPO, SAC, DQN] across three action spaces [discretized, hybrid, continuous] over four lightweight environments [Platform, hybrid-LunarLander, Hybrid-Shoot, CoopPush]. Accounting for some invalid pairings such as joint-continuous, we are left with 220 configurations to analyze each method. We provide two new C++ parallel gymnasium and petting-zoo compliant environments [CoopPush, Hybrid-Shoot] to isolate particular challenges such as state-dependent inter-action dependence. Finally, we introduce VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX which use a branching critic to assign credit to multi-headed PPO. These variants out-perform all other tested PPO factorizations. Our results suggest that branching dueling architectures balance compute and performance most effectively, with Auto-Regressive actions reaching the highest performance overall and native continuous SAC outperforming discrete and hybrid algorithms, albiet both at increased computational cost.

</details>


### [213] [SharQ: Bridging Activation Sparsity and FP4 Quantization for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26587)
*Haoqian Meng,Yilun Luo,Yafei Zhao,Wenyuan Liu,Huaqing Zheng,Xindian Ma,Peng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SharQ: a training-free inference method that enables FP4 activation quantization with online input-adaptive N:M sparsity by decomposing activations into a sparse backbone and a dense residual, achieving notable accuracy recovery and speedups across LLMs and vision-language tasks without calibration or retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of combining low-bit FP4 quantization with semi-structured (N:M) sparsity for activations in large language models, where input-dependent outliers and sparsification-quantization coupling hinder performance.

Method: For each activation tensor, generate an input-adaptive N:M mask to extract an outlier-dominated sparse backbone, quantize it to FP4, and define a dense residual relative to the quantized backbone. A sparse FP4 GEMM processes the backbone while a dense FP4 GEMM compensates for mask-induced loss and sparse-path quantization error. The two paths share a single FP4 weight payload with path-specific scales; a fused kernel performs mask generation, residual construction, and layer normalization in one operator. No calibration data, retraining, or model-specific tuning required.

Result: Achieves 43–63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap across language and vision-language tasks. On RTX 5090, yields 2.2–2.4x latency reduction versus FP16 and 1.2–1.4x throughput improvement over FP8 in language-model serving; up to 1.58x speedup on Wan2.2-T2V-A14B video generation with SageAttention when combined. Generalizes across NVFP4, HiF4, and MXFP4 formats; code available at the provided repo.

Conclusion: SharQ enables training-free activation compression by combining N:M sparsity with FP4 quantization through a sparse–dense decomposition, delivering meaningful accuracy recovery and hardware performance gains across diverse models and formats.

Abstract: Low-bit floating-point formats and semi-structured sparsity are increasingly supported by modern accelerators, yet combining them for LLM activation compression remains challenging: activations contain input-dependent outliers that dominate block scales in FP4 quantization, and directly applying N:M sparsity masks discards moderate values, coupling sparsification loss with quantization error. We introduce SharQ, a training-free inference method that bridges activation sparsity and FP4 quantization through an online sparse--dense decomposition. For each activation tensor, SharQ generates an input-adaptive N:M mask to extract an outlier-dominated sparse backbone, quantizes it to FP4, and defines a dense residual relative to the quantized sparse backbone rather than the unquantized sparse values. A sparse FP4 GEMM processes the backbone while a dense FP4 GEMM compensates for both mask-induced activation loss and sparse-path quantization error. The two paths share a single FP4 weight payload with path-specific scale views, and a fused preparation kernel absorbs mask generation, residual construction, and layer normalization into one operator. SharQ requires no calibration data, retraining, or model-specific tuning. Evaluated on Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, and Qwen3-VL-8B, SharQ recovers 43--63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap across language and vision-language tasks, and generalizes across NVFP4, HiF4, and MXFP4 formats. On an RTX 5090, SharQ delivers 2.2--2.4$\times$ latency reduction over FP16 and 1.2--1.4$\times$ throughput improvement over FP8 in language model serving, and up to 1.58$\times$ speedup on Wan2.2-T2V-A14B video generation when combined with SageAttention. Our code is available at https://github.com/actypedef/SharQ.

</details>


### [214] [Empirical Software Engineering TerraProbe: A Layered-Oracle Framework for Detecting Deceptive Fixes in LLM-Assisted Terraform](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26590)
*Manar Alsaid,Chimdumebi Nebolisa,Faris Abbas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TerraProbe—a five-layer oracle framework—evaluates LLM-assisted Terraform repairs and reveals a gap between scan-passing fixes and true security improvements: high targeted-Checkov removal (83.3%) but low full-cleanliness (10.4%), planning (39.6%), and plan-comparison (38.5%); 71.4% of plan-compared real-world repairs are deceptive fixes that leave vulnerabilities. Effects are consistent across Gemini-2.5-flash-lite, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet. A four-dimensional taxonomy of deceptive fixes is proposed, with strong inter-rater agreement; wildcard IAM grants persist in deceptive cases. TerraProbe provides evaluation methodology, replication package, and a Multi-Layer Oracle Evaluation framework to distinguish intent-aligned repairs from scanner-passing false successes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Security misconfigurations in Infrastructure-as-Code (Terraform) pose risks as large language models are used to repair them; current evaluations focus on removing static-analysis findings and ignore planning validity, behavioral change, and security intent.

Method: Five-layer oracle framework (TerraProbe) applied to 288 first-pass repairs from Gemini-2.5-flash-lite, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet across 68 real TerraDS modules and 28 injected-defect modules; metrics include targeted Checkov removal, full-scanner cleanliness, Terraform planning success, plan comparison; human adjudication; four-dimensional taxonomy of deceptive fixes; IAM permission analysis; replication package and Multi-Layer Oracle Evaluation framework.

Result: Targeted Checkov removal reaches 83.3% for the primary model; full-scanner cleanliness 10.4%; Terraform planning succeeds 39.6%; plan comparison reachable 38.5%; 71.4% of plan-compared repairs are deceptive fixes; deception rates consistent across models (57.1%–71.4%); Fisher p-values >0.10; four-dimensional taxonomy with Cohen’s kappa 0.78 and Krippendorff alpha 0.76; wildcard Resource grants persist in all 9 CKV2 AWS 11 deceptive cases.

Conclusion: TerraProbe provides a robust methodology and framework to distinguish intent-aligned security repairs from scanner-passing false positives, along with a replication package and a Multi-Layer Oracle Evaluation framework for future evaluations.

Abstract: Security misconfigurations in Terraform Infrastructure-as-Code are a growing risk in cloud deployments, and large language models are increasingly used as automated repair agents. Existing evaluations often treat a repair as successful when the targeted static-analysis finding disappears, without checking planning validity, behavioral change, or security intent. This paper presents TerraProbe, a five-layer oracle framework for evaluating LLM-assisted Terraform security repair. We apply TerraProbe to 288 first-pass repairs generated by gemini-2.5-flash-lite, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet across 68 real-world TerraDS modules and 28 controlled injected-defect modules. The results show that targeted Checkov removal overstates repair success. Although targeted removal reaches 83.3 percent for the primary model, full-scanner cleanliness drops to 10.4 percent, Terraform planning succeeds for 39.6 percent, and plan comparison is reachable for 38.5 percent. Human adjudication further shows that 71.4 percent of plan-compared real-world repairs are deceptive fixes that pass automated checks while leaving the underlying vulnerability in place. This pattern is statistically indistinguishable across the three models, with deceptive-fix rates from 57.1 percent to 71.4 percent and pairwise Fisher exact p-values above 0.10. The paper introduces a four-dimensional taxonomy of deceptive fixes, validated with Cohen kappa of 0.78 and Krippendorff alpha of 0.76. IAM permission analysis confirms that wildcard Resource grants persist in all nine CKV2 AWS 11 deceptive-fix cases. TerraProbe contributes an evaluation methodology, a replication package, and the Multi-Layer Oracle Evaluation framework for distinguishing intent-aligned security repairs from scanner-passing false successes.

</details>


### [215] [Sketched Linear Contrastive Learning: Approximation, Optimization, and Statistical Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26617)
*Ziyan Chen,Zhongzhu Zhou,Ding-Xuan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First theoretical scaling law for sketched contrastive learning with two-view interactions; performance scales with sketch dimension M, data size N, and effective optimization horizon L_eff γ; a cross term is bounded and does not affect the upper-bound scaling, guiding resource allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand scaling behavior in contrastive representation learning under sketching, addressing a gap since existing scaling laws cover sketched linear regression but not contrastive settings with two correlated views.

Method: Analyze a sketched linear model for contrastive learning under a paired Gaussian latent-variable model. The learner observes sketched views of two correlated variables and trains a bilinear contrastive score via full-batch empirical gradient descent. A Gaussian-negative quadratic contrastive surrogate is analyzed under aligned power-law spectra and a contrastive source condition. The analysis yields a risk decomposition into irreducible risk, approximation error, GD bias, GD variance, and a cross term; the cross term is controlled by bias and variance. The main theorem provides an explicit scaling law in terms of sketch dimension M, sample size N, and the effective optimization horizon L_eff γ.

Result: Derives an explicit scaling law for contrastive learning in the sketched setting; shows that the cross-term does not affect the upper-bound scaling and that two-view interactions alter optimization and finite-sample noise scaling compared to standard linear regression.

Conclusion: This work offers a first theoretical step toward scaling laws in contrastive learning, highlighting how interactions between two views influence resource trade-offs among model size, data, and training time, and guiding practical balancing of these factors.

Abstract: Scaling laws describe how learning performance varies with model size, data size, and compute. While recent theoretical work has established scaling laws for sketched linear regression, much less is understood for contrastive representation learning. In this paper, we study a sketched linear model for contrastive learning under a paired Gaussian latent-variable setup. The learner observes only sketched views of two correlated variables and trains a bilinear contrastive score by full-batch empirical gradient descent. We analyze a Gaussian-negative quadratic contrastive surrogate under aligned power-law spectra and a contrastive source condition, where we derive a risk decomposition into irreducible risk, approximation error, GD bias, GD variance, and a cross term. The cross term is controlled by the bias and variance and therefore does not affect the upper-bound scaling. Our main theorem gives an explicit scaling law with respect to sketch dimension $M$, sample size $N$, and effective optimization horizon $L_{\mathrm{eff}}γ$. Compared with standard linear-regression scaling laws, the contrastive setting must learn interactions between two views, and this changes how optimization and finite-sample noise scale with model size, data, and training time. This provides a first theoretical step toward understanding scaling behavior in contrastive learning and gives guidance for balancing model size, data, and optimization compute.

</details>


### [216] [Discovering Millions of Interpretable Features with Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26620)
*XinYang He,Wei Wang,Bing Zhao,Xuan Ren,WenBo Li,WeiXu Qiao,Hu Wei,Lin Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Qwen3-Instruct SAE: layer-wise sparse autoencoders trained on Qwen3 instruction-tuned models (1.7B, 4B, 8B) to extract interpretable sparse features; evaluates sparsity–fidelity trade-offs across layers; demonstrates causal steering of refusal behavior; release facilitates study of sparse representations and behavioral interventions in instruction-tuned LMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the computational cost and limited availability of open-source SAEs; leverage the Qwen3 instruction-tuned family to study sparse, interpretable representations across multiple model scales and activation sites.

Method: Train layer-wise SAEs at activation sites (residual streams, MLP outputs, attention outputs) for Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B (three sites), and on a subset of residual streams for Qwen3-8B. Evaluate using activation-level reconstruction metrics and model-level recovery metrics. Conduct a case study on refusal-steering to test causal influence of SAE features.

Result: Reveals distinct sparsity–fidelity trade-offs across layers and components. Demonstrates that selected SAE features can causally steer instruction-tuned Qwen3 models toward refusal behavior.

Conclusion: Provides a practical resource for studying sparse representations, feature-level mechanisms, and behavioral interventions in instruction-tuned language models; enables further research into interpretability and control via sparse representations.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a powerful tool for decomposing superposed language model representations into sparse and interpretable features. However, training SAEs is computationally expensive, and available open-source SAE models remain limited. In this work, we introduce \textbf{Qwen3-Instruct SAE}, a comprehensive suite of SAEs trained on the Qwen3 instruction-tuned model family, covering Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-4B, and Qwen3-8B. For Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B, we train layer-wise SAEs at three key activation sites: residual streams, MLP outputs, and attention outputs. For Qwen3-8B, we train SAEs on a subset of residual stream layers. We systematically evaluate these SAEs using both activation-level reconstruction metrics and model-level recovery metrics, revealing distinct sparsity--fidelity trade-offs across layers and components. Finally, we demonstrate the utility of Qwen3-Instruct SAE through a refusal-steering case study, showing that selected SAE features can causally steer instruction-tuned Qwen3 models toward refusal behavior. Our release provides a practical resource for studying sparse representations, feature-level mechanisms, and behavioral interventions in instruction-tuned language models

</details>


### [217] [From Weights to Features: SAE-Guided Activation Regularization for LLM Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26629)
*Evan Ning,Wei Xue,Dong Lou,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Regularization in activation space via a pretrained Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) dictionary for continual learning in LLMs, offering selective stability without relying on past-task data and improving over EWC on TRACE/MedCL benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EWC-style weight-space regularization underperforms for large, polysemantic models due to coarse per-weight importance estimates. Activation-space methods with a monosemantic feature dictionary aim to isolate knowledge that must be protected.

Method: Use pretrained SAEs as a monosemantic feature dictionary and derive a constrained optimization loss that balances stability and plasticity. Show that EWC is a special case of a one-sided weight-space penalty. Compute an SAE feature mask from current-task data, retain only this mask for future training, and operate in a lower-dimensional feature space for efficiency.

Result: Empirically outperforms traditional weight-space regularization (e.g., EWC) on TRACE and MedCL benchmarks among methods without task-specific architectural components. Provides evidence that task-relevant representations are linearly separable in the SAE feature basis but indistinguishable in the weight basis; weight-space protection is nearly non-selective at the concept level.

Conclusion: Activation-space regularization using a pretrained SAE dictionary is a promising and memory-efficient approach to mitigating catastrophic forgetting in continual learning with LLMs, addressing polysemanticity and surpassing EWC without revisiting past-task data.

Abstract: Weight-space regularization methods such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) are the standard approach to catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. However, those methods tend to underperform when applied to large language models. We argue that such underperformance can be partly explained by the ``polysemantic'' nature of large language models: per-weight importance estimates utilized by EWC-style regularization are too coarse and cannot isolate the knowledge that needs protection. In this paper, we propose regularizing instead in the model's activation space, using pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as a monosemantic feature dictionary. From the perspective of constrained optimization, we derive a new loss function that uses the SAE feature dictionary to explicitly balance stability and plasticity, and show that EWC is a special case in the one-sided weight-space penalty setting. Unlike replay-based methods that store or revisit examples from earlier tasks, our method requires no previous-task data after mask construction: current-task data is used to compute a compact SAE feature mask, and only this mask is retained for later training. Further, since the feature space has significantly lower dimensionality than the parameter space, the proposed method is more memory efficient. On the TRACE and MedCL continual learning benchmarks, the method achieves the strongest result among approaches without introducing task-specific architectural components, also surpassing traditional weight-space regularization methods like EWC. Beyond performance comparisons, we provide empirical evidence for the polysemanticity thesis: task-relevant representations are linearly separable in the SAE feature basis but indistinguishable from chance in the weight basis, and weight-space protection is nearly non-selective at the concept level.

</details>


### [218] [Target-Aware Bandit Allocation for Scalable Surrogate Optimization in Chemical Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26657)
*Mohammad Haddadnia,Yuvan Chali,Abhilash Jayaraj,Constance Kraay,Joana Reis,Felix Strieth-Kalthoff,Haribabu Arthanari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BOBa is a bandit-guided surrogate optimization framework that avoids full-library surrogate inference in ultra-large discrete spaces by partitioning the action space into arms and using optimism-under-uncertainty bandits to allocate inference and evaluations adaptively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle the computational bottleneck of constructing and querying surrogates over billions/trillions of candidate molecules, enabling efficient virtual screening with limited expensive evaluations.

Method: Partition the action space into manageable groups (partitions) and treat each as an arm in a multi-armed bandit. Use optimism-under-uncertainty estimators to allocate inference and expensive evaluations to empirically promising partitions, while ensuring exploration. Validate on real-world synthesis-on-demand libraries.

Result: BOBa demonstrates improved screening efficiency and reduced surrogate-inference cost by focusing computation on promising partitions, with a tunable tradeoff between surrogate cost and screening performance. Supports practical optimization over current ultra-large libraries and enables route to ultra-large library virtual screening.

Conclusion: A practical, scalable framework for high-utility candidate identification in massive discrete spaces, combining bandit-guided inference with partitioned action spaces to circumvent full-library surrogate inference and enable efficient virtual screening at extreme scales.

Abstract: Identifying high-utility candidates from massive discrete spaces under expensive evaluations is a recurring challenge across the sciences, with structure-based drug discovery as a prominent example. While surrogate-based optimization can increase sample efficiency by reducing the number of expensive evaluations, modern molecular libraries have reached billions to trillions of compounds, making full-library surrogate inference itself a major computational bottleneck. We introduce BOBa, a bandit-guided surrogate optimization framework that eliminates full-library inference by adaptively allocating computation across partitions of the action space. By treating partitions as arms in a multi-armed bandit, BOBa concentrates inference and evaluations on empirically promising partitions while maintaining principled exploration. Experiments on real-world synthesis-on-demand libraries demonstrate that optimism-under-uncertainty bandits, combined with meaningful action space partitioning, are essential for effective allocation of inference and evaluations. Our findings reveal a tunable tradeoff between screening performance and surrogate inference cost, which supports practical optimization over current libraries, and establishes a viable route to ultra-large library virtual screening.

</details>


### [219] [Zero-Shot Size Transfer for Neural ODEs on Sparse Random Graphs: Graphon Limits and Adjoint Convergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26662)
*Mingsong Yan,Zhida Wang,Sui Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Paper introduces Graphon-NDEs and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as infinite-node limits of GNDEs to analyze zero-shot size transfer; provides well-posedness, convergence rates on sparse graphs to Graphon-NDEs, and DTO/OTD training consistency; experiments validate rates and zero-shot transfer across graphon classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address generalization and scalability of continuous-time graph dynamics under varying graph sizes; establish theoretical guarantees for zero-shot transfer and training discretization.

Method: Define Graphon-NDEs as mean-field/infinite-node limits; derive well-posedness; prove trajectory-wise convergence rate O((α_n n)^-1/2) up to log factors; uniform-in-time adjoint convergence; analyze DTO and OTD with explicit Euler, M steps; show rates O(1/M) for hidden-state and O(1/M^2) for gradients; experiments on HSBM and tent graphons; zero-shot transfer experiments across four graphon classes.

Result: Theoretical guarantees on convergence and consistency; empirical evidence supporting O(1/√(α_n n)) rates and zero-shot deployment; demonstration of transfer across graphon classes.

Conclusion: Supports practical zero-shot deployment of GNDEs on larger graphs by grounding in Graphon limits; provides rigorous asymptotic analysis and training-physics alignment for GNDEs.

Abstract: Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs) model continuous-time graph dynamics by parameterizing Neural ODE velocity fields with Graph Neural Networks. Their local, size-independent filters suggest a zero-shot size-transfer principle: train on a small graph and deploy on larger, similar graphs without retraining. We develop a quantitative theory for this principle on sparse random graphs sampled from graphons. We consider Graphon Neural Differential Equations (Graphon-NDEs) and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as the infinite-node limits of the forward and adjoint GNDE systems, and establish well-posedness. For an $n$-node random graph with sparsity parameter $α_n$, we prove trajectory-wise convergence of GNDE solutions to Graphon-NDE solutions at rate $O((α_n n)^{-1/2})$, up to logarithmic factors, with high probability. We also establish uniform-in-time convergence bounds for adjoint systems governing hidden-state and parameter gradients. We further study discretize-then-optimize (DTO) and optimize-then-discretize (OTD) training. Under explicit Euler discretization with $M$ steps, we show that DTO and OTD are asymptotically consistent, with hidden-state and local parameter-gradient discrepancies of orders $O(1/M)$ and $O(1/M^2)$, respectively, up to sparsity and logarithmic factors. Experiments on HSBM and tent graphons support the theoretical rates, while zero-shot transfer experiments across four graphon classes demonstrate accurate deployment of learned GNDEs on larger independently sampled graphs.

</details>


### [220] [PersistentKV: Page-Aware Decode Scheduling for Long-Context LLM Serving on Commodity GPUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26666)
*Muhammad Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive, page-aware decode scheduling with PersistentKV improves KV-based LLM serving throughput by reusing KV tiles across grouped-query heads and employing a compact non-empty-task workqueue, achieving up to ~1.4x throughput gains across various workloads and batch sizes on an RTX 3060.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV-cache movement and fragmentation are the primary bottlenecks in autoregressive LLM serving, often outweighing raw attention compute. The work investigates adaptive scheduling to maximize hardware utilization for mixed sequence lengths and long-context decoding.

Method: Introduce PersistentKV: a native block-table decode attention engine that groups by KV-head, reuses K,V tiles across grouped query heads, supports native page tables, and uses a compact workqueue that schedules only non-empty row-KV-head-sequence-splits. A calibrated policy selects between FlashInfer for small active batches, PersistentKV sequence splitting for B1 long-context steps, and PersistentKV workqueue scheduling for B8 long-context steps. Evaluation on RTX 3060 FP16 with page size 16, Hq=32, Hkv=8, d=128, comparing against FlashInfer under identical correctness tolerance.

Result: Calibrated policy yields 1.063–1.265x synchronized wall throughput gains for B8 bimodal, uniform, and Zipf-like workloads, and 1.399x for a B1 bucketed trace. The B4 bimodal boundary case regresses if not switching to FlashInfer. Gains are achieved with fixed calibration thresholds and seed-based trace calibration, demonstrating robust performance improvements across diverse workloads.

Conclusion: Adaptive, page-aware decode scheduling and effective work assignment are decisive factors in KV-cache-based LLM serving, revealing a concrete systems niche where specialized decode engines and scheduling policies outperform or complement standard kernels.

Abstract: Autoregressive large language model (LLM) serving is increasingly limited by key-value (KV) cache movement rather than dense matrix multiplication. Modern paged-attention systems reduce KV-cache fragmentation and mature kernels such as FlashInfer provide highly optimized native-paged decode attention. However, the best single-kernel implementation is not always the best serving schedule: low-active long-context decode can under-utilize commodity GPUs, while mixed sequence lengths introduce a tension between many exact-length launches and coarse padded batches. We present PersistentKV, a native block-table decode attention engine and page-aware scheduling study for grouped-query attention (GQA). PersistentKV maps work by KV-head group, is designed to reuse K,V tiles across grouped query heads, supports native page tables, and adds a compact workqueue schedule that executes only non-empty row-KV-head-sequence-split tasks. On an RTX 3060 with FP16, page size 16, Hq=32, Hkv=8, d=128, and identical correctness tolerance against FlashInfer, a calibrated adaptive policy selects FlashInfer for small active batches, PersistentKV sequence splitting for B1 long-context steps, and PersistentKV workqueue scheduling for B8 long-context steps. With thresholds and split counts fixed on calibration traces, one held-out trace seed improves synchronized wall throughput by 1.063-1.265x on B8 bimodal, uniform, and Zipf-like workloads and by 1.399x on a B1 bucketed trace. On the B4 bimodal boundary case, the policy avoids the PersistentKV regression by selecting FlashInfer. These results identify a concrete systems niche for adaptive page-aware decode scheduling and show that work assignment, not only attention math, is a decisive serving-system variable.

</details>


### [221] [Algorithmic Foundations of Deep Learning: Complexity-Theoretic Rates and a Characterization of Universal Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26705)
*Anastasis Kratsios,Simone Brugiapaglia,Bum Jun Kim,Gregory Cousins,Haitz Sáez de Ocáriz Borde*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reframes neural-network expressivity as a question of computability and algorithmic complexity rather than purely regularity, establishing universal approximation results under a non-affine nonlinearity and showing that NN efficiency can emulate classical algorithms and more complex circuits with provable parameter-count bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Basis-expansion views of NN expressivity fail to distinguish intuitively simple from complex objects with similar regularity. A computation-centric view aims to quantify NN complexity via depth/width/gate-structure, and to identify when a nonlinearity suffices for universality.

Method: Model NN as real-valued circuits over an elementary gate language with a parallelization condition; prove universality iff a non-affine nonlinearity is present. Apply the framework to continuous functions, Besov classes (minimax-optimal rates), and holomorphic functions (logarithmic error). Demonstrate emulation of numerical algorithms (Newton-Raphson, power iteration) and compile tropical dynamic-programming circuits into NNs to bound parameter counts.

Result: Establish universal approximation for continuous functions; obtain minimax-optimal rates for Besov classes; achieve logarithmic error for holomorphic functions; show NN can emulate key numerical algorithms without architecture-specific tricks; demonstrate a shortest-path example where the compiled tropical DP circuit yields NN with O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially better in 1/ε than generic Lipschitz-based bounds (for fixed graph size k).

Conclusion: NN expressivity is governed not only by regularity but by algorithmic complexity; non-affine nonlinearities are essential for universality; the framework unifies approximation guarantees with the ability to emulate computation, enabling efficient NN constructions via circuit compilation and offering concrete parameter-efficiency gains in combinatorial tasks.

Abstract: Feedforward neural network (NN) expressivity is typically studied by emulating optimal basis-expansion schemes. While powerful, this perspective is incomplete: it primarily captures complexity through regularity, and therefore does not distinguish intuitively simple and complicated objects with comparable regularity, such as the square-root function and a typical Brownian path.
  The guiding message is that neural networks should be viewed not only as flexible basis functions, but also as models of computation. If a function is computable by a real-valued circuit over a prescribed elementary gate language, then it can be computed to comparable accuracy by an NN with explicit depth, width, and non-zero-parameter bounds controlled by the depth, width, gate count, and gate structure. Thus, neural-network complexity is not governed by regularity alone, but also by algorithmic complexity. We then show that any definable NN model satisfying a natural parallelization condition, allowing possibly multivariate non-linearities such as attention or layer normalization, is a universal approximator if and only if it contains a non-affine nonlinearity.
  The scope of our theory is illustrated by deducing universal approximation guarantees for continuous functions, minimax-optimal approximation guarantees for Besov classes, logarithmic-error complexity for holomorphic functions, and by showing that NNs can emulate numerical algorithms such as Newton-Raphson root finding and power iteration without architecture-specific arguments. Its precision is illustrated by shortest-path computation on $k$-vertex graphs: compiling the tropical dynamic-programming circuit yields NNs with O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially improving in 1/ε over the generic $O(ε^{-c k^2})$ Lipschitz-approximation scale, for a constant c>0.

</details>


### [222] [HyperDFlash: MHC-Aligned Block Speculative Decoding with Gated Residual Reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26744)
*Luxi Lin,Shuang Peng,Rui Ma,Junhao Hua,Shuwei Fan,Zhengda Qin,Qiang Wang,Hongjian Sun,Fangmin Chen,Songwei Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HyperDFlash introduces a block-parallel speculative decoding approach tailored to DeepSeek-V4's multi-hyper-connection (MHC) architecture. It aligns residual streams with two model-aware optimizations (pre-collapse residual conditioning and a lightweight gated residual reducer) and augments training with targeted KL distillation, achieving superior draft quality and speed than native MTP and vanilla DFlash across math, code, and dialogue tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MHC introduces multi-path residual streams that misalign with traditional drafting; despite strong early-token drafting, error accumulation from unverified intermediate tokens degrades quality at later positions. Existing DFlash cannot seamlessly adapt to MHC due to feature misalignment, necessitating model-aligned strategies to preserve multi-path information and improve robustness.

Method: 1) Use pre-collapse residual states as the sole conditioning signal to preserve multi-path structure and align the drafter with the model's native prediction pathway. 2) Replace a heavy linear compressor with a lightweight gated residual reducer whose parameters are inherited from the built-in hyper-connection head, enabling input-aware path aggregation with ~1000x fewer parameters. 3) Apply a targeted KL distillation loss to the LM-head to regularize predictions toward the full target distribution, boosting early-stage draft quality.

Result: Empirical evaluation on math reasoning, code synthesis, and conversational benchmarks shows HyperDFlash consistently surpasses both the native MTP baseline and vanilla DFlash adaptation, with substantial gains in average accepted draft length and decoding speed.

Conclusion: Model-aligned MHC residual handling, the gated residual reduction, and targeted distillation collectively enable high-performance speculative decoding for MHC-based LMs, delivering reliable quality gains and speedups across diverse tasks.

Abstract: We present HyperDFlash, a block-parallel speculative decoding framework tailored to the novel multi-hyper-connection (MHC) architecture proposed by DeepSeek-V4. Despite the strong initial-token drafting performance of the native Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) module in DeepSeek-V4, its draft accuracy degrades sharply at later positions, as error accumulation from unverified intermediate tokens harms acceptance rates. Although the original DFlash method supports efficient one-pass block drafting, it cannot be seamlessly adapted to the MHC paradigm, since the multi-path residual stream of DeepSeek-V4 induces feature misalignment with conventional drafting designs. To resolve this mismatch, we propose two model-aligned optimizations for MHC residual streams. First, we adopt pre-collapse residual states as the exclusive conditioning signal, preserving multi-path structural information and aligning the drafter with the native prediction pathway of the target model. Second, we replace the heavy generic linear compressor with a lightweight gated residual reducer, whose parameters are inherited from the built-in hyper-connection head. This design yields input-aware path aggregation with three orders of magnitude fewer parameters while maintaining architectural alignment. We further enhance training via a targeted KL distillation loss applied to the LM-head, which regularizes predictions against the full target probability distribution and improves draft quality at early training stages. Experiments across math reasoning, code synthesis, and conversational benchmarks show that HyperDFlash consistently outperforms both the native MTP baseline and vanilla DFlash adaptation. It achieves substantial gains in average accepted draft length and decoding speedup, validating the effectiveness of MHC alignment, gated reduction, and targeted distillation for high-performance speculative decoding.

</details>


### [223] [Structure Before Collapse: Transient semantic geometry in next-token prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26749)
*Yize Zhao,Isabel Papadimitriou,Christos Thrampoulidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Early semantic structure naturally emerges in language-model representations under a one-hot next-token objective, but training dynamics pass through a transient, semantically structured phase before converging to Neural Collapse where all representations become equally separated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconcile Neural Collapse with empirical evidence that LM embeddings reveal semantic grouping despite one-hot supervision and sparse co-occurrences; understand how gradient descent discovers latent structure under high-capacity models.

Method: Three synthetic controlled settings where inputs carry latent semantic factors but are mapped to distinct one-hot labels; train models on next-token prediction; monitor representation geometry via clustering and Gram matrix analysis; compare early- vs late-training regimes; introduce a preliminary modification to unconstrained features model to capture emergent geometry.

Result: Semantic geometry emerges early in training, with representations clustering by shared latent attributes even though supervision is one-hot; this structure is transient and dissolves as capacity/time increases, culminating in the predicted symmetric Neural Collapse state; Gram matrix reveals a phase transition between structured and collapsed regimes.

Conclusion: Training dynamics traverse a phase where latent semantic geometry is present, suggesting Neural Collapse is a late-stage attractor rather than the sole end-point; a modest model tweak can help the unconstrained features framework capture the emergent geometry observed in early training.

Abstract: Neural Collapse predicts that balanced one-hot classification pushes model representations to be equally far from each other; a symmetric configuration that depends only on the output label and ignores any semantic similarity in the inputs. This creates a puzzle: next-token prediction language models are trained predominantly (as context length increases) with one-hot labels: the same context is very unlikely to appear twice in training with different labels. However, they clearly learn latent structural features. That is, despite the one-hot training regime, a language model's contextual embeddings represent the fact that the next word in ''Mary broke the ___'' is likely to be filled by tokens in the latent classes of a) medium-sized, b) rigid, c) inanimate nouns. How does gradient descent find such categorical semantic structure when co-occurrence statistics collapse to one-hot sparsity, eliminating any shared next-tokens among different contexts? To investigate this tension we identify three synthetic controlled settings where inputs have latent semantic factors but are mapped to distinct one-hot labels. We find that semantic geometry emerges early in training, and that representations cluster by shared attributes despite receiving no explicit supervision to do so. This structure is transient: with sufficient capacity and time, the model eventually reaches the predicted symmetric state where all representations are equally separated. We study this phase transition through Gram matrix analysis and propose a preliminary modification to the commonly used unconstrained features model to capture the emergent semantic geometry.

</details>


### [224] [Automating Potential-based Reward Shaping with Vision Language Model Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27180)
*Henrik Müller,Daniel Kudenko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces VLM-PBRS, a framework that learns a potential function for potential-based reward shaping from Vision-Language Model preferences, enabling reward shaping without hand-designed terms and using small, efficient VLMs to improve sample efficiency and robustness against reward hacking in manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse rewards hinder RL exploration and correct credit assignment. Naive reward shaping risks reward hacking and requires hand-crafted terms. PBRS can preserve optimal policies but needs a heuristic potential function. Automating potential learning from perceptual feedback via VLMs could reduce designer effort and improve sample efficiency.

Method: Query a lightweight VLM to obtain preferences over image pairs, train a model of the PBRS potential function from those preferences, and integrate this learned potential into PBRS. Use small,VLMs to keep costs low. Evaluate on Meta-World and Franka Kitchen, analyzing how VLM label quality impacts sample efficiency.

Result: Empirical results show accelerated learning and robustness to reward hacking, despite using less accurate preferences from smaller VLMs. Demonstrates a positive relationship between VLM preference accuracy and sample efficiency. Establishes first application of VLM preference-based learning to synthesize a PBRS potential function.

Conclusion: Presents a principled, low-cost approach to PBRS using VLM-derived preferences, enabling improved sample efficiency without expert-crafted shaping terms and with demonstrated robustness across tasks.

Abstract: Sparse rewards are inherently challenging for reinforcement learning agents as they lack intermediate feedback to guide exploration and to correctly attribute the sparse success rewards to relevant parts of the trajectory. Naive reward shaping can induce reward hacking, yielding policies that exploit auxiliary signals instead of solving the intended task. Potential-based reward shaping (PBRS) guarantees preservation of the optimal policy set, but requires the definition of a heuristic potential function over the state space. In this work, we introduce the VLM-guided PBRS framework VLM-PBRS that learns the potential function directly from vision language model (VLM) feedback. We query a lightweight VLM to obtain preferences over image pairs and train a model of the potential function using these preferences. As this approach is based on potential-based reward shaping, it preserves the original optimal policies, and removes the need for expert-designed reward shaping terms. Because large VLMs are prohibitively expensive to invoke repeatedly during policy learning, we employ smaller, more computationally efficient VLMs. Although the resulting preference labels are less accurate, empirical evidence shows that the preference labels can still be used to accelerate learning.
