We validate our method empirically in the Meta-World and Franka Kitchen environments and highlight the connection between VLM preference label accuracy and sample efficiency improvements. Our contributions are threefold: (1) the first application of VLM preference-based learning to synthesize a potential function for PBRS, (2) a principled, low-cost solution that leverages small VLMs, and (3) extensive empirical demonstration of improved sample efficiency and robustness to reward hacking.

</details>


### [225] [Batch-Invariant Spectral Intelligence for Robust and Explainable Insect Authentication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26757)
*Majharulislam Babor,Giacomo Rossi,Annalisa Altavilla,Oliver Schlüter,Marina M. -C. Höhne*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BISN—a batch-invariant spectral network for NIR-based insect species authentication; it uses a Savitzky-Golay initialized preprocessor with entropy-regularized adversarial training to suppress batch effects, achieving 0.93 leave-one-batch-out accuracy on 2,700 spectra from three species, with interpretable lipid/protein-band decisions; code and data released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable species authentication in edible insects under production variation is essential to control allergens, prevent adulteration, and meet regulatory standards; batch-to-batch spectral variation in NIR data undermines generalization.

Method: End-to-end BISN framework combining a learnable preprocessing module (initialized with Savitzky-Golay filtering) with an entropy-regularised adversarial objective to suppress batch-specific spectral variation before learning species-specific features; contrasts with Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks by addressing batch effects prior to feature extraction; evaluation on 2,700 spectra across three species and three production batches using leave-one-batch-out cross-validation; results include comparison to baselines and explainable AI insights.

Result: Mean leave-one-batch-out accuracy = 0.93 (SD 0.04) on 2,700 spectra from Acheta domesticus, Hermetia illucens, and Tenebrio molitor across three batches, outperforming strongest baseline by ~4 percentage points; model decisions consistently rely on lipid and protein absorption regions across folds; dataset and code publicly available.

Conclusion: BISN improves cross-batch robustness and biochemical interpretability for automated insect species authentication in industrial settings; enables more reliable integration of edible insects into supply chains; resource for reproducibility via public data and code.

Abstract: Edible insects offer an efficient source of alternative protein, requiring less land, water and emitting less greenhouse gas than conventional livestock. However, their successful integration into the food supply chain demands reliable species authentication to control allergen exposure, prevent adulteration, and meet regulatory standards. Near-infrared spectroscopy provides a rapid analytical tool, but its performance drops when applied to production batches unseen during training due to batch-to-batch variation in spectral measurements. We introduce the Batch-Invariant Spectral Network (BISN), an end-to-end framework that combines a learnable preprocessing module, initialised with Savitzky-Golay filtering, with an entropy-regularised adversarial objective to suppress batch-specific spectral variation. In contrast to Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks, which enforce domain adaptation only after feature extraction, BISN suppress batch-effects before species-specific features are learned. Using 2,700 spectra from three species (Acheta domesticus, Hermetia illucens, and Tenebrio molitor) collected across three independent production batches, BISN achieves a mean leave-one-batch-out accuracy of 0.93 (standard deviation 0.04), outperforming the strongest baseline by four percent. Further insights gained by using explainable AI confirm that model decisions consistently rely on the lipid and protein absorption regions across all folds, connecting predictive performance to known insect biochemistry. BISN addresses both cross-batch robustness and biochemical interpretability for automated insect species authentication under realistic industrial conditions. The source code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/majharB/bisn.

</details>


### [226] [Escaping Iterative Parameter-Space Noise: Differentially Private Learning with a Hypernetwork](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26772)
*Naoki Nishikawa,Shokichi Takakura,Satoshi Hasegawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hypernetwork-based DP learning framework maps a privatized, low-dimensional dataset embedding to target model parameters, avoiding iterative DP-SGD updates. It reduces noise impact and shows higher theoretical utility in synthetic tests and lower FID for LoRA-tuned diffusion models versus DP-SGD and other public-data-guided baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DP-SGD introduces substantial utility loss due to high-dimensional, iterative noise injection. The proposed method aims to concentrate privacy noise in a small, low-dimensional representation, enabling a single perturbation step and leveraging public data to learn a mapping to model parameters.

Method: Embed each training example into a low-dimensional representation; aggregate embeddings and inject DP noise to obtain a DP dataset embedding; train a hypernetwork on public data to map the noisy embedding to the target model parameters; use the hypernetwork to generate a model (potentially via LoRA-tuned diffusion models) from the DP embedding; evaluate utility with synthetic settings and FID on diffusion models.

Result: Theoretical analysis in a synthetic setting shows higher utility than DP-SGD at fixed privacy budgets. Empirically, LoRA-finetuned diffusion models guided by public data and generated via the hypernetwork achieve lower FID than LoRA models trained with DP-SGD and other public-data-guided methods.

Conclusion: Shifting privacy from iterative parameter updates to a single, low-dimensional DP embedding via a hypernetwork can improve DP learning efficiency and utility. The approach is particularly effective with LoRA-tuned diffusion models, but requires public data for hypernetwork training and raises considerations about privacy accounting and generalization across tasks.

Abstract: Differentially private (DP) training of neural networks is often hindered by the large amount of noise required by gradient-based methods such as DP-SGD, which repeatedly inject high-dimensional noise in parameter space throughout training. In this paper, we propose a new framework for DP learning that avoids iterative optimization in parameter space. Instead of updating the target model using privatized gradients, we employ a hypernetwork trained on public datasets to map a private dataset to the parameters of the target model. Specifically, each example is embedded into a low-dimensional representation, the embeddings are aggregated and perturbed to obtain a DP dataset embedding, and the hypernetwork generates the target model parameters from this noisy embedding. Because privacy noise is injected only once into a low-dimensional dataset representation, our approach can significantly reduce the adverse effect of noise. We theoretically show in a synthetic setting that, under a fixed privacy budget, models produced by our approach achieve higher utility than those trained with DP-SGD. Moreover, we apply our approach to LoRA fine-tuning of diffusion models and show that it achieves lower FID than LoRA models trained with DP-SGD and other public-data-guided methods.

</details>


### [227] [Hallucination in World Models is Predictable and Preventable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27326)
*Nicklas Hansen,Xiaolong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MMBench2 introduces a large, action-grounded video-world modeling benchmark, characterizes three hallucination modes, and demonstrates a data-coverage–driven mitigation pipeline enabling data-efficient adaptation with ~50 real trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address persistent hallucinations in action-controllable world models, which degrade fidelity in unseen or low-coverage regions, and to provide benchmarks and practical remedies.

Method: Create MMBench2 (427 hours, 210 tasks) with ground-truth actions/rewards and simulators; train a ~350M-param world model; identify perceptual, action-marginalized, and scene-diverging hallucinations; devise three predictive signals; implement coverage-aware training sampling; use hallucination signals as curiosity rewards for targeted data collection; finetune the model with ~50 real trajectories in unseen environments.

Result: Three distinct hallucination modes; reliable predictors for where failures occur; improved training coverage via sampling; data-efficient online data collection and finetuning; model adapted to unseen environments with few trajectories.

Conclusion: Hallucination is fundamentally a data-coverage problem; the same signals used to detect hallucinations can mitigate them, enabling practical adaptation of world models to new environments.

Abstract: Modern generative world models render increasingly realistic action-controllable futures, yet they frequently hallucinate: rollouts remain visually fluent while drifting from the ground-truth dynamics. We hypothesize that hallucination concentrates in low-coverage regions of the state-action space, where lightweight data-centric signals can both detect it and guide mitigation. To test this, we introduce MMBench2, a 427-hour, 210-task dataset for visual world modeling with ground-truth actions, rewards, and live simulators, and train a 350M-parameter world model on it. We identify three distinct hallucination modes: perceptual, action-marginalized, and scene-diverging -- each anchored to a different stage of the pipeline, and develop three signals that accurately predict where the model will fail. To close coverage gaps at training time, we develop a coverage-aware sampling technique; to close them online, our hallucination predictors serve as curiosity rewards for targeted data collection, yielding a data-efficient finetuning recipe that adapts the pretrained world model to entirely unseen environments with as few as 50 real environment trajectories. Overall, our findings reveal that hallucination in world models is inherently a data coverage issue, and that the same signals used to detect it can also be used for mitigation.
  An interactive web version of our paper is available at https://www.nicklashansen.com/mmbench2

</details>


### [228] [Reproducibility Study of "AlphaEdit: Null-Space Constrained Knowledge Editing for Language Models"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26783)
*Ananth K S,Arya Hariharan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reproducibility study of AlphaEdit shows results align with original scope but reveals limitations: metric discrepancies, limited generalization to newer architectures, and degradation under long-horizon editing, with safety and downstream costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess reproducibility and robustness of AlphaEdit's null-space projection approach across model families, benchmarks, and extended editing horizons to understand practical guarantees.

Method: Reproduce original experiments on LLaMA3, GPT2-XL, GPT-J; verify reported metrics; extend evaluation to newer model families; systematically increase sequential edit horizon beyond original study; evaluate on three extra benchmarks (BoolQ, HellaSwag, XSTest) and inspect fluency, consistency, safety refusals.

Result: Replicated the original metrics for the initial models; identified a discrepancy in reported fluency/consistency metrics; AlphaEdit’s advantage is not uniformly observed on newer architectures due to paradigm assumptions; performance declines as the number of edits grows beyond the original scope; extended benchmarks show degradation in downstream task competence and safety-related refusal behavior.

Conclusion: AlphaEdit’s claims hold within the original scope, but core theoretical guarantees are sensitive to model architecture and editing scale. Practically, deployment should account for architecture-specific assumptions and finite editing horizons; further work is needed to strengthen guarantees or adapt the approach for broader model families.

Abstract: Fang et al. (2025) introduced a null-space constrained projection, named AlphaEdit, for locate-then-edit knowledge editing methods, theoretically guaranteeing that edits do not disrupt previously preserved knowledge, and reports substantial gains over existing editing methods on LLaMA3, GPT2-XL, and GPT-J. In this work, we present a reproducibility study of AlphaEdit, reproducing its reported results under the original experimental setup and extending the evaluation along three axes: new model architectures, additional downstream benchmarks, and substantially longer sequential editing horizons. We successfully reproduce AlphaEdit's reported metrics across the original models, though we identify a discrepancy in the reported fluency and consistency metric. Extending AlphaEdit to newer model families, we find that its advantage does not generalize uniformly, which we trace to architectural assumptions in the locate-then-edit paradigm that are violated by these newer models. We further stress-test AlphaEdit's central sequential-editing claim by extending the number of edits well beyond those evaluated in the original paper, and find that performance, which is stable at the originally reported scale, degrades as edits reach a much higher count, indicating that the null-space projection's protection against catastrophic forgetting is bounded rather than unconditional. Finally, we extend evaluation of edited models on three extra benchmarks, namely, BoolQ, HellaSwag, and XSTest, and we find that large-scale sequential editing degrades both general downstream task competence and safety-relevant refusal behavior. Our results confirm that AlphaEdit performs as reported within its original scope, while showing that its core theoretical guarantees are sensitive to model architecture and editing scale in ways that have practical implications for its deployment.

</details>


### [229] [AIGP: An LLM-Based Framework for Long-Term Value Alignment in E-Commerce Pricing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26787)
*Chennan Ma,Yanning Zhang,Siqi Hong,Xiuchong Wang,Fei Xiao,Keping Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AIGP applies an LLM-based, knowledge-aware pricing framework using a Long-Term Value Estimator trained via offline RL and direct preference optimization to improve long-term business metrics, achieving +GMV, +ROI, +milestone over production baseline while providing interpretable rationales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional dynamic pricing lacks interpretability and fails to utilize unstructured information and long-term business goals; the authors propose a framework that integrates domain knowledge, structured and textual data, and long-horizon optimization via LTVE and DPO.

Method: LLM prompted with domain knowledge and data; supervised fine-tuning for knowledge distillation; LTVE trained via offline RL as a reward model; scoring candidate actions and generating preference pairs for DPO; offline evaluations and large-scale online A/B tests.

Result: Significant improvements: GMV +13.21%, ROI +7.59%, milestone rate +8.20% over 14 days; interpretable pricing rationales; validated on Tao Factory.

Conclusion: AIGP demonstrates effective alignment with long-term objectives and interpretability; the combination of LLM, offline RL-based reward model, and DPO yields practical gains for pricing; prompts reliability and deployment efficiency via knowledge distillation are key enablers.

Abstract: Traditional dynamic pricing models in large-scale e-commerce suffer from limited interpretability, poor utilization of unstructured information, and misalignment with long-term business objectives such as cumulative Gross Merchandise Value (GMV), Return on Investment (ROI) and milestone achievement. We propose AIGP, a novel framework that leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) prompted with domain knowledge, structured data and textual context to make interpretable, knowledge-aware pricing decisions. For efficient deployment while maintaining high-quality outputs, we employ supervised fine-tuning for knowledge distillation. Central to AIGP is the Long-Term Value Estimator (LTVE), trained via offline reinforcement learning on historical data, which serves as a reward model to score candidate pricing actions and select preference pairs for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), thereby aligning the pricing policy with long-term business objectives. Extensive offline evaluations and large-scale online A/B tests on Tao Factory demonstrate that AIGP achieves significant improvements: +13.21% in GMV, +7.59% in ROI, and +8.20% in milestone achievement rate over 14 days compared to the production baseline, while simultaneously providing interpretable and transparent pricing rationales.

</details>


### [230] [Reasoning Quality Emerges Early: Data Curation for Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26797)
*Hongyi Henry Jin,Wenhan Yang,Meysam Ghaffari,Carlos Morato,Baharan Mirzasoleiman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A token-level early signal for selecting high-quality SFT data: using the loss of the first 100 reasoning tokens at perturbed checkpoints to identify difficult problems, with consistent loss patterns across 1k tokens predicting similar gradients; yields stronger performance and much lower token usage on LLM fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on large, expensive reasoning models for data curation in supervised fine-tuning, and improve data quality (diversity and difficulty) while cutting token costs.

Method: Compute the loss on the first 100 reasoning tokens at randomly perturbed pretrained checkpoints to flag difficult problems. Extend by analyzing loss patterns over the first 1k tokens across a small set of perturbed checkpoints along the fine-tuning trajectory; argue that similar loss trajectories induce similar gradients. Validate by fine-tuning Qwen2.5-7B and Llama3.1-8B on M23K medical reasoning and OpenThoughts-Math datasets and comparing to baselines.

Result: Outperforms baselines by up to 1.7 percentage points and achieves 91% higher token efficiency during data curation and SFT.

Conclusion: Early-token loss signals provide an effective, cost-efficient data curation strategy for SFT that yields better reasoning capability with substantially reduced token usage, with demonstrated generality across models and reasoning domains.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a small, high-quality set of long reasoning traces is an effective approach for eliciting strong reasoning capabilities in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing methods for curating high-quality SFT data rely heavily on strong reasoning models to filter examples based on diversity and difficulty, making the curation process costly while often yielding suboptimal data quality. In this work, we show that diverse and challenging reasoning examples can be identified using only the initial reasoning tokens. Specifically, we demonstrate that difficult problems can be reliably detected based on the loss of the first 100 reasoning tokens evaluated at a randomly perturbed checkpoint of the pretrained model. We further show that examples exhibiting similar loss patterns over their first 1k reasoning tokens across a small number of perturbed checkpoints extrapolating along the fine-tuning trajectory provably induce similar gradients. We validate our approach through extensive experiments on fine-tuning Qwen2.5-7B and Llama3.1-8B models on the M23K medical reasoning and OpenThoughts-Math datasets. Our method outperforms existing baselines by up to 1.7% while being 91% more token efficient.

</details>


### [231] [Quantization in Federated Learning: Methods, Challenges and Future Directions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26822)
*Farwa Ikram,Dipanwita Thakur,Antonella Guzzo,Giancarlo Fortino*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive FL quantization survey proposing a FL-centric taxonomy to address scalability, privacy, and heterogeneity, and offering guidelines for deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantization is key to mitigating communication and computation bottlenecks in Federated Learning, but existing work lacks a unified, FL-specific perspective that accounts for non-IID data, client heterogeneity, and the interplay with security/privacy.

Method: Systematic literature review and taxonomy development focused on FL-specific dimensions: client heterogeneity, aggregation consistency, communication-scheduling adaptation, non-IID robustness, privacy/security integration, and hardware/energy co-optimization; analyze interactions with client drift, partial participation, secure aggregation, and differential privacy; identify gaps and provide design guidelines.

Result: Introduces a FL-centric taxonomy of quantization methods, analyzes how quantization interacts with core FL behaviors, provides cross-method insights, highlights open research gaps, and offers practical design guidelines for deploying quantized FL on mobile/IoT/edge platforms.

Conclusion: Quantization should be viewed as a fundamental FL system component that shapes performance, robustness, and practicality; the survey offers a roadmap for researchers and practitioners to build robust, efficient quantized FL systems.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) has become a foundational paradigm for privacy-preserving distributed intelligence, yet its scalability remains fundamentally constrained by communication bottlenecks, device heterogeneity, and the challenges of training under statistically non-IID data. Quantization is one of the most effective mechanisms for mitigating these limitations, reducing both uplink/downlink payloads and on-device computation. This paper provides the first FL-centric systematic review of quantization, introducing a novel taxonomy organized around FL-specific dimensions, including client heterogeneity, aggregation consistency, communication-scheduling adaptation, non-IID robustness, privacy/security integration, and hardware/energy co-optimization. Beyond cataloging existing methods, we analyze how quantization interacts with core FL behaviors such as client drift, partial participation, convergence stability, secure aggregation, and differential privacy. We further identify cross-method insights, open research gaps, and design guidelines for practitioners deploying quantized FL on mobile, IoT, and edge platforms. This survey thus establishes quantization not merely as a compression technique, but as a fundamental systems component shaping the performance, robustness, and practicality of modern FL.

</details>


### [232] [Asymptotically Optimal Learning for Parametric Prophet Inequalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26893)
*Jung-hun Kim,Anna Grebennikova,Vianney Perchet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learn online in prophet inequalities with rewards from an exponential-type parametric family with unknown θ. Characterizes optimal full-information asymptotic competitive ratio: unbounded-support limit is ((θ/(θ−c_+))^{c_+/θ}) / Γ(1−c_+/θ); bounded-support limit is 1. Proposes a confidence-based DP policy that uses online data to match the optimal ratio without offline samples, and provides distribution-specific convergence rates and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address online selection under uncertainty where rewards follow a parametric family with unknown θ. The work aims to determine the optimal asymptotic performance under full information and to design an online learning policy that achieves the same performance using only online observations, avoiding reliance on offline data.

Method: (i) Derive the optimal full-information asymptotic competitive ratio for the entire parametric family, giving explicit limits for unbounded and bounded support. (ii) Develop a confidence-based dynamic-programming policy that leverages the parametric structure to learn θ online and achieve the same asymptotic ratio as in full information. (iii) Obtain distribution-specific convergence rates for canonical examples. (iv) Validate numerically on synthetic instances.

Result: Explicit limits for the optimal full-information asymptotic competitive ratio: unbounded-support case limit given by ((θ/(θ−c_+))^{c_+/θ}) / Γ(1−c_+/θ), bounded-support limit equals 1. The proposed online policy attains the same asymptotic ratio using only online data. They also derive distribution-specific convergence rates and report numerical experiments demonstrating the policy's performance.

Conclusion: Online learning under prophet inequalities with a known-parametric structure can recover the full-information optimal asymptotic performance without offline samples, via a confidence-based DP policy. The results include explicit ratio expressions, convergence rates for canonical distributions, and empirical validation.

Abstract: We study learning in prophet inequalities with i.i.d. rewards drawn from an exponential-type parametric family with an unknown parameter $θ$, a class that includes exponential, Pareto, and bounded-support power-family distributions. We first characterize the optimal full-information asymptotic competitive ratio for this family. In the unbounded-support case, the limit is $ {\left(θ/({θ-c_+})\right)^{c_+/θ}}/ {Γ(1-c_+/θ)},$ while in the bounded-support case, the limit is $1$. We then propose a confidence-based dynamic-programming policy for online learning. By exploiting the explicit parametric structure, the policy achieves the same optimal asymptotic competitive ratio using only online observations, without external offline samples. We further derive distribution-specific convergence rates for canonical examples. Finally, numerical experiments on synthetic instances illustrate the performance of our algorithm.

</details>


### [233] [GEOALIGN: Geometric Rollout Curation for Robust LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26917)
*Ting Zhou,Zhenqing Ling,Yiyang Zhao,Ying Shen,Daoyuan Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geoalign is a lightweight rollout-curation plug-in for online RLHF that mitigates directional inconsistency by aligning rollout directions with a batch consensus; it uses within-prompt preference pairs, an online projector on per-rollout hidden states, and angular deviation to filter inconsistent rollouts, with negligible overhead, and improves stability and performance over PF-PPO, PAR, PODS, Seed-GRPO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online reinforcement learning for LLM alignment suffers from instability due to noisy or misspecified rewards, particularly a failure mode termed directional inconsistency where a minority of high-reward rollouts skew representation-space directions.

Method: Form within-prompt preference pairs; learn an online projector on per-rollout hidden states to emphasize reward-ordered displacement directions; detect directionally inconsistent rollouts via angular deviation from a batch consensus prototype; rectify by substituting within-prompt stable alternatives; forward-pass only with negligible overhead.

Result: Geoalign improves final performance and reduces training oscillation in dialogue alignment with a learned reward model and mathematical reasoning with binary verified rewards, outperforming PF-PPO, PAR, PODS, and Seed-GRPO.

Conclusion: Latent directional consensus serves as an effective reliability signal for online LLM RL, and Geoalign demonstrates that simple, online, plug-in curation can stabilize and improve RLHF performance.

Abstract: Online reinforcement learning is widely used to align large language models (LLMs) with reward signals, yet training can be unstable under noisy or misspecified rewards. We identify a failure mode we call directional inconsistency: within a batch, a small set of high-reward rollouts induces representation-space preference directions that sharply disagree with the batch majority, resulting in high-variance and destabilizing updates. We propose geoalign, a lightweight plug-in for rollout curation in iterative policy optimization. Geoalign (i) forms within-prompt preference pairs, (ii) learns an online projector on per-rollout hidden states to concentrate reward-ordered displacement directions, and (iii) detects directionally inconsistent rollouts via their angular deviation from a batch consensus prototype and rectifies them with within-prompt stable alternatives. Geoalign is forward-pass only and adds negligible overhead. Across dialogue alignment with a learned reward model and mathematical reasoning with binary verified rewards, Geoalign improves final performance and reduces training oscillation, outperforming PF-PPO, PAR, PODS, and Seed-GRPO. These results suggest latent directional consensus as an effective reliability signal for online LLM RL.

</details>


### [234] [Decision-Aligned Evaluation of Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26990)
*Annika Schneider,Tommy Rochussen,Joshua Stiller,Vincent Fortuin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decision-aligned uncertainty evaluation using prior-weighted utility metrics; standard UQ metrics may misalign with downstream decision utility; propose a class of proper scoring rules weighted by task priors; validated across benchmarks and real-world cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current UQ evaluation relies on generic metrics (NLL, ECE) that may not reflect usefulness for downstream decisions; identify misalignment and potential pathological priors.

Method: Develop decision-alignment criterion; analyze existing UQ metrics for alignment; define prior-weighted utility metrics as a subclass of proper scoring rules; apply to benchmarks and case studies to compare with conventional metrics.

Result: Prior-weighted utility metrics consistently align with realized decision utility; conventional metrics often do not; reveal flaws in standard UQ evaluation protocols.

Conclusion: Encourages principled, decision-relevant UQ evaluation; extends metrics beyond traditional measures; offers a path to more decision-centered uncertainty quantification.

Abstract: Uncertainty estimates in machine learning are typically evaluated using generic metrics such as the negative log-likelihood and expected calibration error, yet good performance on such metrics does not necessarily imply high utility in downstream decisions. We introduce decision-alignment, a criterion that reveals which evaluation metrics meaningfully align with downstream utilities. Applying this framework, we show that many widely used uncertainty metrics are either misaligned with common decision problems or encode pathological prior beliefs about the downstream task. We then propose prior-weighted utility metrics, a special class of proper scoring rules that provides decision-aligned uncertainty evaluation. Across benchmark experiments and real-world case studies, our metrics consistently align with realized decision utility, while conventional metrics do not. Our results surface flaws in the current UQ evaluation protocol and offer a principled extension of existing metrics toward decision-relevant UQ evaluation.

</details>


### [235] [Uncertainty quantification via conformal prediction in data assimilation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27001)
*Catherine George,Alireza Javanmardi,Tijana Janjić,Eyke Hüllermeier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evaluation of conformal prediction variants (Standard CP, Normalized CP, Conformalized Quantile Regression) for uncertainty quantification in a 1D modified shallow water model; benchmarks against ensemble methods and explores CP-based perturbations in data assimilation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty quantification is crucial for probabilistic forecasting and data assimilation in numerical weather prediction. Conformal prediction (CP) offers calibrated, coverage-controlled prediction sets. This study tests CP in a controlled, idealized convective setting and benchmarks against traditional ensemble-based uncertainty measures.

Method: Use a one-dimensional modified shallow water model as a convective surrogate. Assess three CP variants: Standard CP, Normalized CP, and Conformalized Quantile Regression. Evaluate performance with metrics: average empirical coverage, average interval length, miss low, miss high, and average interval score loss (AISL). Compare CP-based uncertainty with standard deviation intervals and ensemble spread. Investigate integration of CP-derived uncertainty within the data assimilation cycle via CP perturbations.

Result: CP variants provide calibrated uncertainty estimates and reveal strengths and limitations; CP can complement ensemble-based uncertainty quantification in the simplified atmospheric model. The study provides insight into how CP-based uncertainty estimates perform relative to ensemble methods and demonstrates the feasibility of incorporating CP perturbations into data assimilation.

Conclusion: Conformal prediction appears as a promising complement to ensemble-based uncertainty quantification in simplified atmospheric models. The choice among Standard CP, Normalized CP, and Conformalized Quantile Regression influences performance, and CP perturbations offer a pathway for DA integration. Further work is needed to evaluate in more realistic, higher-dimensional settings.

Abstract: Quantifying the evolution of uncertainty is critical to both probabilistic forecasting and data assimilation in numerical weather prediction. In this study, we investigate the applicability of conformal prediction (CP), a recent machine learning (ML) method, to quantify uncertainty in a controlled, idealized setting. We use the one dimensional modified shallow water model, designed to mimic the convective process. CP provides a set of possible outcomes with a chosen confidence level. Here, we compare and evaluate the average empirical coverage, the average interval length, miss low, miss high and average interval score loss (AISL) for three variants of CP, namely a) Standard CP, b) Normalized CP and c) Conformalized Quantile Regression. We further compare these CP-based uncertainty estimates with traditional ensemble-based measures such as standard deviation intervals and ensemble spread. In addition, we investigate the integration of CP-derived uncertainty within the data assimilation cycle through CP perturbations. Our results highlight the strengths and limitations of each approach, providing insight into the effectiveness of CP to complement common ensemble-based uncertainty quantification in simplified atmospheric models.

</details>


### [236] [A Generalization Theory for JEPA-Based World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27014)
*Jingyi Cui,Qi Zhang,Hongwei Wen,Yisen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First generalization theory for JEPA-based world models: JEPA pretraining is equivalent to low-rank factorization of an action-conditioned co-occurrence matrix; links pretraining error to planning regret; provides finite-sample generalization bounds and reveals a latent-dimension trade-off between approximation and sample errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the theoretical underpinnings of JEPA-based world models and quantify how latent predictive representations generalize to planning tasks.

Method: Formulate JEPA pretraining as a conditional spectral graph learning problem; prove equivalence to low-rank factorization of an action-conditioned co-occurrence matrix; derive a finite-sample generalization bound that connects pretraining error to planning regret; analyze error decomposition (approximation vs. sample) with latent dimension.

Result: A generalization bound for JEPA-based world models; theoretical link between pretraining performance and downstream planning performance; characterized trade-off with latent dimension, clarifying when latent predictors outperform input-level predictors.

Conclusion: Latent predictive models incur a bias-variance trade-off via latent dimension; the framework clarifies when JEPA-based approaches are advantageous and exposes limitations relative to input-level prediction.

Abstract: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for world modeling by learning predictive dynamics in a latent space rather than generating future observations at the input level. Despite their empirical success, the theoretical understanding of JEPA-based world models remains limited. In this paper, we develop the first generalization theory for JEPA-based world models. We formulate JEPA pretraining as a conditional spectral graph learning problem and show that the JEPA objective is equivalent to a low-rank factorization of an action-conditioned co-occurrence matrix. Building on this characterization, we establish a connection between JEPA pretraining error and downstream planning regret, leading to a finite-sample generalization bound for JEPA-based world models. Our analysis reveals an inherent trade-off between approximation and sample errors with respect to the latent dimension, providing theoretical insights into the advantages and limitations of latent predictive models compared with input-level predictive approaches.

</details>


### [237] [Just how sure are you? Improving Verbalized Uncertainty Calibration in Medical VQA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27023)
*Eren Senoglu,Federico Toschi,Nicolo Brunello,Andrea Sassella,Mark James Carman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A training-based fine-tuning framework for multimodal LLMs in Medical VQA that improves calibration and discrimination while preserving accuracy, using a composite loss (Brier-like calibration, anchor regularizer, contrastive image-text alignment, KL stabilization), a 2x2 perturbation alignment signal, and a top-K KL constraint; validated on three Medical VQA benchmarks with MedGemma 4B IT and Qwen2 VL 7B Instruct, with substantial gains and code release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical VQA models suffer from overconfident predictions and multimodal calibration gaps; existing verification methods from text-only LLMs do not address multimodal dependencies. Motivation is to develop a training-based calibration framework that accounts for visual input and language priors while maintaining answering ability.

Method: Fine-tune MLLMs with a composite loss: (1) Brier-style calibration term, (2) anchor regularizer to prevent confidence collapse, (3) contrastive image-text alignment term, (4) KL-based model stabilization term. Alignment uses a 2x2 factorial perturbation crossing image presence and text integrity. A top-K KL divergence regularizer protects answering ability. Evaluated on three Medical VQA benchmarks using MedGemma 4B IT and Qwen2 VL 7B Instruct; code released.

Result: Calibration error reduced by 60% or more; discrimination improved by 26% or more; predictive accuracy preserved. Outperforms prompting-based, sampling-based, and training-based baselines. Ablations show each loss component is necessary. Code publicly available.

Conclusion: A multi-term training-based calibration framework effectively improves both calibration and discrimination in multimodal medical VQA across architectures and benchmarks, with a robust alignment signal and preservation of answering capability; the approach is validated by comprehensive ablations and code release.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) applied to Medical Visual Question Answering (VQA) tend to produce overconfident outputs regardless of actual correctness, and existing verbalized confidence calibration methods, developed primarily for text only LLMs, do not account for the multimodal nature of medical image understanding.
  This work proposes a training based framework that finetunes MLLMs to improve their calibration using a composite loss function combining a Brier style calibration term, an anchor regularizer that prevents confidence collapse toward extreme values, a contrastive image text alignment term, and a KL based model stabilization term. The alignment signal is derived from a $2 \times 2$ factorial perturbation design that crosses image presence with text integrity, probing the reliance of the model on visual modality input versus language priors. Finally, a top K KL divergence regularizer is used to protect the answering ability of the model during finetuning.
  Across three Medical VQA benchmarks and two architectures (MedGemma 4B IT and Qwen2 VL 7B Instruct), our method reduces calibration error by 60% or more, and improves discrimination by 26% or more, while preserving predictive accuracy. On average across benchmarks, the technique outperforms prompting based, sampling based, and training based approaches, and ablation experiments confirm that each component of the loss function is indeed necessary for improving the calibration. All code for the experiments is publicly available.

</details>


### [238] [Symplectic Neural Networks for learning Generalized Hamiltonians](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27029)
*Harsh Choudhary,Vyacheslav Kungurtsev,Chandan Gupta,Melvin Leok,Georgios Korpas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Efficiently train Hamiltonian neural networks with implicit symplectic discretization using adjoint sensitivities, enabling accurate, energy-preserving system identification from noisy data with reduced compute/memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify the system Hamiltonian from noisy trajectory data while ensuring long-term fidelity and energy conservation. Symplectic integrators preserve geometric structure but implicit schemes are costly; leveraging adjoint discretizations yields efficient gradient computation.

Method: Train an HNN using an implicit symplectic integrator. Employ a predictor-corrector ODE solver with fixed-point iteration to mitigate implicit timestepping cost. Leverage that symplectic discretizations of the adjoint yield the same sensitivities as backpropagation to compute gradients efficiently. Train under noisy trajectory observations. Post-process the learned Hamiltonian with backward error analysis to obtain a modified Hamiltonian with better approximation of the true Hamiltonian.

Result: Demonstrates numerical advantages: improved energy preservation and system identification on non-separable, chaotic systems. Shows efficient gradient updates and favorable memory complexity. Backward error analysis yields a modified Hamiltonian that more accurately approximates the true Hamiltonian without requiring more accurate discretizations of the flow map.

Conclusion: The proposed implicit symplectic training framework for HNNs provides efficient gradient computation and enhanced fidelity in long-term dynamics, including energy conservation, even under noisy observations. Post-processing via backward error analysis improves the Hamiltonian approximation without extra discretization cost.

Abstract: Hamiltonian Neural Networks (HNNs) integrate physical priors into neural models by learning a system's Hamiltonian, improving generalization and sample efficiency. Identifying the system Hamiltonian from noisy observations of state variables is a challenging task. For simulations to faithfully reflect the long-term behavior of Hamiltonian systems, especially energy conservation, it is essential to use symplectic integrators, which preserve the system's geometric structure. This fidelity comes at a cost: implicit symplectic integrators are more computationally intensive and make backpropagation through the ODE solver non-trivial. However, by leveraging the fact that symplectic discretizations of the adjoint system yield the same sensitivities associated by backpropagation, we obtain an efficient method of training the Neural Network parameters. In our work, we explore this alternate method of HNN training under noisy observation of trajectories with our HNN model based on an implicit symplectic integrator. Computationally, a predictor-corrector based ODE solver and fixed point iteration help to mitigate the computational cost of the implicit timestepping, resulting in more efficient generation of gradient updates. We showcase the numerical advantage, in experiments, in system identification and energy preservation on a range of non-separable, chaotic systems and the efficient computation and memory complexity of our method. We also observe that the post-processing of the learned Hamiltonian using backward error analysis yields a modified Hamiltonian that is a more accurate approximation of the true Hamiltonian without the need to use more accurate discretizations of the flow map.

</details>


### [239] [State Representation Matters in Deep Reinforcement Learning: Application to Energy Trading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27032)
*Jesper Klicks,Sander Vržina,Vincent François-Lavet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Combining feature families for market state representations yields the strongest RL performance in HydroDam; state design is central for transfer across markets, not a minor preprocessing step.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how different market feature representations (absolute, relative, forecast) affect reinforcement learning performance in energy storage arbitrage, given operational constraints.

Method: Fixed HydroDam environment with a Double DQN agent; systematically replace market feature sets (absolute, relative, forecast, and all combinations) while keeping other components constant; train on 2007–2011 Belgian day-ahead prices and evaluate on a 2012–2025 test set and across 39 ENTSO-E market zones.

Result: Absolute-only features yield 28.8% on the test set; relative-only and forecast-only underperform relative to a rolling price-score heuristic. Combining features improves performance: absolute+relative = 49.9% (test) and 39.8% (cross-zone); absolute+relative+forecast = 55.6% (test) and 47.5% (cross-zone). Across-zone medians show robust gains with feature combinations.

Conclusion: State representation is a central design choice for storage-trading RL. Robust cross-market transfer requires integrating price scale, recent relative price context, and short-horizon forecasts rather than relying on any single feature family.

Abstract: Energy trading decisions depend not only on current market prices, but also on expected future market conditions, and operational constraints. This makes the state representation given to a reinforcement learning agent an important design choice. We study this in HydroDam, a pumped-storage arbitrage environment, using a fixed Double DQN agent. The environment, action space, reward function, network, and training protocol are kept fixed; only the market features are changed. We compare absolute price/calendar features, relative features that compare current prices with recent market history, forecast features, and all combinations of these three feature families. Policies are trained and selected using 2007--2011 Belgian day-ahead prices and evaluated on two test settings: a later same-market test set from 2012--2025 and 39 other ENTSO-E market zones. Absolute features only reaches 28.8% on the test set and a median 5.7% across zones. Relative-only and forecast-only states also stay below a rolling price-score heuristic in the cross-zone median. Combining feature families is much stronger: absolute + relative reaches 49.9% on the test set and a 39.8% cross-zone median, while absolute + relative + forecast reaches 55.6% and 47.5%. These results suggest that state representation is not a minor preprocessing choice in storage-trading RL, but a central part of the policy design: robust transfer requires combining price scale, recent relative price context, and short-horizon forecast information, rather than relying on any single feature family.

</details>


### [240] [Finding Stationary Points by Comparisons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27082)
*Helin Wang,Chenyi Zhang,Xiwen Tao,Yexin Zhang,Tongyang Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Classical: finds ε-stationary points of smooth non-convex functions using a comparison oracle in ~ O(n^2 / ε^{3/2}) queries; includes a subroutine to estimate the normalized Hessian in ~ O(n^2 log(1/δ)) queries. Quantum variant achieves ~ O(n / ε^{3/2}) queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address optimization when only pairwise comparison information of function values is available; locate ε-stationary points for Lipschitz-smooth functions; extend to quantum comparison model.

Method: Develop an algorithm leveraging the comparison oracle to navigate toward ε-stationary points; build a Hessian-normalization estimation subroutine with query complexity; analyze the impact on overall complexity; propose a quantum version with superposition queries.

Result: Achieves ε-stationary point in ~ O(n^2 / ε^{3/2}) queries; Hessian-estimation subroutine costs ~ O(n^2 log(1/δ)); quantum algorithm attains ~ O(n / ε^{3/2}) queries.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility and benefits of comparison-based access in non-convex optimization and shows a quantum speedup in the same model; highlights the role of Hessian normalization estimation in the algorithm.

Abstract: We study the problem of finding stationary points of non-convex functions when access to the objective is provided only through a comparison oracle that, given two points, outputs which has the larger function value. For a twice differentiable $f\colon\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R$ with Lipschitz gradient and Hessian, we develop an algorithm that visits an $ε$-stationary point using $\widetilde O(n^2/ε^{1.5})$ queries. Our approach uses a subroutine that estimates the normalized Hessian to accuracy $δ$ using $\widetilde O(n^2\log(1/δ))$ queries. We further study this problem with a quantum comparison oracle model where queries can be made in superpositions, and develop the first quantum algorithm that finds an $ε$-stationary point, which takes $\widetilde O(n/ε^{1.5})$ queries.

</details>


### [241] [Data-Free Reservoir Features for Efficient Long-Horizon Cold-Start Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27095)
*Augustinas Jučas,Yangchen Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CIRCLE uses fixed BiRC2D-inspired reservoir features plus streaming SLDA heads to build a class-incremental classifier without replay or backpropagation, ensembled across multiple random reservoirs. It achieves competitive results for short task horizons and substantially outperforms CS-EFCIL baselines at long horizons while training faster than drift-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the computational and drift challenges of cold-start class-incremental learning by avoiding backbone updates and replay, yet maintain accuracy across many tasks.

Method: Construct fixed, random reservoir features (BiRC2D) fed into streaming SLDA heads. Create an ensemble by combining multiple reservoir instantiations and average their softmax outputs. No replay, no task-boundary information, and no backbone training; online, closed-form updates for the SLDA heads.

Result: On CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet, ImageNet-Subset, and ImageNet-1k, CIRCLE is competitive at 10–20 tasks and substantially outperforms strong CS-EFCIL baselines at 50–500 tasks, while training much faster than drift-compensation methods. Ablations show each component (BiRC2D extractor, SLDA head, and ensembling) contributes to performance.

Conclusion: A fixed-feature extractor with an ensemble of SLDA heads provides an effective bias-variance tradeoff for cold-start ECIL, enabling sample-wise training with no replay or backbone updates. The BiRC2D extractor, the SLDA head, and balanced ensembling are key to the gains.

Abstract: Cold-start exemplar-free class-incremental learning requires learning a growing set of classes without replay, external pretraining, or a large initial task. Existing cold-start methods typically either train the backbone throughout the stream and compensate for semantic drift, or freeze a backbone after the first task, producing features biased toward the initial classes. These choices also create a computational tension: drift-compensation methods require repeated backbone training and increasingly expensive updates as the task horizon grows, while frozen-backbone methods are cheap but weak under cold start. We study a third option: a feature extractor that is never fit to image data at all. We propose CIRCLE, a class-incremental classifier built from fixed bidirectional two-dimensional reservoir features, adapted from BiRC2D for image classification, and streaming linear discriminant analysis heads. CIRCLE groups multiple random reservoir instantiations into feature ensembles and averages the softmax outputs of independent SLDA heads, yielding a tunable bias-variance tradeoff between richer random features and prediction-level ensembling. Because the feature extractor is fixed and the head admits streaming closed-form updates, CIRCLE performs sample-wise training without replay, task-boundary information, or backbone backpropagation. On CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet, ImageNet-Subset, and ImageNet-1k, CIRCLE is competitive at 10-20 task splits and substantially outperforms strong CS-EFCIL baselines at 50, 100, and 500 task splits, while training much faster than trained-backbone drift-compensation methods. Ablations show that the BiRC2D-style extractor, SLDA head, and balanced feature/prediction ensembling each contribute to the final performance.

</details>


### [242] [Transformer-Based Classification of Bacterial Raman Spectra with LOOCV](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27096)
*Jamile Mohammad Jafari,Thomas Bocklitz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer-based Raman spectral classifier outperforms PCA/ICA pipelines across six bacterial species, with replicate-aware nested cross-validation; strong latent-space separation and robustness to raw spectra.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether transformer architectures can provide robust, preprocessing-free Raman spectral classification and how they compare to conventional PCA/ICA-based pipelines.

Method: Nested leave-one-replicate-out cross-validation on a bacterial Raman dataset (5,417 spectra; 6 species; 9 replicates). Compare a transformer model to pipelines combining PCA/ICA with LDA, SVM, and Random Forest. Evaluate on both preprocessed data and raw spectra; analyze latent representations.

Result: The transformer consistently achieved the highest accuracy across independent test replicates and significantly outperformed all conventional methods. Latent feature space showed improved class separation vs. PCA/ICA. The transformer also maintained superior performance on raw spectra without preprocessing.

Conclusion: Transformers are a promising approach for robust Raman spectral classification, and replicate-aware validation is essential for realistic model evaluation.

Abstract: Transformer-based models have recently attracted increasing attention for Raman spectral classification. In this study, a transformer-based approach was systematically evaluated using a nested leave-one-replicate-out cross-validation framework and compared with conventional machine-learning pipelines combining PCA or ICA with LDA, SVM, and Random Forest classifiers. A bacterial Raman dataset comprising 5,417 single-cell spectra from six bacterial species and nine independent measurement replicates was used. The transformer consistently achieved the highest classification performance across independent test replicates and significantly outperformed all conventional approaches. Analysis of the learned latent feature space revealed improved class separation compared with PCA- and ICA-based representations. Furthermore, the transformer maintained superior performance when applied directly to raw Raman spectra without preprocessing, demonstrating robust behavior across measurement replicates. These findings highlight the potential of transformer-based models for robust Raman spectral classification and emphasize the importance of replicate-aware validation for realistic model evaluation.

</details>


### [243] [Heavy-Ball Q-Learning with Residual Weighting Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27112)
*Donghwan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A corrected heavy-ball Q-learning method is proposed with convergence guarantees, and conditions under which it accelerates convergence over standard Q-learning are identified. The approach is extended to Q-learning with linear function approximation, with analogous convergence and acceleration results. A switched linear system (SLS) representation and joint spectral radius (JSR) analysis are used, offering a complementary framework and new insights into momentum-based acceleration in Q-learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve convergence speed and stability of Q-learning by incorporating heavy-ball momentum and providing theoretical guarantees. Extend to linear function approximation to broaden applicability. Use SLS/JSR framework to gain structural insight into acceleration mechanisms.

Method: Introduce a corrected heavy-ball update for Q-learning. Prove convergence under specified conditions. Derive acceleration conditions where convergence is faster than standard Q-learning. Represent the Q-learning updates as a switched linear system and analyze using the joint spectral radius of the switching family. Extend the analysis to Q-learning with linear function approximation to obtain analogous results.

Result: Convergence guarantees for the corrected heavy-ball Q-learning method. Identification of conditions guaranteeing faster convergence than standard Q-learning. Extension of the methodology to linear function approximation with analogous convergence and acceleration statements. A novel SLS/JSR framework for interpreting momentum-based acceleration in Q-learning.

Conclusion: The SLS viewpoint combined with JSR analysis provides a novel and complementary lens to understand how heavy-ball momentum accelerates Q-learning, and it yields theoretical guarantees of convergence and acceleration, with practical extension to function approximation.

Abstract: This paper proposes a corrected heavy-ball Q-learning method for reinforcement learning (RL) and establishes its convergence. It also identifies conditions under which the method is theoretically guaranteed to converge faster than standard Q-learning. The same construction is then extended to Q-learning with linear function approximation, where analogous convergence and acceleration statements are derived. The analysis is based on a switched linear system (SLS) representation of Q-learning algorithms and on the joint spectral radius (JSR) of the associated switching families. This SLS viewpoint is not commonly used in standard analyses of Q-learning, and it provides a complementary framework and new insight into how heavy-ball momentum can accelerate Q-learning.

</details>


### [244] [Cross-Head Attention Uplift Network with Inverse Propensity Score under Unobserved Confounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27114)
*Haoran Zhang,Chuanpu Li,Yuxin Fu,Bin Tong,Guan Wang,Bo Zheng,Feng Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes CHAUN for flexible inter-group correlation via cross-head attention and RA-IPS for robust debiasing under unobserved confounding; demonstrates substantial gains on uplift benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two core challenges in uplift modeling: (1) leveraging cross-group similarity to boost discriminative power, and (2) debiasing when treatment confounding is unobserved or partially observed.

Method: CHAUN uses shared feature embeddings and cross-head attention to dynamically fuse treatment-specific and control-specific representations, improving inter-group correlation modeling. Theoretical identifiability of ITE with access to true propensity scores. RA-IPS adversarially optimizes propensity weights within constrained uncertainty sets to reduce bias when true propensity scores are unavailable.

Result: Empirical results on CRITEO-UPLIFT, LAZADA, and a production e-commerce dataset show CHAUN outperforms state-of-the-art uplift models, with up to 25.6% relative improvement in QINI. RA-IPS improves robustness, outperforming standard IPS by 5.4% under unobserved confounding.

Conclusion: The proposed CHAUN and RA-IPS effectively address inter-group correlation modeling and unobserved confounding in uplift tasks, with strong empirical validation on public and production data.

Abstract: Uplift modeling, crucial for estimating individual treatment effects (ITE), faces dual challenges: flexibly leveraging inter-group similarity to enhance discriminative power and debiasing under unobserved confounding scenarios. In this paper, we propose the Cross-Head Attention Uplift Network (CHAUN) and Robust Adversarial Inverse Propensity Score (RA-IPS) method to address these limitations. CHAUN employs shared feature embeddings and cross-head attention mechanisms to dynamically integrate treatment-specific and control-specific representations, enhancing inter-group correlation modeling. Theoretically, we prove that access to the true propensity scores ensures ITE identifiability even with unobserved confounders. For practical scenarios lacking true propensity scores, RA-IPS adversarially optimizes propensity weights within constrained uncertainty sets to mitigate bias from unobserved variables. Experiments on public datasets (CRITEO-UPLIFT, LAZADA) and a production e-commerce dataset demonstrate CHAUN's superiority over state-of-the-art uplift models, achieving relative improvements of up to 25.6% in QINI scores. RA-IPS further enhances robustness, outperforming standard IPS by 5.4% under unobserved confounding. The results validate the effectiveness of our proposed methods in real-world causal inference tasks.

</details>


### [245] [Kolmogorov Arnold networks (KAN) for aerodynamic prediction: a comparison with MLPs and GNNs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27126)
*Miguel Jaraiz,Fermin Gutierrez,Pablo Yeste,Miguel Sánchez-Domínguez,Eusebio Valero,Gonzalo Rubio,Lucas Lacasa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KANs can interpolate pressure fields for airfoils and may train faster and be simpler, but suffer stability issues and may underperform compared to well-trained MLPs (GNNs can win with longer training).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether Kolmogorov Arnold networks offer empirical advantages over traditional neural architectures (MLPs, GNNs) in fluid dynamics surrogate modeling, specifically surface pressure distribution over airfoils across range of Mach numbers and angles of attack.

Method: Empirical comparison across architectures (KANs, MLPs, GNNs) on a surrogate modeling task: predict surface pressure coefficients for subsonic and transonic airfoils; evaluate interpolation across Mach numbers and AoA; compare performance, training time, and hyperparameter sensitivity.

Result: KANs yield good predictions of the full pressure coefficient field and can interpolate across Mach numbers and AoA, with performance comparable to (marginally inferior to) a well-trained MLP; GNNs achieve best performance but require longer training; KANs have lower model complexity and faster training yet exhibit training instabilities and high sensitivity to hyperparameters.

Conclusion: KANs show potential for efficient surrogate modeling in aerodynamics but currently do not consistently outperform MLPs/GNNs; practical use requires careful hyperparameter optimization and stabilization; further work needed to address training instability and to establish regimes where KANs offer clear benefits.

Abstract: Kolmogorov Arnold networks (KAN) have recently been introduced as a (deep) neural network architecture whose trainable parameters adapt the activation functions, instead of the coefficients of the affine transformations at the core of traditional architectures such as deep multilayer perceptrons (MLPs). This architecture builds on the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem, which endows it with universal approximation properties. While the advent of KANs has been received with excitement, there is a current debate about the possible KAN supremacy over deep multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) for classic fields such as symbolic regression, generic-purpose machine learning, natural language processing or computer vision. Here we assess the performance of KANs --and its nuanced comparison against MLPs and graph neural networks (GNNs)-- in the realm of fluid dynamics surrogate modelling. To that aim, we consider the task of predicting the surface pressure distribution over subsonic and transonic airfoils, a canonical task in aerodynamics. Our results show that KAN models show good performance in predicting the whole pressure coefficients and is able to interpolate across Mach numbers and angles of attack, however its performance is comparable --marginally inferior-- to a suitably trained MLP, where best performance is achieved by a GNN at the expense or requiring lengthier training. While the optimal KAN model have typically much lower complexity than MLP and GNN --hence resulting in faster training--, we find that KANs suffer from training instabilities, and their performance is highly dependent on a proper hyperparameter optimisation.

</details>


### [246] [fTNN: a tensor neural network for fractional PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27140)
*Qingkui Ma,Hehu Xie,Xiaobo Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deterministic fTNN framework for fractional Laplacian PDEs with geometry-adapted integration and boundary-singularity-aware trial functions; extends to time-dependent problems via spatiotemporal separable networks and alternating subspace optimization; outperforms fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines on challenging cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address numerical difficulties in fractional PDEs (kernel singularities, low regularity, long-time integration) by a fully deterministic integration approach and boundary-aware neural representations to improve accuracy and convergence over stochastic methods.

Method: Decompose the fractional Laplacian into near-field singular, interior far-field, and exterior far-field components using a geometry-adapted, spatially varying near-field radius. Apply Gauss-Jacobi quadrature to singular radial integrals, Gauss quadrature to regular radial integrals, and deterministic angular quadrature. Enrich trial spaces with boundary-singularity-aware features; propose automatic strategies to select leading exponents and to compute the loss from singularity structures. For time-dependent problems, employ a spatiotemporally separable neural network that splits the residual into low-dimensional temporal and spatial integrals, coupled with an alternating neural network subspace optimization strategy.

Result: Numerical experiments show high accuracy on benchmarks and substantial improvements over existing fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines, especially for problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.

Conclusion: Provides a fully deterministic integration framework for fractional Laplacian operators with boundary-aware trial functions, enabling effective solution of time-dependent fractional PDEs via a separable NN formulation and subspace optimization; promising improvements over stochastic baselines in challenging regimes.

Abstract: We develop the fTNN, a deterministic tensor neural network subspace method for problems involving the fractional Laplacian on bounded domains, taking the fractional Poisson equation and time-dependent fractional advection-diffusion equation as typical representatives. The work employs a geometry-adapted integration split featuring a spatially dependent near-field radius, which decomposes the fractional Laplacian into three contributions: a singular near field, a regular interior far field, and an analytical exterior far field. Then the singular radial integrals are treated by Gauss-Jacobi quadrature, the regular radial integrals by Gauss quadrature, and the angular variables by deterministic angular quadrature, yielding a fully deterministic integration framework of the fractional Laplacian operator. To accurately resolve low-regularity solutions and the associated loss functional, we construct boundary-singularity-aware trial functions enriched with explicit boundary features, and propose two strategies for automatically selecting the leading exponent and evaluating the loss function from the singularity structure induced by the fractional operator, or jointly by the fractional operator and the source term. For time-dependent fractional PDEs, we design a spatiotemporally separable neural network that factorizes the time-space residual into a sum of low-dimensional temporal and spatial integrals, and we integrate this representation with an alternating neural network subspace optimization strategy for efficient training. Numerical experiments show that the proposed framework attains high accuracy on the tested benchmarks and improves substantially over existing fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines, particularly for problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.

</details>


### [247] [Stochastic Gradient Optimization with Model-Assisted Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27171)
*Jonne Pohjankukka,Jukka Heikkonen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A variance-reduced gradient estimation framework for SGD using survey sampling, introducing a model-assisted sampling approach that leverages auxiliary gradient-prediction models; improves efficiency and generalization with momentum optimizers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stochastic gradient noise slows convergence and harms generalization; existing variance reduction and adaptive methods add computational overhead. A framework that integrates survey sampling theory with ML optimization could reduce gradient variance efficiently without heavy costs.

Method: Treat the dataset as a fixed finite population and gradients as sample-based estimates. Use survey sampling theory to construct improved mini-batch gradient estimators aided by auxiliary gradient-prediction models; uniform sampling is recovered when no auxiliary information is used. The framework integrates with existing optimizers and reduces variance through auxiliary information, without altering optimizer dynamics.

Result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic data and six benchmark datasets shows performance gains in 71–86% of experiments, particularly for medium-sized input spaces. With momentum-based optimizers like AdamW, the proposed estimator achieves better generalization in roughly half the training epochs compared to the baseline estimator.

Conclusion: The work bridges machine learning optimization and survey sampling theory, delivering an easy-to-integrate, variance-reducing gradient estimator that improves efficiency and generalization with modest overhead by leveraging auxiliary gradient-prediction models.

Abstract: This work addresses the problem of variance in stochastic gradient estimation for machine learning optimization. Deep learning relies on mini-batch methods such as stochastic gradient descent, which approximate full gradients but introduce noise, creating trade-offs between convergence stability, speed, and generalization. Existing methods, including variance reduction techniques (e.g., SVRG and SAG) and adaptive optimizers, aim to mitigate gradient noise but may introduce additional computational overhead. We propose a model-assisted sampling framework that interprets mini-batch gradients through survey sampling theory, treating the dataset as a fixed finite population and gradients as sample-based estimates. Our aim is to bridge machine learning optimization and survey sampling theory by combining their perspectives on sample-based estimation and variance reduction. By incorporating auxiliary gradient-prediction models, we construct more efficient gradient estimators, with uniform sampling arising as a special case when no auxiliary information is used. Our approach integrates easily with existing optimizers, improving efficiency without altering their dynamics. Empirical results on synthetic and six benchmark datasets show performance gains in 71-86% of the experiments, particularly for medium-sized input spaces in our benchmarks. Notably, with momentum-based optimizers such as AdamW, the proposed estimator achieves clearly better generalization in roughly half the training epochs compared to baseline estimator.

</details>


### [248] [RecallRisk-BERT: A Multi-Task Framework for Post-Report Medical Device Recall Triage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27174)
*Ali Semih Atalay,Sevgi Yigit-Sert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-task deep learning framework (RecallRisk-BERT) jointly predicts FDA medical device recall severity and root-cause categories by fusing PubMedBERT-derived text with structured features, outperforming baselines and enabling scalable regulatory triage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the growing volume of FDA recall records by enabling joint estimation of recall severity and root-cause categories, improving post-report triage and regulatory decision support.

Method: Evaluate classical ML and boosting models for severity and root-cause prediction; develop RecallRisk-BERT that combines textual representations (PubMedBERT) of recall narratives with embeddings of structured features (product code, regulation number, medical specialty); multi-task learning to predict severity (Class I–III) and 9 root-cause classes; training data: 54,165 recalls (2002–Oct 2025); evaluation via accuracy, macro-precision, macro-recall, macro-F1, ROC-AUC.

Result: Single-task severity prediction: LightGBM with text–tabular features achieved accuracy 0.963, macro-F1 0.856, ROC-AUC 0.974. Multi-task RecallRisk-BERT substantially outperformed the PubMedBERT baseline. Model-derived risk rankings correlated with observed severity (rho = 0.983, p = 1.936e-6).

Conclusion: Text–tabular learning enables scalable post-report recall triage and model-based root-cause risk analysis; multi-task modeling yields superior performance to single-task baselines.

Abstract: Medical device recalls are a critical regulatory mechanism for protecting patient safety. The growing volume of FDA recall records presents challenges in post-report recall triage, severity assessment, and root-cause interpretation. Existing studies mostly address recall occurrence prediction or root-cause analysis separately, while joint modeling of recall severity and root-cause categories has received limited attention. We develop an automated recall triage framework using 54,165 FDA medical device recall records from openFDA, covering the period from 2002 to October 2025. We first evaluate classical machine learning and boosting-based models for recall severity and root-cause category prediction. We then develop RecallRisk-BERT, a multi-task model that combines PubMedBERT-based textual representations of recall narratives with embedding-based representations of structured categorical features, including product code, regulation number, and medical specialty. The model simultaneously predicts recall severity (Class I/II/III) and a consolidated root-cause category (9 classes). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, macro-averaged precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. In single-task severity prediction, our LightGBM-based text--tabular configuration achieved the strongest performance, with an accuracy of 0.963, macro-F1 of 0.856, and ROC-AUC of 0.974. In the multi-task setting, RecallRisk-BERT substantially outperformed the single-task PubMedBERT baseline. Model-derived risk rankings were strongly consistent with observed root-cause severity patterns (rho = 0.983, p = 1.936e-6). These findings indicate that text--tabular learning can support scalable post-report recall triage, regulatory decision support, and model-based root-cause risk analysis.

</details>


### [249] [Explaining Temporal Graph Neural Networks via Feature-induced Information Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27201)
*Ping Xiong,Thomas Schnake,Klaus-Robert Müller,Shinichi Nakajima*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Novel attribution method for ETGNNs using Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) that accounts for full information flow including event-induced variables; employs modular decomposition to handle complex architectures; outperforms existing explanations on synthetic datasets (epidemic tracing, social dynamics) and real-world political event networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainability for event-based temporal graph neural networks (ETGNNs) is limited: existing explanations trace only event embeddings to outputs and neglect event-induced variables that mediate node interactions and long-range temporal dependencies. A full, comparable, and higher-order analysis of information flow is needed.

Method: Extend the Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework to ETGNNs. Quantify information flow from event embeddings and through event-induced variables. Introduce a modular decomposition procedure to construct the relevance structure for complex ETGNN architectures, enabling cross-layer comparability and higher-order interaction analysis.

Result: Qualitative and quantitative experiments show the proposed method consistently outperforms existing explanation approaches and yields more human-interpretable explanations. Evaluations on two synthetic datasets (epidemic tracing and social dynamics) and a real-world political event network dataset.

Conclusion: The proposed NR-based attribution approach provides a comprehensive and interpretable explanation of ETGNNs by capturing the entire information flow through event-associated pathways. The modular decomposition enables scalable analysis of complex architectures and supports higher-order interaction insights.

Abstract: Event-based Temporal Graph Neural Networks (ETGNNs) have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of applications, including social network analysis, epidemic tracing, recommender systems, and political event forecasting. However, their increasing complexity poses significant challenges for explainability. Existing explanation methods focus only on a subset of the information flow within ETGNNs, typically tracing contributions from the event-related embeddings to the output. Consequently, they overlook the important pathways through event-induced variables, which mediate interactions between nodes and thereby play a central role in capturing long-range temporal dependencies. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel attribution method that analyzes the \emph{entire} information flow through all event-associated variables. Our method is built upon the recent Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework, which enables explicit quantification of information flow originating from event embeddings as well as information flow passing through event-induced variables.
