It also ensures comparability of latent variables across layers, and supports higher-order analysis of interactions between events. To handle the architectural complexity of ETGNNs, we extend the NRM framework with a modular decomposition procedure that facilitates the systematic construction of relevance structure for complex neural architectures. We evaluate our approach on two synthetic datasets for epidemic tracing and social dynamics, as well as a real-world dataset of political event networks. Our qualitative and quantitative experiments show that our method consistently outperforms existing explanation approaches while producing more human-interpretable explanations.

</details>


### [250] [Graph Neural Networks Applications Across Domains: All Insights You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27202)
*Abderaouf Bahi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive survey of graph neural networks (GNNs) organized around a unifying design space, deriving spectral/spatial formulations from first principles, and linking expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy. It analyzes twelve application domains (e.g., recommender systems, social networks, knowledge graphs, drug discovery, healthcare, computer vision, traffic, energy, wireless networks, fraud, industrial prognostics, materials science, climate modeling) by detailing graph-construction choices, costs, dominant architectures, and pitfalls of baselines. Key cross-domain patterns include the undercutting effect of heterophily and scale, greater difficulty of temporal graphs, and the gap between top leaderboards and deployment realities. It treats over-smoothing, over-squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability as constraints for adoption rather than checklists.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify GNN research under a single design space, connect theory (Weisfeiler-Leman, spectral/spatial formulations) with practical deployment considerations, and provide a cross-domain assessment of where GNNs are most and least effective, including architectural strengths/weaknesses and domain-specific costs.

Method: (1) Define a unified design space for GNNs, (2) derive spectral and spatial formulations from first principles, (3) connect expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy and delineate capabilities, (4) conduct a cross-domain survey across twelve application domains, detailing graph-construction choices, costs, and dominant architectures, (5) compare reported gains against baselines/splits, (6) identify recurring patterns and constraints affecting adoption, (7) discuss critical issues like over-smoothing, over-squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability.

Result: A cross-domain, principled survey clarifying when and why GNNs work, mapping design choices to costs and performance, highlighting the dominance and failures of architectures across domains, and outlining practical adoption constraints.

Conclusion: GNNs are best viewed through a design-space lens where heterophily, scale, and temporal dynamics frequently undermine common architectures; deployment often lags behind leaderboard performance, and adoption hinges on addressing robustness, fairness, explainability, and distribution shift within the contextual costs of graph construction.

Abstract: Graph neural networks have moved from a niche representation-learning technique to the default model class wherever data carry relational structure. The interesting question is no longer whether message passing helps on a given dataset, but where graph structure earns its computational cost and where it does not. This survey organises the field around a single design space, derives the spectral and spatial formulations from shared first principles, and connects expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy with explicit statements of what current architectures can and cannot separate. Against that methodological backbone we examine twelve application domains, among them recommendation and social networks, knowledge graphs and language-model integration, drug discovery and molecular property learning, healthcare and neuroscience, computer vision, traffic and urban computing, power and renewable-energy systems, wireless and sixth-generation networks, fraud and cybersecurity, industrial prognostics, materials science, and climate modelling. For each domain we specify the graph-construction choices and their costs, identify which architecture families dominate and why, and separate reported gains from artefacts of weak baselines or favourable splits. A cross-domain comparison exposes recurring patterns: heterophily and scale undercut the same models almost everywhere, temporal graphs remain harder than their static counterparts, and the architectures that top public leaderboards are seldom the ones that reach deployment. We treat over-smoothing, over-squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability not as a closing checklist but as the constraints that decide adoption.

</details>


### [251] [The Geometry of Updates: Fisher Alignment at Vocabulary Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27242)
*John Sweeney*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FisherSketch enables training-free source selection for LLMs with shared vocabularies by estimating head Fisher alignment in a streaming, compact signature; reveals non-identifiability of representation metrics and provides a diagnostics toolkit for activation/error coupling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In domains with a shared tokenizer across tasks (e.g., SMILES, proteins, genomics), representation-based metrics like CKA can be uninformative about transfer unless label-conditioned error geometry is considered. Full Fisher geometry is prohibitively expensive at vocabulary scale, motivating a scalable alternative.

Method: Define head Fisher alignment as a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation–error space; introduce FisherSketch to estimate this cosine in a single streaming pass. Use K in {128,256} with m=4096, yielding a 16 KB task signature and ~192 KB per-task streaming state. Works with a shared-output head and examines activation, error, and coupling factors; validated via diagnostics and Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments.

Result: FisherSketch provides informative transfer metrics where activation similarity alone fails to distinguish tasks; yields compact, near-model-agnostic signatures that can be stored alongside model hashes; supports source selection and diagnostic analysis of whether similarity arises from activations, errors, or their coupling; remains informative in activation-similarity-degenerate settings.

Conclusion: The method delivers scalable, informative transfer analysis for shared-vocabulary LLMs, enabling robust source selection and diagnostics about the activation–error coupling that governs cross-task transfer.

Abstract: Training-free source selection for LLM families with shared vocabularies arises in scientific string domains such as SMILES, protein, and genomic sequences, where candidate corpora share a tokenizer but differ in prediction targets. This creates an activation-dark regime: representation-similarity metrics can be uninformative without assumptions about label-conditioned error geometry, while classical update-geometry metrics are computationally prohibitive at vocabulary scale. We show that, in a shared-output head setting, representation metrics (e.g., CKA) are non-identifiable for transfer; models can share identical representations yet have orthogonal head updates. The key identity is that head Fisher alignment is exactly a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation-error space, exposing activation, error, and coupling factors rather than requiring a materialized Fisher matrix. FisherSketch estimates this cosine directly in a single streaming pass, making K=128,256 head Fisher alignment practical with a 16 KB task signature (m=4096) and a 192 KB per-task streaming state, small enough to store next to a model hash, but encoding transfer-relevant update structure. Beyond source selection, the same signatures and marginals provide a diagnostic instrument for studying whether LLM task similarity is driven by activations, errors, or their coupling; shared-parameter and internal-layer validations, together with Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments, show that FisherSketch remains informative when activation similarity cannot distinguish tasks.

</details>


### [252] [Effective Covariance Dynamics in Solvable High-Dimensional GANs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27246)
*Andrew Bond,Zafer Doğan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Solvable high-dimensional GAN training model with structured latent covariance extends previous diagonal latent assumptions to class-dependent, correlated, non-zero-mean latent structures. For a quadratic energy discriminator, heterogeneity is captured by an effective second moment. The training dynamics converge to ODEs in the high-dimensional limit. In the matched-covariance case, stability yields a mode-wise solvable interval dependent on learning rates and noise; learning begins when the leading effective eigenvalue crosses a lower threshold, and full recovery requires all relevant modes to stay within the interval. A signal-boosting mechanism arises from low-rank correlations. Numerical simulations and experiments on MNIST, FashionMNIST, and CIFAR-10 validate the theory and show that informed generator covariance improves alignment with data-driven reference subspaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond unconditional, diagonal latent covariances in solvable GAN analyses and understand how structured, correlated, and non-zero-mean latent factors affect training dynamics, stability, and learnability in high dimensions.

Method: Model a linear generator learning a low-dimensional subspace from data with structured latent covariance. Use a quadratic energy discriminator. Show that heterogeneity enters the training dynamics through a probability-weighted effective second moment. Prove that the stochastic microscopic training process converges, in the high-dimensional limit, to deterministic ODEs governed by the effective covariance. Perform a matched-covariance specialization, analyze stability to obtain a mode-wise solvable interval based on learning rates and noise. Provide a signal-boosting interpretation where low-rank correlations lift weak directions. Validate with numerical simulations and MNIST/FashionMNIST/CIFAR-10 experiments.

Result: Derivation of high-dimensional deterministic ODEs describing GAN training dynamics under structured latent covariance. Identification of a mode-wise solvable interval for the matched covariance case, governed by learning rates and noise. Demonstration of a signal-boosting mechanism where low-rank correlations enhance learnability, while excessive correlations can destabilize recovery. Numerical results align with the ODE and phase boundaries; experiments show that informed generator covariance improves alignment with data subspace.

Conclusion: Structured latent covariance fundamentally shapes GAN learning dynamics via an effective covariance. The work reveals phase-transition-like behavior with threshold-crossing for initiation and full recovery, offers practical guidance for designing generator covariance to boost learnability, and extends solvable GAN analyses to more realistic, heterogeneous latent structures.

Abstract: We study a solvable high-dimensional model of generative adversarial network (GAN) training in which a linear generator learns a low-dimensional subspace from data with structured latent covariance. Prior solvable GAN analyses assume unconditional signals with diagonal latent covariance; we extend the multi-feature discriminator setting to class-dependent, correlated, and non-zero-mean latent structure. For the quadratic energy discriminator, all such heterogeneity enters the dynamics through a probability-weighted effective second moment. We prove that the stochastic microscopic training process converges, in the high-dimensional limit, to deterministic ordinary differential equations governed by this effective covariance. In the matched-covariance specialization, the stability analysis yields a mode-wise solvable interval determined by the learning rates and noise level: learning begins when the leading effective eigenvalue crosses the lower threshold, while full recovery requires all relevant effective modes to remain within the interval. This reveals a signal-boosting mechanism: low-rank correlations can lift weak directions above the learnability threshold, whereas overly strong correlations destabilize recovery. Numerical simulations validate the ODE, phase boundary, and boosting mechanism. Experiments on MNIST, FashionMNIST, and CIFAR-10 further show that informed generator covariance improves alignment with the data-driven reference subspace.

</details>


### [253] [RSPC: A Benchmark for Modeling Stress and Psychiatric Conditions in Digitally Mediated Relationships using Psychiatrist Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27247)
*Parmitha Vangapandu,Sai Ganesh Mokkapati,Sathwik Narkedimilli,MSVPJ Sathvik,Timothy Liu,Simon See,Johannes C. Eichstaedt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Relational Stress and Psychiatry Corpus (RSPC) of 1,799 Reddit posts annotated by psychiatrists for mood disorders, relational triggers, and relationship phase, benchmarks seven fine-tuned transformers and five LLMs on disorder classification, trigger detection, and temporal phase prediction, and finds model- and task-dependent performance differences (Claude-3-Haiku best for disorder classification; GPT-4o best for trigger detection), with a noted link between anxiety and chronic relational uncertainty, promoting context-aware, relationship-informed mental health NLP benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond isolated, person-centric mental health modeling by incorporating interpersonal relational context, leveraging social media data to capture mental distress within relational dynamics, and establishing a benchmark for context-aware NLP tasks in mental health.

Method: Construct the Relational Stress and Psychiatry Corpus (RSPC) with 1,799 Reddit posts annotated by psychiatrists for diagnostic categories (mood disorders like anxiety and depression), relational stressor triggers, and relationship phase. Evaluate seven fine-tuned transformer models and five large language models on three tasks: multi-label disorder classification, relational trigger detection, and temporal phase prediction.

Result: Claude-3-Haiku achieves the best disorder classification Macro-F1 = 0.538; GPT-4o achieves the strongest relational trigger detection Macro-F1 = 0.519. Results show task-dependent differences across model families; discovery of strong associations between anxiety disorders and chronic relational uncertainty; the corpus provides a benchmark for relational-context NLP and supports context-aware mental health modeling that accounts for social and temporal dynamics.

Conclusion: The work shifts mental health modeling toward relationally informed, context-aware NLP. It provides a publicly valuable corpus (RSPC) and a multi-task benchmark showing distinct capabilities across model families, with implications for integrating social and temporal dynamics into mental health assessment.

Abstract: In NLP, mental health conditions are often modeled as isolated phenomena, without interpersonal context. We use Reddit posts about long-distance relationships to capture both mental health distress and associated relational triggers. We introduce the Relational Stress and Psychiatry Corpus (RSPC) containing 1,799 Reddit posts annotated by psychiatrists for diagnostic categories, including the most prevalent mood disorders (anxiety and depression), relational stressor triggers, and indications of relationship phase. We benchmark seven fine-tuned transformer models and five large language models across multi-label disorder classification, relational trigger detection, and temporal phase prediction tasks. We find clear task-dependent differences between model families, with Claude-3-Haiku achieving the best disorder classification performance (Macro-F1 = 0.538) and GPT-4o obtaining the strongest relational trigger detection performance (Macro-F1 = 0.519), suggesting distinct model capabilities. We further find strong associations between anxiety disorders and chronic relational uncertainty. Overall, RSPC establishes a benchmark for NLP tasks that consider relational context and supports a shift from individual-centric to context-aware mental health modeling that captures the social and temporal dynamics of distress.

</details>


### [254] [BetXplain: An Explanation-Annotated Dataset for Detecting Manipulative Betting Advertisements on Social Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27274)
*MSVPJ Sathvik,Parmitha Vangapadu,Nishit Rane,Sathwik Narkedimilli,Mark Lee,Akrati Saxena*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a new, manually annotated dataset of betting-related advertisements from Instagram and Reddit, labeled for manipulative/deceptive practices and including human explanations; analyzes persuasive tactics and discusses practical applications like warning plugins and regulatory crawlers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of publicly available annotated data for detecting manipulative betting ads; concerns about misleading users, risky behavior, and mental well-being; aims to enable explainable detection and regulatory monitoring.

Method: Data collection from two platforms (Instagram, Reddit). Manual annotation of ads for manipulative/deceptive practices. Annotations include labels and explanations. Additional analysis of persuasive strategies and potential mental health impacts. Proposes a framework enabling browser plugins and automated crawlers.

Result: Created and annotated dataset with classification labels and explainable rationale. Provided analysis of common strategies used in betting ads and their potential mental health effects. Proposed practical framework for warnings and regulatory monitoring.

Conclusion: The dataset fills a public-resource gap and supports explainable detection research; facilitates practical tools (warnings, crawlers) for protecting users and assisting regulators; sets stage for future improvements in detection and mental-health impact assessment.

Abstract: The promotion of betting applications on social media platforms has increased significantly in recent years. Many of these advertisements use persuasive techniques that may mislead users, encourage risky behavior, and potentially influence users' mental well-being. However, research on the automated detection of manipulative and deceptive betting advertisements remains limited due to the lack of publicly available annotated datasets. In this work, we introduce a new dataset of betting-related advertisements collected from two widely used social media platforms, Instagram and Reddit. The advertisements were manually annotated for manipulative and deceptive advertising practices. In addition to classification labels, the dataset includes human-provided explanations that describe the reasoning behind each annotation, enabling research into explainable approaches to detecting manipulative advertising. Furthermore, we analyze the strategies commonly used in betting advertisements and examine how these persuasive tactics may impact users' mental health. The proposed framework can also enable practical applications such as browser plugins that warn users about manipulative betting advertisements and automated web crawlers that help regulatory authorities monitor and detect such promotions online.

</details>


### [255] [How Good Can Linear Models Be for Time-Series Forecasting?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27282)
*Lang Huang,Jinglue Xu,Luke Darlow*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Ridge regression with carefully tuned preprocessing suffices to achieve competitive time-series forecasting, challenging the need for larger models; three robust patterns across eight benchmarks; improved performance over linear forecasters and often behind large deep models on six of eight datasets; hyperparameters serve as data diagnostics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the prevailing assumption that increasing model capacity is the primary route to improved accuracy in time-series forecasting; investigate whether preprocessing choices can close the gap.

Method: Eight standard benchmarks; search over context length (lookback), local normalization scope, regularization strength, and augmentation; Ridge regression with closed-form solution and interpretable weights; compare against Transformer, MLP, and CNN baselines; analyze patterns and cross-series sharing.

Result: Identified three robust patterns: (1) optimal lookback is highly series-specific and non-monotonic in forecast horizon, with fitted power-law exponents (e.g., +0.46 on ETTm2, −0.19 on Exchange/Traffic); (2) normalizing over a learned trailing fraction of the context is generally preferred over normalizing the entire context; (3) cross-series sharing of hyperparameters varies across datasets from fully shared to fully per-series; models beat prior linear forecasters on most dataset-horizon entries and surpass Transformer/MLP/CNN baselines on six of eight benchmarks; optimized hyperparameters also diagnose data structures.

Conclusion: Preprocessing and hyperparameter tuning can largely close the forecasting accuracy gap without scaling up models, offering interpretability and diagnostic value. Larger models may absorb structure that can be exposed through targeted preprocessing, suggesting practical guidance for model design and new avenues for data-driven diagnostics.

Abstract: Time-series forecasting research has been moving steadily toward larger architectures, from specialized transformers to general-purpose foundation models, on the assumption that capacity is what unlocks accuracy. We take the opposite position: most of the gap can be closed at far lower cost by tuning preprocessing rather than scaling models. We use Ridge regression as the testbed, since it has a closed-form solution and interpretable weights, which let the optimal hyperparameters be read off the search directly. We search over context length, local normalization, regularization, and augmentation on eight standard benchmarks and find three patterns. (1) Optimal lookback is strongly series-specific and often non-monotonic in forecast horizon, with fitted power-law exponents ranging from $+0.46$ on ETTm2 to $-0.19$ on Exchange and Traffic, challenging the convention that longer horizons need longer history. (2) Normalizing over a learned trailing fraction of the context, rather than its entirety, is almost universally preferred. (3) Series within the same dataset often disagree on hyperparameters; the optimal degree of cross-series sharing varies from fully shared to fully per-series. The resulting models beat prior linear forecasters on most dataset-horizon entries and exceed Transformer, MLP, and CNN baselines on six of eight benchmarks. The optimized hyperparameters also serve as a diagnostic on the data itself, revealing structures that larger models absorb silently into their learned parameters.

</details>


### [256] [Recovering Governing Equations from Solution Data: Identifiability Bounds for Linear and Nonlinear ODEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27285)
*Yang Pan,Helmut Bölcskei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a Hausdorff-distance-based framework to quantify identifiability of governing ODEs from solution data, deriving identifiability conditions, metric entropy, and sample complexity bounds across linear and Lipschitz nonlinear vector fields.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap in theory on when a ground-truth ODE can be uniquely and stably identified from multiple solution observations; no quantitative sample complexity analyses exist for learning ODEs from data.

Method: Define Hausdorff distance on solution sets as the metric for comparing differential equations; establish identifiability bounds for broad ODE classes (linear and Lipschitz/Hölder vector fields); derive metric entropy estimates for these classes; translate into sample complexity bounds.

Result: Identifiability bounds across broad ODE classes; metric entropy estimates for the relevant classes; derived sample complexity bounds indicating how many solution observations are needed to reliably recover the governing equation.

Conclusion: The Hausdorff-solution-set metric provides a minimax-friendly framework for ODE identification, enabling precise identifiability criteria and finite-sample guarantees across linear and nonlinear (Lipschitz/Hölder) dynamics.

Abstract: Learning governing equations from observed solution data is a fundamental challenge in scientific machine learning \cite{bruntonDiscoveringGoverningEquations2016,kovachkiNeuralOperatorLearning2023,longPDENetLearningPDEs2018,rudyDatadrivenDiscoveryPartial2017,raonicConvolutionalNeuralOperators2023}, yet the theoretical conditions under which a ground-truth ODE can be uniquely and stably identified from multiple solution observations remain largely undeveloped, and no quantitative analysis of the sample complexity of such learning tasks exists in the literature. To address this gap, we introduce the Hausdorff distance on solution sets as the natural metric for comparing differential equations, since it captures the worst-case separation between two equations over all admissible initial conditions and thus encodes the minimax structure of the identification problem. We establish identifiability bounds for governing ODEs across a wide class of structure equations--ranging from linear ODEs to nonlinear classes with Lipschitz (Hölder)-continuous vector fields--characterizing precisely when two distinct equations can be distinguished from solution data. Using this metric, we derive metric entropy estimates for the relevant ODE classes and analyze sample complexity bounds, quantifying how many solution observations are needed to reliably recover the governing equation.

</details>


### [257] [Designing Reward Signals for Portable Query Generation: A Case Study in Industrial Semantic Job Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27291)
*Ping Liu,Qianqi Shen,Jianqiang Shen,Wenqiong Liu,Rajat Arora,Yunxiang Ren,Chunnan Yao,Dan Xu,Baofen Zheng,Wanjun Jiang,Andrii Soviak,Kevin Kao,Jingwei Wu,Wenjing Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: End-to-end RLAIF framework to generate portable job-search queries that abstract seeker identifiers while preserving qualifications; reward design dominates optimization performance; critic-free methods resist reward hacking except GRPO, which is sensitive to spurious rewards; adding a deterministic reward floor mitigates verbatim copying; training-time reward model inflates gains by 2.4x, highlighting the primacy of reward-shaping discipline over optimizer choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mismatch between low-bandwidth query interfaces and high-dimensional candidate profiles, by producing portable queries that generalize across seekers while preserving qualifications.

Method: Develop an end-to-end RLAIF pipeline to optimize policies for portable query generation, analyze optimization mechanics vs reward engineering, compare critic-free baselines (RLOO, REINFORCE++) and GRPO, implement a deterministic reward floor to reduce verbatim copying, assess impact through cross-family evaluation and training-time reward modeling.

Result: For critic-free optimizers, performance is driven by reward shaping; GRPO is particularly sensitive to spurious rewards; introducing a deterministic rule-based reward floor yields +0.147 quality on cross-family judge; training-time reward model inflates gains by 2.4x.

Conclusion: Reward-shaping discipline is the primary driver of success in this setup; optimizer choice is secondary when rewards are robustly designed and validated against reward hacking.

Abstract: Job-search platforms rely on low-bandwidth query interfaces that often fail to capture the high-dimensional complexity of candidate profiles. We present an end-to-end RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback) framework to generate \emph{portable} job search queries, terms that abstract away seeker-specific identifiers while preserving generalizable qualifications. This task introduces a highly adversarial reward surface where policy optimization frequently exploits flaws in LLM-as-judge rubrics, resulting in degenerate verbatim-copying behaviors.
  We conducted comprehensive empirical experiments to isolate the impact of optimization mechanics against structured reward engineering. Our results demonstrate that for critic-free optimizers, performance is overwhelmingly dictated by robust reward shaping, rendering the specific choice of algorithm largely immaterial. While critic-free per-rollout baseline methods (RLOO and REINFORCE++) natively resist reward-hacking, the group-relative advantage normalization in GRPO appears uniquely sensitive to spurious reward signals, making it disproportionately susceptible to exploitation. We show that introducing a deterministic, rule-based reward floor to correct for rewards assigned to verbatim copying mitigates this failure mode, resulting in a substantial $+0.147$ quality improvement on a cross-family evaluation judge. Ultimately, we show that the training-time reward model inflates performance gains by $2.4\times$, confirming that the training success is fundamentally dependent on enforcing reward-shaping disciplines rather than selecting alternative optimizers.

</details>


### [258] [A Multi-Fidelity Convolutional Autoencoder-Transfer Learning Framework for Guided-Wave-Based Damage Diagnosis Using Large Simulated and Limited Experimental Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27304)
*Santosh Kapuria,Abhishek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multifidelity transfer learning framework using CAE-based deep features and limited experimental data achieves accurate damage localisation and sizing in plate-like structures with guided waves, outperforming CNNs, aided by cheap 1D physics-based simulations for pretraining and good generalization to unseen data (R^2: localisation >0.93, sizing >0.99).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limited labelled experimental data and high cost of large-scale high-fidelity simulations hinder practical deployment of deep learning in guided wave–based SHM. A computationally efficient approach that leverages physics-based simulations and transfer learning is sought.

Method: Use a lightweight 1D time-domain spectral element model to generate a large synthetic dataset for pretraining. Employ convolutional autoencoder (CAE)–based deep feature learning, followed by a feed-forward neural network. Apply transfer learning to adapt to the experimental domain using only a small amount of labelled data, comparing CAE-based framework to CNN-based approaches.

Result: CAE-based transfer learning significantly outperforms CNN-based counterparts in damage localisation accuracy. Achieves R^2 > 0.93 for localisation and >0.99 for sizing. Demonstrates strong generalisation to previously unseen data, including damage scenarios not present during pretraining or fine-tuning.

Conclusion: The proposed CAE-based multifidelity transfer learning framework is accurate, computationally efficient, and practically viable for real-world GWSHM applications.

Abstract: Guided wave-based structural health monitoring (GWSHM) with onboard transducers offers significant potential for the early diagnosis of damage in engineering structures. However, the practical deployment of deep learning models is often hindered by the limited availability of labelled experimental data and the high computational cost of generating large-scale high-fidelity simulation datasets. This study presents a multifidelity transfer learning framework that integrates lightweight physics-based simulations, convolutional autoencoder (CAE)-based deep feature learning, a feed-forward neural network, and limited experimental measurements for accurate damage localisation and sizing in plate-like structures instrumented with piezoelectric transducers. A computationally efficient one-dimensional time-domain spectral element model is employed to generate a large synthetic dataset for pretraining, while transfer learning adapts the model to experimental domains using only a small amount of labelled data. The CAE-based transfer learning framework significantly outperforms its CNN-based counterpart in damage localisation accuracy. The model achieves excellent predictive performance with $R^2$ scores exceeding 0.93 for damage localisation and 0.99 for damage sizing. Its generalisation capability is demonstrated on previously unseen data, showing high prediction accuracy for damage scenarios not represented during pretraining or fine-tuning. The results establish the proposed framework as an accurate, computationally efficient, and practically viable solution for real-world GWSHM applications.

</details>


### [259] [Blackwell Approachability and Gradient Equilibrium are Equivalent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27315)
*Brian W. Lee,Nika Haghtalab,Michael I. Jordan,Ryan J. Tibshirani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GEQ is algorithmically equivalent to Blackwell approachability, and hence to regret minimization and calibration, via efficient GEQ–oracle reductions. This unifies GEQ with core online-learning frameworks and enables transfer of refined guarantees (e.g., optimism, adaptivity) across them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify how Gradient Equilibrium (GEQ) fits into the online learning landscape by locating precise formal connections to Blackwell approachability, regret minimization, and calibration, and to enable cross-framework guarantees.

Method: Construct algorithmic reductions between GEQ and Blackwell approachability, and show that each can be solved using access to the other’s oracle with no additional asymptotic error. Derive equivalences to regret minimization and calibration through standard reachability/approachability reductions; identify necessary/sufficient conditions for GEQ; compare unconstrained vs constrained decision sets and establish reductions among GEQ variants.

Result: 1) GEQ is equivalent to Blackwell approachability in the algorithmic sense; 2) GEQ and Blackwell approachability can simulate each other without increasing oracle error; 3) GEQ is equivalent to regret minimization and calibration via known equivalences; 4) provides efficient reductions to transfer guarantees like optimism and strong adaptivity; 5) characterizes necessary/sufficient conditions for GEQ and relates unconstrained/constrained GEQ variants.

Conclusion: GEQ occupies a central position in online learning, equivalent to core frameworks (approachability, regret, calibration); the paper provides practical, efficient reductions enabling cross-framework guarantees and clarifies conditions under which GEQ operates.

Abstract: Gradient equilibrium (GEQ) is a recently introduced online optimization framework that generalizes first-order stationarity from offline optimization and abstracts problems like online conformal prediction. While GEQ has curious similarities with known online learning frameworks, namely regret minimization, prior work has shown that GEQ error and regret are incomparable objectives, leaving open a precise understanding of how GEQ fits into the broader online learning landscape. In this work, we show that GEQ is equivalent to Blackwell approachability in the algorithmic sense. That is, a Blackwell approachability problem can always be solved using queries to a black-box GEQ oracle, with no asymptotic loss in the oracle's error rate, and vice versa. Taken together with known equivalences between approachability, regret minimization, and calibration, these results imply that GEQ is equivalent to these frameworks, as well. Our reductions are efficient and can be used to transfer refined guarantees, such as optimism and strong adaptivity, from regret minimization to GEQ. Along the way, we also identify necessary and sufficient conditions for GEQ, and establish reductions between different notions of GEQ with unconstrained and constrained decision sets.

</details>


### [260] [Beyond the Hard Budget: Sparsity Regularizers for More Interpretable Top-k Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27321)
*Nathanaël Jacquier,Maria Vakalopoulou,Mahdi S. Hosseini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hard sparsity (Top-k SAE) combined with two soft sparsity regularizers improves monosemanticity without hurting reconstruction; regularizers operate on batch-active units before Top-k and make representations more compact and robust to the budget k.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of Top-k SAE (fixed budget k, overfitting to k, lack of explicit sparsity regularization) by introducing compatible sparsity regularizers that enhance interpretability while preserving reconstruction across vision foundation models and datasets.

Method: Introduce two regularizers applied to pre-Top-k activations and restricted to batch-active units: (1) an l1 penalty on off-support (unselected) units, and (2) a scale-invariant l1/l2 ratio penalty that concentrates information into fewer active latents. Apply to the batch-active subset and evaluate across two datasets, three vision models, and varying k; assess monosemanticity, reconstruction quality, robustness to k, and linear probing performance.

Result: Both regularizers consistently improve monosemanticity with no loss in reconstruction quality. The l1/l2 ratio penalty yields more compact latent usage, enhancing robustness to inference-time k and improving small-budget linear probing.

Conclusion: Hard architectural sparsity and soft sparsity regularization are complementary; combining Top-k sparsity with these regularizers yields more interpretable, monosemantic representations without sacrificing reconstruction.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become a leading tool for interpreting the representations of vision foundation models, decomposing their polysemantic activations into a larger set of sparse, more monosemantic features. The Top-$k$ SAE, a now-standard variant, enforces sparsity architecturally through its activation function, retaining only the $k$ most active latents per input. Because it was designed precisely to avoid the $\ell_1$ penalty used by earlier SAEs and its known drawbacks, it has not been combined with an explicit sparsity regularizer, despite retaining limitations of its own, such as a budget $k$ that is fixed regardless of input complexity and a tendency to overfit to the training value of $k$. We introduce two sparsity regularizers compatible with the Top-$k$ architecture, both acting on the activations before the Top-$k$ selection: an $\ell_1$ penalty on the unselected (off-support) units, and a scale-invariant $\ell_1/\ell_2$-ratio penalty that concentrates the code onto fewer effective units. Both penalties are applied only to the batch-active units, those selected by the Top-$k$ operator at least once within the batch. Across two datasets, three vision foundation models, and a range of $k$, both regularizers consistently improve monosemanticity at no cost to reconstruction quality. The $\ell_1/\ell_2$ penalty further concentrates information into fewer latents, making reconstruction more robust to the inference-time choice of $k$ and improving small-budget linear probing. Our central finding is that hard architectural sparsity and soft sparsity regularization are complementary rather than mutually exclusive.

</details>


### [261] [Error-Conditioned Neural Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27354)
*Haina Jiang,Liam Wang,Peng-Chen Chen,Min Seop Kwak,Seungryong Kim,Brian Bell,Jeong Joon Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Error-conditioned Neural Solvers (ENS) for PDEs; residual field input guides iterative corrections, achieving higher accuracy and robustness than residual-minimizing hybrids, with up to 10x gains and better generalization, at lower compute cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of purely data-driven PDE surrogates that struggle with constraint violations and extrapolation; residual-minimizing hybrids are costly and unstable in ill-conditioned systems; need a method that uses residual structure to inform updates without explicit optimization.

Method: Propose ENS: feed PDE residual field as input to the network at each iteration; learn an update policy to iteratively correct predictions; no explicit objective to minimize residual; evaluated across four PDE families; compare to standard surrogates and gradient-descent/Gauss-Newton hybrids; test under distribution shift (zero-shot parameter changes and cross-equation transfer).

Result: ENS achieves the highest prediction accuracy in most settings; gains up to 10x on turbulent Kolmogorov flow; avoids the compute costs of hybrid optimization; learned policy generalizes under distribution shift and is most advantageous in ill-conditioned regimes where residual minimization is unreliable.

Conclusion: Error-conditioned neural solvers offer accurate, robust PDE solving with efficient compute, by using residual fields to guide iterative corrections, with strong generalization across tasks and conditions.

Abstract: Neural surrogate models offer fast approximate mappings from PDE parameters to solutions, but they typically treat solving as a purely statistical task: once trained, they struggle to correct their own constraint violations and extrapolate beyond the training distribution. Recent hybrid methods promote physical correctness by targeting the PDE residual via gradient descent or Gauss--Newton steps, but inherit the compute cost and instability of the underlying classical optimizers. We show, theoretically and empirically, that numerically minimizing the PDE residual can be an unreliable proxy for reconstruction accuracy in ill-conditioned systems, explaining why these methods often do not make accurate predictions despite achieving low residuals. We propose error-conditioned Neural Solvers (ENS), built on a different principle: rather than an optimization target, the PDE residual field is passed as a direct input to the network at each iteration, enabling it to read the spatial structure of its own errors and learn an update policy to iteratively correct its predictions. Across four PDE families, ENS attains the highest prediction accuracy in the large majority of settings, with gains reaching $10\times$ on turbulent Kolmogorov flow, while avoiding the expensive compute cost of hybrid methods. ENS's learned correction policy generalizes under distribution shift, including zero-shot parameter changes and cross-equation transfer, where its relative advantage is largest in the ill-conditioned regimes where residual minimization is least reliable. Project website: https://neuralsolver.github.io/.

</details>


### [262] [Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27361)
*Danyal Rehman,Charlie B. Tan,Yoshua Bengio,Avishek Joey Bose,Alexander Tong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators (ArBG) replace flow-based BGs with autoregressive models to improve expressivity and scalability, enabling sequential inference-time interventions. They demonstrate strong performance on peptide systems and introduce Robin (132M params) achieving major energy-error gains; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient Boltzmann sampling of molecular systems at equilibrium is limited by normalizing flows, which face expressivity or likelihood-computation constraints. An autoregressive framework offers a path to greater expressivity and flexible, sequential inference while maintaining Boltzmann-correct sampling.

Method: Introduce Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators (ArBG) that abandon flow-based invertible mappings in favor of autoregressive conditional modeling. Leverage architectures akin to Large Language Models to enable scalable, sequential sampling and inference-time interventions; incorporate exact likelihoods and importance-sampling corrections as in Boltzmann Generators. Demonstrate on peptide benchmarks up to 10-residue Chignolin and train a 132M-parameter transferable model named Robin.

Result: ArBG achieves significant performance gains over flow-based BGs across benchmarks, with pronounced improvements on larger peptides (e.g., 10-residue Chignolin). Robin (132M parameters) surpasses previous state-of-the-art, reducing zero-shot energy error E-W2 on 8-residue systems by over 60%. The authors provide a code release.

Conclusion: Autoregressive BGs offer a viable alternative to flow-based BGs, removing topological constraints and enabling scalable, transfer-ready models for equilibrium sampling. The results suggest substantial gains for larger molecular systems, with Robin serving as a strong transferable baseline. Future work likely includes extending to longer proteins and refining sequential inference strategies.

Abstract: Efficient sampling of molecular systems at thermodynamic equilibrium is a hallmark challenge in statistical physics. This challenge has driven the development of Boltzmann Generators (BGs), which allow rapid generation of uncorrelated equilibrium samples by combining a generative model with exact likelihoods and an importance sampling correction. However, modern BGs predominantly rely on normalizing flows (NFs), which either suffer from limited expressivity due to strict invertibility constraints (discrete time) or computationally expensive likelihoods (continuous time). In this paper, we propose Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators (ArBG) -- a novel autoregressive modelling framework -- that overcomes these limitations by departing from the flow-based BG paradigm. ArBG circumvents the topological constraints of flows and enables sequential inference-time interventions, while offering enhanced scalability by leveraging architectures effective in Large Language Models. We empirically demonstrate that ArBG leads to significant improvements over flow-based models across all benchmarks, but particularly in larger peptide systems such as the 10-residue Chignolin. Furthermore, we introduce Robin, a 132 million parameter transferable model trained with the ArBG framework which improves over the previous state-of-the-art, reducing the zero-shot energy error, E-W$_2$, on 8-residue systems by over 60$\%$. The code can be found at the following link: https://github.com/danyalrehman/autobg.

</details>


### [263] [Reinforcement Learning without Ground-Truth Solutions can Improve LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27369)
*Yingyu Lin,Qiyue Gao,Nikki Lijing Kuang,Xunpeng Huang,Kun Zhou,Tongtong Liang,Zhewei Yao,Yi-An Ma,Yuxiong He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RiVER is a ranking-based, ground-truth-free RL framework that uses calibrated, instance-wise reward shaping to enable score-based optimization for LLM coding tasks; it mitigates scale and frequency issues in group-relative RL and yields transfer gains to exact-solution benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To train LLMs for coding tasks without relying on ground-truth solutions by leveraging continuous execution feedback as supervision, addressing the limitations of reward signals in score-based, RL settings.

Method: Use deterministic execution feedback as continuous-valued rewards; apply group-relative RL on continuous rewards; identify scale dominance and frequency dominance; introduce calibrated reward shaping with instance-wise comparisons that emphasize top-ranked solvers while bounding feedback for other valid solutions; train on 12 AtCoder Heuristic Contest tasks; evaluate on ALE-Bench, LiveCodeBench, and USACO; test on Qwen3-8B and GLM-Z1-9B-0414.

Result: RiVER yields substantial gains: ALE rating rank improvements of 8.9% (Qwen3-8B) and 9.4% (GLM-Z1-9B-0414); improvements on LiveCodeBench and USACO by 2.4% and 3.5% in absolute terms; baselines trained with raw scores improve ALE but fail to transfer to exact-solution benchmarks.

Conclusion: Score-based optimization tasks with calibrated reward shaping can train general coding ability without ground-truth solutions and transfer to both score-based and exact-solution benchmarks; RiVER demonstrates effective training for coding abilities across large LLMs.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for training LLMs typically rely on ground-truth answers to assign rewards, limiting their applicability to tasks where the ground-truth solution is unknown. We introduce a \textbf{R}anking-\textbf{i}nduced \textbf{VER}ifiable framework (RiVER) that trains LLMs on score-based optimization tasks without ground-truth solutions, using deterministic execution feedback as continuous-valued supervision. When applying group-relative RL to such continuous rewards, we identify two key challenges: \emph{scale dominance}, where uncalibrated score magnitudes across test instances distort policy updates, and \emph{frequency dominance}, where repeatedly sampled suboptimal solutions can outweigh rare but stronger candidates. RiVER addresses these challenges with calibrated reward shaping that uses instance-wise comparisons and emphasizes top-ranked solvers while retaining bounded feedback for other valid solutions. We train on 12 AtCoder Heuristic Contest tasks and evaluate on Algorithm Engineering Benchmark (ALE-Bench), LiveCodeBench, and USACO. RiVER advances Qwen3-8B and GLM-Z1-9B-0414 by 8.9\% and 9.4\% in ALE rating rank. More importantly, despite training exclusively on score-based tasks without any ground-truth solutions, RiVER also improves the backbones across exact-solution benchmarks such as LiveCodeBench and USACO by an absolute average improvement of 2.4\% and 3.5\%. By contrast, baselines trained with raw execution scores improve ALE rating but fail to transfer to exact-solution benchmarks. These results suggest that score-based optimization tasks, combined with proper reward calibration, can serve as effective training environments for general coding ability without ground-truth solutions.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [264] [A unified cell-merge algorithm for generating diverse Voronoi diagrams and new tessellations based on spatial chromatic model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27235)
*Weining Zhu*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Introduces a cell-merge Voronoi generation algorithm based on a spatial chromatic model that retrieves and merges cells from a unified database to produce diverse Voronoi diagrams and novel tessellations without designing separate algorithms for each VD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VD types differ by space, entities, distance metrics, and relationships; there is a need for rapid generation of multiple VD variants and new tessellations in location-based analysis, reducing development effort.

Method: Proposes a cell-merge (CM) Voronoi generation algorithm grounded in a spatial chromatic model; central idea is to retrieve and merge cells from a unified database to realize various VD types, avoiding bespoke VD-specific algorithms; enables generation of new tessellations such as competition intensity and couple-cell diagrams.

Result: Qualitative demonstration that the CM framework can quickly generate diverse VD types and new tessellations; applicable to location-based spatial analysis.

Conclusion: The CM approach provides a unified, efficient framework for generating multiple VD types and novel tessellations, reducing algorithmic development effort and broadening applicability in spatial analysis.

Abstract: As a type of spatial tessellation model and an important spatial structure of computational geometry, Voronoi diagrams (VDs) are widely used in many fields. Due to differences in generation spaces, types of spatial entities, distance metrics, and relationships between entities and Voronoi regions, Voronoi diagrams vary into many types, such as the ordinary VD, VD on spheres, VD for linear entities, weighted VD, and ordered higher-order VD. These VDs also have their own generation algorithms. In this study, we propose a new cell-merge (CM) Voronoi generation algorithm based on the spatial chromatic model. The advantage of the CM algorithm is that it can quickly generate diverse VDs by retrieving and merging cells from a unified database, without requiring the development of specific algorithms for each VD. The CM Voronoi algorithm can be particularly applied in cases where a variety of Voronoi diagrams are frequently required for computation and analysis, such as in location-based spatial analysis. Furthermore, the proposed CM method can also generate some new types of spatial tessellations, such as competition intensity and couple-cell diagrams which are different from classical VDs.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [265] [Unsupervised Memory-Enhanced Video Transformers: Obstacle Detection for Autonomous Agricultural Rover](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26151)
*Théo Biardeau,Anne-Sophie Capelle-Laizé,Salwan Alwan,David Helbert*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A fully unsupervised video anomaly detector using a memory-augmented transformer (Video Memory Transformers for Anomaly Detection, VMTAD) to enable real-time obstacle detection in dynamic agricultural scenes, addressing under-canopy obstacles and rover motion; achieves high AUROC and a fast lightweight variant for safe stopping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional safety sensors miss under-canopy obstacles; supervised methods lack generalization to unseen obstacles; unsupervised anomaly detection struggles with dynamic scenes; aim to enable robust, label-free safety in precision farming rover operations.

Method: Proposes Video Memory Transformers for Anomaly Detection (VMTAD) with a transformer-based architecture and a dedicated memory module that consumes encoded representations from prior frames to capture temporal context; trained only on normal operation; includes a lightweight variant for real-time deployment.

Result: On Grillion rover with rapeseed dataset, achieves AUROC of 0.973 for obstacle detection and 0.997 for segmentation; real-time inference at 14 ms; demonstrates safety-critical viability via stopping distance analysis; state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: VMTAD shows that memory-augmented transformers can address dynamic environments for unsupervised anomaly detection in robotics, delivering accurate detection with real-time performance and label-free training; memory component crucial to handling rover motion.

Abstract: While autonomous rovers have become indispensable to precision farming, achieving consistent operational safety remains a critical challenge. Conventional safety sensors, such as LiDAR, fail to detect obstacles positioned below the plant canopy, posing a significant risk. While camera-based supervised learning methods can detect common objects, they perform poorly when faced with obstacles that were not present in their training data. Actual unsupervised anomaly detection offers a solution by learning the normal visual patterns of an environment, but often fails for the dynamic scenes captured by a moving rover.\\ This paper introduces Video Memory Transformers for Anomaly Detection (VMTAD), a fully unsupervised method designed for real-time obstacle detection in dynamic agricultural scenes. VMTAD utilizes a transformer-driven architecture augmented with a dedicated memory module. This memory module leverages temporal context by processing encoded representations of preceding frames. This approach enables the system to effectively address the dynamic context caused by the robot's movement. The model is trained using only images that represent normal operation, requiring no data labels.\\ VMTAD was rigorously evaluated on the 'Grillion' agricultural rover. On a challenging rapeseed dataset, VMTAD achieved state-of-the-art performance, reaching a 0.973 detection and 0.997 segmentation Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve. A lightweight variant provides an optimal balance of high accuracy and real-time inference (14 ms), which is critical for safety, as confirmed by our analysis of the rover's total stopping distance.

</details>


### [266] [Reinforcement Learning Enables Autonomous Microrobot Navigation and Intervention in Simulated Blood Capillaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26154)
*Jannik Drotleff,Samuel Tovey,Paul Hohenberger,Christoph Lohrmann,Julian Hoßbach,Konstantin Nikolaou,Christian Holm*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Physically grounded RL in a realistic capillary network enables universal navigation strategies and flow-control interventions, showing RL can train microrobots for in vivo-like environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in RL studies for microrobotic navigation by moving beyond idealized geometries to realistic hydrodynamics, RBC dynamics, and branching capillary geometry, enabling robust autonomous control for medical interventions.

Method: Develop a physics-based simulation of a blood capillary network with explicit hydrodynamic flow, red blood cell dynamics, and anatomically derived branching. Train deep RL agents to navigate via chemotaxis, systematically map navigation limits across robot size/speed, identify regimes where Brownian motion and flow dominate propulsion, and analyze emergent strategies (e.g., run-and-rotate, search-and-sit). Evaluate transfer: agents perform targeted capillary blocking/unblocking without retraining.

Result: Agents learn universal navigation strategies that are robust across robot parameters. They can perform targeted blocking and unblocking of capillary flow, restoring throughput to baseline without retraining. Demonstrates RL as a viable framework for autonomous microrobotic interventions in complex biological environments.

Conclusion: RL can yield generalizable, parameter-insensitive strategies for microrobotic navigation in realistic vasculature, enabling targeted therapeutic interventions and robust control under hydrodynamic-rich, obstacle-dense conditions.

Abstract: Autonomous microrobots navigating biological vasculature could enable targeted drug delivery and thrombolysis, yet training control policies for realistic environments remains an open challenge. Prior reinforcement learning (RL) studies of microrobotic navigation have been limited to idealized geometries that omit complex hydrodynamic flow fields, confined branching structures, and dense cellular obstacles found in vivo. Here, we develop a physically grounded simulation of a blood capillary network, incorporating realistic hydrodynamic flow fields, explicit red blood cell dynamics, and anatomically derived branching geometry, and train deep RL agents to navigate it via chemotaxis. We systematically map the physical limits of navigation across robot size and swimming speed, revealing a forbidden regime where Brownian motion and flow overcome propulsion. Successful agents independently discover multiple universal strategy types, including run-and-rotate and energy-efficient search-and-sit policies, regardless of robot parameters. Without retraining, these agents perform targeted blocking and unblocking of capillary flow, restoring throughput to healthy baseline levels. These results establish RL as a viable framework for developing autonomous microrobotic intervention strategies in complex biological environments.

</details>


### [267] [RMTL: Reinforced Micro-task Learning for Long-Horizon Manipulation with VLM Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26175)
*Anıl Can Ateş,Orhan Kahraman,Cihan Topal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RMTL decomposes long-horizon robotic manipulation into a set of micro-tasks described by language prompts, using multi-view VLM rewards per active micro-task, with a reverse curriculum and a hierarchical manager to switch tasks; evaluated on Fetch manipulation, showing faster learning and more informative rewards than single-prompt VLM rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single global VLM prompts for long-horizon tasks yield coarse, near-flat rewards and fail under randomized initializations; need structured, informative signals and robustness to occlusions to guide learning.

Method: Decompose the task into a small set of language-described micro-tasks; at each step, compute a VLM reward using the current micro-task prompt, averaged over multiple camera views; apply a reverse curriculum to expose harder initial conditions; begin with a rule-based micro-task selection in PPO, then replace with a learned hierarchical manager that selects micro-tasks. Implemented on Fetch manipulation with three short prompts and no additional prompt tuning.

Result: RMTL yields more informative reward signals than a single global VLM prompt, enabling faster learning; demonstrates improved scalability of language-guided RL for robotic manipulation.

Conclusion: Decomposing VLM rewards into micro-task-specific prompts can substantially improve the effectiveness and scalability of language-guided reinforcement learning for complex robotic manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for robotic manipulation often requires manually designing a dense reward function, which is difficult to tune and often fragile, or learning a reward from human demonstrations or preferences, which can be expensive. A recent line of work uses pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) as zero-shot reward models, replacing these costs with a single text prompt. However, we argue that a single global prompt is too coarse for long-horizon manipulation tasks with randomized initial conditions. The single-prompt VLM reward is near-flat for much of the trajectory, making early progress hard for the agent to detect. We propose Reinforced Micro-Task Learning (RMTL), an approach that decomposes a manipulation task into a small set of language-described micro-tasks and trains the agent to switch between them. At each step, the agent receives a multi-view VLM reward computed using the prompt of the currently active micro-task and averaged across multiple camera views to reduce the effect of view-specific occlusions. A reverse curriculum gradually exposes the agent to harder initial conditions, while a PPO worker is first trained with a fixed distance-based rule that selects the active micro-task. We then replace this rule with a learned hierarchical manager, turning rule-based phase selection into a fully learned hierarchical policy. We instantiate RMTL on the Fetch manipulation environment using three short stage-specific prompts and without additional prompt tuning. Experiments show that RMTL provides more informative reward signals than single-prompt VLM rewards, enabling faster learning. These results suggest that decomposing VLM rewards into micro-task-specific language prompts can substantially improve the scalability of language-guided reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [268] [LiMoDE: Rethinking Lifelong Robot Manipulation from a Mixture-of-Dynamic-Experts Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26183)
*Zhihao Gu,Lin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LiMoDE introduces a two-stage lifelong mixture of dynamic experts for robot manipulation: a pre-training stage with a dynamic MoE to capture varied short-term skills, and an adaptation stage that learns lifelong experts and fuses them with frozen ones to enable knowledge transfer for new tasks, achieving strong lifelong performance with modest parameter and compute costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting and the inability of single-task or shallow fine-tuning methods to extract reusable skills and model interactions among skills in lifelong robot manipulation; existing prompts/pattern-based approaches are insufficient from an architectural perspective.

Method: Stage 1: multi-task pre-training with a dynamic Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) where a variable number of heterogeneous experts are activated based on motion cues to cover different short-term manipulations. Stage 2: lifelong MoE adaptation mechanism that learns lifelong experts and dynamically combines them with frozen ones for new tasks to facilitate knowledge transfer during adaptation.

Result: Empirical evaluation on simulated lifelong learning benchmarks and real-world tasks shows LiMoDE achieves superior performance and robust lifelong adaptation, with moderate additional trainable parameters and inference overhead.

Conclusion: An architectural two-stage framework (LiMoDE) effectively leverages dynamic experts for lifelong robot manipulation, enabling knowledge reuse and transfer with limited extra cost, and offering a viable path beyond prompt-based or parameter-efficient fine-tuning approaches.

Abstract: Building a generalist robot that can leverage prior knowledge for continuous task adaptation remains a significant challenge. Previous works alleviate the catastrophic forgetting problem by parameter-efficient fine-tuning for single-task adaptation. However, they fail to extract reusable skills and model the interaction with other skills effectively. Recent works try to address these issues by learning prompts. Differently, this paper presents an architectural perspective on the Lifelong Mixture of Dynamic Experts (\textit{LiMoDE}), a novel two-stage learning scheme for lifelong robot manipulation. Specifically, a dynamic MoE structure is first proposed in the multi-task pre-training stage to learn prior knowledge, where a varied number of heterogeneous experts are activated based on the motion information to address different short-term manipulations. Subsequently, in the task adaptation stage, we design a lifelong MoE adaptation mechanism % (LiMoEAM) that learns lifelong experts and dynamically combines them with frozen ones for new tasks, facilitating the knowledge transfer during adaptation. The proposed \textit{LiMoDE} is evaluated on both the simulated lifelong learning benchmark and real-world tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate its effectiveness in achieving superior performance and strong lifelong adaptation by introducing a moderate number of additional trainable parameters and inference overhead.

</details>


### [269] [Morphology-Specific Closed-Loop Control of Logarithmic-Spiral Continuum Arms via Online Jacobian Error Compensation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26188)
*Partha Datta,Yi Jin,Wei Lin,C. Chase Cao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A morphology-specific closed-loop task-space controller for logarithmic-spiral continuum arms using a segmented tendon-driven model; analytic Jacobian with online Broyden error compensation and Kalman-filter estimation enables robust, accurate control in simulations and for morphology-enabled manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Logarithmic-spiral morphology offers scalable, wrap-capable continuum manipulators; prior work lacked inverse kinematics and closed-loop control, limiting precision and robustness.

Method:  MuJoCo-based segmented tendon-driven model with a centerline backbone and equilateral tendon routing; derive an analytic task-space Jacobian from logarithmic-spiral kinematics; online Jacobian error compensation via Broyden secant update and Kalman-filter estimation; planar and spatial simulations including trajectory tracking, attitude regulation, disturbance rejection, 3D position/orientation control; comparison with a piecewise-constant-curvature baseline; applications to obstacle-assisted reach-wrap-release, adaptive whole-arm grasping, and multi-arm object handling.

Result: Controller reduces tracking errors, suppresses attitude drift, and maintains a bounded Jacobian estimation error relative to PCC; successful validation across planar/spatial scenarios and morphology-enabled tasks; demonstrates accurate, robust, scalable control of highly underactuated continuum manipulators.

Conclusion: Provides a physics-grounded baseline for hardware implementation and learning-augmented soft robotic control; morphology-specific closed-loop control with online Jacobian compensation enables reliable manipulation of logarithmic-spiral continuum arms and supports future hardware realization.

Abstract: Logarithmic spirals are ubiquitous in biological appendages and provide an attractive morphology for continuum manipulators capable of reaching, wrapping, and grasping. Recently reported logarithmic-spiral robots demonstrated scalable fabrication and versatile grasping but lacked inverse kinematics and closed-loop control. This work presents the first morphology-specific closed-loop task-space control framework for logarithmic-spiral continuum arms. A segmented tendon-driven model with a centerline backbone and equilateral tendon routing is developed in MuJoCo to capture tapered compliance and contact dynamics. An analytical task-space Jacobian is derived directly from the logarithmic-spiral kinematics and combined with online Jacobian error compensation using a Broyden secant update and Kalman-filter estimation. The resulting controller continuously corrects modeling errors arising from nonlinear deformation, contact, and geometric mismatch. The framework is validated through planar and spatial simulations, including trajectory tracking, attitude regulation, disturbance rejection, three-dimensional position tracking, and simultaneous position-orientation control. Compared with a piecewise-constant-curvature (PCC) baseline, the proposed method consistently reduces tracking errors, suppresses attitude drift, and maintains a bounded Jacobian estimation error. The controller is further applied to morphology-enabled manipulation tasks, including obstacle-assisted reach-wrap-release motions, adaptive whole-arm grasping, and cooperative multi-arm object handling. Results demonstrate that combining logarithmic-spiral morphology with online Jacobian compensation enables accurate, robust, and scalable control of highly underactuated continuum manipulators. The proposed framework establishes a physics-grounded baseline for future hardware implementation and learning-augmented soft robotic control.

</details>


### [270] [OmniContact: Chaining Meta-Skills via Contact Flow for Generalizable Humanoid Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26201)
*Runyi Yu,Xiaoyi Lin,Ji Ma,Yinhuai Wang,Koukou Luo,Jiahao Ji,Huayi Wang,Wenjia Wang,Runhan Zhang,Ping Tan,Ting Wu,Ruoli Dai,Qifeng Chen,Lei Han*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a hierarchical contact-flow representation for long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation, with a low-level CF-Track policy and a high-level CF-Gen planner, plus the OmniContact dataset; achieves strong success and improved meta-skill and skill chaining, with VLM-based semantic integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the dual challenge of robust meta-skill execution and their closed-loop, recoverable chaining for long-horizon loco-manipulation. Explicit humanoid-object representations are precise but hard for planning; implicit skill embeddings lack interpretability, hindering reliable composition.

Method: Introduce contact flow (CF): a compact interface of key body trajectories and time-series binary contact signals. Train CF-Track to learn a unified library of loco-manipulation skills. Develop CF-Gen to heuristically synthesize future CF sequences. Collect OmniContact, a MoCap-based humanoid HOI dataset. Enable integration with vision-language models for semantic task decomposition.

Result: OmniContact achieves 98.7% success on Carry Box and 76.5% on Push-Stack Boxes, surpassing baselines by ~40.9% in meta-skill and ~66.5% in skill chaining. The framework supports robust execution, autonomous failure recovery, and flexible skill composition; demonstrates semantically grounded tasks such as arranging boxes into a heart shape.

Conclusion: The CF framework offers an interpretable, composable interface for long-horizon loco-manipulation, yielding strong performance gains and enabling semantic planning via VLMs. The OmniContact dataset supports further research on humanoid HOI.

Abstract: Learning long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation poses a dual challenge: it requires not only the robust execution of meta-skills but also their seamless, closed-loop chaining equipped with autonomous recovery. Existing approaches remain limited: explicit humanoid-object interaction representations offer precision but are notoriously difficult for high-level planning, whereas implicit skill embeddings are compact but lack the interpretability required for reliable composition. We propose \ours, a hierarchical framework centered on \textbf{contact flow (CF)}, a compact representation consisting of key body trajectories and time-series binary contact signals. Leveraging this shared interface, our low-level policy \textbf{CF-Track} learns a unified library of loco-manipulation skills, while our high-level module \textbf{CF-Gen} heuristically synthesizes future contact-flow sequences. To support this setting, we additionally collect the OmniContact dataset, a MoCap-based HOI corpus for humanoid loco-manipulation (Appendix~\ref{sec:dataset}). Together, they enable robust execution, autonomous failure recovery, and flexible composition of meta-skills for long-horizon tasks. Experiments show that OmniContact achieves \(98.7\%\) success on \textit{Carry Box} and \(76.5\%\) on \textit{Push-Stack Boxes}, outperforming prior baselines by average margins of \(40.9\%\) in meta-skill and \(66.5\%\) in skill chaining. Besides, our framework naturally integrates with VLMs for semantic task decomposition, enabling complex, semantically grounded loco-manipulation behaviors, such as arranging scattered boxes into a heart shape.

</details>


### [271] [RoboTales: ROBOTic Anthropomorphic LEarning Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26213)
*Andrew Chen,Ju-Hung Chen,Phurinat Pinyomit,Alexis E. Block*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboTales is a low-cost Baxter-based puppet storytelling system that synchronizes narration, gestures, and mouth movements to deliver embodied, character-driven stories; a pilot shows superiority over gesture-only storytelling in engagement and recall; designed as modular, platform-agnostic, screen-free, with potential for child learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance children's storytelling engagement and comprehension by combining embodied puppetry with autonomous robotic control, addressing limitations of gesture-only approaches and screen-based media.

Method: Implemented RoboTales on a Baxter robot; integrated synchronized narration, gestures, and mouth movements; conducted a pilot comparing puppet-based (puppetry) vs gesture-only mode; measured engagement (HRIES) and recall; designed to be modular/adaptable to other manipulators.

Result: Puppet-based storytelling yielded higher HRIES ratings and better story recall than gesture-only mode, indicating improved engagement and narrative comprehension.

Conclusion: Puppetry-enabled, modular, platform-agnostic storytelling on affordable robots offers a screen-free alternative for child-centered learning and can be extended to other manipulators and settings.

Abstract: RoboTales is a low-cost robotic storytelling system that animates narratives using expressive sock puppetry. Implemented autonomously on a Baxter robot as a test case, RoboTales synchronizes narration, gestures, and mouth movements to perform character-driven stories. In a pilot study, puppet-based storytelling outperformed a gesture-only mode, producing higher HRIES ratings and improved story recall, suggesting that embodied puppetry enhances engagement and narrative comprehension. Designed to be modular and platform-agnostic, RoboTales can be adapted to other manipulators and offers a screen-free alternative to passive media, supporting future deployment in child-centered learning environments.

</details>


### [272] [TaskNPoint: How to Teach Your Humanoid to Hit a Backhand in Minutes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26215)
*Blake Werner,Ilona Demler,Pietro Perona,Aaron D. Ames*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TaskNPoint: a coach-learner training protocol for humanoid robots that exploits a short, crucial interaction window to learn dynamic skills from minimal demonstrations in realistic simulation, enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen goals; demonstrated on a Unitree G1 with sports and object manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic manipulation tasks hinge on a brief, critical portion of the trajectory where control, physics, and morphology must align. Efficiently teaching these skills requires a structured division of labor between a coach and a learner, with robust generalization from scarce data.

Method: TaskNPoint protocol: a human coach provides a discrete set of skills, one demonstration per skill, explicit identification of the interaction window, and the goal. A physically realistic simulator generates complete trajectories for each action. Randomized target sampling during training promotes generalization. Evaluation on a Unitree G1 performing forehands/backhands, soccer kicks, and box picking/placing, with short video demonstrations and under an hour of GPU training, no per-task reward tuning.

Result: Demonstrated successful learning from short human video demonstrations, achieving robust performance across multiple tasks within a relatively small training budget (under 1 hour on a single GPU) without reward shaping.

Conclusion: A coach-learner workflow focusing on a compact interaction window and randomized goal sampling in realistic simulation can efficiently train humanoid agents to perform diverse dynamic skills with minimal data, enabling zero-shot transfer to unseen goals.

Abstract: How do we learn to hit a tennis backhand? Not from a thousand hours of tennis tournaments on TV - we work with a coach and practice. We argue this is also the right recipe for teaching dynamic skills to humanoid robots. This follows from a structural property of dynamic skills: the outcome is decided by a short, crucial portion of the trajectory - for a backhand, the ~20cm of racket travel around ball contact. Getting this interaction window right requires coordinating the whole motion, so that control, physics, and morphology act in concert. Learning thus reduces to mastering a handful of distinct actions and, for each, practicing until the window comes out right. To this end, we introduce TaskNPoint, a training protocol which makes the coach-learner division of labor explicit. The human coach contributes four inputs: a discrete set of skills (e.g. different shots), one demonstration per skill, identification of the interaction window, and the goal. Learning in a physically realistic simulation environment fills in each action trajectory and provides robustness to unmodeled events. Crucially, randomized target sampling during training lets a single demonstration generalize zero-shot to unseen goal locations.
