We test this approach on a Unitree G1 humanoid that hits forehands and backhands against balls thrown by a human, kicks incoming soccer balls, and picks and places boxes from novel locations. We find that learning is successful from short human video demonstrations and under an hour of training on a single GPU, with no per-task reward tuning.

</details>


### [273] [NavIsaacLab: Generating Realistic Crowd via Parallel Robot Learning for Benchmarking Human-aware Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26265)
*Bingyi Xia,Han Bao,Jingyu Zhu,Hanjing Ye,Yuhan Pang,Guangcheng Chen,Liang Lin,Wenjun Xu,Jiankun Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A GPU-accelerated, photo-realistic, physics-based framework (NavIsaacLab) for training and benchmarking human-aware navigation with data-driven, controllable pedestrians.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scarcity of diverse, high-quality scene data and limitations of rule-based simulations and perfect-visibility assumptions in existing platforms.

Method: Physics-based, photo-realistic pedestrian simulation on Isaac Lab; NavIsaacLab framework; parallel GPU simulation; real-time 3D feedback; data-driven pedestrian model using trajectory diffusion and adversarial motion learning controller; cross-scale scene diversity.

Result: Framework enables realistic, scalable benchmarking and training of navigation policies; no quantitative results reported in the abstract; emphasizes realism, controllability, and scalability across diverse scenes.

Conclusion: NavIsaacLab provides a comprehensive, scalable platform for human-aware navigation research with realistic pedestrians and scenes, enabling better training and evaluation.

Abstract: Robot autonomous navigation that accounts for surrounding human activities is crucial for ensuring both safety and natural human-robot interaction in real-world environments shared by humans and robots. Simulation of complex and diverse navigation scenarios serves as the foundation for training reliable robot navigation policies and accurately evaluating the performance of algorithms, offering an efficient alternative to manual supervision of real data. However, current human-aware navigation research faces significant challenges due to the scarcity of diverse, high-quality scene data. Existing simulation platforms often rely on handcrafted rules to approximate pedestrian behavior and lack the capability to provide extensive sensor signals, typically assuming perfect observations. To address these limitations, this paper presents NavIsaacLab, a comprehensive framework for benchmarking and training human-aware navigation policies through physics-based and photo-realistic simulations of pedestrians and scenes. Based on Isaac Lab, the proposed framework employs photo-realistic scene rendering capabilities and supports parallel simulation on GPU, delivering real-time and accurate 3D visual feedback to robots. To enhance the realism of human behavior, a data-driven approach is employed that incorporates a trajectory diffusion model and an adversarial motion learning controller, enabling controllable, physics-based pedestrian simulation. Furthermore, the integration of diverse cross-scale scenes provides a robust benchmark for state-of-the-art human-aware navigation methods.

</details>


### [274] [Racing a Wheeled Quadruped: Active Load Transfer Mitigation via Model Predictive Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26313)
*Marla Eisman,Brian Lam,Samuel Sonnino,Francesco Borrelli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hierarchical MPC-RL framework for active roll control in autonomous racing of a wheeled quadruped; combines offline raceline optimization, online MPC minimizing lateral Load Transfer Ratio (LTR), and a low-level RL policy on 16 actuators to achieve anti-roll via leg actuation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate lateral load transfer and enhance high-speed stability and performance in autonomous quadruped wheeled robots during racing by integrating planning, control, and imitation/DRL-based actuation.

Method: Offline time-optimal raceline generation; online MPC planner using a bicycle-model-based dynamics of the Unitree Go2-W platform to minimize LTR; a low-level whole-body RL policy deployed on 16 actuators controlling leg joints to generate anti-roll torque and stabilize the system. The leg actuators act as active suspension; knee joints generate anti-roll torque. Validation through physical track experiments and supplementary materials (code/video) at the provided GitHub.

Result: Physical experiments show active roll control reduces mean LTR by up to 44%, improves fastest lap time by 8.7%, and increases peak lateral acceleration by 21.3% to 1.98 m/s^2, maintaining robust high-speed stability beyond a non-tilting baseline controller.

Conclusion: The hierarchical MPC-RL framework with active roll control effectively reduces lateral load transfer and enhances racing performance for a wheeled quadruped, as validated on track experiments. Supplementary materials are available for replication.

Abstract: This paper presents a hierarchical control framework using model predictive control (MPC) and reinforcement learning (RL) for active roll control to manage lateral load transfer during autonomous racing of a wheeled quadruped. The framework integrates offline time-optimal raceline generation, an online MPC planner that actively minimizes the lateral Load Transfer Ratio (LTR), and a low-level, whole-body RL policy deployed directly onto the robot's 16 actuators. The MPC is based on a vehicle dynamics bicycle model of the Unitree Go2-W platform. The robot's leg actuators act as active suspension where knee joints generate anti-roll torque to bank into turns. Physical track experiments demonstrate that active roll control reduces mean LTR by up to 44%, improves the fastest lap time by 8.7%, and boosts peak lateral acceleration capability by 21.3% to 1.98 $m/s^2$, maintaining robust high-speed stability beyond the range of a non-tilting baseline controller. Supplementary code and video can be found at https://github.com/meisman-ucb/go2w-roll-control-mpc

</details>


### [275] [Layered Outer-Loop Control for Disturbance-Robust Multi-Waypoint UAV Arrival](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26315)
*Runfeng Ling*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A two-phase, layered terminal-control architecture for multi-waypoint UAV position regulation is evaluated across PyBullet, PX4/Gazebo, and hardware; decouples core controller design from benchmark refinements; yields robust deployment insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disturbance-robust UAV control is easy to demonstrate in benign simulations but hard to achieve fast, well-behaved near targets, and credible beyond a single benchmark. A deployment-oriented evaluation across simulators and hardware is needed to assess generalizability and practicality.

Method: Phase I: PyBullet with stochastic wind to rapidly select a controller core that separates smooth approach generation, persistent-bias compensation, and supervised near-target terminal regulation. Phase II: Transfer the architecture into a PX4/Gazebo closed-loop with a cascaded flight stack, delay-sensitive settling, and stronger transit-to-hover coupling; assess which benchmark gains survive autopilot-mediated dynamics. Evaluation includes a two-level hardware study on a Vicon-tracked Tello stack, and a transfer-oriented final controller selection using coverage across scenarios and deployable supervisory logic; Grace analysis examines completion semantics vs. waypoint miss.

Result: Phase I bare controller achieves 0.024 m mean late-stage wind error. Phase II selects the final controller with a transfer-oriented rule; analysis reveals which benchmark refinements survive deployment dynamics and which are benchmark-specific. Residual failures are sensitive to completion semantics, not gross miss rate. Hardware validation supports the generalizability of the approach across simulation and real-world dynamics.

Conclusion: Separating the main controller design from benchmark-specific refinements yields more informative benchmark success and a defensible, deployable controller under harder, closed-loop evaluation.

Abstract: Disturbance-robust UAV position control is easy to demonstrate in benign simulations but much harder to make fast in approach, well behaved near the target, and credible beyond a single benchmark. This letter presents a layered terminal-control architecture for multi-waypoint UAV position regulation together with a staged evaluation across PyBullet, PX4/Gazebo, and hardware. Phase I uses a PyBullet benchmark with stochastic wind for rapid structural selection, identifying a controller core that separates smooth approach generation, persistent-bias compensation, and supervised near-target terminal regulation. Phase II carries only that main architecture into a more demanding PX4/Gazebo closed loop, where the outer-loop controller acts through a cascaded flight stack with delay-sensitive settling and stronger transit-to-hover coupling. This step exposes which benchmark gains survive autopilot-mediated dynamics and which refinements collapse once the loop becomes more deployment-like. In Phase I, the bare controller attains 0.024 m mean late-stage wind error. In Phase II, the final controller is selected using a transfer-oriented rule emphasizing absence of benchmark priors, cross-scenario balance, and deployable supervisory logic. Strict is used as the primary reporting reference; the supplementary retrospective Grace analysis shows that part of the residual failure set is sensitive to completion semantics rather than gross waypoint-miss behaviour. The evaluation is completed on one Vicon-tracked Tello stack through a two-level hardware study. Taken together, the results suggest that benchmark success becomes more informative when the main controller design is separated from benchmark-specific refinement and remains defensible under harder closed-loop evaluation.

</details>


### [276] [KRVF: A Source-Aware Semantic Voxel World Representation for Edge Mobile Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26321)
*Runfeng Ling*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: KRVF: a source-aware semantic voxel map for edge mobile manipulation that fuses occupancy, color, semantics, time freshness, and evidence sources; decouples measurements from priors; enables depth-failure-aware reasoning and task-level queries; ROS 2 implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge/mobile manipulation requires current, queryable, semantically meaningful world models under edge compute constraints; reconstruction-centric maps struggle to support task-oriented reasoning and robust depth handling.

Method: Task-oriented voxel representation encoding occupancy, color, semantic evidence, temporal freshness, and evidence source; separation of measured occupancy from semantic priors; depth-prior rendering for repair; task-level query operators for semantic objects and grasp candidates; ROS 2 implementation translating online RGB-D observations into a task-facing robot memory.

Result: Formalization of the KRVF representation and documentation of a ROS 2 implementation converting online RGB-D observations into a task-facing robot memory.

Conclusion: KRVF provides a current, semantically rich, and queryable world model suitable for edge constraints, enabling robust object reasoning and grasp planning while maintaining a clear distinction between measured data and semantic priors.

Abstract: Mobile manipulators need world models that are current, queryable, semantically meaningful, and usable under edge-compute constraints. This technical report presents KRVF, a source-aware semantic voxel world representation for edge mobile manipulation. Unlike reconstruction-centric mapping pipelines that primarily optimize global geometric fidelity, KRVF represents local world state as task-oriented voxels that encode occupancy, color, semantic evidence, temporal freshness, and evidence source. The representation separates measured occupancy from semantic-prior hypotheses, enabling depth-failure-aware object reasoning without silently corrupting persistent geometry. KRVF also closes a feedback loop between mapping and sensing by rendering map-prior depth for repair, and exposes task-level query operators for semantic objects and grasp candidates. The report formalizes the KRVF representation and documents a ROS 2 implementation that turns online RGB-D observations into a task-facing robot memory.

</details>


### [277] [Scaling Nonlinear Optimization: Many Problems One GPU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26341)
*John Viljoen,Johanna Haffner,Masayoshi Tomizuka,Negar Mehr*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A GPU-batched NLP solver for robotics (jaxipm) based on IPOPT, implemented in JAX, achieves large throughput gains (up to 32.85x) over CPU-bound IPOPT by heterogeneous iteration fusion and iteration-level batching, enabling scalable, constrained optimization in GPU-based robotics pipelines; open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IPOPT and similar NLP solvers are CPU-bound and solve one problem at a time, hindering integration with GPU-batched learning pipelines in robotics that rely on high-throughput simulators. A GPU-batched NLP solver would unlock parallel constrained optimization at scale.

Method: Redesign IPOPT to remove control flow via heterogeneous iteration fusion and minimize GPU idle time with iteration-level batching, implemented in JAX. Evaluated on quadrotor nonlinear MPC benchmarks including obstacle-augmented tracking, multi-quadrotor collision-free navigation, and cluttered-environment planning.

Result: Demonstrates up to 32.85x throughput gain over IPOPT. Provides open-source code at the stated GitHub repository.

Conclusion: GPU-batched NLP solving is feasible and beneficial for GPU-based robotics workflows, enabling parallelized, constrained optimization at scale and bridging NLP solvers with learning-based pipelines.

Abstract: Many robotics problems, including trajectory optimization, inverse kinematics, and contact-rich motion planning, reduce to nonlinear programs (NLPs). Mature NLP solvers such as IPOPT can solve these problems, offering hard constraint satisfaction, optimality guarantees, and favorable scaling with problem dimension. These solvers underpin gradient-based methods in robotics, yet remain CPU-bound and solve only one problem at a time, preventing their integration into GPU-batched learning pipelines. On the other hand, sampling-based approaches such as reinforcement learning, model predictive path integral, and imitation learning have become the core of modern robotics research due to their ability to leverage GPU-batched simulators. These simulators can generate orders of magnitude more dynamics rollouts per second than was previously possible. If a GPU-batched NLP solver existed, it would unlock similar speedups in the number of constrained, locally optimal solutions generated per second. This regime of solving many problems concurrently versus solving a single problem at a time is a key requirement for integrating NLP solvers in modern GPU-batched robotics frameworks. To this end, we introduce \texttt{jaxipm}, the first GPU-batched NLP solver, based on IPOPT, and implemented in JAX. We accomplish this by redesigning IPOPT's algorithm to eliminate control flow with \textit{heterogeneous iteration fusion}, and by minimizing GPU idle time with \textit{iteration level batching}. We evaluate \texttt{jaxipm} on a variety of quadrotor nonlinear model predictive control benchmarks, including reference tracking in the presence of obstacles, multi-quadrotor navigation without collision, and navigation in a cluttered environment. We demonstrate up to a $32.85\times$ increase in throughput over IPOPT. Our complete open-source codebase is available at https://github.com/johnviljoen/jaxipm.

</details>


### [278] [MPC-Injection: Biasing Off-Policy Locomotion RL Toward Controller-Induced Behavior Basins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26392)
*Roy Xing,Seyoung Ree,Brian Plancher*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MPC-Injection steers RL locomotion toward designer gait by injecting MPC transitions into replay, achieving usable gaits with minimal reward engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL for locomotion often converges to locally optimal but undeployable behaviors; a lightweight method is needed to bias learning toward manufacturable gaits without redesigning rewards or adding adversarial components.

Method: Insert transitions from an MPC controller solving the same MDP into the RL replay buffer; transfers the controller's preferred behavior through the replay state distribution, without reward shaping, discriminators, or retargeting; uses a simple, one- to two-term task reward.

Result: In a 2D walker simulation and Go2 quadruped sim-to-real, MPC-Injection biases the policy toward the controller's behavior basin, producing gaits qualitatively comparable to complex reward shaping and adversarial priors but with lower overhead; demonstrates that injected transitions steer actor-critic updates toward controller-visited states, enabling behaviors that plain RL with simple rewards may miss.

Conclusion: MPC-Injection offers a low-overhead, effective way to align RL with designer preferences, avoiding heavy reward engineering and discriminator-based methods while enabling deployable gaits.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for locomotion frequently converges to locally optimal but undeployable behaviors, such as vibrating limbs or scooting on the torso, that maximize return without producing a usable gait. We present MPC-Injection, a low-overhead method that steers RL toward a designer-preferred gait by inserting transitions into the replay buffer from a model predictive controller solving the same Markov decision process. Unlike reward shaping, MPC-Injection does not require redesigning the task reward, and unlike adversarial imitation learning, it adds no discriminator, no kinematic retargeting, and no auxiliary objective. Instead, the controller's preferred behavior is transferred to the policy purely through the replay state distribution. On a 2D walker in simulation and with sim-to-real evaluation on a Go2 quadruped, we show that MPC-Injection drives the policy into the controller's behavior basin using a one to two-term task reward, producing gaits qualitatively comparable to those of reward shaping with twenty-one tuned terms and of adversarial motion priors without their discriminator and retargeting overhead. We further analyze how the injected transitions bias actor-critic updates toward controller-visited states, allowing the policy to learn behaviors that pure RL may fail to reach under simple reward functions.

</details>


### [279] [Exploring the Intrinsic Geometry of Diffusion Models with Constrained Inverse Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26408)
*Miguel Angel Rogel Garcia,Phone Thiha Kyaw,Jonathan Kelly*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Diffusion models learn and preserve the intrinsic geometry of constrained inverse kinematics manifolds, with evidence on UR5 (6-DoF) and Franka (7-DoF). The intrinsic dimension from the score matches the manifold's degrees of freedom, and latent interpolation remains on the manifold, suggesting geometry is captured beyond dimension alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether diffusion models recover meaningful geometric structure when the data lie on known, analytically tractable manifolds, using constrained IK as a controlled testbed.

Method: Train a single conditional diffusion model across seven constraint families for each robot (6-DoF UR5 and 7-DoF Franka). Analyze the score function to estimate intrinsic dimension and perform linear interpolation in latent space to assess manifold alignment.

Result: The intrinsic dimension inferred from the score matches the analytical DOF of the constraint manifolds for both robots. Latent-space linear interpolation yields generated solutions that remain close to the corresponding constraint manifolds, indicating the learned representation encodes geometric structure beyond mere dimensionality.

Conclusion: Constrained inverse kinematics provides a controlled setting to study the intrinsic geometry learned by diffusion models, demonstrating that diffusion models can recover and respect the geometric structure of known manifolds.

Abstract: Recent studies suggest that diffusion models can recover geometric structure in the data manifolds they are trained on, yet the supporting evidence has so far come mostly from natural-image data, where the underlying geometry itself is unknown. We study this question in a setting where the geometry is analytically tractable: constrained inverse kinematics (IK). Each task-space constraint defines a configuration-space manifold with known intrinsic dimension, giving direct ground truth for evaluating the geometry learned by the model. For each of the 6-DoF UR5 and 7-DoF Franka, we train a single conditional diffusion model across seven constraint families, spanning solution manifolds from discrete IK branches to self-motion manifolds. Our empirical results reveal that the intrinsic dimension recovered from the model's score function matches the analytical degrees of freedom of the corresponding constraint manifold across both robots. Moreover, linear interpolation in the latent space leads to generated solutions that remain close to the appropriate constraint manifold, indicating that the learned representation further captures geometric structure of the constraint family beyond intrinsic dimension alone. Constrained IK therefore offers a controlled setting for studying the intrinsic geometry learned by diffusion models.

</details>


### [280] [CoStream: Composing Simple Behaviors for Generalizable Complex Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26423)
*Haonan Chen,Yuxiang Ma,Stephen Tian,Xiaoshen Han,Wenlong Huang,Feiyang Wu,Yunzhu Li,Jiajun Wu,Edward H. Adelson,Yilun Du*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A modular manipulation framework that composes three independent core behaviors on an SE(3) interface to achieve high-precision, long-horizon manipulation with robust generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation tasks require millimeter precision and generalization; traditional pipelines are brittle and brittle; monolithic end-to-end policies lack structured compositionality and hinder adaptation to new tasks; a decomposable, recomposable architecture may bridge precision and generalization.

Method: Propose ␀ourshort, orchestrating foundation-model-based semantic behavior to extract spatial constraints, predictive behavior to forecast trajectories by tracking keypoints in imagined videos, and reactive behavior to provide high-frequency tactile/force corrections. All outputs map to a shared SE(3) pose interface via right-multiplication and are executed by a compliant controller. Demonstrated on 8 real-world tasks spanning manipulation and precision assembly.

Result: Empirical validation on 8 tasks; strongest gains in contact-rich assembly and object transfer; robust recovery from manual perturbations during execution.

Conclusion: Shows that complex manipulation capabilities can emerge from composing simple, independent behaviors rather than deploying rigid pipelines or monolithic policies, enabling high-precision, generalizable long-horizon manipulation.

Abstract: Long-horizon, contact-rich complex manipulation tasks, such as seating a GPU into a PCIe slot, demand both millimeter high precision and out-of-the-box generalization to new tasks. Existing paradigms struggle to satisfy both: classical pipelines use brittle, task-specific interfaces to achieve high-precision control but require costly pipeline redesigns to adapt to new tasks, whereas monolithic end-to-end policies provide better generalization but lack high precision on complex, out-of-distribution tasks unless retrained with new data. Both paradigms share an implicit assumption: once a manipulation capability is acquired, it must be deployed as a rigid pipeline or monolithic whole, rather than being freely decomposed and recomposed. In this paper, we show that complex manipulation capabilities can emerge naturally from the composition of simple, independent behaviors. Rather than deploying a monolithic policy or a rigid pipeline, we propose \ourshort, a framework orchestrating foundation models and diverse sensing modalities into multiple composable core behaviors: a semantic behavior extracting spatial constraints via foundation models; a predictive behavior forecasting trajectories by tracking keypoints in imagined videos; and a reactive behavior providing high-frequency tactile and force corrections. On a shared $SE(3)$ interface, these outputs compose by right-multiplication into a single pose command at each control step, executed by a compliant controller. We demonstrate \ourshort on 8 real-world tasks spanning everyday manipulation and precision assembly, with the strongest gains in contact-rich assembly and object transfer, and show robust recovery from manual perturbations during execution. {Website:} https://costream-simple.github.io

</details>


### [281] [A System for Fast, Resilient, and Adaptable Loco-Manipulation Behaviors on Humanoid Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26425)
*Duncan William Calvert*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A runtime-editable, robot-centric behavior authoring framework for humanoid locomotion and manipulation, integrating Affordance Templates, Behavior Trees, and runtime-perception with a whole-body controller, enabling rapid creation and deployment of loco-manipulation behaviors on multiple robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the tight coupling of locomotion, manipulation, perception, and operator supervision required for productive humanoid work in human-centric environments. Grounded in Coactive Design principles from the DARPA Robotics Challenge to maximize observability, predictability, and directability.

Method: A robot-local, runtime-editable behavior system that combines object-centric Affordance Templates, Behavior Tree-inspired organization, and runtime-perception through a behavior scene with primitive scene actions. Action primitives run on a whole-body controller (arms while walking) with a concurrent action layering algorithm. A continuously synchronized operator interface enables runtime authoring, monitoring, and repair. A library of 20+ real-robot tasks is implemented and deployed across multiple humanoid platforms.

Result: Demonstrates rapid adaptation, extension, and combination of existing behaviors to create novel loco-manipulation tasks in minutes to hours. Evaluation spans capability, speed, reliability, and speed of behavior creation, with real-robot demonstrations and several deployed platforms (Atlas, Valkyrie, Nadia, H1-2, Alex).

Conclusion: The framework provides observable, predictable, and directly controllable humanoid robot behavior, enabling rapid development and deployment of complex loco-manipulation skills across diverse platforms while maintaining runtime authoring and repair capabilities.

Abstract: Humanoid robots could take on physically demanding, hazardous, and repetitive work in spaces built for humans. However, a useful robot for these spaces must coordinate locomotion, whole body motion, perception, contact, and operator supervision. This thesis presents a robot-local, runtime-editable behavior authoring and runtime system.
  Our system strives to be maximally observable, predictable, and directable following Coactive Design principles developed during the DARPA Robotics Challenge. Our operator interface remains continuously synchronized to the robot for runtime authoring, monitoring, and repair. Our behavior architecture uniquely combines object-centric Affordance Templates, organization and logic inspired by Behavior Trees, and runtime-editable perception through a behavior scene and primitive scene actions. Action primitives build on a whole-body controller that supports moving the arms while walking, and use a concurrent action layering algorithm for speed.
  The behavior library developed during this work covers more than twenty real-robot task variants, including push and pull doors with knob, push-bar, and lever-handle mechanisms, multi-step exploration sequences, obstacle clearing, and reactive table-to-table manipulation tasks. This behavior system has been deployed on many humanoid robots, such as Boston Dynamics' DRC Atlas, NASA's Valkyrie, IHMC and Boardwalk Robotics' Nadia, Unitree's H1-2, and IHMC's Alex.
  We evaluate our system across capability, speed, reliability, and speed of behavior creation, adaptation, extension, and combination. Our experiments demonstrate that we can adapt, extend, and combine existing behaviors to create novel loco-manipulation behaviors in minutes or hours. Videos: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLJK5CTyotYqsfgfnXb-09YNFeBose6uEY.

</details>


### [282] [Play2Perfect: What Matters in Dexterous Play Pretraining for Precise Assembly?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26428)
*Tyler Ga Wei Lum,Kushal Kedia,C. Karen Liu,Jeannette Bohg*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Play2Perfect pretrains versatile manipulation priors through task-agnostic play, then finetunes for precise multi-fingered assembly; achieves 33x sample efficiency and 60% real-world success on tight insertions with 0.5 mm clearance, plus nontrivial success on longer-horizon assembly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Precise, contact-rich assembly with multi-fingered hands is data-inefficient and hard due to sparse rewards and reliance on specialized hardware; task-agnostic pretraining via play could yield reusable manipulation priors for faster, more generalizable assembly.

Method: Task-agnostic RL pretraining via play across diverse objects/goals to learn priors (grasping, in-hand reorientation, pose reaching). Finetune on precise assembly, focusing exploration on final contact-rich interactions. Systematic ablations on object diversity, training objective, trajectory diversity, and goal precision.

Result: Prior yields 33x sample efficiency over RL from scratch, even with dense multi-stage rewards. Demonstrates zero-shot sim-to-real transfer with ~60% success on tight insertions (0.5 mm clearance) and >50% success on long-horizon multi-part assembly and screwing.

Conclusion: Task-agnostic play pretraining can learn reusable manipulation priors that substantially improve sample efficiency and enable effective transfer to precise assembly; finetuning concentrates exploration on the critical final steps to achieve high-precision tasks.

Abstract: Multi-fingered robots promise the speed and dexterity of human hands, yet challenging problems such as precise assembly have remained out of reach. These tasks are contact-rich, making data collection for imitation learning difficult, and sparse-reward, making direct exploration with reinforcement learning (RL) intractable. Consequently, prior work has made progress by structuring the problem with specialized grippers, tool attachments, and environment fixtures. In this work, we argue that before a robot can perfect precise assembly, it must first learn to play. We further ask the question: what factors in the process of learning to play matter for precise assembly? We propose Play2Perfect, an RL framework for task-agnostic pretraining through play on diverse objects and goals, which is then perfected on precise assembly. The goal of play is to acquire reusable manipulation priors, such as grasping, in-hand reorientation and pose reaching. Finetuning then adapts this general prior to assembly, focusing exploration on the final contact-rich, high-precision interactions needed for success. We systematically study key design choices in play pretraining, including object diversity, training objective, trajectory diversity, and goal precision. We show that our prior is 33x more sample-efficient than RL training from scratch, even when provided with dense, multi-stage rewards. We demonstrate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, achieving 60% success on tight insertions with only 0.5 mm contact clearance, and over 50% success on long-horizon multi-part assembly and screwing.

</details>


### [283] [WatchAct: A Benchmark for Behavior-Grounded Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26443)
*Baiqi Li,Ce Zhang,Yu Fang,Yue Yang,Shangzhe Li,Mingyu Ding,Gedas Bertasius*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: WatchAct is a large, long-horizon robot manipulation benchmark grounded in observed human behavior, with 3,000 instances across 14 tasks and a disentangled three-track evaluation; current systems underperform, revealing significant gaps between vision-language planning and real-world manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current manipulation benchmarks couple instructions with a single current image and lack mechanisms to evaluate reasoning over observed human behavior. WatchAct addresses this by grounding tasks in real human-action videos and providing a scalable, reproducible evaluation of multi-step, intention-aware manipulation.

Method: Construct WatchAct by pairing real-world human-action videos and language instructions with aligned simulator scenes and executable LIBERO tasks. The dataset spans 3,000 long-horizon instances across 14 tasks and four cognitive domains: Event Grounding, Procedural Reasoning, Implicit Intent Inference, and Episodic Reasoning. Propose a disentangled evaluation protocol with three tracks: (i) video-to-plan reasoning by vision-language models, (ii) policy execution under oracle plans, and (iii) full task completion by integrated planner–policy pipelines. Evaluation conducted in simulation and on a Franka Research 3 robot.

Result: State-of-the-art pipelines (e.g., Gemini-3.1-Pro with π0.5) achieve only 16.3% Success Rate in simulation and 14.0% on the real robot. Plan SR is 36.8% (humans ~97.1%), Task SR under oracle plans 21.5% (10.6% in out-of-domain settings). These results indicate substantial gaps in video-to-action reasoning and long-horizon manipulation.

Conclusion: WatchAct exposes pronounced deficiencies in current perception–planning–execution systems for watching-another-agent tasks. Its disentangled evaluation enables precise diagnosis of where reasoning fails (event parsing, procedural structure, intent inference, episodic changes) and provides a scalable benchmark with available data and code to spur progress.

Abstract: A robot working alongside people must reason about what they have done, in what order, and with what intent. Video carries the spatial layouts, object histories, and gestures that language leaves underspecified, yet today's manipulation benchmarks pair an instruction with a single current image, offering no way to evaluate reasoning over observed human behavior. We introduce WatchAct, a benchmark for robot manipulation grounded in observed human behavior. Each instance pairs a real-world human-action video and a language instruction with an aligned simulator scene and an executable LIBERO task, enabling scalable and reproducible evaluation. WatchAct comprises 3,000 long-horizon instances across 14 tasks in four capability domains drawn from the cognitive demands of watching another agent: parsing events (Event Grounding), recovering procedural structure (Procedural Reasoning), inferring unstated intent (Implicit Intent Inference), and tracking how the scene was changed (Episodic Reasoning). We further propose a disentangled evaluation protocol that separately measures (i)~video-to-plan reasoning by vision-language models, (ii)~policy execution under oracle plans, and (iii)~full task completion by integrated planner--policy pipelines. In both simulation and on a Franka Research 3 robot, current systems remain far from solving WatchAct. The best pipeline, Gemini-3.1-Pro with $π_{0.5}$, reaches only 16.3% Success Rate (SR) in simulation and 14.0% on the real robot. Gemini-3.1-Pro attains just 36.8% Plan SR (vs. 97.1% for humans), while $π_{0.5}$ reaches only 21.5% Task SR under oracle plans and drops to 10.6% on out-of-domain scenarios. Dataset and code are available at https://baiqi-li.github.io/watchact_page/.

</details>


### [284] [OSC2Runner: OpenSCENARIO 2.x Compliant High-Fidelity AV Simulation in CARLA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26533)
*Thoshitha Gamage,Lasanthi Gamage*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OSC2Runner provides a deterministic backend mapping OpenSCENARIO v2.x to CARLA via a multi-pass transpiler that translates v2.x into type-safe ASTs and dynamic behavior trees, enabling precise, tick-level execution without static playback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OpenSCENARIO 1.x playback causes spatiotemporal drift when evaluating v2.x logic with legacy interpreters; there is a need for a deterministic, type-safe execution backend for SB Testing, co-simulation, HIL, and LLM-driven pipelines.

Method: Formalize scenario translation as a compilation pipeline: multi-pass transpiler converts v2.x DSL into type-safe ASTs, which are then compiled into dynamic deterministic behavior trees (py_trees) mapped to CARLA APIs, bypassing static trajectory playback.

Result: Empirical validation under concurrent adversarial scenarios shows tick-by-tick determinism, exact spatial trigger evaluation, and 100 ms cross-actor blackboard synchronization; kinematic analysis confirms adherence to continuous environmental boundaries.

Conclusion: The architecture transforms Scenario-Based Testing from approximate behavior to mathematically rigorous execution, providing a deterministic backend suitable for co-simulation, hardware-in-the-loop testing, and automated LLM-driven generation pipelines.

Abstract: Scenario-Based Testing predominantly relies on the legacy ASAM OpenSCENARIO 1.x XML standard because existing continuous simulation frameworks lack native execution support for the recently matured v2.x Domain-Specific Language (DSL). Adapting legacy interpreters to evaluate v2.x logic introduces spatiotemporal drift, asynchronous event latencies, and artificial kinematic snapping. Addressing this execution gap, OSC2Runner introduces the first orchestration framework capable of natively mapping the OpenSCENARIO v2.x DSL to CARLA. The framework achieves this by formalizing scenario translation as a compilation pipeline through a multi-pass transpiler architecture. Bypassing static trajectory playback, the architecture synthesizes type-safe Abstract Syntax Trees directly into dynamic deterministic behavior trees (py_trees) natively mapped to CARLA's atomic APIs. Empirical validation in highly concurrent adversarial case studies demonstrates tick-by-tick determinism, exact spatial trigger evaluation, and 100.0 ms cross-actor blackboard synchronization. Kinematic analysis proves the strict adherence to continuous environmental boundaries. This architecture transitions Scenario-Based Testing from approximate behavioral interpretation to mathematically rigorous execution, establishing the deterministic backend required for co-simulation, hardware-in-the-loop testing, and automated LLM-driven generation pipelines.

</details>


### [285] [IDEA: Insensitive to Dynamics Mismatch via Effect Alignment for Sim-to-Real Transfer in Multi-Agent Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26575)
*Chenlong Liu,Zhuohui Zhang,Xinyan Chen,Zhipeng Wang,Bin Cheng,Bin He*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Sim-to-real transfer for multi-agent control that uses semantic actions and effect alignment to reduce dynamics mismatch, with an action synchronization mechanism; improved training efficiency and real-world robustness across four navigation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning-based multi-agent control struggles with sim-to-real transfer due to dynamics mismatch and low-level control sensitivity; need robust, scalable transfer methods that reduce reliance on accurate dynamics and facilitate real-world deployment.

Method: Proposes a sim-to-real approach that operates at a semantic action level by randomizing environmental structure and using discrete semantic actions within a closed-loop control framework; introduces effect alignment to mitigate dynamics mismatch; implements an action synchronization mechanism to reduce inter-agent timing mismatches and improve temporal consistency.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four multi-agent navigation tasks shows substantially improved training efficiency compared to mainstream transfer methods and higher real-world success rates, indicating improved robustness and deployment stability under dynamics mismatch.

Conclusion: The approach lowers sensitivity to dynamics mismatch via effect alignment and semantic action abstraction, enhances temporal coherence through action synchronization, and improves the practicality of learning-based multi-agent control in real-world settings.

Abstract: Complex multi-agent control tasks remain challenging for traditional rule-based and model-based approaches, motivating the adoption of learning-based methods. However, learning-based methods often struggle with sim-to-real transfer because they rely on accurate dynamics modeling or system identification and learn policies in low-level control spaces that are highly sensitive to dynamics mismatch, making them costly and fragile in complex environments. To address this issue, we propose a sim-to-real method for multi-agent control, which is insensitive to dynamics mismatch via effect alignment. Our method combines random environmental structure with discrete semantic actions through closed-loop control, elevating policy learning to a semantic abstraction level. Additionally, we develop an action synchronization mechanism that mitigates inter-agent action timing mismatches, thereby enhancing the temporal consistency of the system. Experiments on four multi-agent navigation tasks demonstrate that our method substantially improves training efficiency over mainstream transfer methods and achieves higher success rates in real-world scenarios, thereby improving the robustness and deployment stability of multi-agent systems under dynamics mismatch.

</details>


### [286] [Inference-Time Robot Behavior Steering through Physically-Aware Reconfiguration of Task-Structure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26588)
*Yiyuan Pan,Hanjiang Hu,Shangtao Li,Xusheng Luo,Changliu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ReStruct uses a neural automaton policy to steer a visuomotor policy at inference by reconfiguring a skeleton state-machine, keeping the low-level controller fixed, to satisfy user preferences with physically-aware control; it achieves up to 25% gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference-time steering of learned robot policies without retraining is challenging. End-to-end methods require fine-tuning or expert guidance; neuro-symbolic approaches rely on predefined symbols that can yield feasible-looking but physically infeasible plans.

Method: Decompose the policy into a high-level skeleton (state-machine) and a low-level residual controller. Use a synchronous product to embed the skeleton into the policy, reconfiguring task structure. Freeze the controller; update action priors from the skeleton to satisfy new preferences under the modified task structure.

Result: Experiments in simulation and on real robots show broad preference steering (object-centric to temporal-logic). ReStruct surpasses existing methods and improves task success and preference-following by up to 25% over VLA models.

Conclusion: ReStruct provides physically-aware, inference-time policy steering by reconfiguring task structure via a neural automaton skeleton while preserving low-level control, enabling flexible preference satisfaction across tasks.

Abstract: A central challenge in deploying learned robot policies is inference-time behavior steering: redirecting a policy at test time to satisfy user preferences not anticipated during training, without retraining. Existing methods fail in two modes: end-to-end methods require fine-tuning or expert-level guidance, while neuro-symbolic methods rely on predefined symbols whose edits can result in logically reasonable but physically infeasible plans. To address this challenge, we propose ReStruct, which builds upon a neural automaton policy that decomposes a visuomotor policy into a high-level state-machine skeleton capturing task structure and a low-level continuous controller represented as a residual policy. Specifically, ReStruct adopts the automaton to represent the preference and incorporates it into the skeleton through a synchronous product, thereby reconfiguring the task structure. With the controller kept frozen, the action priors provided by the skeleton are updated accordingly to enable physically-aware control under a modified task structure. Extensive experiments from simulation and real-world show that ReStruct steers a wide range of preferences, from object-centric specifications to temporal-logic constraints, and after steering surpasses existing methods, exceeding VLA models in both task success and preference-following by up to 25%.

</details>


### [287] [Bridging Handheld and Teleoperated Supervision for Contact-Rich Manipulation via State-Gated Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26603)
*Vidullan Surendran,Neehar Peri,David Watkins*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BRIDGE uses a bi-modal routing between phase-specific diffusion policy experts to combine handheld supervision with minimal teleoperation for contact-rich manipulation, routing according to the current state; yields significant gains over handheld-only baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the data-efficiency vs. fidelity trade-off in robot learning by recognizing that handheld data is valid in tolerant phases but flawed in contact-critical phases; teleoperation provides desired actions but is expensive; a phase-aware approach aims to exploit both sources where each is strongest.

Method: Introduce BRIDGE: a mixture of diffusion policy experts with gated routing between phase-specific heads conditioned on the robot state. Train on predominantly handheld data supplemented with a small amount of targeted teleoperated demonstrations for task segments where handheld policies fail. NAIVELY mixing data from different supervision sources performed worse, so BRIDGE routes actions based on task phase to leverage desired actions during contact-sensitive segments.

Result: BRIDGE improves success rates over handheld-only baselines by up to 36.7% across three contact-rich manipulation tasks, demonstrating improved handling of phase-specific supervision and dynamic feasibility.

Conclusion: Phase-aware, data-efficient imitation learning via BRIDGE enables selective use of desired actions during critical contact phases, achieving substantial performance gains and offering a practical strategy to combine handheld and teleoperated data.

Abstract: Handheld data collection systems, such as the Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI), enable scalable data collection across diverse environments but only capture observed actions rather than the desired actions executed by a robot controller. In contrast, teleoperation captures desired actions directly, but is prohibitively time-consuming to collect. We revisit this trade-off through the lens of action validity across task phases. We observe that handheld trajectories provide valid supervision in tolerant, free-space phases, but lack dynamic feasibility in contact-sensitive phases, where tracking observed trajectories at high stiffness produces large, unsafe contact forces. We study the interaction between these two supervision types for contact-rich manipulation and find that training policies that combine handheld data with a small number of targeted teleoperated demonstrations provide an efficient hybrid strategy. Specifically, rather than teleoperating the entire task, we only collect partial teleoperated demonstrations for task segments where base handheld policies fail. However, naively mixing handheld and teleoperated phase-specific data yields worse performance than training on handheld data alone. To address this mismatch between observed and desired supervision, we propose Bi-modal Routing for Imitation Data via Gated Experts (BRIDGE), a mixture of diffusion policy experts that routes between specialist task phase heads conditioned on the current robot state. Notably, our approach enables task-phase specific use of desired actions during contact sensitive segments and improves success rates over handheld-only baselines by up to 36.7% across three contact-rich manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [288] [A Closed-Form 4-DoF Inter-Robot Pose Estimator using Bearing-only Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26616)
*Qixin De,Ao Zhuang,Yechen Zhang,Zhuozhou Qian,Danping Zou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A closed-form 4-DoF inter-robot pose estimator for bearing-odometry-based cooperative localization, with an observability analysis that identifies degeneracy patterns and an autonomous observability test module. Claims improved accuracy, lower computation, and robust estimation across maneuvers by reducing motion-excitation requirements and avoiding fixed sliding windows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address observability degeneracy and slow convergence in 6-DoF bearing-odometry cooperative localization, and reduce reliance on fixed data windows through an autonomous observability check.

Method: Derive a closed-form 4-DoF estimator by relaxing rotation constraints and applying error projection for translations. Conduct theoretical observability analysis to identify collinear and shape-preserving degeneracies. Introduce an observability test module to autonomously select optimal estimation instants, removing dependence on fixed-length sliding windows.

Result: The 4-DoF estimator achieves higher estimation accuracy at significantly lower computational cost. The observability test module improves reliability and minimizes the data collection interval.

Conclusion: The proposed 4-DoF approach expands the observable maneuver space for bearing-odometry-based cooperative localization, enabling reliable inter-robot pose estimation with reduced sensing/compute demands. Autonomous observability testing enhances robustness by adaptively selecting estimation instants.

Abstract: Bearing-odometry-based cooperative localization has attracted increasing research interest due to its minimal infrastructure requirements, low communication bandwidth and broad applicability in complex environments. However, existing 6-DoF approaches still face challenges in rapidly obtaining accurate and reliable inter-robot pose estimation, as the system is prone to observability degeneracy under specific motion patterns. To address these issues, we first propose a closed-form 4-DoF inter-robot pose estimator, which relaxes nonlinear constraints for rotations estimation and employs error projection for translations estimation. We then conduct a theoretical analysis of the system's observability, identifying degeneracy under two typical motion patterns: collinear and shape-preserving formations. The analysis further shows that the proposed 4-DoF system requires less stringent motion excitation for observability, enabling reliable estimation under a broader range of cooperative maneuvers. Furthermore, an observability test module is introduced to autonomously determine the optimal estimation instant, eliminating reliance on a predefined fixed-length sliding window. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves higher estimation accuracy with significantly low computational cost, and the observability test module ensures estimation reliability while minimizing the data collection interval.

</details>


### [289] [Hardware Design for Table Tennis Robot Capable of Beating Professional Players](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26643)
*Nobuhiko Mukai,Pavel Adodin,Stefan Heusser,Alexander Sigrist,Divij Grover,Guillem Torrente,Farshad Khadivar,Takekazu Kakinuma,Peter Dürr*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An 8-DoF table tennis robot, Ace, designed from elite-player motion analysis, uses topology-optimized lightweight structure, inverse-dynamics torque-based motor selection, and delay-compensated low-order joint dynamics with RL control; it achieves 0.8 s cycles and 22 m/s peak racket velocity and defeats several professionals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable a hardware-accurate, high-performance table tennis robot capable of beating professional players by translating human-propelled motion characteristics into a scalable robotic platform with rigorously defined workspace, payload, force resistance, and end-effector accuracy.

Method: 1) Derive target specifications for workspace, payload, force resistance, performance, serve capability, and end-effector accuracy from elite players. 2) Design an 8-DoF robotic arm (Ace) around these specs. 3) Apply topology optimization to reduce mass while preserving stiffness. 4) Select motors/gears using an inverse-dynamics torque model. 5) Develop low-order per-joint dynamic models with delay compensation; integrate into a simulation environment. 6) Train/apply a reinforcement-learning control policy within the simulated model. 7) Validate through experiments with full-stroke swings and real opponents.

Result: Demonstrated 0.8 s full-stroke swing cycle with a peak racket-center velocity of 22 m/s. The robot successfully defeated multiple professional players, indicating the approach can reach pro-level performance.

Conclusion: An effective hardware-aware design flow combining topology-optimized structure, physics-informed motor selection, delay-compensated joint dynamics, and RL control can produce a table tennis robot capable of beating professionals. Further work could address generalization across players, robustness to variability, serving variation, safety, and real-world deployment considerations.

Abstract: This paper focuses on the hardware specifications required for a table tennis robot to beat professional players. After analyzing the motions of elite players, we defined target specifications for the workspace, payload, external-force resistance, physical performance, serve capability, and end-effector accuracy. Based on these specifications, we developed "Ace", a custom 8-DoF robot. The mechanical structure was improved through topology optimization to minimize mass while preserving stiffness. Motor and gearbox selection was optimized using an inverse-dynamics torque model. Low-order per-joint dynamics models with delay compensation were identified and integrated into simulation to enable the use of an RL control policy. Experiments demonstrated repeated full-stroke swings with a cycle time of 0.8 s and a peak racket-center velocity of 22 m/s. The robot successfully defeated multiple professional players.

</details>


### [290] [LAMP: Lane-Aligned Motion Primitives for Feasible Trajectory Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26661)
*Sangjin Han,Hoseong Jung,Jeongtae Her,Changhyun Choi,H. Jin Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LAMP is a topology-aware, multimodal motion forecasting framework that aligns predictions with lane topology using a VQ-VAE learned set of motion primitives and a feasibility-aware selector, achieving similar accuracy to SOTA while improving feasibility and diversity on Argoverse 2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current predictors optimize displacement errors but neglect lane-topology constraints for multimodal outputs, leading to physically/logically inconsistent trajectories that are unsafe for planning.

Method: Train a vector-quantized variational autoencoder to learn discrete, shape-aware motion primitives (intention queries) that encode spatiotemporal patterns beyond endpoints. Introduce a feasibility-aware intention selector with a lane-topology prior to filter out unreachable intentions, guiding the decoder to favor topology-consistent outputs while maintaining behavioral diversity.

Result: Empirical evaluation on Argoverse 2 shows LAMP achieves competitive accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art baselines and outperforms them in feasibility and diversity metrics.

Conclusion: LAMP demonstrates that incorporating lane topology into the multimodal forecasting framework yields feasible, diverse trajectories without sacrificing predictive accuracy.

Abstract: Motion forecasting is essential for autonomous driving systems to enable safe decision-making and planning in complex driving scenarios. While existing predictors excel at minimizing standard displacement errors, they often overlook the adherence to lane topology of multimodal predictions, particularly for lower-probability modes. Consequently, predicted trajectories may violate physical and logical constraints, making the prediction set unreliable for safety-critical planning. In this paper, we propose LAMP (Lane-Aligned Motion Primitives), a topology-aware forecasting framework that anchors multimodal prediction to structured motion primitives aligned with lane topology. Specifically, we use a VQ-VAE to learn shape-aware motion primitives as discrete intention queries, capturing spatiotemporal patterns beyond endpoint-based intentions. We further introduce a feasibility-aware intention selector trained with a lane-topology prior for filtering unreachable intention queries, guiding the decoder to prioritize topology-consistent intentions while preserving behavioral diversity. Extensive experiments on the Argoverse 2 dataset demonstrate that LAMP achieves prediction accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art baselines while outperforming them in feasibility and diversity metrics.

</details>


### [291] [Tactile-WAM: Touch-Aware World Action Model with Tactile Asymmetric Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26663)
*Siyu Wu,Linjing You,Junjie Zhu,Yaozu Liu,Changhao Zhang,Jian Liu,Weiqiang Wang,Qi Li,Jituo Li,Hengshuang Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Tactile-WAM with TAAM mitigates tactile pollution in video-based WAMs; uses a VideoClean mask and a touch-aware bias; yields substantial improvements on ManiFeel, especially in contact-rich tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In contact-rich manipulation, visually plausible futures can be physically incomplete since tactile interactions (slip, jams, contact normals, misalignment) are weakly visible in RGB. Tactile information could improve decision-making, but unconstrained tactile tokens can degrade visual dynamics (tactile pollution).

Method: Introduce Tactile-WAM with a Tactile Asymmetric Attention Mechanism (TAAM): a VideoClean mask blocks video-query access to tactile key/value tokens while preserving action-query access, protecting visual prediction; a touch-aware bias derived from predicted touch changes modulates action attention toward tactile tokens during denoising.

Result: Mean success rate on ManiFeel improves by 38.9% overall and 86% on contact-rich tasks.

Conclusion: TAAM with VideoClean mitigates tactile pollution and enables tactile information to augment action generation in WAMs, improving performance on contact-rich manipulation tasks.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) generate actions together with predicted futures, offering a powerful interface for robot decision making. In contact-rich manipulation, however, visually plausible futures can be physically incomplete: insertion, assembly, search, and reorientation often depend on slip, jamming, contact normals, or small alignment errors that are weakly visible or hidden in RGB. A natural solution is to predict future tactile states, however, we identify tactile pollution, a failure mode where unconstrained tactile-token injection degrades video and action prediction by forcing a visual dynamics model to absorb sparse, local, event-driven contact signals. To address this, we propose Tactile-WAM, a touch-aware WAM with a Tactile Asymmetric Attention Mechanism (TAAM). TAAM combines a VideoClean mask, which blocks video-query access to tactile key/value tokens while preserving action-query access, with a touch-aware bias for action attention. The VideoClean mask protects visual prediction while keeping contact information available for action generation; the touch-aware bias is derived from predicted touch changes and modulates action attention to tactile tokens during denoising. On ManiFeel, Tactile-WAM improves the mean success rate by 38.9% overall and by 86% on contact-rich tasks.

</details>


### [292] [Learning Motion Feasibility from Point Clouds in Cluttered Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26700)
*Sajid Ansari,Arthi,Girish Varma,Antony Thomas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A large-scale learning-based motion feasibility predictor from RGB-D for a 7-DOF manipulator in clutter; introduces 2.7M labels across 88 objects and 190 cluttered scenes; compares MLP-, volumetric-CNN-, and point-cloud Transformer-based classifiers; GRASPFC-PTX (point-cloud Transformer) achieves AUROC 0.996 on novel objects and runs faster than sampling-based planners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Infeasibility in sampling-based motion planners (SBMPs) in cluttered environments leads to high computational cost; existing feasibility certification methods are limited to low-dimensional spaces and simplistic geometries. There is a need to learn feasibility directly from raw sensory data to improve planning efficiency in realistic scenes.

Method: Collection of a large RGB-D dataset with 2.7 million grasp feasibility labels across 88 scanned objects and 190 cluttered tabletop scenes. Benchmarks of three classifier families: MLP-based, volumetric-CNN, and point-cloud-based Transformer. Evaluation under matched training conditions; identification of GRASPFC-PTX as the best performer.

Result: Best model GRASPFC-PTX (a point-cloud Transformer) achieves AUROC 0.996 on novel objects and provides feasibility predictions significantly faster than SBMPs. The study establishes the first large-scale benchmark for learning motion feasibility from RGB-D in cluttered scenes.

Conclusion: Learning feasibility directly from raw RGB-D observations is viable for accelerating planning in cluttered environments. The introduced benchmark enables robust evaluation and comparison across architectures, with the point-cloud Transformer delivering superior generalization to novel objects and practical speed advantages over traditional planners.

Abstract: Motion feasibility prediction plays a central role in robotics, particularly in task and motion planning and manipulation. A major bottleneck for this problem in cluttered environments is that infeasible planning attempts by Sampling-based motion planners (SBMPs) can incur substantial computational cost. Also existing approaches for infeasibility certification are limited to low-dimensional configuration spaces and often assume simplified geometric environments represented by primitive objects with known parameters. We study the complementary problem of learning motion feasibility prediction directly from raw RGB-D observations for a 7-DOF manipulator operating in realistic cluttered scenes. We introduce the first large-scale benchmark for this setting, comprising 2.7M grasp feasibility labels over 88 scanned objects and 190 cluttered tabletop scenes. We benchmark three representative classifier families spanning MLP- based, volumetric-CNN, and point-cloud-based Transformer architectures under matched training conditions. Our best model, GRASPFC-PTX (a point-cloud transformer), achieves an AUROC of 0.996 on Novel objects while providing predictions significantly faster than SBMPs.

</details>


### [293] [PressMimic: Pressure-Guided Motion Capture and Control for Humanoid Robot Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26741)
*Yi Lu,Shenghao Ren,Tianyu Xiong,Zhaoxiang Li,Jiaqi Li,He Zhang,Tao Yu,Qiu Shen,Xun Cao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PressMimic integrates pressure sensing into perception and control for humanoid motion imitation, addressing contact dynamics to improve physical realism and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current pipelines rely on vision-based capture and kinematic imitation, often neglecting contact dynamics, leading to artifacts like foot sliding and floor penetration. Pressure data can provide explicit contact constraints to ground perception and control.

Method: Perception: FRAPPE++ fuses RGB and pressure to estimate 3D pose and global motion, leveraging pressure-derived contact constraints. Control: a pressure-supervised policy (PSP) uses pressure signals in reinforcement learning to enforce physically consistent contact patterns. Data: MotionPRO dataset with synchronized RGB, pressure, and motion capture.

Result: Pressure improves pose estimation accuracy, trajectory consistency, and execution stability in experiments.

Conclusion: Pressure serves as an effective physical grounding signal that bridges perception and control for more physically consistent humanoid motion imitation.

Abstract: Humanoid motion imitation requires not only accurate perception of human kinematics but also faithful reproduction of physical interactions with the environment. However, existing pipelines rely primarily on vision-based motion capture and kinematic imitation, largely ignoring contact dynamics, leading to artifacts such as foot sliding, floor penetration, and unstable behaviors. In this work, we revisit humanoid motion imitation from the perspective of physical grounding and leverage pressure as a unified modality across perception and control. We present PressMimic, a framework that integrates pressure into the full pipeline from motion capture to humanoid control. In the perception stage, we introduce FRAPPE++, a multimodal model that fuses RGB and pressure to jointly estimate 3D pose and global motion, where pressure provides explicit contact and support constraints to resolve ambiguity in vision-based estimation. In the control stage, we propose a pressure-supervised policy (PSP) that incorporates pressure-derived signals into reinforcement learning, enabling physically consistent contact patterns during execution. We further construct MotionPRO, a large-scale dataset with synchronized RGB, pressure, and motion capture data. Experiments show that pressure improves motion estimation accuracy, trajectory consistency, and execution stability. These results demonstrate that pressure serves as an effective physical grounding signal, bridging perception and control for physically consistent humanoid motion imitation.

</details>


### [294] [SSI-Policy: Learning Structured Scene Interfaces for Vision-Language Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26800)
*Kaijun Wang,Zikai Ouyang,Xuping Wu,Jinyi Hong,Wei Pan,Haibo Lu,Jia Pan,Wei Zhang,Linfang Zheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SSI-Policy introduces a modular RGB-only Structured Scene Interface (SSI) that unifies monocular depth cues, language-grounded object layouts, and instruction-conditioned 2D trajectories, enabling robot-agnostic, action-free pretraining and strong few-shot policy performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world robotic manipulation faces data scarcity and drift when relying on end-to-end or geometry-heavy approaches. A shared, RGB-only geometric representation that supports both perception and control can improve data efficiency and transfer across embodiments.

Method: Propose SSI-Policy built around SSI, a robot-agnostic RGB-only intermediate representation encoding monocular depth features, language-grounded object layouts, and instruction-conditioned 2D motion trajectories. Trainable from action-free video to decouple perception from control; downstream policy learns from few demonstrations. Evaluate on LIBERO with 10 demos per task and validate on 13 real-world tasks.

Result: On LIBERO, SSI-Policy improves over the strongest prior method by ~15% with only 10 demonstrations per task and remains competitive with methods using 50 demonstrations and large pretraining. Ablations show that geometric and motion cues within SSI provide complementary benefits. Real-world validation spans 13 tasks including spatial reasoning, cross-embodiment transfer, and contact-rich manipulation.

Conclusion: An RGB-only, robot-agnostic interface enabling perception-to-control decoupling yields effective few-shot manipulation by combining depth, language-grounded layout, and motion cues in a shared representation; the approach demonstrates data efficiency and broad real-world applicability.

Abstract: Real-world robotic manipulation demands spatial grounding, task-aware reasoning, and precise control. Learning such capabilities becomes particularly challenging in the low-data regime. Prior methods often trade off scalable task-level reasoning and explicit physical structure: video-based approaches can drift geometrically over long horizons, 3D approaches often require depth sensing, and many flow/trajectory interfaces emphasize motion without an explicit RGB-only geometric representation. We introduce SSI-Policy, a modular framework built around a Structured Scene Interface (SSI) -- a unified, RGB-only intermediate representation that jointly encodes monocular depth features, language-grounded object layouts, and instruction-conditioned 2D motion trajectories. Critically, SSI is robot-agnostic and trainable from action-free video, decoupling perception from control so that the downstream policy can learn from few demonstrations. On the LIBERO benchmark with only 10 demonstrations per task, SSI-Policy improves over the strongest prior method by nearly 15\% and remains competitive with 50-demo methods that leverage large-scale external pretraining. Ablations show that geometric and motion cues provide complementary benefits within the shared interface. We further validate on 13 real-world tasks spanning spatial reasoning, cross-embodiment transfer, and contact-rich manipulation.

</details>


### [295] [Improving Vision-Language-Action Model Fine-Tuning with Structured Stage and Keyframe Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26801)
*Yuan Xu,Yixiang Chen,Kai Wang,Jiabing Yang,Peiyan Li,Qisen Ma,Yan Huang,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: StaKe adds two lightweight auxiliary heads (stage classifier and keyframe predictor) to Vision-Language-Action models to provide structured, automatic supervision during fine-tuning, improving long-horizon robotic manipulation by concentrating learning on manipulation stages and next gripper transitions without changing the base policy at inference time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA fine-tuning applies uniform action supervision across all timesteps, neglecting manipulation stages and transition targets, which leads to failures at challenging gripper-event transitions and long-horizon planning.

Method: Introduces StaKe as a plug-in auxiliary supervision module that derives two signals from demonstration gripper states: (1) a stage classifier identifying the current manipulation stage, and (2) a keyframe predictor estimating the target joint action at the next gripper transition. Both are lightweight auxiliary heads trained alongside the base VLA policy, enriching representations during training while leaving the inference architecture unchanged.

Result: Empirical evaluation on bimanual simulation and single-arm Franka real-robot tasks shows consistent improvements in success rates (relative gains of 14% and 56%, respectively), with larger gains on longer-horizon tasks with more gripper-event transitions. Ablation studies corroborate the contribution of each design choice, and qualitative analysis shows learned representations faithfully track manipulation stages.

Conclusion: Structured, plug-in supervision for VLA fine-tuning is an effective and general strategy to enhance long-horizon manipulation performance, enabling better handling of gripper-event transitions without altering the underlying policy or inference pipeline.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for generalizable robotic manipulation. During fine-tuning, however, action supervision applies equally across all timesteps, without structured supervision on which manipulation stage the robot is in or what the next gripper-event target should be. This causes failures to concentrate around challenging gripper-event transitions. To address this, we propose StaKe, a plug-in auxiliary supervision framework that automatically derives two complementary signals from demonstration gripper states without manual annotation: a stage classifier that identifies the current manipulation stage, and a keyframe predictor that estimates the target joint action at the next gripper transition. Both are modeled as lightweight auxiliary heads that enrich the learned representations during training, while leaving the base VLA policy architecture and inference loop unchanged. Experiments on bimanual simulation and single-arm Franka real-robot tasks show that StaKe consistently improves success rates (relative gains of 14% and 56%, respectively), with larger improvements on longer-horizon tasks that involve more gripper-event transitions. Ablation studies validate each design choice, and qualitative analysis confirms that the learned representations faithfully track manipulation stages. These results indicate that structured supervision is an effective and general strategy for enhancing VLA fine-tuning in long-horizon manipulation. Project website: https://hi-yuanxu.github.io/StaKe-Web/

</details>


### [296] [Ordinal Neural Collapse as a Representation Prior for Visual Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26839)
*E-In Son,Jung-Taak Kim,Seung-Woo Seo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ORION imposes an ordinal structure on the visual encoder’s latent space to align representations with navigation action order, reducing action-agnostic features. The pretrained encoder is integrated into a diffusion-based navigation pipeline and fine-tuned end-to-end, yielding robust goal-directed navigation in varied and cluttered environments, outperforming end-to-end and neural collapse baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end imitation learning often yields action-agnostic, entangled representations due to indirect supervision from a single action loss. This problem worsens with diverse environments and visual distractors, leading to inconsistent actions at decision points. An ordinally structured latent space can align representations with the intrinsic order of ego-centric navigation actions (e.g., Far Left to Far Right) to improve decision certainty.

Method: Propose ORION (Ordinal Neural Collapse for Visual Navigation). The encoder’s latent space is organized along a single discriminative axis that encodes the ordinal action structure; neighboring actions share similar visual contexts while distant actions diverge in appearance. The approach encourages within-class variance suppression off the discriminative axis. A pretrained encoder is integrated into a diffusion-based navigation framework, and the full system is fine-tuned end-to-end.

Result: Empirical validation in simulation and real-world experiments shows ORION consistently improves navigation success rate and goal progress over strong baselines (end-to-end and neural-collapse-based) with pronounced gains in visually challenging settings such as complex multi-way intersections.

Conclusion: Structuring the encoder’s latent space with an ordinal navigation-action axis reduces ambiguity in visual representations, yielding more reliable, goal-directed navigation. The combination with diffusion-based planning further enhances robustness across diverse environments.

Abstract: Learning robust navigation policies directly from visual observations remains a fundamental challenge in vision-based robotic navigation. In end-to-end imitation learning approaches, the visual encoder and action decoder are jointly optimized using a single action loss, which provides only an indirect supervisory signal to the encoder. This indirect supervision frequently results in the encoder learning ambiguous, action-agnostic representations. The problem is further complicated by substantial variations in scene structure and appearance across diverse environments, as well as the prevalence of visual distractors inherent to real-world navigation settings. Such action-agnostic features cause the navigation policy to produce inconsistent actions at ambiguous decision points, leading to navigation failure. To overcome these limitations, we propose ORION (Ordinal Neural Collapse for Visual Navigation), a method that explicitly organizes the encoder's representation space according to the ordinal structure of navigation actions. In the context of goal-directed navigation, ego-centric control categories from Far Left to Far Right exhibit a natural ordinal relationship in which neighboring classes share similar visual contexts, while semantically opposing classes differ substantially in appearance. We encourage class representations to be arranged sequentially along a single discriminative axis, while suppressing off-axis variance within each class. The pretrained encoder is then integrated into a diffusion-based navigation framework, and the full pipeline is fine-tuned end-to-end. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world settings show that ORION consistently outperforms end-to-end and neural collapse baselines in navigation success rate and goal progress, with notable gains in visually challenging scenarios such as complex multi-way intersections.

</details>


### [297] [Humanoid-DART: Humanoid Loco-Manipulation using Diffusion-guided Augmentation through Relabeling and Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26855)
*Pranav Debbad,Kanish Thiagarajan,Victor Dhédin,Shafeef Omar,Majid Khadiv*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A self-supervised approach for humanoid loco-manipulation that bootstraps from sparse demonstrations to learn a goal-conditioned policy capable of automatic goal-space exploration, using diffusion-based trajectory generation tracked by reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the high cost of collecting diverse demonstrations and minimize continual human intervention while expanding the agent's behavioral repertoire.

Method: The framework bootstraps from sparse demonstrations and uses diffusion-based trajectory generation to propose goal-conditioned trajectories, which are then tracked by reinforcement learning to learn a policy. This combination enables automatic exploration of the goal space with minimal expert supervision and progressive expansion of skills.

Result: Extensive ablation studies and comparisons with state-of-the-art methods demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework across multiple humanoid loco-manipulation skills.

Conclusion: A self-supervised diffusion-guided planning plus RL framework can scale humanoid loco-manipulation by automatically exploring goals and expanding capabilities with limited expert input.
